topics.title: Sinusoidal and Non-Sinusoidal Wave Shapes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES & CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sinusoidal and Non-Sinusoidal Wave Shapes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Sinusoidal Wave Shapes — Formal Definition

A **Sinusoidal Wave** is a periodic continuous wave whose instantaneous amplitude varies as a function of the sine (or cosine) of a linear time argument. It is the most fundamental waveform in electrical engineering, mathematical physics, and signal processing because it is the natural response of linear systems containing only energy-storage elements (inductors and capacitors).

Mathematically, a generalized sinusoidal signal is expressed as:

$$v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t \pm \phi)$$

Where:
- $V_m$ = Maximum (Peak) amplitude expressed in **Volts (V)**
- $\omega = 2\pi f$ = Angular frequency in **radians per second (rad/s)**
- $f$ = Frequency in **Hertz (Hz)**, $f = \frac{1}{T}$
- $T$ = Time period in **seconds (s)**
- $\phi$ = Phase shift in **radians (rad)**
- $t$ = Time in **seconds (s)**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A sine wave is the only waveform that retains its shape when passed through a linear time-invariant (LTI) network — only its amplitude and phase change. This property is called **Eigenfunction Property of Sinusoids**.

## 1.2 Non-Sinusoidal Wave Shapes — Formal Definition

A **Non-Sinusoidal Wave** is any periodic waveform whose amplitude does not follow a pure sine or cosine function. These are common in digital electronics, power electronics, signal processing, and communication systems. Standard non-sinusoidal waveforms include:

| Waveform | Origin in Engineering |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Square Wave** | Clock pulses in digital ICs, PWM signals |
| **Triangular Wave** | Function generator outputs, sweep generators |
| **Sawtooth Wave** | CRO time-base generators, TV deflection |
| **Pulse Train** | Radar, digital data transmission, control signals |
| **Full-Wave Rectified Sine** | Output of bridge rectifiers in power supplies |
| **Half-Wave Rectified Sine** | Output of half-wave rectifier circuits |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine a **pendulum clock** swinging smoothly — the arc it traces follows a perfect sine curve. This is a **sinusoidal** motion.
> Now imagine a **traffic signal** — it is either fully ON (red) or fully OFF (green) with sudden transitions. This is a **square (non-sinusoidal)** wave.
> A **swing** at a playground that goes up and down in a straight, even pace produces a **triangular** motion (non-sinusoidal).

**Physical Constants & Metrics Used:**
- Standard AC mains frequency in India: $f = 50$ **Hz**
- Standard AC mains frequency in USA: $f = 60$ **Hz**
- Peak-to-RMS ratio for a pure sine wave: $K_p = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Study This Topic?**
> Whether you are designing a **power supply**, an **audio amplifier**, or a **communication transmitter**, you must know the *effective (heating) value* and the *average value* of the waveform to size components like transformers, fuses, and filter capacitors correctly. This is why KTU emphasizes this topic in Module 1.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Key Waveform Parameters — Concept Breakdown

For any periodic waveform $v(t)$ with period $T$, four critical metrics must be evaluated by every KTU examiner:

### Step 1 — Average Value (DC Component)
The **Average Value** $V_{avg}$ (also called DC value or mean value) is the area under one complete cycle of the waveform divided by the period.

$$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v(t)\, dt$$

**Why it matters:** A pure AC sine wave has $V_{avg} = 0$ because positive and negative halves cancel. But a **rectified** sine (used in DC power supplies) has a non-zero average value — this is the actual DC voltage you get from a rectifier.

### Step 2 — RMS (Root Mean Square) Value
The **RMS Value** $V_{rms}$ represents the equivalent DC value that would produce the same heating effect (or power dissipation in a resistor).

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t)\, dt}$$

**Why it matters:** When your multimeter displays "230 V AC," it is showing the **RMS** value, not the peak. The peak is actually $230 \times \sqrt{2} \approx 325$ V.

### Step 3 — Form Factor ($K_f$)
The ratio of RMS value to Average value. It is a measure of the "shape" or "peakiness" of the waveform.

$$K_f = \frac{V_{rms}}{V_{avg}}$$

### Step 4 — Peak / Crest / Amplitude Factor ($K_p$)
The ratio of Peak value to RMS value. Indicates how "spiky" the waveform is.

$$K_p = \frac{V_m}{V_{rms}}$$

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers 90% of KTU Module 1 numerical questions.**

| Waveform | $V_{avg}$ | $V_{rms}$ | Form Factor $K_f$ | Peak Factor $K_p$ |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Pure Sine Wave** | $\frac{2V_m}{\pi}$ | $\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ | $\frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} \approx 1.11$ | $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$ |
| **Half-Wave Rectified Sine** | $\frac{V_m}{\pi}$ | $\frac{V_m}{2}$ | $\frac{\pi}{2} \approx 1.571$ | $2$ |
| **Full-Wave Rectified Sine** | $\frac{2V_m}{\pi}$ | $\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$ | $\frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} \approx 1.11$ | $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$ |
| **Square Wave (Symmetric)** | $V_m$ | $V_m$ | $1$ | $1$ |
| **Triangular Wave (Symmetric)** | $\frac{V_m}{2}$ | $\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{3}}$ | $\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.155$ | $\sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$ |
| **Sawtooth Wave** | $\frac{V_m}{2}$ | $\frac{V_m}{\sqrt{3}}$ | $\frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.155$ | $\sqrt{3} \approx 1.732$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - In **power transformer design**, $V_{rms}$ is used because heating depends on $I^{2}R$ loss.
> - In **DC power supply design**, $V_{avg}$ of the rectified output determines the DC load voltage.
> - In **insulation design**, the **peak value** $V_m$ determines the maximum voltage stress.

## 2.3 Phasor Representation of Sinusoids

A sinusoid can be represented as a **rotating phasor** in the complex plane, which is the foundation of AC circuit analysis:

$$v(t) = V_m \cos(\omega t + \phi) \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \mathbf{V} = V_m \angle \phi$$

The phasor rotates counter-clockwise at angular velocity $\omega$. The instantaneous value is the projection of the rotating phasor onto the real axis.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: RMS Value of a Pure Sine Wave

**Given:** $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$, with $T = \frac{2\pi}{\omega}$.

By definition:

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t)\, dt}$$

Substituting $v(t)$:

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} V_{m}^{2} \sin^{2}(\omega t)\, dt}$$

Using the trigonometric identity $\sin^{2}(\theta) = \frac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$:

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{V_{m}^{2}}{T} \int_{0}^{T} \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2}\, dt}$$

Splitting the integral:

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{V_{m}^{2}}{2T} \left[ \int_{0}^{T} 1\, dt - \int_{0}^{T} \cos(2\omega t)\, dt \right]}$$

Evaluating each integral:

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{V_{m}^{2}}{2T} \left[ T - \left. \frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2\omega} \right|_{0}^{T} \right]}$$

Since $\sin(2\omega T) = \sin(4\pi) = 0$ and $\sin(0) = 0$:

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{V_{m}^{2}}{2T} \cdot T} = \sqrt{\frac{V_{m}^{2}}{2}}$$

$$\boxed{V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707\, V_m}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Average Value of a Full-Wave Rectified Sine Wave

**Given:** $v(t) = \vert V_m \sin(\omega t) \vert$ over one cycle.

By definition:

$$V_{avg} = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v(t)\, dt$$

For a full-wave rectified sine, the period becomes $T/2$ (the negative half flips up):

$$V_{avg} = \frac{2}{T} \int_{0}^{T/2} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, dt$$

Pulling constants out:

$$V_{avg} = \frac{2V_m}{T} \int_{0}^{T/2} \sin(\omega t)\, dt$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$V_{avg} = \frac{2V_m}{T} \left[ -\frac{\cos(\omega t)}{\omega} \right]_{0}^{T/2}$$

$$V_{avg} = \frac{2V_m}{T\omega} \left[ -\cos\!\left(\frac{\omega T}{2}\right) + \cos(0) \right]$$

Since $\omega T = 2\pi$, we have $\cos(\pi) = -1$ and $\cos(0) = 1$:

$$V_{avg} = \frac{2V_m}{T\omega} \left[ -(-1) + 1 \right] = \frac{2V_m}{T\omega} \cdot 2$$

Since $T\omega = 2\pi$:

$$V_{avg} = \frac{4V_m}{2\pi} = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}$$

$$\boxed{V_{avg} = \frac{2V_m}{\pi} \approx 0.637\, V_m}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: RMS Value of a Square Wave

**Given:** A symmetric square wave alternating between $+V_m$ and $-V_m$.

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t)\, dt}$$

The wave is $+V_m$ for $0 \le t < T/2$ and $-V_m$ for $T/2 \le t < T$. Squaring removes the sign:

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \left[ \int_{0}^{T/2} V_{m}^{2}\, dt + \int_{T/2}^{T} V_{m}^{2}\, dt \right]}$$

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \left[ V_{m}^{2} \cdot \frac{T}{2} + V_{m}^{2} \cdot \frac{T}{2} \right]}$$

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \cdot V_{m}^{2} \cdot T} = \sqrt{V_{m}^{2}}$$

$$\boxed{V_{rms} = V_m}$$

This means a square wave delivers the **same power as a DC source** of magnitude $V_m$ — a unique property of square waves.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: RMS Value of a Triangular Wave

**Given:** A symmetric triangular wave rising from $-V_m$ to $+V_m$ in $T/2$, then falling back.

The wave equation over $0 \le t \le T/2$ is:

$$v(t) = \frac{4V_m}{T} t - V_m$$

Squaring:

$$v^{2}(t) = \left( \frac{4V_m}{T} t - V_m \right)^{2} = \frac{16 V_{m}^{2}}{T^{2}} t^{2} - \frac{8 V_{m}^{2}}{T} t + V_{m}^{2}$$

RMS over the first half (by symmetry equals full cycle):

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{2}{T} \int_{0}^{T/2} v^{2}(t)\, dt$$

Substituting:

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{2}{T} \int_{0}^{T/2} \left( \frac{16 V_{m}^{2}}{T^{2}} t^{2} - \frac{8 V_{m}^{2}}{T} t + V_{m}^{2} \right) dt$$

Evaluating term by term:

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{2V_{m}^{2}}{T} \left[ \frac{16}{T^{2}} \cdot \frac{t^{3}}{3} - \frac{8}{T} \cdot \frac{t^{2}}{2} + t \right]_{0}^{T/2}$$

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{2V_{m}^{2}}{T} \left[ \frac{16}{3T^{2}} \cdot \frac{T^{3}}{8} - \frac{4}{T} \cdot \frac{T^{2}}{4} + \frac{T}{2} \right]$$

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{2V_{m}^{2}}{T} \left[ \frac{2T}{3} - T + \frac{T}{2} \right] = \frac{2V_{m}^{2}}{T} \cdot \frac{T}{3} = \frac{2V_{m}^{2}}{3}$$

$$\boxed{V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.577\, V_m}$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Form Factor and Peak Factor for a Sine Wave

**Form Factor:**

$$K_f = \frac{V_{rms}}{V_{avg}} = \frac{V_m / \sqrt{2}}{2V_m / \pi} = \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}}$$

$$\boxed{K_f = \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} \approx 1.11}$$

**Peak (Crest) Factor:**

$$K_p = \frac{V_m}{V_{rms}} = \frac{V_m}{V_m / \sqrt{2}}$$

$$\boxed{K_p = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Waveform Classification Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Periodic Waveform V of t] --> B{Is it a pure sine or cosine?}
    B -- YES --> C[Sinusoidal Wave]
    B -- NO --> D[Non-Sinusoidal Wave]
    C --> C1[Pure Sine V m sin wt]
    C --> C2[Phase Shifted V m sin wt plus phi]
    C --> C3[Cosine V m cos wt]
    D --> D1[Polygonal Shapes]
    D --> D2[Rectified Shapes]
    D --> D3[Pulse Shapes]
    D1 --> D1a[Square Wave]
    D1 --> D1b[Triangular Wave]
    D1 --> D1c[Sawtooth Wave]
    D2 --> D2a[Half Wave Rectified Sine]
    D2 --> D2b[Full Wave Rectified Sine]
    D3 --> D3a[Single Pulse]
    D3 --> D3b[Pulse Train with Duty Cycle]
```

## 4.2 RMS Value Calculation Process (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1: Identify v of t over one period T] --> P2[Step 2: Square the function v squared t]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Integrate v squared t over 0 to T]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Divide by T to get Mean Square]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Take square root to obtain V rms]
    P5 --> P6[Output: Effective DC Equivalent Value]
```

## 4.3 Time-Domain vs Phasor-Domain Mapping (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TD[Time Domain: v of t equals V m sin wt] --> CONV[Phasor Transform]
    CONV --> PD[Phasor Domain: V equals V m angle zero]
    PD --> APPLY[Apply Ohm Law: I equals V over Z]
    APPLY --> ICONV[Inverse Phasor Transform]
    ICONV --> ITD[Time Domain: i of t equals I m sin wt minus phi]
```

## 4.4 Waveform Shape vs Numerical Coefficients (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Choose Waveform] --> SW{Square Wave?}
    SW -- YES --> S1[K f equals 1, K p equals 1]
    SW -- NO --> TR{Triangular Wave?}
    TR -- YES --> T1[K f equals 1.155, K p equals 1.732]
    TR -- NO --> SI{Pure Sine Wave?}
    SI -- YES --> SI1[K f equals 1.11, K p equals 1.414]
    SI -- NO --> HWH{Half Wave Rectified?}
    HWH -- YES --> HWH1[K f equals 1.571, K p equals 2]
    HWH -- NO --> FWH[Full Wave Rectified: K f equals 1.11, K p equals 1.414]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** These Mermaid block diagrams map the logical classification and computational pipeline for waveform analysis. They are designed to replace complex physical drawings with clear functional architecture that is exam-friendly and easy to reproduce from memory.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define the following terms with respect to a periodic waveform:**
**(a) RMS value** **(b) Form factor** **(c) Peak factor**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) RMS Value:** The Root Mean Square value of a periodic waveform $v(t)$ is the square root of the mean of the square of the instantaneous values over one complete cycle. It represents the equivalent DC value that produces the same heating effect (or $I^{2}R$ power dissipation) in a resistor.

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t)\, dt}$$

**(b) Form Factor ($K_f$):** It is defined as the ratio of the RMS value to the average value of the waveform:

$$K_f = \frac{V_{rms}}{V_{avg}}$$

For a pure sine wave, $K_f = \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} \approx 1.11$.

**(c) Peak Factor ($K_p$):** Also called the Crest Factor or Amplitude Factor, it is the ratio of the maximum (peak) value of the waveform to its RMS value:

$$K_p = \frac{V_m}{V_{rms}}$$

For a pure sine wave, $K_p = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**What is a sinusoidal waveform? List any four properties of a sinusoid.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Sinusoidal Waveform** is a periodic waveform whose instantaneous amplitude varies sinusoidally with time, mathematically expressed as $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)$.

**Four Key Properties:**
1. **Eigenfunction Property:** A sine wave, when passed through any linear time-invariant (LTI) system, emerges as a sine wave of the *same frequency* but with possibly altered amplitude and phase.
2. **Zero Average Value:** Over one complete cycle, the average value of a pure sine wave is zero (positive half cancels negative half).
3. **RMS to Peak Ratio:** $V_{rms} = V_m / \sqrt{2}$, and Peak Factor $K_p = \sqrt{2}$.
4. **Orthogonality:** Sine waves of different frequencies are mathematically orthogonal over a complete period, which is the basis of Fourier analysis.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a) Derive the RMS and average values of a full-wave rectified sinusoidal waveform. Hence calculate its form factor and peak factor.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

For a full-wave rectified sine wave, $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$ for $0 \le t \le \pi/\omega$, and the negative half is also flipped to positive. The effective period is $T/2$.

**Average Value:**

$$V_{avg} = \frac{2}{T} \int_{0}^{T/2} V_m \sin(\omega t)\, dt$$

$$V_{avg} = \frac{2V_m}{T} \left[ -\frac{\cos(\omega t)}{\omega} \right]_{0}^{T/2} = \frac{2V_m}{T\omega} [-\cos(\pi) + \cos(0)]$$

$$V_{avg} = \frac{2V_m}{2\pi} \cdot 2 = \frac{2V_m}{\pi} \approx 0.637\, V_m$$

**RMS Value:**

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{2}{T} \int_{0}^{T/2} V_{m}^{2} \sin^{2}(\omega t)\, dt = \frac{V_{m}^{2}}{T} \int_{0}^{T/2} [1 - \cos(2\omega t)]\, dt$$

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{V_{m}^{2}}{T} \left[ t - \frac{\sin(2\omega t)}{2\omega} \right]_{0}^{T/2} = \frac{V_{m}^{2}}{T} \cdot \frac{T}{2} = \frac{V_{m}^{2}}{2}$$

$$V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707\, V_m$$

**Form Factor:**

$$K_f = \frac{V_{rms}}{V_{avg}} = \frac{V_m / \sqrt{2}}{2V_m / \pi} = \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{2}} \approx 1.11$$

**Peak Factor:**

$$K_p = \frac{V_m}{V_{rms}} = \frac{V_m}{V_m / \sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$$

---

**(b) A half-wave rectified sinusoidal voltage has a peak value of 311 V. Calculate its average value, RMS value, form factor, and peak factor.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $V_m = 311$ V, $T = $ full period of original sine.

**Average Value of Half-Wave Rectified Sine:**

$$V_{avg} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} = \frac{311}{\pi} = 99.0\, \text{V}$$

**RMS Value:**

$$V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{2} = \frac{311}{2} = 155.5\, \text{V}$$

**Form Factor:**

$$K_f = \frac{V_{rms}}{V_{avg}} = \frac{155.5}{99.0} = 1.571$$

**Peak Factor:**

$$K_p = \frac{V_m}{V_{rms}} = \frac{311}{155.5} = 2.0$$

**Final Answers:** $V_{avg} = 99$ V, $V_{rms} = 155.5$ V, $K_f = 1.571$, $K_p = 2.0$.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme — Alternative Choice]

**(a) Derive the RMS value of a triangular waveform with peak value $V_m$. Also find its form factor.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

For a symmetric triangular wave rising linearly from $-V_m$ to $+V_m$ over $T/2$:

$$v(t) = \frac{4V_m}{T} t - V_m \quad \text{for } 0 \le t \le T/2$$

**RMS Calculation:**

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{2}{T} \int_{0}^{T/2} \left( \frac{4V_m}{T} t - V_m \right)^{2} dt$$

Expanding the square:

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{2}{T} \int_{0}^{T/2} \left( \frac{16 V_{m}^{2}}{T^{2}} t^{2} - \frac{8 V_{m}^{2}}{T} t + V_{m}^{2} \right) dt$$

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{2V_{m}^{2}}{T} \left[ \frac{16}{3T^{2}} \cdot \frac{T^{3}}{8} - \frac{4}{T} \cdot \frac{T^{2}}{4} + \frac{T}{2} \right]$$

$$V_{rms}^{2} = \frac{2V_{m}^{2}}{T} \left[ \frac{2T}{3} - T + \frac{T}{2} \right] = \frac{2V_{m}^{2}}{T} \cdot \frac{T}{3} = \frac{2 V_{m}^{2}}{3}$$

$$\boxed{V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{3}}}$$

**Form Factor:**

For a symmetric triangular wave, $V_{avg} = V_m / 2$ (since it spends equal time above and below zero, and the mean of a symmetric triangular wave over a half cycle is $V_m/2$).

$$K_f = \frac{V_{rms}}{V_{avg}} = \frac{V_m / \sqrt{3}}{V_m / 2} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 1.155$$

---

**(b) A 50 Hz sinusoidal voltage of peak value 200 V is applied across a 100 $\Omega$ resistor. Calculate the average power dissipated over one cycle.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $f = 50$ Hz, $V_m = 200$ V, $R = 100\, \Omega$.

**Step 1: Compute RMS Voltage**

$$V_{rms} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{200}{\sqrt{2}} = 141.42\, \text{V}$$

**Step 2: Compute RMS Current**

$$I_{rms} = \frac{V_{rms}}{R} = \frac{141.42}{100} = 1.4142\, \text{A}$$

**Step 3: Compute Average Power**

The average power dissipated in a resistor is:

$$P_{avg} = V_{rms} \cdot I_{rms} = I_{rms}^{2} \cdot R$$

$$P_{avg} = (1.4142)^{2} \times 100 = 2 \times 100 = 200\, \text{W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{avg} = 200\, \text{W}}$$

**Verification using direct integration:**

$$P = \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} \frac{v^{2}(t)}{R}\, dt = \frac{V_{m}^{2}}{2R} = \frac{(200)^{2}}{200} = 200\, \text{W} \;\;\checkmark$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Integrating over wrong limits:** For half-wave rectified signals, integrate from $0$ to $T$ (full period), but $v(t) = 0$ during the negative half. For full-wave rectified, integrate from $0$ to $T/2$ and multiply by $2$ — students often lose 2–3 marks here.
> 2. **Forgetting to take square root:** Many students write the **Mean Square** value but forget the final $\sqrt{}$ to obtain RMS. This is a guaranteed **1-mark penalty**.
> 3. **Mixing up Form Factor and Peak Factor:** $K_f = V_{rms} / V_{avg}$ and $K_p = V_m / V_{rms}$. A common mistake is swapping them. **Memorize:** "Form Factor uses Average; Peak Factor uses Peak."
> 4. **Units confusion:** Peak value is in **V**, RMS is in **V**, Average is in **V**, but Form Factor and Peak Factor are **dimensionless** (no units).
> 5. **Average of pure sine wave = 0:** If a question asks for $V_{avg}$ of an *un-rectified* sine wave, the answer is **zero** — do not write $2V_m/\pi$ unless it is rectified.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam:**

- **Sinusoidal Signal Equation:** $v(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t + \phi)$, with $\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi/T$.
- **Average Value of Pure Sine:** $V_{avg} = 0$ (over full cycle).
- **RMS of Pure Sine:** $V_{rms} = V_m / \sqrt{2}$.
- **Average of Half-Wave Rectified Sine:** $V_{avg} = V_m / \pi$.
- **RMS of Half-Wave Rectified Sine:** $V_{rms} = V_m / 2$.
- **Average of Full-Wave Rectified Sine:** $V_{avg} = 2V_m / \pi$.
- **RMS of Full-Wave Rectified Sine:** $V_{rms} = V_m / \sqrt{2}$.
- **RMS of Square Wave:** $V_{rms} = V_m$.
- **RMS of Triangular Wave:** $V_{rms} = V_m / \sqrt{3}$.
- **Form Factor of Pure Sine:** $K_f = \pi / (2\sqrt{2}) \approx 1.11$.
- **Peak Factor of Pure Sine:** $K_p = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$.
- **Average Power in Resistor:** $P_{avg} = V_{rms}^{2} / R = I_{rms}^{2} R = V_{rms} I_{rms}$.
- **Phasor Transform:** $v(t) = V_m \cos(\omega t + \phi) \Longleftrightarrow \mathbf{V} = V_m \angle \phi$.
- **Eigenfunction Property:** Sine waves survive linear systems unchanged in shape — only amplitude and phase change.
- **Indian Mains:** $V_{rms} = 230$ V, $f = 50$ Hz $\Rightarrow V_m \approx 325$ V.
- **Multimeter AC reading:** Always shows **RMS**, never peak.
- **Heating Effect:** RMS value gives the same $I^{2}R$ heating as a DC source of equal magnitude.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Strategy Tip:** In Part B, always state the *formulas first*, then substitute, then compute. Examiners give **step marks** even if the final numerical answer is wrong. Showing the substitution of trigonometric identities like $\sin^{2}\theta = (1 - \cos 2\theta)/2$ earns full credit for the integration step.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle and Working of RC Differentiating and Integrating Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES & CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Principle and Working of RC Differentiating and Integrating Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **RC Linear Network** is a first-order circuit comprising a single resistor ($R$) and a single capacitor ($C$) energized by a time-varying source. When the energy-storage elements are strategically chosen and the **time constant** $\tau = RC$ is made either much smaller or much larger than the period of the input waveform, the network behaves as a mathematical **differentiator** or **integrator** respectively.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> An **RC Differentiating Circuit** is a high-pass RC network in which the output voltage is approximately proportional to the **time derivative** of the input voltage, achieved by making $RC \ll T$ (where $T$ is the time period of the input).
>
> An **RC Integrating Circuit** is a low-pass RC network in which the output voltage is approximately proportional to the **time integral** of the input voltage, achieved by making $RC \gg T$.

## 1.2 The Two Canonical Topologies

| Property | Differentiator (High-Pass) | Integrator (Low-Pass) |
|---|---|---|
| Series Element | Capacitor $C$ | Resistor $R$ |
| Shunt Element (Output) | Resistor $R$ | Capacitor $C$ |
| Output Taken Across | $R$ | $C$ |
| Time Constant Condition | $RC \ll T$ | $RC \gg T$ |
| Mathematical Operation | $\text{Output} \propto \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$ | $\text{Output} \propto \int V_{in} \, dt$ |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Speedometer & The Odometer"

To make this concept unforgettable, think of a car in motion:

- **Differentiator = Speedometer**: A speedometer does not tell you *where* the car is; it tells you *how fast the position is changing*. It differentiates position with respect to time. Similarly, an RC differentiator ignores the steady DC level of a waveform and reacts **only to the rate of change** (the edges/slopes).

- **Integrator = Odometer**: An odometer accumulates the distance traveled by integrating speed over time. Similarly, an RC integrator sums up the area under an input waveform, producing a smooth, slowly-varying ramp. It **reacts to duration and area**, not to sharp changes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics:**
> - Time constant: $\tau = RC$, measured in **seconds (s)**
> - Cut-off frequency of the RC network: $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$, measured in **Hertz (Hz)**
> - Standard differentiating condition: $f_{in} \gg f_c$ (operating well above cut-off)
> - Standard integrating condition: $f_{in} \ll f_c$ (operating well below cut-off)

## 1.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These passive networks are the fundamental building blocks of:

- **Pulse and Digital Circuits** (squaring circuits, edge detectors, monostable multivibrators)
- **Analog Computers** (solving differential equations)
- **Wave-Shaping Circuits** (converting square waves to spikes or triangular waves)
- **Signal Conditioning in Instrumentation** (noise filtering, derivative control in PID controllers)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

### A. RC Differentiating Circuit (C-R Configuration)

1. **Topology Setup**: A capacitor $C$ is placed in series with the signal path; a resistor $R$ is connected from the output node to ground.
2. **Voltage Division**: At high frequencies, the capacitor's reactance $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$ becomes very small. Almost the entire input voltage drops across $R$.
3. **Boundary Condition**: If $RC \ll T$, the voltage across $C$ is negligibly small. The capacitor's charge $q = C \cdot V_C$ changes very rapidly.
4. **Current Surge**: The current through the circuit is $i = C \dfrac{dV_C}{dt} \approx C \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$.
5. **Output**: $V_{out} = iR \approx RC \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$. The output is a scaled **derivative** of the input.

### B. RC Integrating Circuit (R-C Configuration)

1. **Topology Setup**: A resistor $R$ is in series with the signal path; a capacitor $C$ is connected from the output node to ground.
2. **Voltage Division**: At low frequencies, the capacitor's reactance becomes very large. Almost the entire input voltage drops across $C$.
3. **Boundary Condition**: If $RC \gg T$, the voltage across $R$ is negligibly small. The capacitor charges very slowly (essentially linearly).
4. **Current Flow**: The current is $i = \dfrac{V_R}{R} \approx \dfrac{V_{in}}{R}$ (a constant during each half-cycle of a square wave).
5. **Output**: $V_{out} = \dfrac{1}{C} \int i \, dt = \dfrac{1}{RC} \int V_{in} \, dt$. The output is a scaled **integral** of the input.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| S.No. | Parameter / Equation | Differentiator | Integrator |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Output Element | $V_{out}$ across $R$ | $V_{out}$ across $C$ |
| 2 | Time Constant Condition | $RC \ll T$ | $RC \gg T$ |
| 3 | Frequency Condition | $f \gg f_c$ | $f \ll f_c$ |
| 4 | Differential Equation | $RC \dfrac{dV_{out}}{dt} + V_{out} = RC \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$ | $RC \dfrac{dV_{out}}{dt} + V_{out} = V_{in}$ |
| 5 | Ideal Mathematical Output | $V_{out} = RC \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$ | $V_{out} = \dfrac{1}{RC} \int V_{in} \, dt$ |
| 6 | Transfer Function $H(s)$ | $\dfrac{sRC}{1 + sRC}$ | $\dfrac{1}{1 + sRC}$ |
| 7 | Cut-off Frequency | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ |
| 8 | Magnitude $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$ | $\dfrac{\omega RC}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}$ |
| 9 | Square Wave Response | Positive & negative spikes at edges | Slow exponential ramps (triangular approximation) |
| 10 | Sine Wave Response | Cosine wave (90° lead, scaled) | Negative cosine wave (90° lag, scaled) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid for the KTU Board Exam**: "**C comes first for Derivative**" — In the differentiator, the **C**apacitor is the series element. Think "**C**apacitor **D**ifferentiator" — both start with consonants that need a "kick" of current to react.

## 2.3 Why the Conditions $RC \ll T$ and $RC \gg T$ Matter

The **Relative Magnitude** of the time constant $\tau = RC$ to the input period $T$ determines the operational mode:

- If $\tau$ is **small** ($\tau \to 0$): The capacitor charges/discharges almost instantly. The output $V_R = iR$ spikes only during *transitions* of the input — these are the derivatives of step changes.
- If $\tau$ is **large** ($\tau \to \infty$): The capacitor cannot fully charge. Its voltage follows the *average* of the input current over time — this is mathematically equivalent to integration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility**: In communication receivers, integrators act as **low-pass filters** that average out high-frequency noise. Differentiators act as **high-pass filters** that reject DC drift and isolate rapidly changing edges — essential for FM demodulation and digital clock recovery.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Output of an RC Differentiator for a Step Input

**Given:** A step input of magnitude $V_m$ applied at $t = 0$ to a series-$C$, shunt-$R$ differentiator with time constant $\tau = RC$.

**Governing KVL Equation:**

$$
V_{in}(t) = V_C(t) + V_R(t)
$$

**Step 1:** Express the capacitor voltage in terms of current. The fundamental capacitor law is:

$$
i(t) = C \dfrac{dV_C(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the current expression into the resistor voltage. Since $V_{out} = V_R = i \cdot R$:

$$
V_{out}(t) = R \cdot i(t) = R \cdot C \dfrac{dV_C(t)}{dt} = RC \dfrac{dV_C(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the differentiating condition $RC \ll T$. Under this regime, $V_C \ll V_R$, so $V_{in} \approx V_C$:

$$
V_{out}(t) = RC \dfrac{dV_{in}(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 4:** For a step input $V_{in}(t) = V_m \cdot u(t)$ (where $u(t)$ is the unit step), the derivative is the Dirac delta function. In practice, the output is a sharp **positive exponential decay spike**:

$$
V_{out}(t) = V_m \cdot e^{-t/RC} \quad \text{(for } t \geq 0 \text{ after a positive step)}
$$

**Step 5:** At $t = 0^+$, $V_{out}(0^+) = V_m$ (the full input voltage appears momentarily across $R$). As $t \to \infty$, $V_{out} \to 0$ (the capacitor becomes fully charged and blocks DC).

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic of the Spike**: The capacitor initially behaves like a *short circuit* (uncharged state), so the entire input voltage drops across $R$. As the capacitor charges, it gradually acts like an *open circuit* for DC, and the output decays to zero.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Output of an RC Integrator for a Square-Wave Input

**Given:** A square wave of amplitude $\pm V_m$ and period $T$ applied to a series-$R$, shunt-$C$ integrator with $\tau = RC \gg T$.

**Governing KVL Equation:**

$$
V_{in}(t) = V_R(t) + V_C(t) = V_R(t) + V_{out}(t)
$$

**Step 1:** The current through the circuit is given by Ohm's law on the resistor:

$$
i(t) = \dfrac{V_R(t)}{R}
$$

**Step 2:** The output (capacitor voltage) is the integral of the current:

$$
V_{out}(t) = \dfrac{1}{C} \int_{0}^{t} i(\tau) \, d\tau + V_{out}(0)
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the integrating condition $RC \gg T$. The voltage drop across $R$ is small compared to the drop across $C$, so $V_{in} \approx V_R$:

$$
i(t) \approx \dfrac{V_{in}(t)}{R}
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute the approximate current into the integral:

$$
V_{out}(t) \approx \dfrac{1}{C} \int_{0}^{t} \dfrac{V_{in}(t)}{R} \, dt = \dfrac{1}{RC} \int_{0}^{t} V_{in}(t) \, dt
$$

**Step 5:** For a positive half-cycle of duration $T/2$ where $V_{in} = +V_m$:

$$
V_{out}(t) = \dfrac{V_m}{RC} \cdot t + V_{out}(0)
$$

This is a **linear ramp** rising from $V_{out}(0)$ to $V_{out}(0) + \dfrac{V_m \cdot T}{2RC}$.

**Step 6:** For a negative half-cycle, the ramp descends with slope $-\dfrac{V_m}{RC}$, producing a **triangular waveform** at the output (provided $\dfrac{V_m T}{2RC} \ll V_m$, i.e., the swing is small).

$$
\boxed{\ V_{out}^{\text{peak-to-peak}} = \dfrac{V_m \cdot T}{RC} = \dfrac{V_m}{f \cdot RC}\ }
$$

This is the most critical formula for KTU board problems on integrators.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation Tip:** Examiners often award full marks only if students explicitly show the **differentiating condition** ($RC \ll T$) or the **integrating condition** ($RC \gg T$) before writing the output equation. Skipping this justification costs at least 2 marks.

---

## 3.3 Sine-Wave Response Analysis (Frequency Domain)

For a sinusoidal input $V_{in}(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$, the steady-state outputs are derived using phasors:

**Differentiator Output:**

$$
V_{out}(t) = \dfrac{\omega RC \cdot V_m}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}} \sin\left(\omega t + 90^\circ - \tan^{-1}(\omega RC)\right)
$$

For $\omega RC \ll 1$ (differentiating regime):

$$
V_{out}(t) \approx \omega RC \cdot V_m \cdot \cos(\omega t)
$$

This is a **scaled, phase-shifted cosine** — the derivative of $\sin(\omega t)$ is $\omega \cos(\omega t)$.

**Integrator Output:**

$$
V_{out}(t) = \dfrac{V_m}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}} \sin\left(\omega t - \tan^{-1}(\omega RC)\right)
$$

For $\omega RC \gg 1$ (integrating regime):

$$
V_{out}(t) \approx \dfrac{V_m}{\omega RC} \cos\left(\omega t - 90^\circ\right) = -\dfrac{V_m}{\omega RC} \cos(\omega t)
$$

The output is **inverted and scaled**, with a 90° phase lag — the integral of $\sin(\omega t)$ is $-\dfrac{1}{\omega} \cos(\omega t)$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Circuit Schematic — RC Differentiator


## 4.4 ASCII Waveform Sketch — Square-Wave Response

Below is a textual sketch of the input and output waveforms for both circuits, designed for reproduction in exam answer sheets.

```
=== RC DIFFERENTIATOR RESPONSE TO SQUARE WAVE (RC << T) ===

  VIN:  ___        ___        ___
       |   |      |   |      |   |
   ____|   |______|   |______|   |____
                T

  VOUT:    +Vm
          /|\
         / | \           -Vm
  ______/  |  \         /|\
                \       / | \        /|
                 \_____/  |  \______/ | \_____
                  spike    spike      spike
                (positive) (negative) (positive)


=== RC INTEGRATOR RESPONSE TO SQUARE WAVE (RC >> T) ===

  VIN:  ___        ___        ___
       |   |      |   |      |   |
   ____|   |______|   |______|   |____

  VOUT:        /\        /\
              /  \      /  \
             /    \    /    \       (Triangular approximation)
  __________/      \__/      \__________
            T/2    T/2     T
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Drawing Tip**: Always label the **time axis** (with $T$ or $T/2$ markings) and the **voltage axis** (with $\pm V_m$ markings) on waveform sketches. Examiners deduct marks for unlabelled axes.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]`

**Define an RC differentiating circuit. State the condition required for a square wave input.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> An RC differentiating circuit is a high-pass RC network consisting of a capacitor $C$ in series with the input and a resistor $R$ in parallel with the output, designed to produce an output voltage proportional to the time derivative of the input voltage. **(1.5 marks)**
>
> The condition for proper differentiation is that the time constant of the circuit must be much smaller than the time period of the input waveform, i.e., $RC \ll T$. **(1 mark)**
>
> Under this condition, the output is $V_{out} = RC \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$, producing sharp positive and negative spikes at the rising and falling edges of a square wave. **(0.5 marks)**

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Sessional Exam]`

**Sketch the output waveform of an RC integrator when a symmetrical square wave of amplitude $V_m$ and period $T$ is applied. State the necessary condition.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The necessary condition is $RC \gg T$ (time constant much greater than the time period). **(1 mark)**
>
> The output is a **triangular waveform** with peak-to-peak amplitude $\dfrac{V_m T}{RC}$. During each positive half-cycle of duration $T/2$, the output rises approximately linearly with slope $\dfrac{V_m}{RC}$, and during each negative half-cycle, it falls with slope $-\dfrac{V_m}{RC}$. **(2 marks)**

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and typically has two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Main Question Paper]`

**(a) Derive the output voltage expression of an RC differentiating circuit for a square wave input. Explain why the output consists of sharp spikes.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Circuit Description:** Consider a series capacitor $C$ and shunt resistor $R$ with input $V_{in}$ and output $V_{out}$ across $R$. **(0.5 marks)**

**Step 2 — KVL Application:**
$$
V_{in} = V_C + V_R = V_C + V_{out}
$$
**(0.5 marks)**

**Step 3 — Capacitor Current:** The current through the circuit is:
$$
i = C \dfrac{dV_C}{dt}
$$
and $V_{out} = iR = RC \dfrac{dV_C}{dt}$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Differentiating Condition:** When $RC \ll T$, the capacitor voltage $V_C$ is negligibly small compared to $V_R$, so $V_{in} \approx V_C$. Therefore:
$$
V_{out} = RC \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}
$$
**(2 marks)**

**Step 5 — Spike Explanation:** For an ideal square wave with instantaneous transitions, $\dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$ is theoretically infinite at the edges, producing Dirac-delta-like impulses. In a real RC circuit with finite $\tau$, the output is a positive exponential spike of initial amplitude $V_m$ that decays as $e^{-t/RC}$ after a positive step, and a negative spike of amplitude $-V_m$ after a negative step. **(2 marks)**

**Step 6 — Sketch:** [Draw the input square wave and the output bipolar spike train with labels on both axes.] **(1 mark)**

---

**(b) With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of an RC integrating circuit when a square wave is applied. Derive the expression for the output voltage.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Circuit Diagram:** Draw a series resistor $R$ and shunt capacitor $C$ with input $V_{in}$ and output $V_{out}$ across $C$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — KVL Equation:**
$$
V_{in} = V_R + V_{out}
$$
**(0.5 marks)**

**Step 3 — Current Expression:** The current is $i = \dfrac{V_R}{R} = \dfrac{V_{in} - V_{out}}{R}$. **(0.5 marks)**

**Step 4 — Integrating Condition:** For $RC \gg T$, the voltage across $R$ is small, so $V_{in} \approx V_R$ and the current is approximately constant during each half-cycle:
$$
i \approx \dfrac{V_{in}}{R}
$$
**(1.5 marks)**

**Step 5 — Capacitor Voltage (Integration):**
$$
V_{out} = \dfrac{1}{C} \int i \, dt = \dfrac{1}{C} \int \dfrac{V_{in}}{R} \, dt = \dfrac{1}{RC} \int V_{in} \, dt
$$
**(1.5 marks)**

**Step 6 — Square-Wave Result:** For a positive half-cycle ($V_{in} = +V_m$ for $0 \leq t \leq T/2$):
$$
V_{out}(t) = \dfrac{V_m \cdot t}{RC} + V_{out}(0)
$$
The output rises linearly; for a negative half-cycle, it falls linearly. The peak-to-peak swing is $\dfrac{V_m T}{RC}$, producing a triangular waveform. **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 7 — Final Output Expression:**
$$
\boxed{\ V_{out}(t) = \dfrac{1}{RC} \int V_{in}(t) \, dt\ }
$$
**(0.5 marks)**

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary Exam]`

**(a) Explain the principle of operation of an RC low-pass filter used as an integrator. Derive the condition for which it behaves as a near-ideal integrator.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Principle:** An RC low-pass filter with $R$ in series and $C$ in shunt acts as an integrator when the time constant $\tau = RC$ is much greater than the period $T$ of the input signal. The capacitor voltage (output) is unable to follow the rapid changes of the input and instead **accumulates the charge** delivered over time, producing the mathematical integral of the input. **(2 marks)**

**Step 2 — Transfer Function:** The transfer function of the RC low-pass network is:
$$
H(s) = \dfrac{V_{out}(s)}{V_{in}(s)} = \dfrac{1}{1 + sRC}
$$
**(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Integrator Approximation:** For an ideal integrator, we need $H(s) \approx \dfrac{1}{sRC}$, which requires $\vert sRC \vert \gg 1$ in the operating frequency range. In the frequency domain, this means $\omega RC \gg 1$, or equivalently, $f \gg f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ — the input frequency must be much higher than the cut-off frequency. **(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Time-Domain Derivation:** Starting from the governing ODE:
$$
RC \dfrac{dV_{out}}{dt} + V_{out} = V_{in}
$$
When $RC \gg T$, the term $V_{out}$ changes very slowly, so $RC \dfrac{dV_{out}}{dt} \gg V_{out}$, and the equation reduces to:
$$
RC \dfrac{dV_{out}}{dt} \approx V_{in} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{out} \approx \dfrac{1}{RC} \int V_{in} \, dt
$$
**(1.5 marks)**

**Step 5 — Final Condition:** The circuit behaves as a near-ideal integrator when **$RC \geq 10T$** (a common engineering rule of thumb; some texts use $5T$). **(0.5 marks)**

---

**(b) An RC differentiator has $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.01 \, \mu F$. A square wave of amplitude $10 \text{ V}$ and frequency $10 \text{ kHz}$ is applied. Verify that the circuit acts as a differentiator and find the peak output voltage.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Time Constant Calculation:**
$$
\tau = RC = (10 \times 10^3) \times (0.01 \times 10^{-6}) = 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 100 \, \mu s
$$
**(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Time Period Calculation:**
$$
T = \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{10 \times 10^3} = 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 100 \, \mu s
$$
**(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Condition Check:** $\tau = T$, which is **not** strictly $RC \ll T$. However, in a practical differentiator, the critical requirement is that the output spike decays significantly before the next edge occurs. With $\tau = T/2$ for each half-cycle, we get a partial differentiation — the output will be a sharp exponential transition but not an ideal impulse. **(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Peak Output Voltage:** At $t = 0^+$, the uncharged capacitor acts as a short circuit, so the entire input voltage appears across $R$:
$$
V_{out}^{\text{peak}} = V_{in} = 10 \text{ V}
$$
**(1.5 marks)**

**Step 5 — Decay Form:** The output then decays exponentially:
$$
V_{out}(t) = 10 \cdot e^{-t/\tau} = 10 \cdot e^{-t/100\mu s} \text{ V}
$$
By the time of the next transition ($t = 50 \, \mu s$):
$$
V_{out}(50\mu s) = 10 \cdot e^{-0.5} \approx 6.07 \text{ V}
$$
The output does not fully decay before the next edge, so the differentiator is **marginally functional**. To make it ideal, $RC$ should be reduced (e.g., to $10 \, \mu s$ by using $C = 1 \text{ nF}$). **(1.5 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Skipping the time-constant condition** ($RC \ll T$ or $RC \gg T$): This is the most common reason for losing 2–3 marks. Always state and verify the condition explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing the topology**: Students often write the formula for a differentiator but draw the integrator circuit (or vice versa). Memorize: "**C**apacitor first = **D**ifferentiator."
> 3. **Wrong output location**: The differentiator output is across $R$, the integrator output is across $C$. Drawing output across the wrong element is an instant 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Omitting units**: Numerical answers without units (V, s, Hz) lose 0.5 marks each in KTU valuation.
> 5. **Forgetting to sketch waveforms**: For 7-mark questions, an unlabelled or missing waveform sketch costs a minimum of 1 mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Differentiator Topology**: **C** in series, **R** in shunt (output across $R$). Mnemonic: "**CR** circuit = **C**oming **R**ight at you with a spike."
- **Integrator Topology**: **R** in series, **C** in shunt (output across $C$). Mnemonic: "**RC** circuit = **R**amp **C**apacitor charge."
- **Time Constant Condition for Differentiator**: $\tau = RC \ll T$ (typically $RC \leq T/10$).
- **Time Constant Condition for Integrator**: $\tau = RC \gg T$ (typically $RC \geq 10T$).
- **Differentiator Output (Ideal)**: $V_{out} = RC \cdot \dfrac{dV_{in}}{dt}$.
- **Integrator Output (Ideal)**: $V_{out} = \dfrac{1}{RC} \int V_{in} \, dt$.
- **Differentiator Square-Wave Response**: Bipolar exponential spikes with peak amplitude equal to the input step magnitude.
- **Integrator Square-Wave Response**: Triangular waveform with peak-to-peak amplitude $\dfrac{V_m T}{RC}$.
- **Cut-off Frequency**: $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ — differentiator operates at $f \gg f_c$, integrator operates at $f \ll f_c$.
- **Transfer Functions**: Differentiator $H(s) = \dfrac{sRC}{1 + sRC}$, Integrator $H(s) = \dfrac{1}{1 + sRC}$.
- **Sine-Wave Phase**: Differentiator leads by approximately 90°; Integrator lags by approximately 90° (in their respective operating regimes).
- **Board Exam Drawing Must-Haves**: Always draw (1) the circuit with clear component labels, (2) input and output waveforms on aligned time axes, and (3) the governing equation written below the sketch.
- **Most-Asked KTU Question Type**: "Derive the output of an RC differentiator/integrator for a square wave input and sketch the waveforms" — this appears in nearly every exam cycle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of RC Integrator and Differentiator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES & CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Applications of RC Integrator and Differentiator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The RC Integrator — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RC Integrator (Definition):** An RC low-pass circuit in which the output is taken across the **capacitor**, and whose time constant ($\tau = RC$) is chosen to be **much greater** than the time period ($T$) of the input signal ($\tau \gg T$). Under this condition, the capacitor charges/discharges only a small fraction of the total signal swing, and the output voltage waveform approximates the **time-integral of the input voltage** (scaled by the factor $1/RC$).

Mathematically, the governing input-output relationship is:

$$v_{o}(t) \;\approx\; \frac{1}{RC}\int_{0}^{t} v_{in}(t)\,dt \;+\; v_{o}(0)$$

## 1.2 The RC Differentiator — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RC Differentiator (Definition):** An RC high-pass circuit in which the output is taken across the **resistor**, and whose time constant ($\tau = RC$) is chosen to be **much smaller** than the time period ($T$) of the input signal ($\tau \ll T$). Under this condition, the output voltage waveform approximates the **time-derivative of the input voltage** (scaled by the factor $RC$).

The governing input-output relationship is:

$$v_{o}(t) \;\approx\; RC\,\frac{d\,v_{in}(t)}{dt}$$

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

### The Integrator — "A Slow-Filling Bucket"
Think of a **wide-mouth water tank** connected to a tap through a **very thin pipe**. The thin pipe allows water to flow in *very slowly* (large $\tau$). When you pour a steady stream into the tank (a step input) and then close the tap, the water level rises **gradually and smoothly** — it does NOT shoot up instantly. The water level at any time is essentially the **time-integral** of the inflow rate. In the same way, an RC integrator "smooths out" sudden changes and produces a slowly rising/falling output (triangular shape) from abrupt square-wave transitions.

### The Differentiator — "A Lightning Sensor"
Imagine a **pressure sensor** connected to a slowly-leaking balloon. When you *suddenly* squeeze the balloon (a fast edge), the pressure spike is detected instantly. But when the balloon is held at a constant squeeze (DC level), the pressure slowly leaks away and the sensor reads **zero**. The output is only non-zero during *changes*. That is exactly what a differentiator does — it responds **only to the rate of change** of the input, producing sharp spikes at the rising and falling edges of a square wave and zero during the flat portions.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> - **Time Constant:** $\tau = RC$ — measured in **seconds (s)**
> - **Corner/Cutoff Frequency:** $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ — measured in **Hertz (Hz)**
> - **Integrator Condition:** $RC \geq 10\,T$ (commonly $RC = 10T$ for near-perfect integration)
> - **Differentiator Condition:** $RC \leq 0.1\,T$ (commonly $RC = T/10$ for near-perfect differentiation)
> - **Amplitude Scaling:** Integrator output amplitude $\approx V_{in}\cdot T/(2RC)$; Differentiator spike amplitude $\approx V_{in}\cdot RC/T$ relative to the half-period.

## 1.5 Why These Circuits Matter in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Integrators and differentiators are the **building blocks of analog computation** (analog computers of the 1960s), **wave-shaping circuits** (square → triangle, square → spikes), **pulse-conditioning networks** in radar/communication receivers, **sample-and-hold and timer circuits** (the 555 timer uses integrator principles), **active filter design** (low-pass/ high-pass biquads are integrator-derived), and **PI/PD controllers** in industrial automation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Working of the RC Integrator

Let us analyze a square-wave input of amplitude $V_m$ and period $T$ applied to an RC integrator (output across capacitor).

- **Step 1 — Input edge arrives (say, rising edge at $t = 0$).** The capacitor voltage cannot change instantaneously (it obeys $i = C\,dv/dt$). Therefore, the *entire input voltage appears across the resistor* initially, producing a large charging current $i(0^+) = V_m/R$.

- **Step 2 — Capacitor begins to charge exponentially.** With $\tau = RC \gg T$, only the **initial linear portion** of the exponential charge curve is "seen" by the circuit during one half-period. An exponential curve's initial segment is mathematically linear.

- **Step 3 — Output becomes a linear ramp.** The capacitor voltage rises almost linearly with slope $\dfrac{dv_o}{dt} \approx \dfrac{V_m}{\tau}$ (from $i = C\,dv/dt \Rightarrow dv/dt = V_m/(RC)$).

- **Step 4 — At $t = T/2$**, the input suddenly drops to $-V_m$. The capacitor now discharges. Because $\tau \gg T/2$, the discharge is also nearly linear, producing a **downward ramp**.

- **Step 5 — Net result:** A near-perfect **triangular waveform** at the output, with peak-to-peak amplitude $V_{pp} = \dfrac{V_m\,T}{2RC}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Why "linear" and not "exponential"?** The Taylor expansion of $e^{-t/\tau}$ for small $t/\tau$ gives $e^{-t/\tau} \approx 1 - t/\tau$. Hence the capacitor voltage $v_o = V_m(1 - e^{-t/\tau}) \approx V_m \cdot t/\tau$, which is exactly the linear ramp of an ideal integrator output.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Working of the RC Differentiator

Now consider the same square wave applied to a differentiator (output across resistor) with $\tau = RC \ll T$.

- **Step 1 — Rising edge at $t = 0$.** The capacitor (initially uncharged) acts momentarily as a short circuit. The full input $V_m$ appears across the resistor, giving a sharp positive spike of amplitude $V_m$ that decays exponentially with time constant $\tau$.

- **Step 2 — During the flat-top** ($T/2 > t > 0$, while input = $+V_m$), the capacitor charges to $V_m$. Since $\tau \ll T$, it reaches full charge **very quickly**. Once charged, no current flows, so the resistor voltage drops to **zero**. The output is essentially zero.

- **Step 3 — Falling edge at $t = T/2$.** The input drops from $+V_m$ to $-V_m$, a sudden change of $-2V_m$. The capacitor voltage (still $+V_m$) cannot change instantly, so the resistor sees a voltage of $-2V_m$ — a sharp **negative spike** that decays rapidly.

- **Step 4 — Net result:** A series of **sharp positive and negative spikes** (alternating) at the rising and falling edges, with amplitude $\approx V_m$ and exponentially decaying shape of width $\approx 5\tau$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Integrator Formula | Differentiator Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Time Constant | $\tau = RC$ (with $\tau \gg T$) | $\tau = RC$ (with $\tau \ll T$) |
| 2 | Output Location | Across **Capacitor** | Across **Resistor** |
| 3 | Network Type | **Low-Pass** Filter | **High-Pass** Filter |
| 4 | Ideal Input–Output Relation | $v_{o}(t) = \dfrac{1}{RC}\int v_{in}\,dt$ | $v_{o}(t) = RC\,\dfrac{d v_{in}}{dt}$ |
| 5 | Cutoff Frequency | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ |
| 6 | Condition for "Good" Operation | $RC \geq 10T$ | $RC \leq 0.1T$ |
| 7 | Square-Wave Output Shape | **Triangular Wave** | **Spike Pulses** (positive & negative) |
| 8 | Peak-to-Peak Output (Square In) | $V_{pp} = \dfrac{V_m\,T}{2RC}$ | Spike Amplitude $\approx V_m$ (decays in $\approx 5\tau$) |
| 9 | Slope of Output Ramp | $\dfrac{dv_o}{dt} = \dfrac{V_m}{RC}$ | N/A (impulsive) |
| 10 | Output Average Value (Symmetric In) | **0 V** (AC-coupled) | **0 V** (AC-coupled) |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL TABLE RULE:** Note that in the table above, the absolute-value bars around $V_m$ have been replaced with descriptive text ("Amplitude") to avoid breaking the markdown pipe-syntax. In handwritten/LaTeX solutions, write $\vert V_m \vert$ explicitly.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Where you will see these circuits in practice:**
> - **Analog Computers (Historical):** Solved differential equations; an integrator + summer + inverter could implement any linear ODE.
> - **555 Timer IC:** The timing capacitor charges through a resistor, producing a ramp — fundamentally an integrator action.
> - **TV/CRT Deflection Circuits:** Sawtooth generators use RC integrators to produce the linear horizontal/vertical sweep.
> - **Radar Pulse Sharpening:** Differentiators are used to mark the leading/trailing edges of received pulses for timing measurement.
> - **Communication Receivers:** Differentiators act as **edge detectors** in FM demodulators and **click detectors** in audio.
> - **Operational Amplifier Circuits:** The op-amp integrator and differentiator (Miller effect) are foundational active building blocks in modern signal processing.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Integrator's Linear-Ramp Output for a Square-Wave Input

We will rigorously derive why a square wave input produces a triangular output across the capacitor when $\tau = RC \gg T$.

### Setup

A square wave of amplitude $V_m$ (peak) and period $T$ is applied at $t = 0$ to a series RC network. Output is taken across the capacitor.

The governing KVL/KCL equation is:

$$v_{in}(t) = i(t)\,R + v_o(t)$$

And the capacitor constitutive relation:

$$i(t) = C\,\frac{dv_o(t)}{dt}$$

### Substitution

Substitute $i(t) = C\,dv_o/dt$ into the KVL equation:

$$v_{in}(t) = RC\,\frac{dv_o(t)}{dt} + v_o(t)$$

This is a first-order linear ODE with constant coefficients.

### Solving the ODE (Rising Half-Cycle, $0 \le t \le T/2$)

For $0 \le t \le T/2$, $v_{in}(t) = +V_m$. Assume initial condition $v_o(0) = -V_{peak}$ (so the output is symmetric about 0). The solution is the sum of the *forced (steady-state)* and *natural (transient)* responses:

$$v_o(t) = V_m + A\,e^{-t/RC}$$

### Applying Initial Condition

At $t = 0$: $\;v_o(0) = -V_{peak}$. Therefore:

$$-V_{peak} = V_m + A \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = -(V_m + V_{peak})$$

So the output during the rising half-cycle becomes:

$$v_o(t) = V_m - (V_m + V_{peak})\,e^{-t/RC}$$

### Taylor Expansion for $\tau \gg T$

Since $RC \gg T$, the quantity $t/RC$ is very small for all $t$ in $[0, T/2]$. Using the Maclaurin expansion $e^{-x} \approx 1 - x + \dfrac{x^2}{2!} - \cdots$ :

$$e^{-t/RC} \approx 1 - \frac{t}{RC} + \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{t}{RC}\right)^2 - \cdots$$

Retaining only the first two terms (linear approximation):

$$e^{-t/RC} \approx 1 - \frac{t}{RC}$$

### Final Linearized Output

Substituting back:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_o(t) &\;\approx\; V_m - (V_m + V_{peak})\left(1 - \frac{t}{RC}\right) \\
&\;=\; V_m - (V_m + V_{peak}) + (V_m + V_{peak})\,\frac{t}{RC} \\
&\;=\; -V_{peak} + \frac{(V_m + V_{peak})}{RC}\,t
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus $v_o(t)$ is a **straight line** (linear ramp) with slope $\dfrac{V_m + V_{peak}}{RC}$ and starting value $-V_{peak}$. By symmetry, the falling half-cycle produces a downward ramp from $+V_{peak}$ to $-V_{peak}$. The peak-to-peak swing of the triangular wave is:

$$V_{pp,\text{triangular}} \;=\; \frac{V_m \cdot T}{2RC}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mark-Allocation Note (KTU):** Examiners award full marks only if (i) the ODE is correctly set up, (ii) the general solution is written, (iii) the IC is applied, AND (iv) the Taylor/linearization step is explicitly invoked. Skipping the Taylor step is the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks on this derivation.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Differentiator's Spike Output

We now derive the differentiator's response to the same square wave, with output across $R$.

### Setup

Using the same KVL, but with output across the resistor:

$$v_o(t) = i(t)\,R \;=\; RC\,\frac{dv_o^{C}(t)}{dt}$$

where $v_o^{C}(t)$ is the capacitor voltage. From the same circuit ODE:

$$v_{in}(t) = RC\,\frac{dv_o^C}{dt} + v_o^C(t)$$

But since $v_o = v_{in} - v_o^C$, we can write:

$$v_o(t) = v_{in}(t) - v_o^C(t)$$

For a step change $\Delta v_{in}$, if $\tau = RC \ll T$, the capacitor voltage cannot change significantly during the edge. Hence $v_o^C$ is approximately constant during a fast transition, and the output is:

$$v_o(t) \;\approx\; \Delta v_{in}\,e^{-t/RC}$$

### Specific Application: Rising Edge of a Square Wave

For a $+V_m$ to $0$... wait, let us use a more careful model. A square wave going from $0$ to $+V_m$ at $t = 0$ represents a step of magnitude $+V_m$. The capacitor initially at 0 V cannot change instantly, so the entire $V_m$ appears across $R$:

$$v_o(0^+) = V_m$$

It then decays exponentially:

$$v_o(t) = V_m\,e^{-t/RC} \quad \text{for } t \ge 0^+$$

### Falling Edge (at $t = T/2$)

At $t = T/2$, the input drops from $+V_m$ to $-V_m$ (a step of $-2V_m$). With the capacitor still charged to $+V_m$, the new output is:

$$v_o(T/2^+) \;=\; v_{in}(T/2^+) - v_o^C(T/2) \;=\; -V_m - V_m \;=\; -2V_m$$

It then decays:

$$v_o(t) = -2V_m\,e^{-(t - T/2)/RC} \quad \text{for } t \ge T/2$$

For symmetric square wave (equal positive and negative halves), the spikes are $\pm V_m$ and $\pm V_m$ alternating (the negative spike has amplitude $-V_m$ if measured from the new baseline of $-V_m$, but the absolute swing from the previous level is $-2V_m$ — this depends on the convention used in the problem).

### Spike Width

The spike is conventionally considered "ended" when its amplitude has decayed to **5%** of its peak. Using $e^{-t/\tau} = 0.05$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t/\tau &= -\ln(0.05) \;=\; 3.0 \\
\text{Spike Width} &\;\approx\; 3\tau \;=\; 3RC
\end{aligned}
$$

(Using a stricter 1% criterion gives $4.6\tau$, and the engineering "5$\tau$ rule" is often used.)

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Integrator | Differentiator |
|---|---|---|
| $\tau = RC$ vs. $T$ | $\tau \gg T$ (typically $10T$) | $\tau \ll T$ (typically $T/10$) |
| Output Element | Capacitor | Resistor |
| Mathematical Operation | Integration ($\int$) | Differentiation ($d/dt$) |
| Square-Wave Response | Triangular | Spike Pulses |
| Frequency Response | Attenuates high frequencies | Attenuates low frequencies |
| Output Amplitude vs. Input | $V_{pp} = V_m T/(2RC)$ — **decreases** with larger $RC$ | Spike amplitude $\approx V_m$ — **independent** of $RC$ (for small $RC$) |
| Energy Storage | Capacitor stores charge | Capacitor stores charge but is "transparent" to fast edges |
| Noise Susceptibility | **Low** (smooths noise) | **High** (amplifies high-freq noise) |
| Phase Shift at $f_c$ | $-45^{\circ}$ (output lags) | $+45^{\circ}$ (output leads) |
| Use in Wave Generation | Square → Triangle | Square → Spike / Edge Marker |
| Use in Filtering | **Low-Pass** Filter | **High-Pass** Filter |

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Problem)

> **Problem (Style of KTU University Exam):** A square wave of amplitude $5$ V and frequency $1$ kHz is applied to an RC integrator with $R = 10$ k$\Omega$ and $C = 10$ $\mu$F. Determine whether the circuit acts as a good integrator, and find the peak-to-peak output voltage.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Compute time constant:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau = RC = (10 \times 10^3\,\Omega)(10 \times 10^{-6}\,F) = 0.1 \text{ s} = 100 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute period of input:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T = \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{1000} = 1 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Check integrator condition:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{\tau}{T} = \dfrac{100 \text{ ms}}{1 \text{ ms}} = 100 \;\gg\; 10
\end{aligned}
$$
$\therefore$ The circuit acts as an **excellent integrator**.

**Step 4 — Compute peak-to-peak triangular output:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{pp} = \dfrac{V_m \cdot T}{2RC} = \dfrac{(5)(1 \times 10^{-3})}{2(0.1)} = 25 \text{ mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> The output is small in amplitude (25 mV) because the time constant is so large. In practice, an **op-amp based active integrator** is used to overcome this attenuation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: RC Integrator Signal Flow

The following Mermaid block represents the conceptual signal flow inside an RC integrator, capturing the functional relationship between the input square wave, the RC time-constant action, and the resulting triangular output.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Square Wave Input V_in] -->|Rising Edge at t=0| B[Resistor R: Limits Current]
    B --> C[Capacitor C: Slow Charging]
    C -->|Linear Ramp| D[Output V_o across C]
    D --> E{Tau vs T}
    E -->|Tau greater than 10T| F[Triangular Wave Output]
    E -->|Tau approx T| G[Exponential Charge Curve]
    E -->|Tau less than 0.1T| H[Square Wave Output Unchanged]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: RC Differentiator Signal Flow

The diagram below shows the functional flow of an RC differentiator, highlighting the spike-pulse generation and the role of the time constant.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Square Wave Input V_in] -->|Fast Edge Detected| B[Capacitor C: Acts as Short at t=0]
    B --> C[Resistor R: Develops Voltage Spike]
    C -->|Exponential Decay| D[Output V_o across R]
    D --> E{Tau vs T}
    E -->|Tau less than 0.1T| F[Sharp Spike Pulses Output]
    E -->|Tau approx T| G[Distorted Pulses]
    E -->|Tau greater than 10T| H[No Output During Flat Tops]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: Equivalent Functional Architecture

Below is a higher-level functional-architecture diagram comparing the integrator and differentiator roles within a generic analog signal-processing chain.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Src[Signal Source: Square Wave] --> Sw{Select Mode}
    Sw -->|Integrator Path| IntRC[RC Integrator: R series, C to GND, Vout across C]
    Sw -->|Differentiator Path| DifRC[RC Differentiator: C series, R to GND, Vout across R]
    IntRC --> IntOut[Output 1: Triangular Wave]
    DifRC --> DifOut[Output 2: Spike Pulses]
    IntOut --> App1[Use: 555 Timer, TV Sweep, Analog Computer]
    DifOut --> App2[Use: Edge Detector, FM Demod, Pulse Marker]
```

## 4.4 Waveform Timing Diagram (Mermaid Timeline)

The following diagram illustrates the temporal relationship between the input square wave, the integrator output, and the differentiator output.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T[Time Axis]
        direction LR
        T0[t = 0] --> T1[t = T/2] --> T2[t = T] --> T3[t = 3T/2] --> T4[t = 2T]
    end
    subgraph IN[Input V_in]
        direction LR
        IN0[+Vm] --> IN1[+Vm] --> IN2[-Vm] --> IN3[-Vm] --> IN4[+Vm]
    end
    subgraph INT[Integrator Output V_o]
        direction LR
        IO0[Linear Ramp Up] --> IO1[+Vpeak] --> IO2[Linear Ramp Down] --> IO3[-Vpeak] --> IO4[Linear Ramp Up]
    end
    subgraph DIF[Differentiator Output V_o]
        direction LR
        DO0[Spike +Vm] --> DO1[Zero] --> DO2[Spike -Vm] --> DO3[Zero] --> DO4[Spike +Vm]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The integrator output (IO) is a *continuous* triangular wave that lags the input transitions. The differentiator output (DO) is *discontinuous* — non-zero only at the instants of input change. This contrast is the single most important visual takeaway from this topic.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`
**Q: Define an RC integrator. State the condition for a good integrator and write its output expression for a square-wave input.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition:** An RC integrator is a low-pass RC network in which the output is taken across the **capacitor** and the time constant $\tau = RC$ is chosen to be **much greater** ($RC \geq 10T$) than the period $T$ of the input signal.

> **Condition for good integration:** $RC \geq 10T$

> **Output expression:** $v_{o}(t) = \dfrac{1}{RC}\int v_{in}(t)\,dt$

> **Square-wave response:** A **triangular waveform** of peak-to-peak amplitude $V_{pp} = \dfrac{V_m\,T}{2RC}$.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`
**Q: With a neat sketch, explain the working of an RC differentiator when a square wave is applied. State the condition under which it works as a good differentiator.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Circuit:** Capacitor $C$ in series, with output across resistor $R$ to ground.

> **Working:** During the **rising edge**, the capacitor initially acts as a short, producing a positive spike of amplitude $V_m$ that decays exponentially. During the **flat top**, the capacitor charges fully, output becomes zero. During the **falling edge**, a negative spike of amplitude $-V_m$ appears. Net output: alternating positive and negative spike pulses.

> **Condition:** $RC \leq 0.1\,T$ (or $RC \ll T$).

> **Sketch description:** A square wave input with sharp positive and negative exponential-decay spikes at each transition.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Following KTU 2024 ESE regulations, Module 1 carries 14 marks. Students must answer either **Question A** or **Question B** (full internal choice, no sub-choice within).

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**Q: (a)** Derive the expression for the output of an RC integrator when a square wave of amplitude $V_m$ and period $T$ is applied. Show that the output is approximately triangular when $RC \gg T$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An RC integrator has $R = 100$ k$\Omega$ and $C = 0.1$ $\mu$F. A symmetric square wave of $\pm 5$ V at 500 Hz is applied. Verify that the circuit acts as a good integrator and calculate the peak-to-peak output voltage. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Circuit Equation:**
For output across capacitor in a series RC circuit:

$$v_{in}(t) = i(t)R + v_o(t), \quad i(t) = C\dfrac{dv_o}{dt}$$

Combining:

$$RC\,\frac{dv_o}{dt} + v_o(t) = v_{in}(t)$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — General Solution:**
For a constant input $v_{in} = V_m$ during the rising half-cycle ($0 \le t \le T/2$):

$$v_o(t) = V_m + A\,e^{-t/RC}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Initial Condition:**
Let $v_o(0) = -V_{peak}$:

$$-V_{peak} = V_m + A \;\Rightarrow\; A = -(V_m + V_{peak})$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Linearization (Taylor Expansion):**
Since $RC \gg T$, we use $e^{-t/RC} \approx 1 - \dfrac{t}{RC}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_o(t) &\;\approx\; V_m - (V_m + V_{peak})\left(1 - \frac{t}{RC}\right) \\
&\;=\; -V_{peak} + \frac{(V_m + V_{peak})}{RC}\,t
\end{aligned}
$$

This is a **linear ramp** in $t$, with slope $\dfrac{V_m + V_{peak}}{RC}$.

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Conclusion:**
By symmetry, the falling half-cycle gives a downward linear ramp. Hence the output is a **triangular waveform** with peak-to-peak amplitude:

$$V_{pp} = \frac{V_m \cdot T}{2RC}$$

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Time Constant:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau = RC = (100 \times 10^3)(0.1 \times 10^{-6}) = 0.01 \text{ s} = 10 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Period of Input:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T = \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{500} = 2 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Verifying Integrator Condition:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{\tau}{T} = \dfrac{10 \text{ ms}}{2 \text{ ms}} = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

Although $\tau > T$ (the output will be triangular), the ratio is **less than 10**, so the circuit acts as a **fair integrator** (noticeable curvature at peaks but still predominantly triangular). For "good" integration, we need $RC \geq 10T$, i.e., $C \geq 0.2\,\mu$F.

**Step 4 (3 marks) — Peak-to-Peak Output Voltage:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{pp} = \dfrac{V_m \cdot T}{2RC} = \dfrac{(5)(2 \times 10^{-3})}{2(0.01)} = 0.5 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** The output is a triangular wave of peak-to-peak amplitude **0.5 V** with a small exponential "kink" at the peaks (since $\tau/T = 5$, not $>10$).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**Q: (a)** With the help of necessary waveforms, explain the working of an RC differentiator for a square-wave input. Derive the expression for the spike amplitude and width. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A $2$ kHz square wave of $\pm 10$ V is applied to an RC differentiator with $R = 1$ k$\Omega$ and $C = 10$ nF. Verify the differentiator condition and find the spike amplitude and the time taken for the spike to decay to 5% of its peak. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

### Part (a) — Theory + Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Working Principle:**
A square wave with amplitude $\pm V_m$ and period $T$ is applied. Output is taken across $R$. With $RC \ll T$:

- **At each rising edge** ($0$ to $V_m$): Capacitor acts momentarily as a short → a positive spike of $\approx V_m$ appears across $R$, decaying exponentially.
- **During flat top** ($V_m$ constant): Capacitor charges fully → no current → $V_o = 0$.
- **At each falling edge** ($V_m$ to $-V_m$): Capacitor voltage cannot change instantly → a negative spike of $\approx -V_m$ appears, decaying exponentially.

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Spike Amplitude:**
For a step input of magnitude $\Delta V$, the spike amplitude equals $\Delta V$. For a symmetric square wave going from $+V_m$ to $-V_m$, $\Delta V = 2V_m$ (swing), so the negative spike reaches $-2V_m$ from the previous $+V_m$ level. By convention, peak amplitude **above the local baseline** is $V_m$.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Spike Decay Equation:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
v_o(t) = \Delta V \cdot e^{-t/RC} \quad \text{for } t \ge 0^+
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Spike Width (5% Criterion):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
0.05 \cdot \Delta V &= \Delta V \cdot e^{-t/RC} \\
e^{-t/RC} &= 0.05 \\
t = -RC \ln(0.05) = 3 RC \ln 20 \approx 3 RC
\end{aligned}
$$

So the spike width (to 5% level) is approximately $3RC$. Using the engineering 5-$\tau$ rule, it is $5RC$.

---

### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Time Constant:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau = RC = (1 \times 10^3)(10 \times 10^{-9}) = 10\,\mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Period:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
T = \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{2000} = 0.5 \text{ ms} = 500\,\mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Verifying Differentiator Condition:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{\tau}{T} = \dfrac{10\,\mu\text{s}}{500\,\mu\text{s}} = 0.02 \;<\; 0.1
\end{aligned}
$$

$\therefore$ The condition $RC \ll T$ is **well satisfied** (ratio is $1/50$). The circuit is an **excellent differentiator**.

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Spike Amplitude:**
For a square wave going from $+V_m$ to $-V_m$ at each edge, the spike amplitude (from local baseline) equals $V_m$:

$$\boxed{\text{Spike Amplitude} = \pm V_m = \pm 10 \text{ V}}$$

(Strictly, the negative-going transition produces a swing of $-2V_m = -20$ V from the previous $+V_m$ level, but the spike peak measured from its own adjacent baseline is $V_m$.)

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Time to Decay to 5%:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{5\%} = 3RC = 3 \times 10\,\mu\text{s} = 30\,\mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** Spike amplitude = **10 V**, decay-to-5% time = **30 $\mu$s** (well within the 250-$\mu$s half-period).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing output element** — students frequently swap $R$ and $C$ in the schematic. *Integrator → output across C; Differentiator → output across R.* Examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for this.
> 2. **Forgetting the linearization step** — the Taylor expansion $e^{-t/RC} \approx 1 - t/RC$ is **mandatory** to justify "linear" output. Skipping it loses **2 marks**.
> 3. **Unit mismatch in $\tau$ vs. $T$** — convert both to the same unit (typically seconds or ms) before comparing. A common error is to write "$RC = 10$ ms" and "$T = 1$ ms" without checking that the integrator condition is $RC/T \ge 10$.
> 4. **Spike amplitude confusion** — for a symmetric square wave, the spike **measured from the local baseline** is $V_m$, but the **peak-to-peak swing** of the spike is $2V_m$. Examiners look for the convention used in the question.
> 5. **Sign errors in ODE solution** — when applying the initial condition $v_o(0) = -V_{peak}$, students often drop the negative sign and end up with an inverted triangular wave.
> 6. **Skipping the verification step** — for numerical problems, always **explicitly compute and compare** $\tau/T$ and state whether the circuit is a *good*, *fair*, or *poor* integrator/differentiator. Examiners allocate **at least 1 mark** for this comment.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — RC Integrator and Differentiator Applications**

- **RC Integrator:** Output is taken across the **capacitor**; condition is $\mathbf{RC \geq 10T}$ (low-pass network).
- **RC Differentiator:** Output is taken across the **resistor**; condition is $\mathbf{RC \leq 0.1T}$ (high-pass network).
- The **integrator's** square-wave response is a **triangular wave** with $V_{pp} = V_m T / (2RC)$.
- The **differentiator's** square-wave response is **alternating positive/negative spike pulses** of amplitude $\approx V_m$, decaying exponentially with time constant $RC$.
- **Time constant formula:** $\tau = RC$ in **seconds**; **Cutoff frequency:** $f_c = 1/(2\pi RC)$ in **Hz**.
- The **Taylor linearization** $e^{-t/RC} \approx 1 - t/RC$ is the key mathematical step that justifies the "linear ramp" output of an integrator. **Never skip this in derivations.**
- **Integrators are attenuating** (output amplitude $\propto 1/RC$, decreases with larger time constant). Active (op-amp) integrators are used to overcome this.
- **Differentiators are high-pass** and thus **amplify high-frequency noise** — this is a major practical limitation in noisy environments.
- **Applications summary:**
  - *Integrator:* 555 timer, TV deflection, analog computer, low-pass filter, PI controller, square → triangle conversion.
  - *Differentiator:* Edge detector, FM demodulator, pulse marker in radar, square → spike conversion, high-pass filter, PD controller.
- **Phase relationship at cutoff frequency $f_c$:** Integrator output **lags** by $45°$; differentiator output **leads** by $45°$.
- **Symmetric square wave input** always produces an output with **zero average (DC) value** when the circuit is AC-coupled.
- For KTU derivations, always state the **governing ODE** $RC\,\frac{dv_o}{dt} + v_o = v_{in}$ before solving.
- In numerical problems, **always verify the condition** ($\tau/T$ ratio) explicitly — this is a guaranteed 1–2 mark allocation.
- **Memorize the output formulas:** $V_{pp,\text{int}} = V_m T/(2RC)$ and $V_{\text{spike}} = V_m$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Clipping Circuits - Positive, Negative and Biased
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCCIT302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES & CIRCUITS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Clipping Circuits - Positive, Negative and Biased # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Clipping Circuit (Limiter):** A clipping circuit is a **wave-shaping circuit** that limits or "clips" a portion of an input signal voltage that exceeds a predefined reference voltage level (either positive, negative, or a biased threshold), without distorting the remaining part of the waveform. It uses the **non-linear switching action of a PN-junction diode** along with DC biasing elements (resistors and DC voltage sources) to perform this selective voltage limiting. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCIT302 - Electronic Devices & Circuits, Module 1)**, clipping circuits are introduced as the most fundamental application of diode **non-linear behavior**, sitting alongside clamping circuits and rectifiers as core diode wave-shaping networks. The circuit essentially performs the mathematical operation: $$ V_{out}(t) = \begin{cases} V_{ref} \quad & \text{if } V_{in}(t) > V_{ref} \text{ (for positive clipping)} \\ V_{in}(t) \quad & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} $$ ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Garden Water Sprinkler:** Imagine a garden water sprinkler connected to a tap. If you place a **physical barrier (a lid)** on the sprinkler at a certain height, water that tries to shoot *above* that lid is blocked, but water that is *below* the lid height flows freely. The lid acts like a **clipper diode** — it "clips" or limits the water (voltage) above a certain level but allows everything below it to pass unchanged. If you put the lid on the *bottom*, it clips the negative portion.
