If you set the lid on a *small pedestal above the ground*, it clips only the part that exceeds that pedestal — this is the **biased clipper** behavior. ## 1.3 Classification of Clipping Circuits The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies clippers into **three primary families**: | # | Type | What It Clips | Reference Level | |---|------|---------------|-----------------| | 1 | **Positive Clipper** | Removes the positive half-cycle | $V_{ref} = 0\text{ V}$ (ground) | | 2 | **Negative Clipper** | Removes the negative half-cycle | $V_{ref} = 0\text{ V}$ (ground) | | 3 | **Biased Clipper** | Clips above or below a chosen DC level | $V_{ref} = V_{bias} \neq 0\text{ V}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A biased clipper is essentially a **generalized** form of the basic clipper. Every positive or negative clipper is just a *special case* of a biased clipper where $V_{bias} = 0\text{ V}$. Examiners often ask students to derive one from the other. ## 1.4 The Underlying Diode Action > [!NOTE] > **Key Operating Principle:** A diode acts as a **closed switch (short circuit, ideally)** when forward biased ($V_{AK} \geq 0$ in ideal case, or $\geq 0.7\text{ V}$ in practical Si diode), and an **open switch** when reverse biased ($V_{AK} < 0$). This binary switching behavior is what allows the clipping action — the diode "decides" whether to pass the input to the output or to clamp the output to a fixed reference. The standard **cut-in (knee) voltage** for a silicon diode is: $$ V_\gamma \approx \mathbf{0.7\text{ V}} \quad \text{(Silicon)}, \qquad V_\gamma \approx \mathbf{0.3\text{ V}} \quad \text{(Germanium)} $$ # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Circuit Topology and Operational Logic A generic single-diode clipper consists of **three elements**: 1. A **diode** ($D$) 2. A **reference voltage source** ($V_R$) — this can be **0 V** (ground) for basic clippers, or a DC battery for biased clippers 3. A **series resistor** ($R$) — protects the circuit and defines the current path; the output is taken across either the diode or the resistor depending on topology. The two fundamental **output measurement configurations** are: - **Shunt Clipper:** Output is taken across the **diode branch**. The diode is in *parallel* (shunt) with the load. - **Series Clipper:** Output is taken across the **resistor**. The diode is in *series* with the signal path. ## 2.2 Positive Clipper (Shunt Configuration) **Circuit:** A diode $D$ is connected with its **cathode at the input node** and **anode at ground**, output is taken at the input node (across the diode-resistor parallel combination). ### Operational Logic (Ideal Diode) **Step 1 — When $V_{in} > 0$:** - The diode anode is at 0 V, cathode is at $V_{in} > 0$. - Therefore, the diode is **reverse biased** (OFF). - No current flows through $R$ from the source (assuming load is high impedance). - **Output follows input:** $V_{out} = V_{in}$. **Step 2 — When $V_{in} < 0$:** - The diode anode is at 0 V, cathode is at $V_{in} < 0$. - The diode is **forward biased** (ON), acts as a short circuit. - The output node is **clamped to ground**: $V_{out} = 0\text{ V}$. - The negative portion is "clipped off." $$ V_{out}(t) = \begin{cases} V_{in}(t) & \text{for } V_{in}(t) \leq 0 \quad \text{(diode OFF)} \\ 0 & \text{for } V_{in}(t) > 0 \quad \text{(diode ON)} \end{cases} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Note:** Wait — the *operational logic above is reversed* depending on diode orientation. In the **shunt positive clipper** (cathode at input, anode at ground), the diode conducts for $V_{in} < 0$, clamping output to 0. To clip the **positive half**, reverse the diode (anode at input, cathode at ground). The KTU 2024 examiner expects the student to **state the diode orientation explicitly** and verify it via a sketch. ## 2.3 Negative Clipper (Shunt Configuration) The diode is **reversed** compared to the positive clipper: - **Anode** at input, **cathode** at ground. $$ V_{out}(t) = \begin{cases} V_{in}(t) & \text{for } V_{in}(t) \geq 0 \quad \text{(diode OFF)} \\ 0 & \text{for } V_{in}(t) < 0 \quad \text{(diode ON)} \end{cases} $$ ## 2.4 Biased Clipper — The Generalized Case A **DC bias voltage $V_R$** is inserted between the diode's lower terminal and ground. The clipping level is no longer 0 V, but a **user-defined threshold $V_{ref}$**. For a **biased positive clipper** (diode cathode at input, anode at $V_R$): - Diode ON when: $V_{in} < V_R$ → $V_{out} = V_R$ - Diode OFF when: $V_{in} \geq V_R$ → $V_{out} = V_{in}$ For a **biased negative clipper** (diode anode at input, cathode at $V_R$): - Diode ON when: $V_{in} > V_R$ → $V_{out} = V_R$ - Diode OFF when: $V_{in} \leq V_R$ → $V_{out} = V_{in}$ ## 2.5 Practical Diode Considerations (Silicon, $V_\gamma = 0.7\text{ V}$) When using a **practical silicon diode** instead of an ideal diode, the diode does not conduct until $V_{AK} \geq 0.7\text{ V}$. This shifts the clipping level by **+0.7 V** or **-0.7 V** depending on orientation. $$ V_{out}^{\text{clipped}} = V_R \pm V_\gamma $$ ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Configuration | Diode Orientation | $V_{in}$ Range | Diode State | Output $V_{out}$ | |---|---------------|-------------------|----------------|-------------|------------------| | 1 | Positive Clipper (Ideal) | Cathode at input, Anode at GND | $V_{in} > 0$ | OFF | $V_{in}$ | | 2 | Positive Clipper (Ideal) | Cathode at input, Anode at GND | $V_{in} \leq 0$ | ON | $0\text{ V}$ | | 3 | Negative Clipper (Ideal) | Anode at input, Cathode at GND | $V_{in} \geq 0$ | OFF | $V_{in}$ | | 4 | Negative Clipper (Ideal) | Anode at input, Cathode at GND | $V_{in} < 0$ | ON | $0\text{ V}$ | | 5 | Biased Positive (Ideal) | Cathode at input, Anode at $V_R$ | $V_{in} \geq V_R$ | OFF | $V_{in}$ | | 6 | Biased Positive (Ideal) | Cathode at input, Anode at $V_R$ | $V_{in} < V_R$ | ON | $V_R$ | | 7 | Biased Negative (Ideal) | Anode at input, Cathode at $V_R$ | $V_{in} \leq V_R$ | OFF | $V_{in}$ | | 8 | Biased Negative (Ideal) | Anode at input, Cathode at $V_R$ | $V_{in} > V_R$ | ON | $V_R$ | | 9 | Practical Si Diode Offset | All configurations | All | — | $V_{out}^{\text{clipped}} = V_R \pm 0.7$ | | 10 | Series Resistor (current limit) | $I_{max} = \frac{V_{in}^{\text{peak}} - V_R}{R}$ | — | ON | $R$ chosen for safe $I_{max}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** Clipping circuits are widely used in: > - **TV receivers** — to separate sync pulses from composite video signals > - **Overshoot protection** — protecting sensitive electronics from voltage spikes > - **AM/FM demodulation** — limiting the signal amplitude before detection > - **Function generators** — producing square waves from sine waves > - **ESD protection** — clamping transient voltages to safe levels ## 2.7 Design Rules of Thumb - **Choose $R \geq 1\text{ k}\Omega$** to limit diode current to safe values ($I_{max} \approx 10\text{ mA}$ for small-signal diodes like 1N4148). - **For high-frequency applications**, the **junction capacitance** of the diode ($C_j \approx 2\text{-}5\text{ pF}$ for 1N4148) becomes the bandwidth-limiting factor. - **The clipping level accuracy** depends on the diode's $V_\gamma$ tolerance ($\pm 0.05\text{ V}$ for 1N4148) and the **stability of $V_R$**. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Transfer Characteristic of an Ideal Positive Shunt Clipper **Given:** - Series resistor $R$ from source $V_{in}$ to output node. - Diode $D$ from output node to ground (cathode at output, anode at ground). - Output is taken at the output node. - Diode is **ideal** (zero forward drop, zero reverse leakage). **Find:** The mathematical relationship $V_{out} = f(V_{in})$. ### Step-by-Step Analysis **Case 1: $V_{in} \geq 0\text{ V}$** Assume the diode is **OFF** (reverse biased). Then no current flows through the diode branch. Applying KCL at the output node (assuming load is open circuit / very high impedance): $$ I_R = \frac{V_{in} - V_{out}}{R}, \quad I_D = 0 $$ For an open-circuited output (no load current): $$ I_R = I_D = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{out} = V_{in} $$ **Verify assumption:** $V_{AK} = V_{anode} - V_{cathode} = 0 - V_{in} = -V_{in} \leq 0$ when $V_{in} \geq 0$. The diode is indeed reverse biased. **Assumption is valid.** **Case 2: $V_{in} < 0\text{ V}$** Assume the diode is **ON** (forward biased, ideal). Then the diode acts as a short circuit, so the output is clamped to the diode's anode voltage: $$ V_{out} = V_{anode} = 0\text{ V} $$ **Verify assumption:** With $V_{out} = 0$, $V_{AK} = 0 - 0 = 0$. The diode is at the boundary of conduction — for an ideal diode, this is treated as ON. **Assumption is valid.** ### Final Transfer Function $$ \boxed{V_{out} = \begin{cases} V_{in} & V_{in} \geq 0\text{ V} \\ 0 & V_{in} < 0\text{ V} \end{cases}} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Biased Positive Clipper with Practical Silicon Diode **Given:** - $V_R = +5\text{ V}$ DC reference. - Silicon diode ($V_\gamma = 0.7\text{ V}$) with cathode at input, anode at $V_R$. - Series resistor $R = 1\text{ k}\Omega$. **Find:** Output expression and clipping threshold. ### Step-by-Step Analysis **Case 1: Diode OFF** — $V_{in} \geq V_R + V_\gamma = 5 + 0.7 = 5.7\text{ V}$ No diode current. Output follows input: $$ V_{out} = V_{in} $$ **Case 2: Diode ON** — $V_{in} < 5.7\text{ V}$ When ON, the diode drop is exactly $V_\gamma = 0.7\text{ V}$. The output is: $$ V_{out} = V_R + V_\gamma = 5 + 0.7 = 5.7\text{ V} $$ Wait — let me carefully re-derive this. The diode's anode is at $V_R$, cathode is at $V_{out}$ (since output is at the input node through R, but actually for shunt clipper output is at the diode's cathode node). For a shunt biased clipper, output is taken at the **cathode** of the diode. When the diode conducts: $$ V_{out} = V_{anode} - V_\gamma = V_R - V_\gamma $$ So for **anode at $V_R$, cathode at output**, when ON: $$ \boxed{V_{out}^{\text{clipped}} = V_R - V_\gamma = 5 - 0.7 = 4.3\text{ V}} $$ The clipping threshold (transition point) is at $V_{in} = 4.3\text{ V}$. For $V_{in} > 4.3\text{ V}$, diode is OFF and $V_{out} = V_{in}$. For $V_{in} \leq 4.3\text{ V}$, diode is ON and $V_{out} = 4.3\text{ V}$. ### Final Transfer Function $$ V_{out}(t) = \begin{cases} V_{in}(t) & V_{in}(t) \geq 4.3\text{ V} \\ 4.3\text{ V} & V_{in}(t) < 4.3\text{ V} \end{cases} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Diode Current and Resistor Sizing For a series resistor $R$, when the diode is conducting and the input is at its negative peak $V_{in,\min}$: $$ I_{D,\text{peak}} = \frac{V_{in,\min} - V_R}{R} $$ **Design example:** Suppose $V_{in}$ is a sine wave with peak $\pm 10\text{ V}$, $V_R = 0\text{ V}$, and we want peak diode current limited to $I_{D,\text{peak}} = 10\text{ mA}$ for a 1N4148 diode (max forward current $I_F = 300\text{ mA}$). For ideal diode, $V_\gamma = 0$: $$ R = \frac{V_{in,\min} - V_R}{I_{D,\text{peak}}} = \frac{-10 - 0}{-10 \times 10^{-3}} = 1\text{ k}\Omega $$ For practical Si diode ($V_\gamma = 0.7\text{ V}$): $$ R = \frac{V_{in,\min} - V_R + V_\gamma}{I_{D,\text{peak}}} = \frac{-10 + 0.7}{-10 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.93\text{ k}\Omega \approx 1\text{ k}\Omega $$ ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — All Clipper Variants | # | Circuit Name | Diode Position | $V_R$ Value | Input $\geq$ Threshold | Input $<$ Threshold | Practical Offset | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Positive Shunt (Ideal) | Cathode at input | $0\text{ V}$ | $V_{out} = V_{in}$ | $V_{out} = 0$ | $+0.7\text{ V}$ | | 2 | Negative Shunt (Ideal) | Anode at input | $0\text{ V}$ | $V_{out} = V_{in}$ | $V_{out} = 0$ | $-0.7\text{ V}$ | | 3 | Biased Positive Shunt | Cathode at input | $+V_R$ | $V_{out} = V_{in}$ | $V_{out} = V_R - 0.7$ | $V_R - 0.7$ | | 4 | Biased Negative Shunt | Anode at input | $+V_R$ | $V_{out} = V_R + 0.7$ | $V_{out} = V_{in}$ | $V_R + 0.7$ | | 5 | Biased Positive (Neg $V_R$) | Cathode at input | $-V_R$ | $V_{out} = V_{in}$ | $V_{out} = -V_R - 0.7$ | $-V_R - 0.7$ | | 6 | Series Positive | Diode in series with $R$ | — | $V_{out} = V_{in} - 0.7$ | $V_{out} = V_R$ | $\pm 0.7$ shift | ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style) **Problem:** A biased positive clipper has $V_R = 3\text{ V}$, $R = 2.2\text{ k}\Omega$, and uses a silicon diode ($V_\gamma = 0.7\text{ V}$). The input is $V_{in}(t) = 8 \sin(\omega t)\text{ V}$. Plot $V_{out}(t)$ for one full cycle and find the peak diode current. **Solution:** **Step 1:** Identify the diode orientation. For a biased **positive** clipper, the diode is connected with **cathode at the input node** and **anode at $V_R = +3\text{ V}$**. **Step 2:** The diode conducts when $V_{anode} > V_{cathode} + V_\gamma$, i.e., when $3 > V_{in} + 0.7$, i.e., when $V_{in} < 2.3\text{ V}$. **Step 3:** When the diode conducts, the output is clamped: $$ V_{out} = V_{anode} - V_\gamma = 3 - 0.7 = 2.3\text{ V} $$ **Step 4:** When the diode is OFF ($V_{in} \geq 2.3\text{ V}$), $V_{out} = V_{in}$. **Step 5:** Peak diode current occurs when $V_{in} = -8\text{ V}$ (most negative point): $$ I_{D,\text{peak}} = \frac{V_{in} - V_{out}}{R} = \frac{-8 - 2.3}{2200} = \frac{-10.3}{2200} \approx -4.68\text{ mA} $$ The negative sign indicates current flows from the diode's anode ($V_R$) into the source — this is the **discharging** current through the diode. The magnitude is $|I_{D,\text{peak}}| \approx 4.68\text{ mA}$, well within the 1N4148's rating. **Output waveform (textual representation):** $$ V_{out}(t) = \begin{cases} 2.3\text{ V} & \text{when } 8\sin(\omega t) < 2.3\text{ V (i.e., most of the negative half-cycle)} \\ 8\sin(\omega t) & \text{when } 8\sin(\omega t) \geq 2.3\text{ V (positive peaks above } 2.3\text{ V)} \end{cases} $$ The positive peaks from $2.3\text{ V}$ to $+8\text{ V}$ are preserved; everything else is clipped at $2.3\text{ V}$. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Clipping Circuit ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeSrc["AC VOLTAGE SOURCE
Vin t"]:::input nodeR["SERIES RESISTOR
R = 1K OHM"]:::resistor nodeOut["OUTPUT NODE
Vout"]:::output nodeD["DIODE BRANCH
D with bias VR"]:::diode nodeRef["DC BIAS
VR POSITIVE OR NEGATIVE"]:::reference nodeGnd["GROUND
0V"]:::ground nodeSrc -- "Signal path" --> nodeR nodeR -- "To output terminal" --> nodeOut nodeOut -- "Shunt path" --> nodeD nodeD -- "Connected to" --> nodeRef nodeRef --> nodeGnd classDef input fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef resistor fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef output fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef diode fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef reference fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef ground fill:#A9A9A9,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF ``` ## 4.2 Decision Flow — How the Diode Operates ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart["INPUT SIGNAL Vin arrives"]:::start nodeCheck1{"Compare Vin with
reference level VR plus 0.7V"}:::decision nodeOn["Diode is FORWARD BIASED
Acts as short circuit
Vout clamped to VR minus 0.7V"]:::active nodeOff["Diode is REVERSE BIASED
Acts as open circuit
Vout equals Vin"]:::passive nodeEnd1["OUTPUT Vout follows input"]:::output nodeEnd2["OUTPUT Vout fixed at clipping level"]:::output nodeStart --> nodeCheck1 nodeCheck1 -- "Vin greater than VR plus 0.7" --> nodeOff nodeCheck1 -- "Vin less than VR plus 0.7" --> nodeOn nodeOff --> nodeEnd1 nodeOn --> nodeEnd2 classDef start fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#DAA520,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef active fill:#FF6347,stroke:#B22222,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF classDef passive fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef output fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Comparison of Three Clipper Families ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeFamily["CLIPPING CIRCUIT FAMILY
Diode Wave Shaping"]:::family nodePos["POSITIVE CLIPPER
Removes positive half cycle
Diode cathode at input"]:::pos nodeNeg["NEGATIVE CLIPPER
Removes negative half cycle
Diode anode at input"]:::neg nodeBiased["BIASED CLIPPER
Clips at custom level VR
Diode plus DC bias source"]:::biased nodeBiasedPos["BIASED POSITIVE
Clips above VR"]:::subtype nodeBiasedNeg["BIASED NEGATIVE
Clips below VR"]:::subtype nodeDouble["DOUBLE CLIPPER
Two diodes in parallel
Clips both extremes"]:::subtype nodeFamily --> nodePos nodeFamily --> nodeNeg nodeFamily --> nodeBiased nodeBiased --> nodeBiasedPos nodeBiased --> nodeBiasedNeg nodeBiased --> nodeDouble classDef family fill:#4B0082,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF classDef pos fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#CD5C5C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef neg fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef biased fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef subtype fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Signal Path — Transfer Characteristic Block ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["INPUT REGION
Vin less than VR
Diode is OFF"]:::region nodeB["TRANSITION POINT
Vin equals VR plus 0.7V
Knee of curve"]:::transition nodeC["OUTPUT REGION
Vin greater than VR
Diode is ON clipping"]:::region nodeShape["TRANSFER CURVE
Slope 1 in pass region
Flat in clip region"]:::curve nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeShape -.describes.-> nodeA nodeShape -.describes.-> nodeB nodeShape -.describes.-> nodeC classDef region fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef transition fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef curve fill:#FAFAD2,stroke:#BDB76B,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Define a clipping circuit. With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **positive clipper** using an ideal diode. Sketch its input and output waveforms for a sinusoidal input.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** A clipping circuit is a wave-shaping circuit that uses a diode to remove a portion of the input signal that lies above or below a reference voltage level, without affecting the remaining part of the waveform. **Circuit and Operation (1 Mark):** In a positive clipper, the diode has its **cathode connected to the input node** and its **anode connected to ground**. Output is taken across the diode. When $V_{in} > 0$, the diode is reverse biased (OFF), so $V_{out} = V_{in}$. When $V_{in} \leq 0$, the diode is forward biased (ON), clamping the output to $V_{out} = 0\text{ V}$. **Waveform (1 Mark):** The output consists of only the negative half-cycle of the input (since the positive half is clipped to 0 V), and the negative half-cycle is preserved with unity gain. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students draw the diode in the wrong orientation (anode at input, cathode at ground) and end up describing a **negative clipper** instead of a positive clipper. The examiner **deducts 1 mark** for this diode-orientation error. Always verify: the diode must conduct during the half-cycle you want to *clip off*. --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **What is the difference between a **clamping circuit** and a **clipping circuit**? State one application of each.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Aspect | Clipping Circuit | Clamping Circuit | |--------|------------------|------------------| | Function | Limits the *amplitude* of the signal to a reference level | Shifts the entire signal *vertically* (DC level) by adding a DC offset | | Waveform shape | The shape of the clipped portion is **lost** (flattened) | The original waveform **shape is preserved** | | Element used | Diode + resistor (no capacitor in basic version) | Diode + capacitor (the capacitor stores the DC shift) | | Energy storage | No storage element | Capacitor stores the shift voltage | **Application of Clipper (0.5 Marks):** Used in TV receivers for **sync pulse separation** from composite video signals. **Application of Clamper (0.5 Marks):** Used as a **DC restorer** in oscilloscope inputs to remove baseline wander. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently write "clipping adds a DC offset" — this is **wrong**. Clipping **removes** a portion; clamping **adds** a DC offset. Confusing these two concepts costs the full 3 marks in a definition question. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks and offer an **internal choice** between two questions. Each question typically has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. You may attempt **either** option in full. ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, End Semester]** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **With a neat circuit diagram and waveform sketches, explain the operation of a **biased positive clipper** with a DC reference voltage $V_R = 4\text{ V}$. Use a silicon diode ($V_\gamma = 0.7\text{ V}$). The input is a triangular wave with peak amplitude $\pm 10\text{ V}$. Derive the transfer characteristic and plot the output waveform.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Circuit Description (2 Marks):** The biased positive clipper has a diode with its **cathode at the input node** and its **anode connected to a DC reference $V_R = +4\text{ V}$** (with the reference source's other terminal at ground). Output is taken at the input/output node (shunt configuration). A series resistor $R$ is included to limit the diode current. **Step 2 — Diode State Analysis (2 Marks):** The diode conducts (is ON) when its anode voltage exceeds its cathode voltage by $V_\gamma$: $$ V_{anode} - V_{cathode} \geq V_\gamma $$ $$ 4 - V_{out} \geq 0.7 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{out} \leq 3.3\text{ V} $$ When $V_{in} \leq 3.3\text{ V}$, the diode is ON and $V_{out} = 3.3\text{ V}$. When $V_{in} > 3.3\text{ V}$, the diode is OFF and $V_{out} = V_{in}$. **Step 3 — Transfer Characteristic (2 Marks):** $$ V_{out} = \begin{cases} V_{in} & V_{in} > 3.3\text{ V} \\ 3.3\text{ V} & V_{in} \leq 3.3\text{ V} \end{cases} $$ **Step 4 — Output Waveform for Triangular Input (1 Mark):** For a $\pm 10\text{ V}$ triangular input: - From $V_{in} = -10\text{ V}$ rising up to $V_{in} = 3.3\text{ V}$: output is **clamped at 3.3 V** (flat horizontal line). - From $V_{in} = 3.3\text{ V}$ rising up to $V_{in} = +10\text{ V}$: output **follows input** linearly. - From $V_{in} = +10\text{ V}$ falling back down to $V_{in} = 3.3\text{ V}$: output **follows input** linearly. - From $V_{in} = 3.3\text{ V}$ falling to $V_{in} = -10\text{ V}$: output is **clamped at 3.3 V**. The result is a "rounded-off" clipped waveform with the lower 13.3 V of the original signal range replaced by a flat 3.3 V line. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **For the circuit in part (a), if $R = 1.2\text{ k}\Omega$, calculate the peak diode current and the power dissipated in the diode at the moment when $V_{in} = -8\text{ V}$. Assume the diode's average forward resistance is $r_f = 10\ \Omega$ during conduction.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Equivalent Circuit at $V_{in} = -8\text{ V}$ (2 Marks):** When $V_{in} = -8\text{ V}$ and the diode is conducting, the output node is clamped at $V_{out} = 3.3\text{ V}$. The series resistor $R = 1.2\text{ k}\Omega$ has the full voltage difference $V_{in} - V_{out} = -8 - 3.3 = -11.3\text{ V}$ across it. **Step 2 — Diode Current Calculation (3 Marks):** Using the piecewise model (battery $V_\gamma = 0.7\text{ V}$ + resistance $r_f = 10\ \Omega$): $$ I_D = \frac{V_{in} - V_{out}}{R} = \frac{-11.3}{1200} = -9.42\text{ mA} $$ The negative sign indicates the current direction is from the diode toward the source (the diode is "absorbing" the excess input voltage by sinking it through $R$). The **magnitude** is: $$ \boxed{\vert I_D \vert = 9.42\text{ mA}} $$ (If we also include $r_f$ in the model: the additional 0.7 V drop is already accounted for in $V_{out} = V_R - V_\gamma$. The $r_f$ would create a small additional drop of $I_D \times r_f = 9.42 \times 10^{-3} \times 10 = 0.094\text{ V}$, but for hand calculations this is usually neglected.) **Step 3 — Power Dissipation (2 Marks):** Power dissipated in the diode: $$ P_D = V_D \times I_D = 0.7\text{ V} \times 9.42\text{ mA} = 6.59\text{ mW} $$ $$ \boxed{P_D \approx 6.6\text{ mW}} $$ This is well within the 1N4148's power rating of 500 mW. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, End Semester]** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Design a **double clipper** circuit (clipper that limits both positive and negative peaks) using two diodes and two DC reference voltages to produce an output that lies between $+3\text{ V}$ and $-2\text{ V}$ from a sinusoidal input of peak $\pm 12\text{ V}$. Assume silicon diodes with $V_\gamma = 0.7\text{ V}$. Draw the circuit and explain its operation.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Reference Voltage Selection (2 Marks):** For the output to be limited between $+3\text{ V}$ (upper) and $-2\text{ V}$ (lower): - **Upper clipper:** To clip at $+3\text{ V}$ on the positive side, we need a diode with its **cathode at the output** and **anode at $V_{R1} = +3.7\text{ V}$** (so that $V_{out} = 3.7 - 0.7 = 3.0\text{ V}$ when conducting). - **Lower clipper:** To clip at $-2\text{ V}$ on the negative side, we need a diode with its **anode at the output** and **cathode at $V_{R2} = -2.7\text{ V}$** (so that $V_{out} = -2.7 + 0.7 = -2.0\text{ V}$ when conducting). **Step 2 — Circuit Description (2 Marks):** The double clipper consists of: - A series resistor $R$ (e.g., $1\text{ k}\Omega$) connected from $V_{in}$ to $V_{out}$. - Diode $D_1$ (cathode at $V_{out}$, anode at $+3.7\text{ V}$) — conducts on negative-going input. - Diode $D_2$ (anode at $V_{out}$, cathode at $-2.7\text{ V}$) — conducts on positive-going input. - Two DC reference batteries: $V_{R1} = +3.7\text{ V}$ and $V_{R2} = -2.7\text{ V}$. **Step 3 — Operation (2 Marks):** - When $V_{in} > +3\text{ V}$: $D_1$ is OFF (anode at $3.7\text{ V}$, cathode at $V_{out} > 3\text{ V}$ means $V_{AK} < 0.7$ V), $D_2$ is ON (cathode at $-2.7\text{ V}$, anode at $V_{out} > -2.7 + 0.7 = -2\text{ V}$, so $V_{AK} \geq 0.7$ V when $V_{out} \geq -2\text{ V}$)... - Wait, the analysis needs to be checked. Let me re-examine. **Corrected Operation:** - When $V_{in}$ tries to **exceed +3 V**: $D_2$ would need to conduct to clamp the output. $D_2$ is on if $V_{out} > -2.7 + 0.7 = -2.0\text{ V}$ — this is satisfied. But $D_2$ clamps output to $-2.7 + 0.7 = -2.0\text{ V}$... Hmm, this is a sign error. Let me reconsider — actually for a **positive-side clipper at +3 V**, we need a diode that conducts when $V_{in} > +3\text{ V}$. This diode should clamp $V_{out}$ to $+3\text{ V}$. So: - The **cathode of this diode** is at $V_{out}$ and its **anode** is at $V_{R1}$ such that $V_{out} = V_{R1} - 0.7$. - To clamp at $V_{out} = +3\text{ V}$, we need $V_{R1} = +3.7\text{ V}$. ✓ - This diode conducts when $V_{out} \leq V_{R1} - 0.7 = +3\text{ V}$, i.e., when $V_{in} \leq +3\text{ V}$. So actually this diode is OFF when $V_{in} > +3\text{ V}$ (because then $V_{out} = V_{in} > 3\text{ V}$ means $V_{AK} = V_{R1} - V_{out} = 3.7 - V_{in} < 0.7$, so OFF), and ON when $V_{in} \leq +3\text{ V}$. I need to reverse the diode for the **positive side clipper**: - **For positive-side clipping at +3 V**: Diode $D_1$ has **anode at $V_{out}$** and **cathode at $V_{R1} = +3.7\text{ V}$**. - $D_1$ conducts when $V_{out} - V_{R1} \geq 0.7$, i.e., $V_{out} \geq 4.4\text{ V}$, i.e., $V_{in} \geq 4.4\text{ V}$. - When conducting, $V_{out} = V_{R1} + 0.7 = 4.4\text{ V}$... that's not what we want. The issue is that the clipping **threshold** and the **clamped output level** differ by $V_\gamma$ in the same direction for one polarity and opposite for the other. Let me redo this cleanly. **Clean Derivation:** We want $V_{out}$ to be limited to the range $[-2\text{ V}, +3\text{ V}]$. **Diode $D_1$ (positive-side clipper):** - Orientation: **anode at $V_{out}$, cathode at $V_{R1}$**. - $D_1$ ON when $V_{out} - V_{R1} \geq 0.7$, i.e., $V_{out} \geq V_{R1} + 0.7$. - When ON, $V_{out} = V_{R1} + 0.7$. - We want $V_{out} = +3\text{ V}$ when $D_1$ conducts, so $V_{R1} = 3 - 0.7 = +2.3\text{ V}$. - $D_1$ ON threshold: $V_{out} \geq 2.3 + 0.7 = 3.0\text{ V}$, so $V_{in} \geq 3.0\text{ V}$ (since when $D_1$ is OFF, $V_{out} = V_{in}$). - **$D_1$ clips the positive peaks at +3 V.** ✓ **Diode $D_2$ (negative-side clipper):** - Orientation: **cathode at $V_{out}$, anode at $V_{R2}$**. - $D_2$ ON when $V_{R2} - V_{out} \geq 0.7$, i.e., $V_{out} \leq V_{R2} - 0.7$. - When ON, $V_{out} = V_{R2} - 0.7$. - We want $V_{out} = -2\text{ V}$ when $D_2$ conducts, so $V_{R2} = -2 + 0.7 = -1.3\text{ V}$. - $D_2$ ON threshold: $V_{out} \leq -1.3 - 0.7 = -2.0\text{ V}$, so $V_{in} \leq -2.0\text{ V}$. - **$D_2$ clips the negative peaks at -2 V.** ✓ **Corrected Final Values:** $$ \boxed{V_{R1} = +2.3\text{ V}, \quad V_{R2} = -1.3\text{ V}} $$ **Step 4 — Final Output Equation (1 Mark):** $$ V_{out}(t) = \begin{cases} +3\text{ V} & V_{in}(t) \geq +3\text{ V} \\ -2\text{ V} & V_{in}(t) \leq -2\text{ V} \\ V_{in}(t) & -2\text{ V} < V_{in}(t) < +3\text{ V} \end{cases} $$ The output is a "truncated sine wave" with both peaks flattened. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **A positive shunt clipper uses a silicon diode with $V_\gamma = 0.7\text{ V}$ and $R = 1\text{ k}\Omega$. The input is $V_{in}(t) = 15\sin(100\pi t)\text{ V}$. Find: (i) the output waveform expression, (ii) the peak diode current, (iii) the time during one full cycle for which the diode conducts.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Identify Configuration (1 Mark):** Positive shunt clipper: diode **cathode at input node**, **anode at ground**. Output is across the diode (at the input/output node). **Step 2 — Diode State (1 Mark):** The diode is forward biased (ON) when $V_{in} < V_\gamma = 0.7\text{ V}$, i.e., the diode conducts for the **negative half-cycle plus the small positive portion from 0 to 0.7 V**. When diode is ON: $V_{out} = V_{anode} + V_D = 0 + 0.7 = 0.7\text{ V}$ (the diode clamps the output to +0.7 V — not exactly 0 V as in the ideal case). When diode is OFF ($V_{in} \geq 0.7\text{ V}$): $V_{out} = V_{in}$. **Step 3 — Output Expression (1 Mark):** $$ V_{out}(t) = \begin{cases} V_{in}(t) & V_{in}(t) \geq 0.7\text{ V} \\ 0.7\text{ V} & V_{in}(t) < 0.7\text{ V} \end{cases} $$ **Step 4 — Peak Diode Current (2 Marks):** Peak diode current occurs at the most negative input ($V_{in,\min} = -15\text{ V}$): $$ I_{D,\text{peak}} = \frac{V_{in,\min} - V_{out}}{R} = \frac{-15 - 0.7}{1000} = -15.7\text{ mA} $$ $$ \boxed{\vert I_{D,\text{peak}} \vert = 15.7\text{ mA}} $$ **Step 5 — Conduction Time (2 Marks):** The diode conducts when $V_{in}(t) = 15\sin(100\pi t) < 0.7\text{ V}$. Solve: $\sin(100\pi t) < 0.7/15 = 0.0467$ $$ 100\pi t < \sin^{-1}(0.0467) = 2.677° \text{ or } 100\pi t > 180° - 2.677° = 177.32° $$ Converting to radians: $\sin^{-1}(0.0467) \approx 0.0467\text{ rad}$ (small angle approximation) and $180° = \pi\text{ rad}$. Conduction starts at: $\omega t_1 = \pi - \sin^{-1}(0.0467) = \pi - 0.0467 \approx 3.095\text{ rad}$ Conduction ends at: $\omega t_2 = 2\pi + \sin^{-1}(0.0467) = 2\pi + 0.0467 \approx 6.330\text{ rad}$ (next cycle) Conduction angle: $\Delta \theta = 2\pi - 2\sin^{-1}(0.0467) = 2(\pi - 0.0467) \approx 6.094\text{ rad}$ Conduction time: $T_{cond} = \dfrac{\Delta \theta}{\omega} = \dfrac{6.094}{100\pi} = 19.4\text{ ms}$ Period of the signal: $T = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega} = \dfrac{2\pi}{100\pi} = 20\text{ ms}$ $$ \boxed{T_{cond} \approx 19.4\text{ ms} \quad \text{(diode conducts for 97\% of one full cycle)}} $$ This makes physical sense: the diode conducts for almost the entire negative half-cycle plus a tiny portion of the positive half-cycle (between 0 and 0.7 V). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Clipping Circuits** - [x] **Definition:** Clipper = wave-shaping circuit that limits signal amplitude to a reference level using diode switching action. - [x] **Three Families:** Positive, Negative, and Biased clippers. - [x] **Diode Orientation is Critical:** - Positive clipper (cathode at input, anode at GND or $V_R$). - Negative clipper (anode at input, cathode at GND or $V_R$). - [x] **Ideal Diode Assumption:** $V_\gamma = 0\text{ V}$, no reverse leakage. Use this in the first pass; correct for $V_\gamma = 0.7\text{ V}$ (Si) or $0.3\text{ V}$ (Ge) only when explicitly asked. - [x] **Biased Clipper Threshold:** $V_{ref} = V_R \pm V_\gamma$ (sign depends on diode orientation). - [x] **Series Resistor Role:** Limits diode current; chosen so that $I_{D,\text{peak}} < I_{F,\text{max}}$ of the diode. - [x] **Practical Si Diode:** Always remember the $0.7\text{ V}$ offset — it shifts the clipping level by $\pm 0.7\text{ V}$. - [x] **Transfer Function Format:** Always express $V_{out}$ as a **piecewise function** of $V_{in}$ in your answer. - [x] **Clipping vs. Clamping:** Clipping removes a portion (changes shape); Clamping shifts the level (preserves shape). - [x] **KTU 2024 Hot Topics:** 1. Biased clipper with non-zero $V_R$ (most common 7-mark question). 2. Transfer characteristic plotting. 3. Double clipper (two-diode design problem). 4. Peak diode current and power dissipation calculations. - [x] **Common Pitfalls:** - Wrong diode orientation → wrong clipper type → 1-2 mark deduction. - Forgetting the $V_\gamma = 0.7\text{ V}$ in silicon diode problems. - Confusing clipping with clamping in definition questions.

topics.title: Clamping Circuits - Positive, Negative and Biased
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES & CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Clamping Circuits - Positive, Negative and Biased

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Clamping Circuit** (also known as a **DC Restorer** or **Level Shifter**) is an electronic network consisting of a **diode**, a **capacitor**, and a **resistor** that shifts an entire periodic waveform either **upward** (positive clamping) or **downward** (negative clamping) along the voltage axis, without altering the peak-to-peak amplitude or fundamental shape of the signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** A clamper adds a **DC component** to the input AC signal. The output waveform is a **replica of the input** but shifted by some reference voltage level. The clamping level can be the **zero-volt line** (unbiased) or an **arbitrary DC bias voltage** $V_{REF}$ (biased clamping).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Identity of a Clamper:**
> - Peak-to-Peak Voltage: $V_{pp(\text{out})} = V_{pp(\text{in})}$
> - DC Level Shifted: Yes
> - Waveform Shape Preserved: Yes

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **swimming pool with a movable floor**. The pool itself is your AC signal (it has waves going up and down). The movable floor is the **capacitor's stored charge**. By adjusting the floor's height (i.e., charging the capacitor to a specific DC voltage), you can shift the *entire water surface* up or down without changing how tall the waves are. The **diode** acts like a one-way valve that only lets the floor move in one direction (charging), while the **resistor** allows slow leakage (discharging) so the floor can adjust to the next wave.

In simple terms:
- **Clamper = "Wave with a new origin."**
- It does **not** clip the signal (unlike a clipper).
- It **adds or subtracts** a DC offset.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Clamping Circuits

A clamping circuit can be classified into three principal families based on the reference level:

| Type | Reference Level | DC Shift Direction |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Positive Clamper** | $0\text{ V}$ (ground) | Shifts waveform **upward** |
| **Negative Clamper** | $0\text{ V}$ (ground) | Shifts waveform **downward** |
| **Biased Positive Clamper** | $+V_{REF}$ | Shifts waveform upward by $V_{REF}$ |
| **Biased Negative Clamper** | $-V_{REF}$ | Shifts waveform downward by $V_{REF}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Naming Convention (KTU Standard):** A "positive clamper" places the **negative peak of the output at the zero-volt line**, while a "negative clamper" places the **positive peak of the output at the zero-volt line**. The terminology refers to the **direction of the DC shift**, not the polarity of the diode used.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle — The Charge-Pump Mechanism

Every clamper relies on the **asymmetric RC time constant principle**:

$$\tau_{\text{charge}} \ll \tau_{\text{discharge}}$$

- **During one half-cycle**, the diode is **forward-biased (ON)**. The capacitor charges **rapidly** through the **small diode forward resistance** $r_d$ (typically $\approx 50\ \Omega$ to $100\ \Omega$).
- **During the other half-cycle**, the diode is **reverse-biased (OFF)**. The capacitor **slowly discharges** through the **large load resistor** $R$ (typically $10\ \text{k}\Omega$ to $100\ \text{k}\Omega$).

This ensures the capacitor holds nearly its peak charge value, effectively acting as a **DC battery** in series with the signal source.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

### A. Positive Clamper (using ideal diode, $V_D = 0$)
1. **Input negative half-cycle peak** = $-V_m$ reaches the diode anode more negative than cathode.
2. Diode is **forward-biased** → capacitor charges to $V_C = V_m$ (right plate positive, left plate negative).
3. **Input positive half-cycle peak** = $+V_m$ arrives. The diode becomes **reverse-biased** (OFF).
4. Output = $V_{\text{in}} + V_C = V_{\text{in}} + V_m$.
5. Maximum output = $+V_m + V_m = +2V_m$; Minimum output = $-V_m + V_m = 0\text{ V}$.
6. **Net effect:** waveform shifted **upward** by $V_m$; negative peak now sits at $0\text{ V}$.

### B. Negative Clamper
1. Diode is **reversed** compared to the positive clamper.
2. Capacitor charges to $V_C = -V_m$ during the positive half-cycle.
3. Output = $V_{\text{in}} - V_m$.
4. **Net effect:** waveform shifted **downward** by $V_m$; positive peak now sits at $0\text{ V}$.

### C. Biased Clamper
1. A **DC voltage source** $V_B$ is placed in **series with the diode** (or with $R$).
2. The diode now conducts only when the input exceeds $V_B$ in the appropriate polarity.
3. The capacitor charges to $V_C = V_m - V_B$ (or $V_m + V_B$ depending on bias polarity).
4. Output DC shift becomes $(V_m - V_B)$ instead of $V_m$.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All values below are exam-ready. Memorize this table for KTU 2024 ESE questions.**

| Parameter | Formula / Value | Physical Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Charging time constant | $\tau_c = r_d \cdot C$ | Time during diode conduction |
| Discharging time constant | $\tau_d = R \cdot C$ | Time during diode non-conduction |
| Design condition | $\tau_d \gg T$ (period of input) | Ensures capacitor holds charge |
| Output DC shift (positive clamper) | $V_{\text{shift}} = V_m$ | Total upward translation |
| Output DC shift (negative clamper) | $V_{\text{shift}} = -V_m$ | Total downward translation |
| Output DC shift (biased positive) | $V_{\text{shift}} = (V_m - V_B)$ | Reduced upward shift |
| Output DC shift (biased negative) | $V_{\text{shift}} = -(V_m - V_B)$ | Reduced downward shift |
| Peak inverse voltage on diode (unbiased) | $V_{PIV} = 2V_m$ | Maximum reverse stress on diode |
| Output maximum | $+2V_m$ (positive clamper) | Peak of shifted waveform |
| Output minimum | $0\text{ V}$ (positive clamper) | Reference level |
| Peak-to-peak preserved | $V_{pp(\text{out})} = V_{pp(\text{in})}$ | Identity of a clamper |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Clamping circuits are the **workhorses** of analog signal processing. They are used in:

- **TV Receivers:** As **DC restorers** to recover the lost DC component of a video signal after AC coupling.
- **Radar Systems:** To baseline-shift pulse trains for proper triggering.
- **Communication Receivers:** To protect sensitive inputs from negative voltage spikes.
- **Function Generators:** To generate waveforms biased at arbitrary levels without re-designing the oscillator.
- **Test & Measurement:** In oscilloscope input stages for AC coupling with auto-baselining.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Output Voltage Expression for a Positive Clamper

### Step 1: Identify the diode conduction instant
During the **negative half-cycle** of the input $v_{\text{in}}(t) = -V_m$, the diode is forward-biased.

### Step 2: Write KVL around the loop during conduction
The capacitor charges to the peak of the input:

$$V_C = -v_{\text{in(peak)}} = -(-V_m) = +V_m$$

This voltage is **trapped** across the capacitor with the right plate positive.

### Step 3: Write KVL during the non-conducting half-cycle
With the diode OFF, the circuit reduces to a series loop of source, capacitor, and resistor. Applying KVL:

$$v_{\text{out}}(t) = v_{\text{in}}(t) + V_C$$

### Step 4: Substitute peak values to find bounds

$$v_{\text{out(max)}} = v_{\text{in(max)}} + V_C = +V_m + V_m = +2V_m$$

$$v_{\text{out(min)}} = v_{\text{in(min)}} + V_C = -V_m + V_m = 0\text{ V}$$

### Step 5: Compute the DC shift

$$V_{\text{DC shift}} = V_{\text{out(max)}} - V_{\text{in(max)}} = +2V_m - V_m = +V_m$$

This confirms the positive clamper shifts the entire waveform **upward by $V_m$**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Peak Inverse Voltage on the Diode

When the input is at its **positive peak** $+V_m$ and the capacitor holds $V_C = +V_m$, the voltage across the diode (cathode to anode) is:

$$V_{D,\text{reverse}} = v_{\text{out(max)}} - V_C = +2V_m - V_m = +V_m$$

When the input is at its **negative peak** $-V_m$, the diode conducts, so the reverse voltage is $0\text{ V}$. The maximum reverse stress is therefore the **positive peak output** minus the capacitor voltage:

$$V_{PIV} = V_{\text{out(max)}} - V_C = 2V_m - V_m = V_m$$

Wait — careful re-examination. The cathode of the diode in a positive clamper is tied to the output node. At the positive peak, $v_{\text{out}} = +2V_m$, while the capacitor holds $V_m$. The voltage across the diode (anode-cathode) is $V_{\text{in}} - v_{\text{out}} = +V_m - 2V_m = -V_m$. Thus:

$$\boxed{V_{PIV} = 2V_m}$$

This is the correct **peak inverse voltage** rating the diode must survive.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Biased Positive Clamper Output

Consider a positive clamper with a bias battery $V_B$ in series with the diode (cathode side, opposing diode conduction).

### Step 1: Diode conduction condition
The diode conducts only when the input anode voltage exceeds the cathode voltage by $V_D$ (ideal: $0\text{ V}$). With the bias, conduction begins when:

$$v_{\text{in}} < -V_B$$

### Step 2: Capacitor charge value
At the conduction instant, the input is at $v_{\text{in}} = -V_B$ (if conduction starts at this threshold). The capacitor charges to:

$$V_C = -v_{\text{in(peak-conduction)}} = -(-V_B) = +V_B$$

Wait — for a **biased** clamper, the capacitor charges to the **peak of the unclamped part** minus the bias. A more rigorous analysis:

If the diode is biased such that it only conducts when the input is more negative than $-V_B$, the capacitor charges to:

$$V_C = V_m - V_B$$

### Step 3: Output during diode-OFF half-cycle
Applying KVL:

$$v_{\text{out}}(t) = v_{\text{in}}(t) + V_C = v_{\text{in}}(t) + (V_m - V_B)$$

### Step 4: Output bounds

$$v_{\text{out(max)}} = +V_m + (V_m - V_B) = 2V_m - V_B$$

$$v_{\text{out(min)}} = -V_m + (V_m - V_B) = -V_B$$

### Step 5: Net DC shift

$$V_{\text{DC shift}} = V_{\text{out(min)}} - v_{\text{in(min)}} = -V_B - (-V_m) = V_m - V_B$$

The waveform is shifted upward by $(V_m - V_B)$, placing the **negative peak at $-V_B$** instead of $0\text{ V}$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Summary of All Clamper Variants

| Clamper Type | Diode Orientation | $V_C$ on Capacitor | $v_{\text{out}}(t)$ | Max Output | Min Output | $V_{PIV}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Positive (unbiased) | Cathode at output | $+V_m$ | $v_{\text{in}} + V_m$ | $+2V_m$ | $0\text{ V}$ | $2V_m$ |
| Negative (unbiased) | Anode at output | $-V_m$ | $v_{\text{in}} - V_m$ | $0\text{ V}$ | $-2V_m$ | $2V_m$ |
| Biased Positive | With $+V_B$ aiding | $V_m - V_B$ | $v_{\text{in}} + (V_m - V_B)$ | $2V_m - V_B$ | $-V_B$ | $2V_m$ |
| Biased Negative | With $-V_B$ aiding | $-(V_m - V_B)$ | $v_{\text{in}} - (V_m - V_B)$ | $+V_B$ | $-(2V_m - V_B)$ | $2V_m$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Positive Clamper — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[AC INPUT SOURCE vi]:::source
    nodeB[DIODE D1 Anode Cathode Down]:::diode
    nodeC[CAPACITOR C Charge Pump]:::cap
    nodeD[RESISTOR R Discharge Path]:::res
    nodeE[OUTPUT NODE vo]:::output
    nodeF[GROUND REFERENCE 0V]:::ground

    nodeA -- positive cycle flows --> nodeB
    nodeB -- charges to Vm --> nodeC
    nodeC -- holds DC level --> nodeE
    nodeD -- slow discharge --> nodeE
    nodeA -- direct path --> nodeE
    nodeE -- reference level --> nodeF

    classDef source fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef diode fill:#4169E1,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef cap fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef res fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef output fill:#FF69B4,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef ground fill:#808080,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
```

**Working Summary:** Input enters series capacitor $C$. Diode $D_1$ (cathode toward output) shunts to ground when forward-biased. Resistor $R$ in parallel at the output provides the slow discharge path.

---

## 4.2 Negative Clamper — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[AC INPUT SOURCE vi]:::source
    nodeB[DIODE D2 Anode UP Cathode DOWN]:::diode
    nodeC[CAPACITOR C Negative Charge]:::cap
    nodeD[RESISTOR R Discharge Path]:::res
    nodeE[OUTPUT NODE vo]:::output
    nodeF[GROUND REFERENCE 0V]:::ground

    nodeA -- negative cycle conduction --> nodeB
    nodeB -- charges to NEGATIVE Vm --> nodeC
    nodeC -- holds negative DC --> nodeE
    nodeD -- slow discharge --> nodeE
    nodeA -- direct path --> nodeE
    nodeE -- positive peak at 0V --> nodeF

    classDef source fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef diode fill:#DC143C,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef cap fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef res fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef output fill:#FF69B4,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef ground fill:#808080,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
```

**Working Summary:** Diode is **flipped** relative to the positive clamper. The capacitor now charges to $-V_m$, shifting the entire waveform downward.

---

## 4.3 Biased Positive Clamper — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[AC INPUT SOURCE vi]:::source
    nodeB[BIAS BATTERY VB Positive Terminal]:::bias
    nodeC[DIODE D3 Conducts only above VB]:::diode
    nodeD[CAPACITOR C Reduced Charge]:::cap
    nodeE[RESISTOR R Discharge Path]:::res
    nodeF[OUTPUT NODE vo]:::output
    nodeG[REFERENCE LINE NEGATIVE PEAK SITS AT NEGATIVE VB]:::ground

    nodeA -- input signal --> nodeB
    nodeB -- adds DC bias --> nodeC
    nodeC -- partial conduction --> nodeD
    nodeD -- holds Vm minus VB --> nodeF
    nodeE -- slow discharge --> nodeF
    nodeF -- shifted waveform --> nodeG

    classDef source fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef bias fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef diode fill:#4169E1,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef cap fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef res fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef output fill:#FF69B4,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
    classDef ground fill:#808080,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFFFFF
```

**Working Summary:** The bias battery $V_B$ in series with the diode raises the conduction threshold, so the capacitor charges to a smaller value $(V_m - V_B)$, resulting in a smaller upward shift.

---

## 4.4 Output Waveform Progression (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[INPUT Sinusoid vi center 0V amplitude Vm]:::input
    nodeB[POSITIVE CLAMPER vo peaks 2Vm trough 0V]:::posclamp
    nodeC[NEGATIVE CLAMPER vo peaks 0V trough negative 2Vm]:::negclamp
    nodeD[BIASED POSITIVE CLAMPER vo peaks 2Vm minus VB trough negative VB]:::biaspos
    nodeE[BIASED NEGATIVE CLAMPER vo peaks VB trough negative 2Vm plus VB]:::biasneg

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeA --> nodeD
    nodeA --> nodeE

    classDef input fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef posclamp fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef negclamp fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef biaspos fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef biasneg fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Architecture Map:** The input is the *parent node* feeding all four clamper variants. Each variant produces a uniquely shifted version of the same input sinusoid while preserving the peak-to-peak value $2V_m$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Q: Define a clamping circuit. Why is it called a "DC restorer"?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A clamping circuit is a waveform-shaping network using a diode, capacitor, and resistor that shifts a periodic AC waveform up or down along the voltage axis **without changing its peak-to-peak amplitude or shape**.

It is called a **"DC restorer"** because in many practical systems (such as TV video amplifiers), the signal is passed through **AC coupling** (capacitive coupling) which strips away the DC component. The clamper re-inserts the lost DC level by clamping one peak of the waveform to a known reference, thereby **restoring** the original DC bias of the signal. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Q: List the differences between a clipper and a clamper. State two applications of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Clipper | Clamper |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Function | Limits/clamps the **peak** of waveform | Shifts the **DC level** of waveform |
| Waveform shape | **Distorted** (peaks chopped) | **Preserved** (only shifted) |
| Components | Diodes + Resistor | Diode + Capacitor + Resistor |
| DC component added | No | Yes |

**Applications of Clippers:** (1) Overvoltage protection, (2) Square wave generation from sine.
**Applications of Clampers:** (1) DC restoration in TV receivers, (2) Level shifting in function generators. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a **positive clamping circuit**. Derive the expression for the output voltage and state the value of PIV across the diode. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A negative clamping circuit is fed with an input $v_{\text{in}}(t) = 10\sin(100\pi t)\text{ V}$. Sketch the input and output waveforms clearly marking all voltage levels. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Circuit Description (2 marks):**
A positive clamper consists of:
- A capacitor $C$ in series with the input source.
- A diode $D$ in **shunt** (parallel) with the load, oriented with its **cathode connected to the output node** and **anode to ground**.
- A resistor $R$ in parallel at the output providing the discharge path.

**Working Explanation (3 marks):**
1. During the **negative half-cycle** peak (input = $-V_m$), the diode is **forward-biased**. The capacitor charges rapidly to $V_C = V_m$ through the small diode forward resistance $r_d$.
2. During the **positive half-cycle**, the diode is **reverse-biased** and acts as an open circuit. The capacitor cannot discharge quickly (since $RC \gg T$), so it holds nearly $V_m$.
3. The output is the sum of the input and the capacitor voltage:
$$v_{\text{out}}(t) = v_{\text{in}}(t) + V_m$$
4. The waveform is shifted **upward by $V_m$**; the negative peak is clamped to $0\text{ V}$.

**Output Voltage and PIV (2 marks):**
- Output max: $v_{\text{out(max)}} = +V_m + V_m = +2V_m$
- Output min: $v_{\text{out(min)}} = -V_m + V_m = 0\text{ V}$
- **Peak Inverse Voltage on diode:** $V_{PIV} = 2V_m$

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:** $v_{\text{in}}(t) = 10\sin(100\pi t)\text{ V}$ → $V_m = 10\text{ V}$.

**Negative Clamper Output Calculation (3 marks):**
For a negative clamper, the diode is reversed. The capacitor charges to $V_C = -V_m = -10\text{ V}$.

The output voltage is:
$$v_{\text{out}}(t) = v_{\text{in}}(t) - V_m = 10\sin(100\pi t) - 10\text{ V}$$

**Output bounds:**
- $v_{\text{out(max)}} = 10 - 10 = 0\text{ V}$
- $v_{\text{out(min)}} = -10 - 10 = -20\text{ V}$

**Waveform Sketch (4 marks):**
The input sinusoid oscillates between $+10\text{ V}$ and $-10\text{ V}$ about the $0\text{ V}$ axis. The output is an **identical sinusoid** shifted **downward by $10\text{ V}$**, now oscillating between $0\text{ V}$ (positive peak) and $-20\text{ V}$ (negative peak). The peak-to-peak remains $20\text{ V}$ as in the input. The waveform must be drawn on a labeled time-voltage grid with the $0\text{ V}$ line clearly marked as the new positive peak.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks (Alternative to Question A)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the operation of a **biased positive clamping circuit** with a circuit diagram. Derive the expression for the DC shift. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An unbiased positive clamper is designed with $R = 100\text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.1\ \mu F$. The input is a $1\text{ kHz}$ sinusoid with $V_m = 8\text{ V}$. Verify whether the design satisfies the **clamping condition** $\tau_d \gg T$. Also determine the output peak voltages. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

**Circuit Description (2 marks):**
A biased positive clamper adds a **DC bias voltage source $V_B$** in series with the diode, oriented such that the diode is **reverse-biased by $V_B$** during normal operation. The diode conducts only when the input anode voltage drops sufficiently below the cathode to overcome the bias.

**Working Explanation (3 marks):**
1. The diode remains reverse-biased for most of the cycle, blocking current flow.
2. Conduction occurs only when $v_{\text{in}} < -V_B$ (i.e., during the deepest part of the negative half-cycle).
3. The capacitor charges to $V_C = V_m - V_B$ (reduced from the unbiased value of $V_m$).
4. The output becomes:
$$v_{\text{out}}(t) = v_{\text{in}}(t) + (V_m - V_B)$$
5. The waveform is shifted upward by $(V_m - V_B)$ instead of $V_m$. The negative peak is now clamped to $-V_B$ rather than $0\text{ V}$.

**DC Shift Expression (2 marks):**
$$\boxed{V_{\text{DC shift}} = V_m - V_B}$$

This shows that **increasing the bias reduces the shift**. If $V_B = V_m$, no shift occurs. If $V_B > V_m$, the shift reverses direction.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Given Data:**
- $R = 100\text{ k}\Omega = 10^5\ \Omega$
- $C = 0.1\ \mu\text{F} = 10^{-7}\text{ F}$
- $f = 1\text{ kHz}$, so $T = 1/f = 1\text{ ms} = 10^{-3}\text{ s}$
- $V_m = 8\text{ V}$

**Step 1: Compute the discharge time constant (2 marks)**
$$\tau_d = RC = 10^5 \times 10^{-7} = 10^{-2}\text{ s} = 10\text{ ms}$$

**Step 2: Compare with the input period (2 marks)**
$$\frac{\tau_d}{T} = \frac{10\text{ ms}}{1\text{ ms}} = 10$$

Since $\tau_d = 10T \gg T$, the **clamping condition is satisfied**. The capacitor holds its charge for at least 10 full cycles, ensuring faithful clamping with minimal tilt (less than 5% droop).

**Step 3: Compute output peak voltages (3 marks)**
For an unbiased positive clamper:
- $v_{\text{out(max)}} = +V_m + V_m = +2V_m = +16\text{ V}$
- $v_{\text{out(min)}} = -V_m + V_m = 0\text{ V}$
- DC shift = $+V_m = +8\text{ V}$
- $V_{PIV} = 2V_m = 16\text{ V}$

The waveform is shifted upward by $8\text{ V}$, with the negative peak clamped to $0\text{ V}$ and the positive peak rising to $+16\text{ V}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing "Positive" and "Negative" Clamper Orientation (–2 marks):** A "positive clamper" uses a diode with **cathode at the output**, NOT a positive-biased diode. The naming refers to the **direction of the DC shift**, not the diode's terminal voltage.
> 2. **Forgetting PIV Calculation (–1 mark):** Examiners frequently allocate a dedicated mark for the $V_{PIV}$ rating. Always state $V_{PIV} = 2V_m$ explicitly.
> 3. **Ignoring the RC Design Constraint (–2 marks):** When a numerical design check is asked, students often skip the $\tau_d \gg T$ verification, which is a KTU 2024 Scheme favorite.
> 4. **Mislabelling the Y-axis in Waveform Sketches (–1 mark):** Always mark the **$0\text{ V}$ reference line** and the **shifted peak levels** ($+2V_m$ and $0\text{ V}$ for positive clamper) explicitly on the time-voltage plot.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] A **clamper shifts the DC level** of a waveform; it does **not clip** it.
- [x] Peak-to-peak voltage is **preserved**: $V_{pp(\text{out})} = V_{pp(\text{in})}$.
- [x] Components: **Diode + Capacitor + Resistor**; design condition: $\tau_d = RC \gg T$.
- [x] **Positive Clamper** → Diode cathode at output → shift = $+V_m$ → negative peak at $0\text{ V}$.
- [x] **Negative Clamper** → Diode anode at output → shift = $-V_m$ → positive peak at $0\text{ V}$.
- [x] **Biased Positive Clamper** → shift = $V_m - V_B$ → negative peak at $-V_B$.
- [x] **Biased Negative Clamper** → shift = $-(V_m - V_B)$ → positive peak at $+V_B$.
- [x] **PIV on the diode** = $2V_m$ for all four variants.
- [x] Real-world name: **DC Restorer** (used in TV video amplifiers).
- [x] The capacitor acts as a **DC battery** in series with the AC source during the non-conducting half-cycle.
- [x] KTU 2024 favorite question pattern: **Design verification** using $RC \gg T$ for a given input frequency.
- [x] Always sketch the **input and output on the same time axis** with reference levels clearly marked.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Voltage Multipliers - Voltage Doubler and Tripler
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES & CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Voltage Multipliers - Voltage Doubler and Tripler

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **Voltage Multiplier** is a rectifier circuit that produces a DC output voltage which is an integer multiple (2×, 3×, 4×, ...) of the peak value of the applied AC input voltage. It is constructed by cascading **clamper** and **peak-detector** stages formed by diodes and capacitors, without the need for a step-up transformer.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to fill a water tank at the top of a hill using a hand-pump that only delivers water to half the height. The **pump alone cannot reach the top**. So, you use a clever trick:
- **Stage 1 (Clamper):** You pour the water into a *reservoir at the half-way point* — this "lifts" the water's reference level.
- **Stage 2 (Peak Rectifier):** You then pump water from this raised reservoir to the top of the hill, gaining another "half-height" of lift.
- **Result:** The water now reaches the summit — equivalent to **2× the pump's capacity**.

> **Voltage Multipliers work on the exact same principle**: The *clamper* stage shifts the DC reference level of the AC waveform, and the *peak rectifier* stage then extracts the new maximum voltage. Cascading these stages produces **2×, 3×, 4×, …** multiples of the input peak voltage.

### Why Voltage Multipliers Are Needed

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Motivation:**
> In many high-voltage, low-current applications (e.g., CRT televisions, X-ray tubes, photocopiers, laser power supplies, particle accelerators), a step-up transformer is undesirable because of its **size, weight, cost, and insulation requirements at high voltage**. Voltage multipliers provide a compact, lightweight, transformerless alternative for generating high DC voltages from a low-voltage AC mains.

### Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- The peak input voltage: **V_m = V_rms × √2**
- For Indian domestic mains: **V_rms = 230 V**, **f = 50 Hz** → **V_m ≈ 325.27 V**
- Output voltages are quoted relative to the **peak** value **V_m**, not the RMS value.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Building Block 1 — The Clamper (DC Level Shifter)

A clamper consists of a **diode D**, a **capacitor C**, and a load resistor (often infinite). It does **not change the peak-to-peak amplitude** of the waveform; it merely **shifts the DC level**.

- **Positive Clamper (with D cathode towards source):** During the negative half cycle, the diode conducts and the capacitor charges to **V_m**. The output DC level is shifted **upward** by **V_m**, so the waveform swings from **0 to +2V_m**.
- **Negative Clamper (with D anode towards source):** Shifts the DC level **downward** by **V_m**, so the waveform swings from **0 to −2V_m**.

### 2.2 Building Block 2 — The Peak Rectifier (Detector)

A peak rectifier (half-wave rectifier with a capacitor filter) holds its output capacitor at the **peak** of the input waveform. The diode conducts only when the instantaneous input exceeds the capacitor voltage; otherwise, the capacitor holds the charge.

- **Output:** V_out = V_peak (input)
- **Ripple:** δV = I_load / (f × C)

### 2.3 Half-Wave Voltage Doubler (Villard Cascade — 2 Stages)

A doubler is the cascade of **one positive clamper** + **one peak rectifier**:

**Stage 1 (Clamper — D₁, C₁):** Shifts the AC waveform so that it now swings from **0 to +2V_m**.
**Stage 2 (Peak Rectifier — D₂, C₂):** Extracts the new peak → **V_out = 2V_m**.

**Operation Summary:**
- **Negative half cycle:** D₁ conducts → C₁ charges to **V_m** (top plate positive).
- **Positive half cycle:** D₁ OFF, D₂ conducts → C₂ charges to **(V_s + V_C1) = V_m + V_m = 2V_m**.
- **V_out across C₂ = 2V_m** (no load) or slightly less under load due to ripple.

### 2.4 Full-Wave Voltage Doubler (Delon / Greinacher Circuit)

This circuit uses **two diodes** and **two capacitors** with the AC source connected to the **mid-point** of the capacitor stack. It uses **both** half-cycles, giving **better ripple** and **higher ripple frequency (2f)**.

- **Negative half cycle:** D₁ conducts → C₁ charges to **V_m**.
- **Positive half cycle:** D₂ conducts → C₂ charges to **V_m**.
- **Output (across C₁ + C₂ in series):** **V_out = 2V_m**.
- **Ripple frequency:** **f_r = 2 × f_supply** (significant advantage over half-wave).

### 2.5 Voltage Tripler

A voltage tripler is obtained by adding **one more diode-capacitor stage** to the doubler, so the output capacitor charges to **3V_m**.

- **C₁ charges to V_m** (after first negative half cycle).
- **C₂ charges to 2V_m** (after next positive half cycle — source + C₁ in series).
- **C₃ charges to 3V_m** (after next negative half cycle — source + C₂ in series).
- **V_out = 3V_m**

### 2.6 Cockcroft–Walton (CW) N-Stage Multiplier — The Generalised Form

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Cockcroft–Walton multiplier** is the generalised voltage multiplier that cascades **N identical stages** of two diodes + two capacitors each, producing an output of **V_out = 2N × V_m**. It was invented by John Cockcroft and Ernest Walton (Nobel Laureates, 1951) to power their particle accelerator that first split the atom.

**Key Parameters of an N-Stage CW Multiplier (under load current I):**
- **DC Output:** V_out = 2N · V_m − ΔV
- **Voltage Drop:** ΔV = (I / (2 f C)) · (2N³ / 3 + N² / 2 − N / 6)   ≈   (I N³) / (6 f C)   for large N
- **Ripple Voltage:** δV = I · N(N+1) / (2 f C)
- **Optimum number of stages** (for minimum total drop): N_opt = √(V_out · f · C / I)

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Circuit | Output Voltage V_out | Ripple Frequency | Components Required |
|---|---------|----------------------|------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Half-Wave Rectifier (1-stage) | V_m | f | 1 D, 1 C |
| 2 | Half-Wave Voltage Doubler | 2V_m | f | 2 D, 2 C |
| 3 | Full-Wave Voltage Doubler (Delon) | 2V_m | 2f | 2 D, 2 C |
| 4 | Voltage Tripler | 3V_m | f | 3 D, 3 C |
| 5 | Voltage Quadrupler | 4V_m | f | 4 D, 4 C |
| 6 | N-stage Cockcroft–Walton | 2N · V_m | 2f | 2N D, 2N C |
| 7 | Clamper (DC shift only) | V_m (AC swing = 0 to 2V_m) | — | 1 D, 1 C |
| 8 | Peak Rectifier | V_peak of input | f | 1 D, 1 C |

| # | Standard Numerical Conversions | Formula / Value |
|---|--------------------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Peak Voltage | V_m = V_rms · √2 |
| 2 | Average Voltage (Half-Wave) | V_avg = V_m / π |
| 3 | Average Voltage (Full-Wave) | V_avg = 2V_m / π |
| 4 | Ripple (Half-Wave) | δV = I / (fC) |
| 5 | Ripple (Full-Wave / CW) | δV = I / (2fC) |
| 6 | CW Voltage Drop (Large N) | ΔV ≈ I N³ / (6 f C) |
| 7 | CW Ripple | δV = I N(N+1) / (2 f C) |
| 8 | Doubler Output (mains 230 V) | 2 × 230√2 ≈ 650.5 V DC |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> Voltage multipliers are the **backbone of high-voltage DC power supplies** in television CRTs (25 kV), X-ray machines (50–150 kV), laser systems, photocopiers, air ionisers, electrostatic precipitators, and particle accelerators. The **Cockcroft–Walton** design, in particular, is widely used in **neutron generators** and **scanning electron microscopes**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Peak Input Voltage from RMS

Let the AC mains supply be:
$$V_s(t) = V_m \sin(\omega t)$$

The RMS value is defined as:
$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} V_s^2(t)\, dt}$$

Substituting V_s(t) = V_m sin(ωt) and noting that the mean square of a sinusoid over a full period is **1/2**:

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} V_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t)\, dt} = \sqrt{\frac{V_m^2}{T}\cdot \frac{T}{2}} = \frac{V_m}{\sqrt{2}}$$

Rearranging to find the peak value:
$$V_m = V_{rms}\cdot \sqrt{2}$$

**Numerical Example (Indian Mains):** For V_rms = 230 V:
$$V_m = 230 \times \sqrt{2} = 230 \times 1.4142 \approx 325.27 \text{ V}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Half-Wave Voltage Doubler Output

Let the AC source be **V_s(t) = V_m sin(ωt)**. Consider the two-half-cycle sequence of events.

**Step 1 — First Negative Half Cycle (V_s reaches −V_m):**
With C₁ initially uncharged, the diode **D₁** is forward biased (its anode is at V_s and its cathode is at the C₁ node, which is at 0 V). Current flows and C₁ charges.
$$V_{C1} = V_m \quad \text{(top plate positive, bottom plate grounded)}$$

**Step 2 — First Positive Half Cycle (V_s reaches +V_m):**
Now D₁ is reverse biased (anode +V_m, cathode +V_m from C₁, so V_D1 = 0, non-conducting). D₂ sees a potential at its anode equal to the source voltage, but its cathode is connected to C₂ (initially 0 V). When V_s exceeds 0 V, D₂ becomes forward biased.
$$V_{\text{anode of } D_2} = V_s + V_{C1} = V_m + V_m = 2V_m$$

(The voltage at the anode of D₂ is the sum of the source voltage and the C₁ voltage, because C₁ is in series with the source as seen from the D₂ anode node.)

**Step 3 — C₂ Charges to Peak:**
D₂ conducts and charges C₂ to this peak value. After a few cycles, in steady state:
$$V_{C2} = V_{C1} + V_m = V_m + V_m = 2V_m$$

Therefore, the no-load DC output is:
$$\boxed{V_{out} = 2 V_m = 2\sqrt{2}\, V_{rms}}$$

**Step 4 — Under Load (with current I_L):**
C₂ discharges through the load between charging pulses. The ripple voltage is:
$$\delta V = \frac{I_L}{f \cdot C_2}$$

The DC output under load becomes:
$$V_{out} \approx 2V_m - \frac{I_L}{2 f C_2}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Voltage Tripler Output

The tripler is a doubler extended by one more diode-capacitor stage (D₃, C₃).

**Step 1 — Initial Negative Half Cycle:** D₁ conducts; C₁ charges.
$$V_{C1} = V_m$$

**Step 2 — Next Positive Half Cycle:** D₂ conducts; the voltage at D₂'s anode is V_s + V_C1. The peak of this is **2V_m**. C₂ charges to:
$$V_{C2} = V_m + V_{C1} = V_m + V_m = 2V_m$$

**Step 3 — Next Negative Half Cycle:** D₃ conducts; the voltage at D₃'s anode is V_s + V_C2. At the negative peak, V_s = −V_m, so:
$$V_{\text{anode of } D_3} = -V_m + V_{C2} = -V_m + 2V_m = +V_m$$

Wait — D₃ will conduct when its anode is more positive than its cathode (which is at V_{C3}, initially 0). So D₃ conducts and charges C₃ to:
$$V_{C3} = V_m + V_{C2} = V_m + 2V_m = 3V_m$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that each successive stage adds **one V_m** to the output because the *previously charged capacitor acts as a "battery"* in series with the source, raising the peak voltage seen by the next diode.

Therefore, the no-load DC output of the tripler is:
$$\boxed{V_{out} = 3 V_m = 3\sqrt{2}\, V_{rms}}$$

### 3.4 Derivation of Cockcroft–Walton N-Stage Output

In an N-stage CW multiplier, the AC source drives **N clamper columns** and **N rectifier columns**, alternating. Each stage contributes **2V_m** to the DC stack. With N stages:

**No-Load Output:**
$$V_{out}^{NL} = 2N \cdot V_m$$

**Under Load (Rigorous Derivation):**
The voltage drop across the multiplier has two components — one from the **column capacitors** and one from the **row capacitors**. Summing the drops from N stages:
$$\Delta V = \frac{I}{f} \left[ \frac{2N^3}{3} \cdot \frac{1}{2C} + \frac{N^2}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2C} - \frac{N}{6} \cdot \frac{1}{2C} \right] = \frac{I}{2 f C}\left(\frac{2N^3}{3} + \frac{N^2}{2} - \frac{N}{6}\right)$$

Simplifying (and dropping the small −N/6 term for large N):
$$\Delta V \approx \frac{I N^3}{6 f C}$$

**Ripple Voltage:**
$$\delta V = \frac{I N(N+1)}{2 f C}$$

**Loaded DC Output:**
$$\boxed{V_{out} = 2N V_m - \frac{I N^3}{6 f C}}$$

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Half-Wave Doubler | Full-Wave Doubler | Voltage Tripler | CW (N-Stage) |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|-----------------|--------------|
| No. of Diodes | 2 | 2 | 3 | 2N |
| No. of Capacitors | 2 | 2 | 3 | 2N |
| Output V_out (No-Load) | 2V_m | 2V_m | 3V_m | 2N V_m |
| Ripple Frequency | f | 2f | f | 2f |
| Ripple Magnitude | Higher | Lower (~ half) | Higher | I·N(N+1)/(2fC) |
| Utilises Both Half Cycles | No | Yes | No | Yes (rectifier columns) |
| Voltage Regulation | Poor | Better | Poor | Worsens with N³ |
| Typical Application | Low-power HV | TV HV supply | HV bias | Particle accelerators |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Half-Wave Voltage Doubler — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **functional flow** of a half-wave voltage doubler, mapping how the AC source, the two diode-capacitor stages, and the load interact to produce the doubled DC output.

```mermaid
graph LR
    ACSRC["AC SOURCE Vs = Vm sin wt"] --> D1A["DIODE D1 ANODE"]
    D1A --> D1C["DIODE D1 CATHODE"]
    D1C --> NODE1["NODE A CLAMPER OUTPUT"]
    NODE1 --> C1PLATE["C1 TOP PLATE"]
    C1PLATE -.-> C1GND["C1 BOTTOM PLATE GROUND"]
    NODE1 --> D2A["DIODE D2 ANODE"]
    D2A --> D2C["DIODE D2 CATHODE"]
    D2C --> NODE2["NODE B PEAK RECTIFIER OUTPUT"]
    NODE2 --> C2PLATE["C2 TOP PLATE"]
    C2PLATE -.-> C2GND["C2 BOTTOM PLATE GROUND"]
    NODE2 --> LOADP["LOAD POSITIVE TERMINAL"]
    C2GND --> LOADN["LOAD NEGATIVE TERMINAL GROUND"]
    LOADP --> VOUTDC["DC OUTPUT Vout approx 2 Vm"]
    VOUTDC --> VLABEL["INDICATES 650 V DC FOR 230 V RMS MAINS"]
```

### 4.2 Voltage Tripler — Stage-by-Stage Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    AC2["AC SOURCE Vs"] --> TRI1["STAGE 1 D1 AND C1 CLAMPER"]
    TRI1 --> OUT1["NODE A Vc1 approx Vm"]
    OUT1 --> TRI2["STAGE 2 D2 AND C2 PEAK DOUBLER"]
    TRI2 --> OUT2["NODE B Vc2 approx 2Vm"]
    OUT2 --> TRI3["STAGE 3 D3 AND C3 PEAK TRIPLER"]
    TRI3 --> OUT3["NODE C Vc3 approx 3Vm"]
    OUT3 --> TRILOAD["LOAD"]
    TRILOAD --> TRIVOUT["DC OUTPUT Vout approx 3 Vm"]
    TRIVOUT --> TRILABEL["INDICATES 975 V DC FOR 230 V RMS MAINS"]
```

### 4.3 Cockcroft–Walton N-Stage Multiplier — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    CWSRC["AC SOURCE Vs at 2fs"] --> CWN1["STAGE 1 COLUMN CAPACITORS C1A AND C1B"]
    CWN1 --> CWN2["STAGE 2 COLUMN CAPACITORS C2A AND C2B"]
    CWN2 --> CWDOTS["STAGE 3 TO STAGE N-1 ELLIPSIS"]
    CWDOTS --> CWNN["STAGE N COLUMN CAPACITORS CNA AND CNB"]
    CWNN --> CWSTACK["DC STACK OF 2N CAPACITORS IN SERIES"]
    CWSTACK --> CWFILTER["FILTER CAPACITOR CF"]
    CWFILTER --> CWLOAD["HIGH VOLTAGE LOAD"]
    CWLOAD --> CWOUT["DC OUTPUT Vout equals 2N x Vm minus Delta V"]
    CWOUT --> CWLBL["USED IN X RAY MACHINES CRT AND ACCELERATORS"]
```

### 4.4 Operation Timing Diagram — Half-Wave Voltage Doubler

```mermaid
graph LR
    T1["TIME T1 NEGATIVE PEAK OF Vs"] --> OP1["D1 ON D2 OFF C1 CHARGES TO Vm"]
    T2["TIME T2 POSITIVE PEAK OF Vs"] --> OP2["D1 OFF D2 ON C2 CHARGES TO 2Vm"]
    T3["TIME T3 NEXT NEGATIVE PEAK"] --> OP3["D1 ON D2 OFF C1 RECHARGES C2 HOLDS 2Vm"]
    T4["TIME T4 NEXT POSITIVE PEAK"] --> OP4["D1 OFF D2 ON C2 RECHARGES TO 2Vm"]
    T1 --> WAVEFORM["Vs SINUSOIDAL WAVEFORM"]
    T2 --> WAVEFORM
    T3 --> WAVEFORM
    T4 --> WAVEFORM
    OP1 --> STEADY["STEADY STATE Vc2 approx 2Vm WITH SMALL RIPPLE"]
    OP2 --> STEADY
    OP3 --> STEADY
    OP4 --> STEADY
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents a *functional element*, not a literal circuit. The arrows indicate the **direction of signal/charge flow** through the multiplier stages. In a real schematic, the diodes would be drawn as triangle-bar symbols and the capacitors as parallel lines, but the **functional sequence** is the same.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper 1]**
*Define a voltage multiplier. List any two applications of voltage multipliers.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** A **voltage multiplier** is a circuit consisting of diodes and capacitors that produces a DC output voltage equal to an integer multiple (2×, 3×, 4×, etc.) of the peak value of the AC input voltage, **without using a step-up transformer**. It works on the principle of cascading **clamper** and **peak-rectifier** stages.

**Applications (1 mark — any two):**
1. **Cathode Ray Tube (CRT) televisions and computer monitors** — to generate the 20–25 kV anode voltage.
2. **X-ray machines** — to produce the 50–150 kV required for the X-ray tube.
3. **Photocopiers and laser printers** — to charge the photoconductor drum.
4. **Particle accelerators** (Cockcroft–Walton generators) — to accelerate charged particles.
5. **Air ionisers, electrostatic precipitators, neutron generators**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Paper 2]**
*Compare the half-wave and full-wave voltage doublers in terms of (a) number of components, (b) output voltage, (c) ripple frequency.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Half-Wave Doubler | Full-Wave Doubler (Delon) |
|---|---|---|
| (a) Diodes & Capacitors | 2 D, 2 C | 2 D, 2 C |
| (b) Output V_out | 2 V_m | 2 V_m |
| (c) Ripple Frequency | f (supply frequency) | 2f (double the supply) |

(1 mark for each correct comparison row.)

> **Conclusion:** The full-wave doubler provides the **same output voltage** with **double the ripple frequency**, which means the **ripple amplitude is halved** for the same load and capacitor value — making it superior for high-current applications.

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 3]**
**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram and waveforms, explain the operation of a **half-wave voltage doubler**. Derive the expression for its DC output voltage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A half-wave voltage doubler is supplied from 230 V rms, 50 Hz mains. Calculate: (i) the DC output voltage, (ii) the peak-inverse voltage (PIV) of each diode, and (iii) the value of the filter capacitor required to keep the ripple below 5 V for a load current of 10 mA. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q3A:**

**(a) Operation of Half-Wave Voltage Doubler (7 marks):**

**Circuit Description (2 marks):**
The half-wave voltage doubler consists of two diodes **D₁, D₂** and two capacitors **C₁, C₂**, with the AC source V_s connected as shown. C₁ acts as the **clamping capacitor** and C₂ acts as the **filter/output capacitor**. The output is taken across C₂.

**Working — Cycle-by-Cycle (4 marks):**

*During the first negative half cycle (V_s = −V_m):*
- D₁ is forward biased; D₂ is reverse biased.
- C₁ charges through D₁ to the peak source voltage:
$$V_{C1} = V_m \quad \text{(top plate of C}_1\text{ is positive)}$$

*During the first positive half cycle (V_s = +V_m):*
- D₁ is reverse biased (because C₁ holds V_m on its cathode side and the source rises to V_m, then above).
- D₂ becomes forward biased when the voltage at its anode exceeds its cathode.
- The voltage at D₂'s anode = V_s + V_{C1} = V_m + V_m = 2V_m (because C₁ is in series with the source).
- C₂ charges to this peak:
$$V_{C2} = 2 V_m$$

*Steady State:*
C₂ holds **2V_m** between successive charging pulses, discharging slightly into the load. The output has a small ripple of frequency **f**.

**Output Derivation (1 mark):**
$$\boxed{V_{out} = 2 V_m = 2 \sqrt{2}\, V_{rms} \approx 2.828\, V_{rms}}$$

**Waveform Sketches (must be drawn):**
- V_s : sinusoid (0 to +V_m and 0 to −V_m)
- V_C1 : charge to +V_m, then remain at +V_m
- V at node A (= V_s + V_C1) : sinusoid shifted up, swinging from 0 to +2V_m
- V_C2 : rises to +2V_m with small ripple

---

**(b) Numerical Calculations (7 marks):**

*Given:* V_rms = 230 V, f = 50 Hz, I_L = 10 mA = 10 × 10⁻³ A, allowed ripple δV = 5 V.

**(i) DC Output Voltage (2 marks):**
$$V_m = V_{rms} \cdot \sqrt{2} = 230 \times 1.4142 \approx 325.27 \text{ V}$$
$$V_{out} = 2 V_m = 2 \times 325.27 \approx 650.54 \text{ V DC}$$

**(ii) Peak Inverse Voltage of Each Diode (2 marks):**
- **PIV of D₁:** When D₂ is conducting at the positive peak, D₁ sees V_{C2} on its cathode side and V_s = +V_m on its anode. So:
$$PIV_{D_1} = V_{C2} - V_s = 2V_m - V_m = V_m \approx 325.27 \text{ V}$$
- **PIV of D₂:** During the negative half cycle, D₂ is reverse biased. Its anode is at V_s = −V_m and cathode is at V_{C2} = +2V_m:
$$PIV_{D_2} = V_{C2} - V_s = 2V_m - (-V_m) = 3V_m \approx 975.8 \text{ V}$$

**(iii) Filter Capacitor Value (3 marks):**
The ripple for a half-wave doubler is:
$$\delta V = \frac{I_L}{f \cdot C}$$
Rearranging:
$$C = \frac{I_L}{f \cdot \delta V} = \frac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{50 \times 5} = \frac{10^{-2}}{250} = 4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ F}$$
$$\boxed{C = 40\, \mu\text{F}}$$

---
