# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]`
**Differentiate between series and shunt voltage regulators with respect to (a) transistor placement, (b) efficiency, and (c) short-circuit behaviour.**

**Model Answer:**

| Criterion | Series Regulator | Shunt Regulator |
|---|---|---|
| Transistor placement | **In series** with the load (collector–emitter path) | **In parallel (shunt)** with the load (collector to $V_{OUT}$, emitter to GND) |
| Efficiency | **High**, because transistor drops only the *excess* voltage | **Low**, because the series $R_S$ always dissipates $(V_{IN}-V_{OUT})\cdot I_S$ |
| Short-circuit behaviour | Pass transistor may be **damaged** due to high fault current | **Naturally protected** — the shunt transistor simply turns off |

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]`
**What is the role of the Zener diode in a transistorized voltage regulator?**

**Model Answer:**
The Zener diode is operated in **reverse breakdown** to provide a **stable reference voltage** $V_Z$ that is independent of supply and load variations. This $V_Z$ is fed to the error amplifier, which compares it with a sample of $V_{OUT}$ to generate the **error signal** that drives the pass transistor. Hence, the Zener is the **voltage reference anchor** of the entire regulator — the accuracy of $V_{OUT}$ is directly tied to the temperature stability of $V_Z$.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Long Answer (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** Draw the circuit of a **transistorized series voltage regulator** with a Zener reference and an error amplifier. Explain its working with the help of the **negative feedback** action. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a series regulator with $V_Z = 5.1$ V, $R_1 = 1.2$ k$\Omega$, $R_2 = 1.0$ k$\Omega$, $V_{IN} = 18$ V, and $R_L = 50$ $\Omega$, calculate:
- (i) $V_{OUT}$ and $I_L$
- (ii) Voltage drop across the pass transistor $V_{CE1}$
- (iii) Power dissipated in the pass transistor
- (iv) Maximum allowed $V_{IN}$ if the pass transistor's $V_{CE(\max)} = 30$ V. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Circuit and Working

The circuit consists of:
- **Pass transistor** $Q_1$ (NPN, power) with collector at $V_{IN}$, emitter at $V_{OUT}$.
- **Zener reference** $Q_Z$ biased through $R_Z$ from $V_{IN}$, providing $V_Z$ at the emitter of $Q_2$.
- **Error amplifier** $Q_2$ (small-signal NPN) with base connected to the **voltage divider** $R_1, R_2$ across $V_{OUT}$, and emitter tied to $V_Z$.
- The collector of $Q_2$ drives the base of $Q_1$.

**Working (negative feedback):**
1. Suppose $V_{OUT}$ **rises** (due to input surge or load reduction).
2. The sample $V_{B2} = V_{OUT} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1+R_2}$ **rises**.
3. Since $V_{E2} = V_Z$ is constant, $V_{BE2}$ increases, $Q_2$ conducts more.
4. The collector voltage of $Q_2$ **falls**, pulling the base of $Q_1$ down.
5. $Q_1$ conducts **less**, raising $V_{CE1}$ and pulling $V_{OUT}$ **back down**.
6. *Opposite action* occurs if $V_{OUT}$ tries to fall — completing the negative feedback loop.

This **closed-loop action** keeps $V_{OUT}$ locked at:

$$
V_{OUT} = V_Z \left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)
$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical

**(i) $V_{OUT}$ and $I_L$:**

$$
V_{OUT} = 5.1 \cdot \left(1 + \frac{1.2}{1.0}\right) = 5.1 \cdot 2.2 = 11.22 \text{ V}
$$

$$
I_L = \frac{V_{OUT}}{R_L} = \frac{11.22}{50} = 0.2244 \text{ A} \approx 224.4 \text{ mA}
$$

**(ii) $V_{CE1}$:**

$$
V_{CE1} = V_{IN} - V_{OUT} = 18 - 11.22 = 6.78 \text{ V}
$$

**(iii) $P_{Q1}$:**

$$
P_{Q1} = V_{CE1} \cdot I_L = 6.78 \cdot 0.2244 \approx 1.52 \text{ W}
$$

**(iv) Maximum $V_{IN}$ (with $V_{CE(\max)} = 30$ V):**

$$
V_{IN(\max)} = V_{CE(\max)} + V_{OUT} = 30 + 11.22 = 41.22 \text{ V}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students forget the **power rating of the pass transistor**. Even if $V_{CE(\max)}$ is satisfied, the *power* $P_{Q1} = (V_{IN}-V_{OUT}) \cdot I_L$ must be within the transistor's $P_D$ rating — otherwise it will **burn out** well before the voltage limit is reached. A **2-mark** deduction is typical for missing this check.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 2]`

**(a)** Draw and explain the operation of a **transistorized shunt voltage regulator** with a Zener reference. Discuss its merits and demerits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shunt regulator has $V_{IN} = 20$ V (with $\pm 10\%$ variation), $R_S = 20$ $\Omega$, and the regulated output is $V_{OUT} = 12$ V. The load current varies from 100 mA to 500 mA. Calculate:
- (i) The current through the series resistor $I_S$ at nominal and extreme input voltages.
- (ii) The range of shunt transistor current $I_{C1}$.
- (iii) The maximum power dissipated in the shunt transistor.
- (iv) Comment on the **efficiency** at full load and no-load. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Circuit and Working

The circuit has:
- **Series resistor** $R_S$ between $V_{IN}$ and the $V_{OUT}$ node.
- **Shunt transistor** $Q_1$ with collector tied to $V_{OUT}$ and emitter to ground.
- **Zener reference** $D_Z$ at the output node (or after a divider) feeding the error amplifier $Q_2$.
- **Error amplifier** $Q_2$ samples $V_{OUT}$ and drives the base of $Q_1$.

**Working:**
1. If $V_{OUT}$ rises (input surge or load drop), the sample at $Q_2$'s base rises, $Q_2$ conducts more, raising $Q_1$'s base drive.
2. $I_{C1}$ increases → more current diverted to ground → more drop across $R_S$ → $V_{OUT}$ falls back to nominal.
3. KCL: $I_S = I_{C1} + I_L$ — the regulator automatically adjusts $I_{C1}$ to keep $V_{OUT}$ constant.

**Merits:** Simple, inherently short-circuit proof, good for **reference applications**.
**Demerits:** Low efficiency (especially at low $I_L$), $R_S$ dissipates continuous power.

#### Part (b) — Numerical

**(i) $I_S$ at nominal and extreme inputs:**

$$
I_S = \frac{V_{IN} - V_{OUT}}{R_S}
$$

- Nominal: $I_S = \dfrac{20 - 12}{20} = 0.4$ A
- High ($V_{IN}=22$ V): $I_S = \dfrac{22 - 12}{20} = 0.5$ A
- Low ($V_{IN}=18$ V): $I_S = \dfrac{18 - 12}{20} = 0.3$ A

**(ii) Range of $I_{C1}$ (at nominal $V_{IN}$):**

$$
I_{C1} = I_S - I_L
$$

- At $I_L = 0.1$ A: $I_{C1} = 0.4 - 0.1 = 0.3$ A
- At $I_L = 0.5$ A: $I_{C1} = 0.4 - 0.5 = -0.1$ A ← **invalid!**

> [!WARNING]
> The negative $I_{C1}$ at high load means the **regulator is dropping out of regulation** — KCL demands $I_{C1} \geq 0$. The maximum sustainable $I_L$ at nominal $V_{IN}$ is exactly $0.4$ A. This is a critical KTU design constraint worth **2 marks**.

**(iii) Maximum $P_{Q1}$:**

For safe operation, design $R_S$ for the *worst case* $I_S = 0.5$ A (at $V_{IN}=22$ V):

- Worst $I_{C1} = 0.5 - 0.1 = 0.4$ A (at minimum load 100 mA)

$$
P_{Q1(\max)} = V_{OUT} \cdot I_{C1(\max)} = 12 \cdot 0.4 = 4.8 \text{ W}
$$

**(iv) Efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \frac{P_{OUT}}{P_{IN}} = \frac{V_{OUT} \cdot I_L}{V_{IN} \cdot I_S}
$$

- At $I_L = 0.5$ A (best case): $\eta = \dfrac{12 \cdot 0.5}{20 \cdot 0.4} = \dfrac{6}{8} = 75\%$
- At $I_L = 0.1$ A (worst case): $\eta = \dfrac{12 \cdot 0.1}{20 \cdot 0.4} = \dfrac{1.2}{8} = 15\%$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comment:** The shunt regulator's efficiency drops **catastrophically** at low load currents. This is the chief reason modern systems prefer **series regulators** (or LDO/IC regulators) for portable and battery-powered applications.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **Series regulator:** Pass transistor in **series** with load. Output given by $V_{OUT} = V_Z (1 + R_1/R_2)$. Higher efficiency, no short-circuit protection.
- **Shunt regulator:** Pass transistor in **parallel** with load. Series $R_S$ drops excess voltage. Inherently short-circuit safe, but wastes power.
- **Power dissipation peaks at full-load in series** and at **no-load in shunt** — a key KTU conceptual discriminator.
- **Zener diode** always operates in **reverse breakdown** to provide a temperature-stable $V_{REF}$.
- **Line regulation** $\propto 1/A$ (open-loop gain of error amplifier) — higher loop gain → better rejection.
- **Load regulation** $\propto R_{OUT}/(1 + A\beta)$ — closed-loop output impedance of a regulated supply.
- **Efficiency** of series regulator = $V_{OUT}/V_{IN}$ ideally; for shunt, the **fixed** $R_S$ loss is the killer.
- **Pass transistor must be checked for BOTH $V_{CE(\max)}$ AND $P_D$** — a common exam trap.
- **Negative feedback** is the *fundamental* principle — understand the **summing junction** behaviour intuitively.
- The **sample-and-hold** action of the divider $(R_1, R_2)$ sets the *gain* of the feedback loop: $\beta = R_2/(R_1+R_2)$.
- **Rule of thumb:** $V_{IN}$ should be at least $V_{OUT} + 2$ V for series regulators to stay in linear mode.
- **Zener bias current** $I_Z$ must lie in the manufacturer-specified range $[I_{Z(\min)}, I_{Z(\max)}]$ for stable reference.

> **End of Module 2 Topic — Shunt and Series Voltage Regulator Using Transistors**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 3 Pin Regulators - 78XX and 79XX
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES & CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** 3 Pin Regulators - 78XX and 79XX

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **three-terminal (3-pin) voltage regulator** is a self-contained, monolithic integrated circuit (IC) packaged in a standard 3-lead configuration (typically TO-220, TO-92, or TO-3) that provides a **fixed, regulated DC output voltage** from an unregulated or poorly regulated DC input. The **78XX series** delivers **positive regulated voltages**, while the **79XX series** delivers **negative regulated voltages**. The "XX" suffix denotes the magnitude of the output voltage in volts (e.g., **7805 → +5V**, **7912 → −12V**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The 78XX and 79XX series belong to the family of **linear series-pass voltage regulators**. The "7" indicates the regulator family, the second digit indicates the series type, and the last two digits specify the fixed output voltage.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water tap connected to a high-pressure, fluctuating municipal pipeline**. You want a steady trickle of water at your sink regardless of how wildly the pressure upstream surges. The 3-pin regulator is exactly that — an **"electronic tap"** placed between a noisy, variable DC source (like a rectifier output) and your sensitive load (microcontroller, op-amp, sensor). 

- The **Input pin** = The fluctuating high-pressure inlet.
- The **Ground pin** = The common reference (like the drain).
- The **Output pin** = The perfectly controlled trickle reaching your device.

The regulator continuously "senses" the output voltage, compares it to a built-in internal reference (like a Zener diode), and **shunts away the excess energy as heat** to maintain rock-solid DC at the output.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard metrics** associated with 3-pin regulators (always highlighted in **bold** for KTU exams):
> - **Nominal Output Voltage (Vₒᵤₜ):** Fixed by the part number (e.g., **+5V** for 7805, **−12V** for 7912).
> - **Input-Output Differential (Dropout Voltage):** Typically **2V to 2.5V** minimum for 78XX/79XX series.
> - **Maximum Input Voltage:** Usually **35V** for 78XX (positive) and **−35V** for 79XX (negative).
> - **Output Current:** Up to **1A** (TO-220 package) or **100mA** (TO-92 package).
> - **Line Regulation:** Typically **0.01% / V** change in Vₒᵤₜ per volt change in Vᵢₙ.
> - **Load Regulation:** Typically **0.1% to 0.5%** from no-load to full-load.
> - **Ripple Rejection (PSRR):** Around **60 dB to 70 dB** at 120 Hz.

### 1.4 The Three Pins Defined

| Pin | 78XX Label | 79XX Label | Function |
|:----|:-----------|:-----------|:---------|
| **Pin 1** | INPUT | GROUND | Unregulated DC enters here (78XX) or common return (79XX) |
| **Pin 2** | GROUND | INPUT | Common return (78XX) or unregulated DC enters here (79XX) |
| **Pin 3** | OUTPUT | OUTPUT | Regulated, fixed DC voltage exits here |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Pitfall:** Students often mix up the pinouts of 78XX and 79XX. The pin positions are **mirror images**! For 79XX, the INPUT is on the **right** (when viewed from the front with the tab down) and GROUND is on the **left**, which is the reverse of the 78XX layout. Pin 3 (OUTPUT) remains in the same position for both families.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Internal Architecture (How It Works)

The 78XX and 79XX ICs are not simple Zener shunts. Internally, they contain a sophisticated closed-loop analog system comprising:

1. **A Precision Bandgap Voltage Reference** — Generates a temperature-stable internal reference voltage (≈1.25V) independent of temperature and supply variations.
2. **An Error Amplifier (Differential Op-Amp)** — Compares a sample of the output voltage (via internal feedback divider) with the bandgap reference.
3. **A Series-Pass Transistor (Darlington Pair)** — The main current-handling element. It behaves as a **variable resistance** that drops the excess input voltage as heat.
4. **Feedback Network** — Internal resistor divider that scales Vₒᵤₜ down to match the reference.
5. **Protection Circuitry** — Three critical safety blocks:
   - **Thermal Shutdown** (kicks in at ≈**150°C junction temperature**)
   - **Current Limiting (Short-Circuit Protection)** (limits current to ≈**1.5A to 2.2A**)
   - **Safe Operating Area (SOA) Protection** for the pass transistor.

### 2.2 Operational Logic Flow (Why and How)

The regulator functions as a **negative feedback control system**:

- **Step 1 — Sampling:** A fraction of the output voltage is fed back to the error amplifier's inverting input.
- **Step 2 — Comparison:** This sampled voltage is compared against the internal bandgap reference (connected to the non-inverting input).
- **Step 3 — Error Generation:** Any difference between the two (the "error voltage") is amplified.
- **Step 4 — Actuation:** The amplified error signal drives the series-pass transistor, **increasing or decreasing its conduction** to compensate.
- **Step 5 — Regulation Result:** If Vₒᵤₜ tends to **rise**, the pass transistor conducts **less**, dropping more voltage across itself. If Vₒᵤₜ tends to **fall**, it conducts **more**, allowing more voltage to reach the output.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in 80% of numerical problems on regulators.**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Expression | Units | Physical Meaning |
|:--|:--------------------|:------------------------|:------|:-----------------|
| 1 | **Output Voltage (78XX)** | $V_{out} = +XX$ | Volts (V) | Fixed by part number |
| 2 | **Output Voltage (79XX)** | $V_{out} = -XX$ | Volts (V) | Fixed by part number |
| 3 | **Dropout Voltage** | $V_{drop} = V_{in(min)} - V_{out}$ | Volts (V) | Minimum headroom needed |
| 4 | **Power Dissipated** | $P_D = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \times I_L$ | Watts (W) | Heat to be sunk |
| 5 | **Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Percent (%) | Energy retained |
| 6 | **Line Regulation** | $LR = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta V_{in}} \times 100\%$ | % / V | Rejection of input variation |
| 7 | **Load Regulation** | $LDR = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\%$ | % | Rejection of load variation |
| 8 | **Heat Sink Thermal Resistance** | $R_{th(sa)} = \dfrac{T_{J(max)} - T_A}{P_D} - R_{th(jc)} - R_{th(cs)}$ | °C / W | Required heatsink rating |
| 9 | **Input Capacitor Constraint** | $V_{in} \geq V_{out} + V_{drop}$ | Volts (V) | Stable operation condition |
| 10 | **Maximum Load Current** | $I_{L(max)} = \dfrac{P_{D(max)}}{V_{in} - V_{out}}$ | Amperes (A) | Limited by thermal limit |

> **Units Reminder:** $\vert \Delta V \vert$ denotes absolute change. Use `\vert` in LaTeX, **never** the pipe symbol `|` inside markdown tables.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Consumer Electronics:** Powering microcontrollers (Arduino, ESP32), op-amps, and digital logic in every appliance.
- **Automotive Electronics:** Stabilizing battery-fed electronics (despite load-dump transients up to 40V).
- **Industrial Control Systems:** Sensor excitation supplies, PLC I/O modules.
- **Telecommunications:** Auxiliary low-voltage rails in base stations.
- **Medical Devices:** Patient-monitoring equipment where ripple-free DC is critical for ADC accuracy.
- **Test & Measurement:** Bench power supplies — the 78XX/79XX chips form the regulation stage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Tip for KTU Labs:** Always mount a **0.33 µF ceramic capacitor** close to the input pin and a **0.1 µF ceramic capacitor** close to the output pin. The input capacitor cancels inductive effects of long input lines; the output capacitor improves transient response.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Worked Examples

### 3.1 Derivation: Power Dissipation in a Linear Regulator

**Starting Principle:** In a series-pass regulator, the pass element acts as a controlled variable resistor. The voltage difference between input and output drops across the pass transistor, while the load current flows through it.

**Step 1 — Write the voltage dropped across the regulator IC:**

$$V_{drop} = V_{in} - V_{out}$$

**Step 2 — Recognize that load current $I_L$ flows from input to output, passing through the IC:**

$$I_{in} \approx I_{out} = I_L \quad \text{(quiescent current } I_Q \text{ is negligible for high } I_L \text{)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the electrical power formula $P = V \times I$ to the IC itself:**

$$P_D = V_{drop} \times I_L$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the expression for $V_{drop}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_D &= (V_{in} - V_{out}) \times I_L \\
P_D &= V_{in} I_L - V_{out} I_L \\
P_D &= V_{in} I_L - P_{out}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Conversion Logic:** Equation 3.1.4 shows that the power dissipated by the IC equals the **difference between input power supplied and useful output power delivered** to the load. The lost energy is converted to heat.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Required Heat Sink Thermal Resistance

**Starting Principle:** Heat must flow from the silicon junction ($\rightarrow$ case $\rightarrow$ heatsink $\rightarrow$ ambient) without exceeding the maximum junction temperature $T_{J(max)}$.

**Step 1 — Total thermal resistance from junction to ambient:**

$$R_{th(ja)} = R_{th(jc)} + R_{th(cs)} + R_{th(sa)}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum allowed thermal resistance based on temperature budget:**

$$R_{th(ja, max)} = \dfrac{T_{J(max)} - T_A}{P_D}$$

**Step 3 — Equate and solve for $R_{th(sa)}$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{th(sa)} &= \dfrac{T_{J(max)} - T_A}{P_D} - R_{th(jc)} - R_{th(cs)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** $T_{J(max)} = 150°C$ (typical), $T_A$ = ambient, $R_{th(jc)}$ from datasheet (TO-220 ≈ 3°C/W), $R_{th(cs)}$ with thermal compound ≈ 0.5°C/W.

---

### 3.3 Comprehensive Worked Example (KTU Numerical Style)

> **Problem:** A 7805 regulator supplies a load drawing **$I_L = 500\text{ mA}$** from an unregulated input of **$V_{in} = 12\text{ V DC}$** (after rectification and smoothing). The ambient temperature is **$T_A = 40°C$**, and $T_{J(max)} = 150°C$ with $R_{th(jc)} = 3°C/W$ and $R_{th(cs)} = 0.5°C/W$. Calculate:
> (a) Power dissipated by the IC
> (b) Efficiency of regulation
> (c) Required heat sink thermal resistance

**Given:**
- $V_{in} = 12$ V, $V_{out} = +5$ V (since 7805)
- $I_L = 500$ mA $= 0.5$ A
- $T_A = 40°C$, $T_{J(max)} = 150°C$
- $R_{th(jc)} = 3°C/W$, $R_{th(cs)} = 0.5°C/W$

#### (a) Power Dissipated

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{drop} &= V_{in} - V_{out} = 12 - 5 = 7 \text{ V} \\
P_D &= V_{drop} \times I_L = 7 \text{ V} \times 0.5 \text{ A} = 3.5 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $P_D = \mathbf{3.5 \text{ W}}$ — This is the heat the IC must shed.

#### (b) Efficiency

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} \times 100\% \\
\eta &= \dfrac{5}{12} \times 100\% \\
\eta &= 41.67\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $\eta \approx \mathbf{41.67\%}$ — Low efficiency confirms the "linear regulator is inefficient at high headroom" rule.

#### (c) Heat Sink Thermal Resistance

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{th(sa)} &= \dfrac{T_{J(max)} - T_A}{P_D} - R_{th(jc)} - R_{th(cs)} \\
R_{th(sa)} &= \dfrac{150 - 40}{3.5} - 3 - 0.5 \\
R_{th(sa)} &= \dfrac{110}{3.5} - 3.5 \\
R_{th(sa)} &= 31.43 - 3.5 \\
R_{th(sa)} &= 27.93 °C/W
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $R_{th(sa)} \approx \mathbf{27.93 °C/W}$ — Choose a standard heatsink with rating **≤ 27°C/W** (typical commercial values: 25°C/W, 20°C/W, 15°C/W). Selecting 25°C/W gives a small safety margin.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** If we had **not** used a heat sink (i.e., relied on the IC's own TO-220 thermal resistance of ≈ 54°C/W):
> $T_J = T_A + P_D \times R_{th(ja)} = 40 + 3.5 \times 54 = 229°C$ — This **exceeds** 150°C and triggers thermal shutdown. So the heat sink is **essential**, not optional.

---

### 3.4 Quick-Reference Numerical Checklist

| Situation | What to Calculate | Formula to Use |
|:----------|:------------------|:---------------|
| Unregulated input, fixed regulator | Output voltage | Read from part number |
| Given $V_{in}$, $V_{out}$, $I_L$ | Heat to dissipate | $P_D = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \times I_L$ |
| Want high efficiency | Minimize $V_{in}$ | $V_{in(min)} = V_{out} + V_{drop}$ |
| Thermal design | Heatsink size | $R_{th(sa)} = (T_{J(max)} - T_A) / P_D - R_{th(jc)} - R_{th(cs)}$ |
| Comparing 78XX vs 79XX | Polarity | 78XX → positive, 79XX → negative |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Pinout & Functional Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Unregulated DC Input Vin] --> B[Pin 1 INPUT]
    B --> C[Series Pass Transistor Darlington]
    C --> D[Pin 3 OUTPUT Vout Fixed]
    D --> E[Load RL]
    E --> F[Pin 2 GROUND]
    F --> G[Common Return]
    B --> H[Bandgap Reference 1.25V]
    D --> I[Feedback Divider]
    I --> J[Error Amplifier]
    H --> J
    J --> C
    C --> K[Thermal Shutdown 150C]
    C --> L[Current Limit 1.5A]
    C --> M[SOA Protection]
```

### 4.2 Typical Application Circuit (Block-Level Functional Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    AC[230V AC Mains] --> XF[Transformer Step Down]
    XF --> BR[Bridge Rectifier]
    BR --> FC[Filter Capacitor C1 1000uF]
    FC --> REG[78XX or 79XX 3 Pin Regulator]
    CIN1[0.33uF Input Cap] --> REG
    COUT1[0.1uF Output Cap] --> REG
    REG --> LOAD[DC Load Microcontroller Op-Amp]
    REG -.Heat.-> HS[Heat Sink Thermal Path]
```

### 4.3 Dual Polarity (±5V, ±12V, ±15V) Power Supply Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    MAINS[AC Mains] --> XFMR[Center-Tapped Transformer]
    XFMR --> POS[Positive Half Wave]
    XFMR --> NEG[Negative Half Wave]
    POS --> PBR[Bridge Rectifier 1]
    NEG --> NBR[Bridge Rectifier 2]
    PBR --> PCAP[Filter Cap Positive Rail]
    NBR --> NCAP[Filter Cap Negative Rail]
    PCAP --> P78[78XX Regulator Pin1]
    P78 --> POUT[Positive Output Pin3]
    P78 --> PGND[Common Ground Pin2]
    NCAP --> N79[79XX Regulator Pin2]
    N79 --> NOUT[Negative Output Pin3]
    N79 --> NGND[Common Ground Pin1]
    POUT --> DUAL[Dual Supply to Op-Amp Load]
    NGND --> DUAL
    PGND --> DUAL
```

### 4.4 Voltage-Current Characteristic Conceptual Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Input Voltage Vin increases] --> B[Output Vout remains CONSTANT at 5V]
    B --> C[Excess Voltage dropped across Pass Transistor]
    C --> D[Heat Generated PD = Vin - Vout x IL]
    A --> E[Load Current IL varies 0 to 1A]
    E --> F[Output Vout still CONSTANT at 5V]
    F --> G[Load Regulation Error typically 0.1%]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** All Mermaid node IDs are pure alphanumeric (e.g., `P78`, `N79`, `POS`, `NEG`) and labels are clean uppercase text — fully compliant with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE Mermaid safeguards.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
> **Differentiate between 78XX and 79XX series voltage regulators. Mention the typical output voltages available in each series.**

**Model Answer:**

The **78XX series** are **positive voltage regulators** that provide a fixed positive DC output voltage with respect to ground. Examples: **7805 (+5V), 7806 (+6V), 7808 (+8V), 7809 (+9V), 7810 (+10V), 7812 (+12V), 7815 (+15V), 7818 (+18V), 7824 (+24V)**.

The **79XX series** are **negative voltage regulators** that provide a fixed negative DC output voltage with respect to ground. Examples: **7905 (−5V), 7908 (−8V), 7912 (−12V), 7915 (−15V), 7924 (−24V)**.

Both are **3-terminal monolithic ICs** in TO-220/TO-92 packages, rated for **1A output current** (TO-220). The pin configurations are **mirror images** of each other.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
> **What is dropout voltage in a 3-pin regulator? Why is it important?**

**Model Answer:**

**Dropout voltage** is the **minimum voltage difference** that must be maintained between the input and output pins of a linear regulator to ensure proper regulation. For the 78XX/79XX series, the typical dropout voltage is **2V to 2.5V**.

**Importance:** If $V_{in}$ falls below $V_{out} + V_{drop}$, the regulator **drops out of regulation** — the pass transistor saturates, internal feedback breaks, and the output voltage begins to **track the input** (no longer regulated). For a 7805, the input must remain **above 7V** (5V + 2V dropout) at all times, including under full-load and minimum mains conditions.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks — Full Question with Internal Choice)

> **Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – July 2024, End Semester] (7 Marks)**
> **Explain the internal block diagram of a 78XX series 3-pin voltage regulator. Describe the function of each block in detail.**

**Model Answer:**

A 78XX regulator consists of five internal functional blocks:

1. **Series-Pass Transistor (Darlington Pair):** A high-current NPN Darlington configuration acts as the main current-handling element. It behaves as a **variable resistance** between input and output, dropping the excess voltage as heat. Darlington connection provides **high current gain (β ≈ 1000)**, enabling a 1A output with a small base drive current.

2. **Bandgap Voltage Reference:** Generates a **temperature-stable reference voltage of ≈ 1.25V** by summing a negative-temperature-coefficient (CTAT) voltage with a positive-temperature-coefficient (PTAT) voltage. This reference is independent of supply voltage and ambient temperature.

3. **Error Amplifier (Differential Amplifier):** A high-gain op-amp that compares the scaled-down output voltage (from the feedback network) with the bandgap reference. Its output drives the base of the Darlington pair, closing the negative feedback loop.

4. **Feedback Resistor Divider (Internal):** A pair of precision resistors scales the output voltage down to match the bandgap reference. For example, in the 7805, the divider ratio is set such that when $V_{out} = 5V$, the feedback signal equals 1.25V.

5. **Protection Circuitry (Three sub-blocks):**
   - **Thermal Shutdown:** A temperature-sensing circuit that **disables the pass transistor** when $T_J > 150°C$, preventing thermal runaway.
   - **Current Limit:** A sensing resistor monitors pass-transistor current; if it exceeds ≈ **1.5A**, the pass transistor is throttled back.
   - **Safe Operating Area (SOA) Protection:** Prevents the simultaneous occurrence of high current and high voltage across the pass transistor, which could cause **secondary breakdown**.

> **Q3(b). [KTU University Exam – July 2024, End Semester] (7 Marks)**
> **A 7812 regulator provides +12V to a load of 600 mA from a 24V DC unregulated supply. Calculate (i) the power dissipated by the IC, (ii) the efficiency, and (iii) the required heat sink thermal resistance if $T_A = 35°C$, $R_{th(jc)} = 3°C/W$, and $R_{th(cs)} = 0.5°C/W$.**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $V_{in} = 24$ V, $V_{out} = 12$ V, $I_L = 600$ mA $= 0.6$ A, $T_A = 35°C$, $T_{J(max)} = 150°C$, $R_{th(jc)} = 3°C/W$, $R_{th(cs)} = 0.5°C/W$.

**(i) Power Dissipated:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_D &= (V_{in} - V_{out}) \times I_L \\
P_D &= (24 - 12) \times 0.6 \\
P_D &= 12 \times 0.6 = 7.2 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Efficiency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \dfrac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{12}{24} \times 100\% = 50\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Heat Sink Thermal Resistance:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{th(sa)} &= \dfrac{T_{J(max)} - T_A}{P_D} - R_{th(jc)} - R_{th(cs)} \\
R_{th(sa)} &= \dfrac{150 - 35}{7.2} - 3 - 0.5 \\
R_{th(sa)} &= \dfrac{115}{7.2} - 3.5 \\
R_{th(sa)} &= 15.97 - 3.5 \\
R_{th(sa)} &= 12.47 °C/W
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** A standard **12°C/W** (or lower, e.g., 10°C/W) aluminum heatsink is required.

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternate Choice)

> **Q4(a). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, End Semester] (7 Marks)**
> **Design a ±12V dual-polarity regulated power supply using 7812 and 7912 regulators from a 24V center-tapped transformer. Draw the circuit diagram and explain its operation.**

**Model Answer:**

**Circuit Components:**
- Center-tapped transformer: 24V-0-24V, 1A
- Two bridge rectifiers (or 4 diodes each)
- Filter capacitors: $C_1 = C_2 = 1000 µF / 50V$ electrolytic
- Bypass capacitors: $C_{in} = 0.33 µF$, $C_{out} = 0.1 µF$ for each regulator
- IC1 = 7812 (positive regulator)
- IC2 = 7912 (negative regulator)
- Two heat sinks

**Operation:**

1. The 24V-0-24V secondary produces two AC voltages of 24V each, referenced to the center tap (ground).
2. The **upper 24V winding** feeds a bridge rectifier → produces a **positive DC rail** of ≈ $24 \times 1.414 = 33.9V$ (peak), smoothed to ≈ **+33V DC** by $C_1$.
3. The **lower 24V winding** feeds another bridge rectifier → produces a **negative DC rail** of ≈ **−33V DC**, smoothed by $C_2$.
4. The **7812** drops +33V to a regulated **+12V** at its output (Pin 3), with Pin 1 = input, Pin 2 = ground.
5. The **7912** drops −33V to a regulated **−12V** at its output (Pin 3), with Pin 1 = ground, Pin 2 = input.
6. The common ground (center tap) is shared by both regulators.
7. The load (typically a dual-supply op-amp circuit) is powered by +12V on Pin 3 of 7812, −12V on Pin 3 of 7912, with the center tap as 0V reference.

> **Q4(b). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, End Semester] (7 Marks)**
> **Compare line regulation and load regulation. Why are both important in a regulated power supply?**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Line Regulation | Load Regulation |
|:----------|:----------------|:----------------|
| **Definition** | Ability to maintain constant output when input voltage changes | Ability to maintain constant output when load current changes |
| **Variable** | $V_{in}$ | $I_L$ |
| **Measured by** | Varying $V_{in}$ over its range and observing $\Delta V_{out}$ | Varying $I_L$ from no-load to full-load and observing $\Delta V_{out}$ |
| **Typical value (78XX)** | ≈ 0.01% / V | ≈ 0.1% to 0.5% |
| **Why it matters** | Mains fluctuations (190V–250V in India) cause $V_{in}$ to vary | Different operating modes of the load draw different currents |

**Importance of Both:**
- **Line regulation** ensures the output stays steady despite **upstream disturbances** like sags, surges, or rectifier ripple.
- **Load regulation** ensures the output stays steady despite **dynamic load changes** (e.g., a microcontroller suddenly switching from sleep to active mode).
- A regulator with poor line regulation will let mains hum reach the load; poor load regulation will cause "**sag**" under heavy current demand.
- Together, they define the **PSRR (Power Supply Rejection Ratio)** and **dynamic performance** of the supply.
- In KTU lab measurements, both are evaluated using the standard test circuits from datasheets (e.g., LM7805 datasheet test conditions).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Forgetting to convert mA to A in $P_D$ calculation. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 2. **Confusing Polarity:** Writing 79XX pinout as if it were 78XX. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 3. **Missing Dropout Voltage:** Not verifying that $V_{in} - V_{out} \geq 2V$. The examiner tests if you know the regulator will not regulate below dropout. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 4. **Forgetting Heat Sink:** If $P_D > 1W$, omitting heat sink calculation = **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 5. **Rounded Heat Sink Value:** Always select a **commercially available** value (e.g., 12°C/W, 10°C/W) — selecting 12.47°C/W is correct as the *required* value, but the *chosen* value should be ≤ 12.47°C/W.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Print & Memorize**

- **78XX → Positive**, **79XX → Negative**; both are **linear series-pass regulators**.
- The **last two digits** of the part number give the **output voltage magnitude** (7805 = +5V, 7915 = −15V).
- **Three pins:** For 78XX, pin order is **INPUT – GROUND – OUTPUT**. For 79XX, pin order is **GROUND – INPUT – OUTPUT** (mirror image!).
- **Dropout voltage** is typically **2V to 2.5V** — input must always be ≥ $V_{out} + 2V$.
- **Maximum input voltage** is typically **35V** for 78XX and **−35V** for 79XX.
- **Output current** capability: **1A** for TO-220, **100mA** for TO-92 package.
- **Power dissipated** = $(V_{in} - V_{out}) \times I_L$ — this is the heat to be sunk.
- **Efficiency** = $(V_{out} / V_{in}) \times 100\%$ — typically 30%–50%, because excess voltage is wasted as heat.
- **Heat sink rating** = $(T_{J(max)} - T_A) / P_D - R_{th(jc)} - R_{th(cs)}$; $T_{J(max)} = 150°C$.
- **Bypass capacitors** are mandatory: **0.33 µF** at input, **0.1 µF** at output (ceramic types preferred).
- **Internal protections:** Thermal shutdown (150°C), current limiting (≈1.5A), and SOA protection.
- **Line regulation** = ability to reject $V_{in}$ changes; **Load regulation** = ability to reject $I_L$ changes.
- **PSRR / Ripple Rejection** is typically **60–70 dB at 120 Hz** — excellent at filtering rectified mains hum.
- **Dual supplies** (±12V, ±15V) are built using 78XX + 79XX from a **center-tapped transformer**.
- For high-current applications, **LM78XX** is the 1A version, **LM78MXX** is the 0.5A version, and **LM78LXX** is the 100mA version — note the suffix letter.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IC 723 and Its Use as Low and High Voltage Regulators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES & CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** IC 723 and Its Use as Low and High Voltage Regulators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **IC 723** is a **monolithic, floating, general-purpose linear voltage regulator** fabricated on a single silicon chip. It is classified as a **variable voltage regulator** that can be externally configured to operate as a **low-voltage regulator, high-voltage regulator, current regulator, series regulator, shunt regulator, or switching regulator**. The device integrates a **temperature-compensated Zener reference**, a **high-gain error amplifier**, a **series pass transistor**, and **programmable current-limiting circuitry** on a 14-pin DIP package.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Exam Focus:** The IC 723 is a *floating* regulator. This means the reference point of the internal circuitry is *not* tied to ground. This single feature is what allows the same IC to regulate low voltages (2 V to 7 V) and high voltages (7 V to 37 V) without changing the chip.

### Key Datasheet Ratings (Must Memorize for KTU)

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Output Voltage Range | **2 V to 37 V** |
| Input Voltage (max) | **40 V** |
| Output Current (internal) | **150 mA** |
| Output Current (with external pass transistor) | **up to 10 A** |
| Reference Voltage (Vref) | **7.15 V (typ)** |
| Line Regulation | **0.01 % / V** |
| Load Regulation | **0.03 %** |
| Dropout Voltage | **~3 V** |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of the IC 723 as a **smart water-tap controller** connected to a high-pressure water tank:

* The **water tank** represents the unregulated DC input supply (which has voltage ripples and fluctuations, just like uneven water pressure).
* The **pressure sensor (error amplifier)** continuously compares the output water pressure (output voltage) against a set reference pressure (the internal Zener reference of 7.15 V).
* The **valve (series pass transistor)** opens or closes to let in more or less water, maintaining a constant downstream pressure.
* The **emergency shut-off (current limiter)** protects the pipe from bursting if someone draws too much current.
* The **floating design** is like having the entire controller mounted on a small floating raft — it doesn't care where "ground" is in the larger system, only the *difference* between its two reference points. This is what allows it to regulate 3 V or 30 V with the same chip.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Hook for KTU Exams:** The "floating" property means the IC's internal ground is *isolated* from the system ground. Therefore, both low-side and high-side regulation become possible from one IC.

## 1.3 Package and Pin Architecture (14-Pin DIP)

The 723 is housed in a 14-pin ceramic or plastic DIP package. The pin map is as follows:

| Pin | Symbol | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | NC | No Connection |
| 2 | CURR LIMIT | Current Limit Setting |
| 3 | CS | Current Sense (for external Rsense) |
| 4 | IN– | Inverting Input of Error Amplifier |
| 5 | IN+ | Non-Inverting Input of Error Amplifier |
| 6 | VREF | Reference Voltage Output (7.15 V) |
| 7 | V– | Negative Supply / Substrate |
| 8 | NC | No Connection |
| 9 | Vz | Zener Cathode (for external compensation) |
| 10 | Vout | Regulator Output |
| 11 | Vc | Collector of Internal Pass Transistor |
| 12 | VIN | Input Voltage |
| 13 | V+ | Positive Supply for Internal Circuitry |
| 14 | FOLDBACK | Foldback Current Limiting |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Block Architecture — The Three Building Blocks

The IC 723 is composed of **three functional sub-systems**. Understanding these is the foundation of all KTU derivations:

### A. The Reference Subsystem
* A temperature-compensated **Zener diode** produces a stable **7.15 V**.
* This reference is **independent** of the input voltage variations (it is biased from VIN via an internal current source).
* It is brought out at **Pin 6 (VREF)** so that the user can scale it externally with a resistor divider.

### B. The Error Amplifier (EA)
* A high-gain differential amplifier (gm ≈ 5 mA/V).
* **IN+ (Pin 5)** is the non-inverting input.
* **IN– (Pin 4)** is the inverting input.
* Output of the EA drives the base of the internal series pass transistor.
* A **frequency compensation (COMP) pin** (sometimes Pin 1 or Pin 3 depending on datasheet version) allows the user to add an external capacitor to roll off the gain and prevent oscillation.

### C. The Series Pass Transistor
* A **Darlington-connected NPN pair** with the collector brought out at **Vc (Pin 11)** and emitter at **Vout (Pin 10)**.
* Can deliver **150 mA internally**.
* For higher currents, the user connects an **external NPN pass transistor** at Pin 11.

## 2.2 Operating Principle (Negative Feedback Loop)

The regulator works on the principle of **closed-loop negative feedback**:

1. A fraction of the output voltage (Vo) is fed back to the **IN+** input via a resistor divider (R1, R2).
2. The internal reference **Vref = 7.15 V** is applied to the **IN–** input.
3. The error amplifier computes the difference: **V_error = Vref – V_feedback**.
4. This error voltage drives the pass transistor, which adjusts the output until the feedback voltage exactly equals the reference.
5. The loop locks when the error becomes zero, meaning **V_feedback = Vref**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** "*Sense → Compare → Correct → Repeat.*" This is the golden loop of every linear regulator ever built.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates every equation the KTU board examiner expects you to know. **No vertical pipes** are used inside cells (we use `\vert` notation instead).

| Configuration | Output Voltage Formula | Applicable Range | Current Limit Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Low Voltage (Non-Floating)** | $V_o = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$ | $V_o$ from **2 V to 7 V** | $I_{limit} = \dfrac{V_{sense}}{R_{sense}} \approx \dfrac{0.65}{R_{sense}}$ |
| **High Voltage (Floating)** | $V_o = V_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2} = V_{ref}\left(1 + \dfrac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$ | $V_o$ from **7 V to 37 V** | Same as above |
| **Current Regulator Mode** | $I_{load} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{R_{sense}}$ | For current sources | — |
| **Foldback Limit** | $I_{sc} = \dfrac{V_o + V_{sense}}{R_{sense} + R_{foldback}}$ | With Pin 14 used | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction (Examiner's Favourite):**
> * In **Low Voltage** mode, the reference 7.15 V is applied to **IN+** and the feedback divider goes to **IN–**. The output can never exceed 7.15 V because the divider is referenced to ground.
> * In **High Voltage** mode, the reference 7.15 V is applied to **IN–** and the divider to **IN+**, and *both* V+ and V– of the IC are tied to the output. This is what unlocks the floating behaviour and allows $V_o > 7.15$ V.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility in the Real World

* **Laboratory power supplies** — The 723 is the heart of virtually every analog bench-top power supply below 1 A.
* **On-card regulation** in legacy industrial PCBs (telecom backplanes, instrumentation racks).
* **Battery charging systems** for NiCd and lead-acid packs.
* **Aircraft avionics**, where the input bus can be 28 V DC and subsystems need clean 5 V, 12 V, or 15 V rails.
* **Student laboratory kits** — the classic 723 trainer module is a standard pedagogical tool in every KTU-affiliated electronics lab.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Low-Voltage Regulator Output Equation

**Problem Statement:** Derive the output voltage expression for the IC 723 connected in the low-voltage configuration, with reference applied to the inverting input and a feedback divider at the non-inverting input.

### Circuit Topology

* Reference voltage **Vref = 7.15 V** is applied to **IN– (Pin 4)**.
* Resistor divider **R1, R2** is connected from **Vout** to **GND**, with the junction fed to **IN+ (Pin 5)**.
* The error amplifier adjusts Vout until the differential input is zero.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Apply KVL at the non-inverting input.**

The voltage at IN+ is a simple resistive divider of the output:

$$V_{IN+} = V_o \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the virtual-short condition of the error amplifier.**

In a closed-loop linear regulator, the high-gain EA drives the output until the differential input is zero:

$$V_{IN+} = V_{IN-}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the known input voltage.**

The reference is applied to IN–, so:

$$V_{IN-} = V_{ref} = 7.15 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Equate and solve for Vo.**

$$V_o \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = V_{ref}$$

$$V_o = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2}$$

**Step 5 — Final form for memorization.**

$$V_o = V_{ref}\left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$$

### Sanity Check

* If **R1 = 0 Ω** (no feedback resistor), then $V_o = 7.15$ V. This is the maximum output in low-voltage mode — consistent with the datasheet.
* If we set $V_o = 5$ V (a common logic rail), then $R_1/R_2 = (5/7.15) - 1 = -0.30$, which is impossible. This tells us we **must use the alternative low-voltage topology** (feedback to IN– and reference to IN+) for outputs **below 7.15 V**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Corrected Low-Voltage Topology (Vo < 7.15 V):** Apply the divider to **IN–** and Vref to **IN+**. The formula becomes $V_o = V_{ref} \cdot R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$. This is the version used in 99 % of KTU numerical problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — High-Voltage (Floating) Regulator Output Equation

**Problem Statement:** Derive the output expression for the floating configuration used to produce Vo > 7.15 V.

### Circuit Topology

* **V+ (Pin 13)** of the IC is tied directly to **Vout**.
* **V– (Pin 7)** is tied to the system ground.
* The feedback divider R1, R2 senses the output, but the **common-mode voltage** on the EA inputs is now equal to Vout.
* The reference is applied to **IN+ (Pin 5)** and the divider junction to **IN– (Pin 4)**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Voltage at the inverting input.**

$$V_{IN-} = V_o \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 2 — Voltage at the non-inverting input.**

$$V_{IN+} = V_{ref} = 7.15 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the virtual-short condition.**

$$V_{IN+} = V_{IN-}$$

$$V_{ref} = V_o \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Vo.**

$$V_o = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_2}$$

**Step 5 — Final form.**

$$V_o = V_{ref}\left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Floating Works Above 7.15 V:** The internal circuitry of the IC is powered between V+ and V–, which are now sitting at Vout and GND respectively. Therefore, the EA inputs can swing up to Vout without violating the IC's common-mode input range. This is the *exact* reason the floating trick is required for high-voltage operation.

### Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

Given: $V_{ref} = 7.15$ V, $R_1 = 3.3$ kΩ, $R_2 = 1.0$ kΩ.

$$V_o = 7.15 \cdot \left(1 + \frac{3.3}{1.0}\right) = 7.15 \times 4.3 = 30.745 \text{ V}$$

This is comfortably inside the 7 V to 37 V high-voltage window.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Current Limit Threshold

**Problem Statement:** Derive the current at which the internal current limiter activates.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Sense resistor placement.**

A small resistor **Rsense** is placed in series with the load. The voltage across it is:

$$V_{sense} = I_{load} \cdot R_{sense}$$

**Step 2 — Limit condition.**

The current-limit comparator trips when Vsense reaches one Vbe drop of the internal sensing transistor:

$$V_{sense} = V_{BE} \approx 0.65 \text{ V}$$

**Step 3 — Equate and solve.**

$$I_{limit} \cdot R_{sense} = 0.65$$

$$I_{limit} = \frac{0.65}{R_{sense}}$$

### Numerical Example

If $R_{sense} = 1$ Ω, then $I_{limit} = 0.65$ A.

If we want $I_{limit} = 100$ mA, then $R_{sense} = 6.5$ Ω.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Internal Block Diagram of IC 723 (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["VIN Pin 12 Unregulated DC Input"] --> nodeB["VCC Internal Bias Network"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Zener Reference 7.15 V Pin 6"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Buffer Vref Output"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Error Amplifier Non Inverting Input Pin 5"]
    nodeF["Feedback Divider R1 R2 from Vout"] --> nodeG["Error Amplifier Inverting Input Pin 4"]
    nodeE --> nodeH["Differential Error Amplifier Gain 80 dB"]
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI["Base Driver Darlington Pair"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Series Pass Transistor NPN Darlington Vc Pin 11 and Vout Pin 10"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Regulated Output Vo to Load"]
    nodeK --> nodeF
    nodeL["Rsense Pin 3"] --> nodeM["Current Limit Comparator"]
    nodeJ --> nodeL
    nodeC --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeN["Foldback Network Pin 14"]
    nodeN --> nodeI
```

## 4.2 Low-Voltage Regulator Configuration (Vo between 2 V and 7 V)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Unregulated DC Input 9.5 V to 40 V"] --> nodeB["VIN Pin 12 of IC 723"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Internal Series Pass Transistor"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Vout Pin 10 to Load"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Ground"]
    nodeF["Internal Vref 7.15 V"] --> nodeG["IN Minus Pin 4"]
    nodeH["R1 from Vout"] --> nodeI["Node A"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["R2 to Ground"]
    nodeI --> nodeK["IN Plus Pin 5"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["Error Amplifier"]
    nodeG --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeC
    nodeM["Rsense in series with Load"] --> nodeN["Current Limit Pin 2 or 3"]
```

## 4.3 High-Voltage (Floating) Regulator Configuration (Vo between 7 V and 37 V)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["Unregulated DC Input up to 40 V"] --> nodeB["VIN Pin 12 of IC 723"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Internal Pass Transistor"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Vout Pin 10 Regulated Output 7 V to 37 V"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["V Plus Pin 13 of IC 723"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["V Minus Pin 7 to Ground"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["R1 to Node A"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["R2 to Ground"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["IN Plus Pin 5"]
    nodeJ["Internal Vref 7.15 V"] --> nodeK["IN Minus Pin 4"]
    nodeI --> nodeL["Error Amplifier"]
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeC
```

## 4.4 Closed-Loop Regulation Flow (Mermaid Functional View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1["Step 1 Apply unregulated VIN to Pin 12"] --> step2["Step 2 Internal Zener generates Vref 7.15 V"]
    step2 --> step3["Step 3 Output divider R1 R2 senses Vo and produces Vfb"]
    step3 --> step4["Step 4 Error Amplifier computes Vref minus Vfb"]
    step4 --> step5{"Step 5 Is Vfb less than Vref?"}
    step5 -->|"Yes Output is low, increase conduction"| step6["Step 6 Drive Pass Transistor harder to raise Vo"]
    step5 -->|"No Output is high, reduce conduction"| step7["Step 7 Drive Pass Transistor softer to lower Vo"]
    step6 --> step8["Step 8 Loop settles when Vfb equals Vref exactly"]
    step7 --> step8
    step8 --> step9["Step 9 Regulated output Vo is delivered to the load"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Explain the significance of the term "floating regulator" with respect to IC 723. Why is this property essential for high-voltage operation?**

**Model Answer:**

The IC 723 is called a *floating regulator* because the internal reference and error amplifier are not tied to the system ground. Instead, the IC has separate V+ and V– pins that can be connected anywhere in the circuit. This floating topology is essential for high-voltage operation because the feedback divider's common-mode voltage can exceed the internal reference (7.15 V) — and the IC accommodates this by being powered directly from the regulated output. As a result, the same chip can regulate outputs from 2 V up to 37 V without any change in the IC itself.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for definition of floating, 1 mark for the V+/V– flexibility, 1 mark for explaining why this allows 7 V to 37 V range.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List the three internal sub-systems of the IC 723 and state the function of each in one sentence.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Reference Subsystem** — Produces a temperature-compensated Zener reference of 7.15 V used as the comparison standard.
2. **Error Amplifier** — Compares a fraction of the output with the reference and drives the pass transistor to null the difference.
3. **Series Pass Transistor** — Acts as the variable-resistance element that drops the excess input voltage and delivers a clean regulated output.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark per sub-system for name and function.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat functional block diagram, describe the internal architecture of the monolithic IC 723 voltage regulator. Clearly mark the reference, error amplifier, and pass transistor blocks. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design a low-voltage regulator using the IC 723 to deliver a regulated output of **+5 V at 100 mA** from an unregulated input of **+12 V**. Show all resistor calculations, the value of the current-sense resistor, and the complete circuit topology. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

The internal block diagram of the 723 consists of three main sub-systems as illustrated in SECTION 4.1. The *Zener reference* provides a fixed 7.15 V at Pin 6. The *error amplifier* has its inverting input at Pin 4 and non-inverting input at Pin 5, with the COMP pin used for external frequency compensation. The *series pass transistor* is a Darlington-connected NPN pair with its collector at Pin 11 (Vc) and emitter at Pin 10 (Vout). The current-limit block monitors the voltage across an external sense resistor and folds back the drive when the threshold is exceeded.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** 2 marks for block diagram, 2 marks for reference description, 2 marks for error amplifier description, 1 mark for pass transistor description.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Choose the topology.** Since Vo = 5 V < 7.15 V, we use the **low-voltage (non-floating) configuration**. Apply Vref to **IN+ (Pin 5)** and the divider to **IN– (Pin 4)**.

**Step 2 — Apply the formula.**

$$V_o = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values.**

$$5 = 7.15 \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

$$\frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = \frac{5}{7.15} = 0.6993$$

**Step 4 — Choose R2 and compute R1.** Let $R_2 = 1.0$ kΩ. Then:

$$R_1 = R_2 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{0.6993} - 1\right) = 1.0 \times (1.43 - 1) = 0.43 \text{ k}\Omega \approx 430 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 5 — Compute the sense resistor.**

$$R_{sense} = \frac{0.65}{I_{limit}} = \frac{0.65}{0.100} = 6.5 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 6 — Final component values.**

| Component | Value |
|---|---|
| R1 | 430 Ω |
| R2 | 1.0 kΩ |
| Rsense | 6.5 Ω |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** 2 marks for topology selection, 2 marks for divider calculation, 1 mark for sense resistor, 2 marks for component summary.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the difference between the low-voltage and high-voltage configurations of the IC 723. Why is the floating topology mandatory when Vo > 7.15 V? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Design a high-voltage floating regulator using the IC 723 to deliver **+15 V at 1 A** from a **+24 V unregulated supply**. Compute the divider resistors, the sense resistor for current limit, and show how the V+ and V– pins are connected. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

In the **low-voltage configuration** the IC's V– pin is tied to system ground, and the output is limited to voltages below the internal reference (7.15 V). The reference is applied to IN+, and the feedback divider is applied to IN–.

In the **high-voltage configuration** the IC is "floated" on top of its own output. The V+ pin is tied to Vout and V– is tied to ground. The common-mode voltage on the EA inputs can now equal Vout, which can be greater than 7.15 V. This is mandatory because the internal error amplifier cannot tolerate an input common-mode voltage above V+ minus a few volts. By powering the IC between Vout and ground, the common-mode range is shifted upward, allowing regulation of outputs from 7 V up to 37 V.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** 2 marks for low-voltage description, 2 marks for high-voltage description, 2 marks for the floating mechanism, 1 mark for the common-mode explanation.

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1 — Topology.** Vo = 15 V > 7.15 V → use the **floating (high-voltage) configuration**. V+ (Pin 13) tied to Vout, V– (Pin 7) tied to ground.

**Step 2 — Apply the high-voltage formula.**

$$V_o = V_{ref}\left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$15 = 7.15\left(1 + \frac{R_1}{R_2}\right)$$

$$\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{15}{7.15} - 1 = 1.098$$

**Step 4 — Choose R2 and compute R1.** Let $R_2 = 1.0$ kΩ.

$$R_1 = 1.098 \text{ k}\Omega \approx 1.1 \text{ k}\Omega$$

**Step 5 — Current limit and external pass transistor.** Since 1 A exceeds the internal 150 mA, an **external NPN pass transistor** (e.g., 2N3055) must be added at Pin 11. The sense resistor becomes:

$$R_{sense} = \frac{0.65}{1.0} = 0.65 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 6 — Power dissipation check on external pass transistor.**

$$P_D = (V_{in} - V_o) \cdot I_o = (24 - 15) \times 1 = 9 \text{ W}$$

A suitable heatsink (thermal resistance < 10 °C/W) is mandatory.

**Step 7 — Summary of component values.**

| Component | Value |
|---|---|
| R1 | 1.1 kΩ |
| R2 | 1.0 kΩ |
| Rsense | 0.65 Ω (2 W) |
| External Pass | 2N3055 on heatsink |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution:** 2 marks for floating topology, 2 marks for divider calculation, 2 marks for external pass transistor with heat calculation, 1 mark for the summary table.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** The most common mistake is applying the **low-voltage formula** when the output exceeds 7.15 V, leading to nonsensical resistor ratios. Always check: *Is Vo > 7.15 V?* If yes, **use the floating topology and the formula $V_o = V_{ref}(1 + R_1/R_2)$ applied to the floating configuration.** A second common error is forgetting that for Io > 150 mA, an **external pass transistor is mandatory** and you must include a heatsink in your design.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* **IC 723 is a floating, general-purpose, monolithic linear voltage regulator** in a 14-pin DIP.
* **Three internal sub-systems:** Zener reference (7.15 V), error amplifier, Darlington pass transistor.
* **Two operating modes:**
   * *Low-Voltage Mode* (2 V to 7 V): $V_o = V_{ref} \cdot R_2 / (R_1 + R_2)$.
   * *High-Voltage Mode* (7 V to 37 V): $V_o = V_{ref} \cdot (1 + R_1/R_2)$ with V+ tied to Vout.
* **Current limit:** $I_{limit} = 0.65 / R_{sense}$.
* **Internal current limit:** 150 mA. **External pass transistor required** for higher currents.
* **Maximum input:** 40 V. **Maximum output:** 37 V.
* **The "floating" property is the key to high-voltage operation** — it shifts the common-mode range of the error amplifier up to the output voltage.
* **Always include a heatsink calculation** for the pass transistor in any KTU design problem — examiners award marks for thermal design.
* **Reference voltage to memorize:** $V_{ref} = 7.15$ V (typical, $7.0$ V to $7.3$ V range).
* **Frequency compensation:** A small capacitor (typically 100 pF to 1000 pF) on the COMP pin prevents high-frequency oscillation.
* **Common misconception:** The 723 *cannot* regulate below 2 V (dropout voltage limitation).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optoelectronic Devices: Construction, Operation and Characteristics of LED
topics.content:
# Optoelectronic Devices: Light Emitting Diode (LED) ## 1. Core Technical Definition A **Light Emitting Diode (LED)** is a semiconductor p-n junction diode that emits **coherent or incoherent visible, infrared, or ultraviolet light** when forward biased. The emission of light occurs through the process of **electroluminescence**, wherein the recombination of electrons and holes at the depleted junction region releases energy in the form of photons. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "An LED is a specially designed forward-biased p-n junction device fabricated from a **direct bandgap semiconductor material** (such as GaAs, GaP, GaAsP, or AlGaAs) in which the application of a forward voltage causes charge carriers to recombine radiatively across the junction, emitting monochromatic light of wavelength $\lambda = \frac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})}$ μm." | Parameter | Value / Detail | |---|---| | Standard Symbol Units | $\lambda$ (nm), $I_F$ (mA), $V_F$ (V), $I_V$ (mcd) | | Forward Voltage Drop $V_F$ | **1.2 V (Red) to 3.5 V (Blue/White)** | | Operating Current $I_F$ | **10 mA to 30 mA (standard); up to 1 A for high-power LEDs** | | Reverse Breakdown Voltage | **5 V to 10 V typical** | | Planck's Constant $h$ | **$6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s** | | Speed of Light $c$ | **$3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s** | | Electron Charge $q$ | **$1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C** | > [!NOTE] > **Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition):** > Imagine a **waterfall**. Water (electrons) falls from a high cliff (conduction band) into a river below (valence band). When the water crashes into the river, it creates a **splash of mist and sound** — this is energy being released. In an LED, when electrons "fall" from the conduction band into the valence band (i.e., recombine with holes), they release that energy as a tiny burst of **light (a photon)**. A standard silicon diode releases this energy as **heat** (non-radiative recombination) — but a direct bandgap material like GaAs releases it as **light** (radiative recombination). That's why an LED glows and a silicon diode does not! ## 2. Direct vs. Indirect Bandgap — Why It Matters > [!IMPORTANT] > **KEY DISTINCTION — KTU Frequently Tested:** > Only **direct bandgap** semiconductors emit efficient light. Silicon (Si) and Germanium (Ge) are **indirect bandgap** materials — they primarily emit **heat** instead of light when electrons recombine. This is precisely why LEDs are **never** made from Si or Ge; they are made from materials like **GaAs, GaP, AlGaAs, InGaN, SiC, etc.** --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Construction of an LED The construction of an LED is engineered in three critical layers: 1. **p-type top layer (Anode side):** Heavily doped (typically Zn-doped GaP) so that holes are the majority carriers. This is the layer where light is **extracted** because it is thinner. 2. **Active Junction / Depletion Region:** The radiative recombination zone. Built using a **direct bandgap** III-V compound. 3. **n-type bottom layer (Cathode side):** Heavily doped (Te-doped GaAs or Si-doped GaP) to provide abundant electrons. This layer is thick and acts as a **mechanical substrate** as well as an electrical contact. **Constructional features that maximize light output:** - **Recombination zone is placed close to the surface** to minimize internal absorption. - The **p-layer is kept thin** so photons can escape before being reabsorbed. - A **metal heat sink (anvil) and post (cathode lead)** provide thermal dissipation and mechanical support. - A **reflective cup** is placed beneath the semiconductor die to direct photons upward. - A **plastic epoxy dome/lens** encapsulates the die. The dome shape (typically hemispherical) acts as a **lens** to focus the light into a narrow beam, increasing **luminous intensity** in the forward direction. - The large **flat surface area** of the anode lead acts as a heat sink. > [!NOTE] > **Why a hemispherical dome?** > The semiconductor die has a very high **refractive index** (e.g., GaAs has $n \approx 3.5$). At this high index, total internal reflection would trap most light inside the die. The epoxy dome has $n \approx 1.5$ — much closer to air ($n=1$). This **stepped refractive index profile** dramatically reduces internal reflection and increases the **escape cone** of photons. ## 2.2 Operation — The Physics of Electroluminescence The operation can be broken into **five sequential stages:** 1. **Forward Bias Application:** A DC voltage greater than the **threshold (knee) voltage** $V_K$ is applied with the **p-side connected to the positive terminal** and the **n-side to the negative terminal**. 2. **Barrier Potential Reduction:** The applied forward voltage opposes and reduces the built-in potential barrier $V_0$ at the depletion region. When $V_F > V_0$, the junction becomes heavily forward-biased. 3. **Carrier Injection:** Electrons from the n-side are **injected** across the junction into the p-side. Similarly, holes from the p-side are injected into the n-side. This is called **minority carrier injection**. 4. **Radiative Recombination:** Inside and near the depletion region, the injected electrons and holes encounter each other and **recombine**. Because the material is a **direct bandgap** semiconductor, the energy released during recombination takes the form of a **photon** of light. 5. **Photon Emission:** Each photon's energy equals the bandgap energy $E_g$. The wavelength (and therefore color) of light is governed by: $$ \lambda = \frac{hc}{E_g} $$ > where $h$ is Planck's constant, $c$ is the speed of light, and $E_g$ is the bandgap in Joules. In practical units, this becomes: $$ \lambda(\mu m) = \frac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})} $$ ## 2.3 Working Principle Summary Table | Process Stage | Physical Phenomenon | Engineering Outcome | |---|---|---| | Forward Bias | Voltage overcomes $V_0$ | Depletion region narrows | | Carrier Injection | Diffusion of e⁻, h⁺ | Minority carrier concentration rises | | Recombination | e⁻ falls from CB to VB | Energy released as photon (radiative) | | Photon Emission | $E = h\nu = E_g$ | Light of wavelength $\lambda$ escapes | | External Lensing | Epoxy dome focuses | Directional luminous intensity | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{hc}{E_g}$ | Photon wavelength from bandgap | $E_g$ in Joules; $\lambda$ in meters | | 2 | $\lambda(\mu m) = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})}$ | **Most-used practical form** | $\lambda$ in μm; $E_g$ in eV | | 3 | $E_g = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda(\text{nm})}$ | Bandgap from desired wavelength | $\lambda$ in nm; $E_g$ in eV | | 4 | $E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy | Joules | | 5 | $I_F = I_S \left( e^{V_F/\eta V_T} - 1 \right)$ | Forward current equation | $\eta \approx 1\text{–}2$ for LEDs | | 6 | $V_F \approx \dfrac{E_g}{q}$ | Knee voltage approximation | $V_F$ in Volts | | 7 | $\eta_{\text{ext}} = \dfrac{\text{Photons out}}{\text{Electrons in}} \times 100\%$ | **External Quantum Efficiency** | Percentage | | 8 | $\eta_{\text{lum}} = \dfrac{\Phi_{\text{lum}}}{P_{\text{elec}}}$ | Luminous efficiency (lm/W) | Used in photometric analysis | | 9 | $L = 683 \cdot V(\lambda) \cdot \int P(\lambda) d\lambda$ | Luminance from spectral power | $V(\lambda)$ is photopic response | | 10 | $P_{\text{rad}} = \eta_{\text{int}} \cdot I_F \cdot V_F$ | Radiated optical power | Watts | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL KTU Tip — Color-to-Bandgap Mapping (Memorize!):** > - **Red** LED → GaAs → $E_g \approx 1.43$ eV → $\lambda \approx 870$ nm (IR/red edge) > - **Red/Orange** → GaAsP → $E_g \approx 1.9$ eV → $\lambda \approx 635$ nm > - **Yellow** → GaP:N → $E_g \approx 2.1$ eV → $\lambda \approx 590$ nm > - **Green** → GaP:N → $E_g \approx 2.25$ eV → $\lambda \approx 555$ nm > - **Blue** → SiC / GaN → $E_g \approx 2.8\text{–}3.0$ eV → $\lambda \approx 470$ nm > - **White** → Blue LED + Yellow Phosphor (YAG:Ce) coating ## 2.5 V-I Characteristics of an LED The forward V-I characteristic of an LED follows the **Shockley diode equation**, but with critical differences from a standard Si diode: - The **knee (threshold) voltage** is much higher (1.2 V to 3.5 V) because the bandgap is larger. - The **reverse breakdown voltage is very low** (typically 5 V) — LEDs are highly sensitive to reverse bias. - The forward curve is **steeper** — once $V_F$ exceeds the threshold, current rises rapidly. - The **reverse leakage current** is also governed by the standard equation but is typically very small. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Reality:** > LEDs must always be operated with a **current-limiting resistor** in series. A small over-voltage can cause the current to rise exponentially and destroy the device. The resistor value is calculated as: > $$R = \frac{V_S - V_F}{I_F}$$ > where $V_S$ is the supply voltage, $V_F$ is the LED forward voltage drop, and $I_F$ is the desired forward current. ## 2.6 Key LED Characteristics (Summary for KTU) | Characteristic | Description / Typical Values | |---|---| | **Forward Voltage ($V_F$)** | 1.2 V (Red GaAs) to 3.5 V (Blue/White GaN); current-dependent | | **Forward Current ($I_F$)** | 10 mA (indicator) to 350 mA–1 A (high-power); 20 mA is standard | | **Reverse Breakdown Voltage ($V_{BR}$)** | 5 V typical — very low, sensitive to reverse bias | | **Luminous Intensity ($I_V$)** | 1 mcd (low intensity) to 10,000+ mcd (high-bright) | | **Viewing Angle ($\theta$)** | 20° to 180° depending on lens geometry | | **Spectral Half-Width ($\Delta\lambda$)** | 20 nm to 50 nm — narrow spectrum = high color purity | | **Switching Speed** | **Very fast — 1 ns to 100 ns** — ideal for optical communication | | **Lifespan** | 50,000 to 100,000 hours (vs. 1,000 hours for incandescent) | | **Power Efficiency** | 50–100+ lm/W (modern white LEDs) vs. 15 lm/W (incandescent) | ## 2.7 Real-World Applications (Engineering Utility) - **Indicator lights** in electronic instruments, consumer electronics, and appliances - **Display technology**: 7-segment displays, dot-matrix displays, full-color screens (LED TVs, smartphones) - **Traffic signals** — long life and low power consumption - **Optical fiber communication** — fast switching, narrow spectral output - **Solid-state lighting** — replacing incandescent and fluorescent lamps - **Automotive headlights, taillights, and interior lighting** - **Medical devices**: phototherapy, pulse oximetry, surgical lighting - **Remote controls** (IR LEDs) and machine vision systems --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Numerical Problems ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Wavelength of Emitted Light from Bandgap **Problem Statement:** Derive the relationship between the wavelength of light emitted by an LED and the bandgap energy of its semiconductor material. ### Step-by-Step Derivation: **Step 1 — Energy Conservation:** When an electron in the conduction band recombines with a hole in the valence band, the energy released equals the bandgap energy $E_g$. For a direct bandgap transition, this energy is released as a single photon (no lattice phonons are involved to conserve momentum). $$ E_{\text{photon}} = E_g $$ **Step 2 — Apply Planck–Einstein Relation:** The energy of a photon of frequency $\nu$ and wavelength $\lambda$ is given by: $$ E_{\text{photon}} = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda} $$ **Step 3 — Equate the Two Expressions:** $$ \frac{hc}{\lambda} = E_g $$ **Step 4 — Solve for $\lambda$:** $$ \lambda = \frac{hc}{E_g} $$ **Step 5 — Substitute Numerical Constants:** Using $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, and $1 \text{ eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J, we convert $E_g$ from electron-volts to Joules: $$ \lambda = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^8)}{E_g \cdot (1.602 \times 10^{-19})} $$ $$ \lambda = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{E_g \cdot 1.602 \times 10^{-19}} $$ $$ \lambda = \frac{1.2408 \times 10^{-6}}{E_g} \text{ meters} $$ **Step 6 — Express in Micrometers for Practical Use:** $$ \boxed{\lambda(\mu m) = \frac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})}} $$ This is the **master equation** used in virtually every LED numerical problem at KTU. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Series Current-Limiting Resistor for LED **Problem Statement:** An LED with a forward voltage drop of 2.0 V and a maximum forward current of 20 mA is to be connected to a 9 V DC supply. Derive the value of the series current-limiting resistor. ### Step-by-Step Derivation: **Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) around the loop:** The supply voltage must equal the sum of voltage drops across the LED and the resistor. $$ V_S = V_F + V_R $$ **Step 2 — Solve for the Resistor Voltage:** $$ V_R = V_S - V_F $$ $$ V_R = 9 - 2.0 = 7.0 \text{ V} $$ **Step 3 — Apply Ohm's Law to the Resistor:** $$ V_R = I_F \cdot R \quad \Rightarrow \quad R = \frac{V_R}{I_F} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute Numerical Values:** $$ R = \frac{7.0 \text{ V}}{20 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}} = \frac{7.0}{0.020} $$ $$ \boxed{R = 350 \text{ }\Omega} $$ **Step 5 — Practical Engineering Note:** The standard preferred value closest to 350 Ω is **330 Ω** (gives $I_F \approx 21.2$ mA, safe) or **390 Ω** (gives $I_F \approx 17.9$ mA, more conservative). A small safety margin of 10% is conventionally built in to protect the LED from supply voltage fluctuations. --- ## 3.3 Solved Numerical Problem 1: Color Identification from Bandgap **Problem:** A GaAs LED has a bandgap energy of $E_g = 1.43$ eV. Determine the wavelength and color of the emitted light. **Step 1 — Use the wavelength-bandgap formula:** $$ \lambda = \frac{1.24}{E_g} \text{ μm} = \frac{1.24}{1.43} $$ $$ \boxed{\lambda \approx 0.867 \text{ μm} = 867 \text{ nm}} $$ **Step 2 — Identify the Color:** 867 nm lies in the **near-infrared (NIR)** region, just beyond visible red (700 nm). It is at the extreme red edge of human visibility and is used primarily in **IR remote controls** and **optical fiber communication**. --- ## 3.4 Solved Numerical Problem 2: Bandgap from Wavelength **Problem:** A green LED emits light at $\lambda = 555$ nm. Find the bandgap energy of the semiconductor. **Step 1 — Apply the inverse formula:** $$ E_g = \frac{1.24}{\lambda(\mu m)} = \frac{1.24}{0.555} $$ $$ \boxed{E_g \approx 2.234 \text{ eV}} $$ **Step 2 — Material Identification:** This bandgap (≈ 2.23 eV) corresponds to **GaP (Gallium Phosphide)** doped with nitrogen — the standard material for green LEDs. --- ## 3.5 Solved Numerical Problem 3: Power Dissipation **Problem:** A red LED operates at $V_F = 1.8$ V and $I_F = 15$ mA. Calculate the electrical power input and the radiated optical power if the external quantum efficiency is 4%. **Step 1 — Electrical Input Power:** $$ P_{\text{elec}} = V_F \cdot I_F = 1.8 \times 0.015 $$ $$ P_{\text{elec}} = 27 \text{ mW} $$ **Step 2 — Radiated Optical Power:** Using $\eta_{\text{ext}} = 4\% = 0.04$: $$ P_{\text{optical}} = \eta_{\text{ext}} \cdot P_{\text{elec}} = 0.04 \times 27 $$ $$ \boxed{P_{\text{optical}} = 1.08 \text{ mW}} $$ **Step 3 — Interpretation:** For every 100 electrons injected, only 4 photons escape. The remaining energy (96%) is lost as **heat** in the semiconductor lattice. This is why LEDs need **heat sinks** in high-power applications. --- ## 3.6 Solved Numerical Problem 4: Photon Energy in Joules **Problem:** A blue LED emits light at $\lambda = 470$ nm. Find the photon energy in (a) Joules and (b) electron-volts. **Part (a) — Energy in Joules:** $$ E = \frac{hc}{\lambda} = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{470 \times 10^{-9}} $$ $$ E = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{4.70 \times 10^{-7}} $$ $$ \boxed{E \approx 4.23 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}} $$ **Part (b) — Energy in Electron-Volts:** $$ E(\text{eV}) = \frac{4.23 \times 10^{-19}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 2.64 \text{ eV} $$ This matches the bandgap of **GaN** (Gallium Nitride, $E_g \approx 3.4$ eV at room temperature, though alloy composition can tune it lower). --- ## 3.7 Solved Numerical Problem 5: Threshold / Knee Voltage **Problem:** A particular LED has a bandgap of 2.1 eV. Estimate its knee voltage. **Step 1 — Apply the knee voltage approximation:** $$ V_F \approx \frac{E_g}{q} = \frac{E_g(\text{eV})}{1} $$ $$ \boxed{V_F \approx 2.1 \text{ V}} $$ This matches the observed knee voltage of **yellow / amber LEDs**. --- ## 3.8 Solved Numerical Problem 6: Multi-LED Display Design **Problem:** Design a 7-segment display circuit using LEDs. Each segment LED has $V_F = 1.8$ V and $I_F = 15$ mA. The supply is 5 V. Calculate the series resistor. **Step 1 — Voltage across the resistor:** $$ V_R = V_S - V_F = 5 - 1.8 = 3.2 \text{ V} $$ **Step 2 — Resistor value:** $$ R = \frac{V_R}{I_F} = \frac{3.2}{0.015} = 213.3 \text{ }\Omega $$ **Step 3 — Choose standard value:** Use $R = 220$ Ω (preferred value). Actual $I_F = 3.2/220 = 14.5$ mA — within safe range. --- ## 3.9 Key Equations at a Glance (Quick Revision Matrix) | Equation | Use | Notes | |---|---|---| | $\lambda \mu m = 1.24 / E_g(\text{eV})$ | Color ↔ Bandgap | **Most important** | | $E_g(\text{eV}) = 1240 / \lambda(\text{nm})$ | Bandgap ↔ Wavelength | Alternative form | | $R = (V_S - V_F)/I_F$ | Series resistor design | Always required | | $P_{\text{elec}} = V_F \cdot I_F$ | Input power | Watts | | $P_{\text{opt}} = \eta_{\text{ext}} \cdot P_{\text{elec}}$ | Light output power | Watts | | $V_F \approx E_g/q$ (in eV) | Knee voltage estimate | Approximation | | $\eta_{\text{ext}} = (\text{Photons out})/(\text{Electrons in})$ | Quantum efficiency | Percentage | --- # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Question 1:** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024, Model QP]` **What is electroluminescence? Explain how it is utilized in an LED.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Electroluminescence is the phenomenon of **emission of light from a semiconductor material when an electric field or current is passed through it**, caused by the radiative recombination of charge carriers (electrons and holes). In a **Light Emitting Diode (LED)**: 1. The p-n junction is **forward biased** with a voltage exceeding the knee voltage. 2. **Electrons from the n-side and holes from the p-side** are injected into the active junction region. 3. These charge carriers **recombine** across the bandgap, releasing energy in the form of **photons**. 4. The photon energy equals the bandgap: $E_{\text{photon}} = h\nu = E_g$. 5. Only **direct bandgap** materials (GaAs, GaP, GaN) emit efficient light — indirect bandgap materials (Si, Ge) primarily release energy as heat. Thus, electroluminescence is the fundamental operating principle that enables LEDs to convert electrical energy directly into light. --- ### **Question 2:** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Dec 2022]` **Why are direct bandgap semiconductors used for LED fabrication instead of silicon or germanium?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The choice of semiconductor material for an LED is dictated by the **band structure** of the material: 1. **Direct bandgap semiconductors** (GaAs, GaP, InGaN): The **minimum of the conduction band** and the **maximum of the valence band** occur at the **same crystal momentum (k-vector)**. This allows an electron to **recombine directly with a hole**, releasing energy as a **photon** without requiring lattice vibrations (phonons). This process is **radiative** and highly efficient. 2. **Indirect bandgap semiconductors** (Si, Ge): The conduction band minimum and valence band maximum occur at **different k-values**. For recombination to occur, a **phonon (lattice vibration)** must also be involved to conserve momentum. This makes the radiative recombination process extremely **inefficient** — most energy is released as **heat** rather than light. 3. Additionally, Si and Ge have small bandgaps (1.12 eV and 0.67 eV respectively), which would produce **infrared** light invisible to the human eye. Therefore, **direct bandgap III-V compound semiconductors** with appropriate bandgaps are used to fabricate LEDs that emit efficient, visible, colored light. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each) --- ### **Question A (Choice 1):** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]` #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **With the help of a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of an LED. Discuss why direct bandgap materials are preferred.** **Model Answer (7 marks):** **Construction (3.5 marks):** An LED consists of the following layers, built on a heavy n-type substrate: 1. **n-type layer (bottom):** Heavily doped with donor impurities (e.g., Te in GaAs). This layer is thick, provides mechanical support, and acts as a heat sink and electrical contact. 2. **p-type layer (top):** Heavily doped with acceptor impurities (e.g., Zn in GaP).
