This layer is intentionally kept **thin** to allow photons to escape with minimum re-absorption. 3. **Junction / Active region:** The thin depletion region where radiative recombination occurs. A direct bandgap material (GaAs, GaP, InGaN) is used. 4. **Reflective cup / anvil:** A parabolic metal reflector beneath the die that directs photons upward through the dome. 5. **Epoxy dome (lens):** A hemispherical transparent encapsulation. It serves three purposes: (a) **mechanical protection**, (b) **reduction of refractive index mismatch** (semiconductor has $n \approx 3.5$, epoxy has $n \approx 1.5$, air has $n = 1$) which increases the **escape cone** of photons, and (c) **focusing** the emitted light into a directional beam. 6. **Two metal leads:** The flat, larger anode lead also functions as a **heat sink**. The cathode lead (post) supports the die. **Working (2.5 marks):** 1. When **forward biased** ($V_F > V_K$), the depletion region narrows. 2. **Electrons from the n-region** are injected into the p-region (and holes from p into n). 3. These minority carriers **recombine** with majority carriers in and near the junction. 4. Because the material is a **direct bandgap** semiconductor, the recombination is **radiative** — the energy is released as a **photon**. 5. The photon wavelength is given by $\lambda = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})}$ μm. 6. The photons travel through the thin p-layer, through the epoxy dome, and emerge as a narrow, directional light beam. **Why direct bandgap? (1 mark):** Direct bandgap materials allow **momentum-conserving** recombination without phonon involvement, making the process highly efficient (>50% internal quantum efficiency possible). Indirect bandgap materials (Si, Ge) require phonon assistance, making radiative recombination extremely inefficient and the device useless for light emission. --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Draw and explain the V-I characteristics of an LED. Compare its characteristics with a standard silicon p-n junction diode.** **Model Answer (7 marks):** **V-I Characteristics Description (3 marks):** The V-I characteristic of an LED has two regions: **Forward Bias Region:** - At low forward voltages, current is negligible. - Once $V_F$ exceeds the **knee voltage $V_K$**, the current rises **steeply and almost linearly** with voltage. - $V_K$ depends on color: **1.2 V (Red) → 3.5 V (Blue/White)**. - The characteristic follows $I_F = I_S(e^{V_F/\eta V_T} - 1)$ with ideality factor $\eta \approx 1\text{–}2$. **Reverse Bias Region:** - A small reverse leakage current flows ($I_R \approx 1$ μA). - The **reverse breakdown voltage is very low** (≈ 5 V) — much less than silicon diodes (≈ 50–1000 V). - Beyond breakdown, the LED is permanently damaged. **Graph (using schematic representation):** | Region | Behavior | |---|---| | Reverse | I_R ≈ 0 until V_BR ≈ 5 V, then destructive breakdown | | Forward (0 → V_K) | Negligible current | | Forward (> V_K) | Sharp, near-linear rise in I_F | **Comparison with Silicon Diode (4 marks):** | Parameter | Silicon Diode | LED | |---|---|---| | **Knee Voltage $V_K$** | 0.7 V | 1.2 V to 3.5 V (color-dependent) | | **Reverse Breakdown** | 50 V to 1000 V | ~ 5 V (very low) | | **Output** | Heat (no light) | Light + heat | | **Material** | Indirect bandgap (Si) | Direct bandgap (GaAs, GaP, etc.) | | **Recombination** | Mostly non-radiative (phonon) | Radiative (photon) | | **Reverse Sensitivity** | Robust | Highly sensitive to reverse bias | | **Application** | Rectifier, regulator | Display, indicator, lighting | | **Forward Curve Slope** | Gradual exponential | Sharper rise beyond $V_K$ | **Conclusion:** The LED differs from a Si diode primarily in its **higher knee voltage** (due to larger bandgap), **low reverse breakdown voltage** (sensitivity to reverse bias), and the emission of **monochromatic light** through radiative recombination. --- ### **Question B (Choice 2):** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Paper]` #### **Part (a) — 7 Marks** **Derive the relationship between the wavelength of emitted light and the bandgap energy of an LED. A GaAs LED has $E_g = 1.43$ eV. Find the wavelength and identify the color.** **Model Answer (7 marks):** **Derivation (4 marks):** When an electron in the conduction band recombines with a hole in the valence band across a direct bandgap, the energy released is given by: $$ E_g = E_{\text{photon}} $$ Using the Planck–Einstein relation for the photon energy: $$ E_{\text{photon}} = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda} $$ Equating the two expressions: $$ \frac{hc}{\lambda} = E_g $$ Solving for $\lambda$: $$ \lambda = \frac{hc}{E_g} $$ Substituting $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, and converting $E_g$ from eV to Joules using $1 \text{ eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J: $$ \lambda = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^8)}{E_g(\text{eV}) \cdot (1.602 \times 10^{-19})} $$ $$ \lambda = \frac{1.2408 \times 10^{-6}}{E_g(\text{eV})} \text{ meters} $$ Converting to micrometers (1 μm = 10⁻⁶ m): $$ \boxed{\lambda(\mu m) = \frac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})}} $$ **Numerical Calculation (2 marks):** For GaAs, $E_g = 1.43$ eV: $$ \lambda = \frac{1.24}{1.43} = 0.867 \text{ μm} = 867 \text{ nm} $$ **Color Identification (1 mark):** A wavelength of **867 nm** lies in the **near-infrared (NIR)** region, just beyond the visible red edge (700 nm). It appears as a **deep red** at the limit of human vision. Such LEDs are extensively used in **IR remote controls, optical fiber communication, and night-vision equipment**. --- #### **Part (b) — 7 Marks** **Design a driving circuit for a red LED with $V_F = 1.8$ V and $I_F = 20$ mA, using a 9 V DC supply. Calculate the series resistance and power dissipation in the circuit. Explain why a current-limiting resistor is mandatory.** **Model Answer (7 marks):** **Circuit Design (3 marks):** A red LED requires a **series resistor** $R_S$ to limit the forward current to a safe value of 20 mA. The circuit consists of: - **9 V DC supply** in series with: - **Current-limiting resistor $R_S$** (connected to the anode side) - **LED** (cathode connected to ground) **Calculating the Series Resistor (2 marks):** Applying Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the loop: $$ V_S = V_R + V_F $$ The voltage across the resistor is: $$ V_R = V_S - V_F = 9 - 1.8 = 7.2 \text{ V} $$ Using Ohm's Law: $$ R_S = \frac{V_R}{I_F} = \frac{7.2}{20 \times 10^{-3}} = 360 \text{ }\Omega $$ **Practical choice:** Use the standard preferred value $R_S = 330$ Ω (giving $I_F \approx 21.8$ mA, still within safe limits) or $R_S = 390$ Ω (giving $I_F \approx 18.5$ mA, safer). **Power Dissipation (1 mark):** - Power dissipated in resistor: $P_R = V_R \cdot I_F = 7.2 \times 0.020 = 0.144$ W → use a **¼ W (0.25 W) resistor** (standard). - Power consumed by LED: $P_{\text{LED}} = V_F \cdot I_F = 1.8 \times 0.020 = 0.036$ W = 36 mW. - Total power: $P_{\text{total}} = 0.18$ W. **Why a Current-Limiting Resistor is Mandatory (1 mark):** The LED's V-I characteristic is **exponentially steep** beyond the knee voltage. Even a small increase in $V_F$ causes a large increase in $I_F$. Without a current-limiting resistor: - The current will rise to destructive levels within microseconds. - The LED's **thermal runaway** will permanently damage the p-n junction. - LEDs are **not** self-current-limiting like resistors or zener diodes — they MUST be externally limited. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting to include the series resistor:** The single most common mistake. Marks are lost if the LED is shown connected directly across the supply — the examiner will deduct for "unsafe, unworkable design." > > 2. **Wrong unit conversion in the wavelength formula:** Many students write $\lambda = hc/E_g$ but use $E_g$ in eV without converting to Joules, leading to an answer off by a factor of 10⁶. Always use the **simplified form** $\lambda(\mu m) = 1.24/E_g(\text{eV})$ to avoid errors. > > 3. **Confusing direct vs. indirect bandgap:** Students sometimes claim silicon can be used for LEDs because "all diodes work the same way." This is a **fundamental conceptual error** — silicon's indirect bandgap means radiative recombination is essentially zero, so no light is emitted. This alone can cost 2–3 marks. > > 4. **Omitting the color-wavelength-bandgap mapping table:** Examiners expect students to know that Red ≈ 1.9 eV, Green ≈ 2.25 eV, Blue ≈ 2.8 eV, etc. Memorize these values. > > 5. **Drawing the LED symbol without the emission arrows:** The LED symbol is identical to a regular diode but with **two small arrows pointing away** from the cathode. Forgetting these arrows in a diagram is a recurring mark-loss issue. > > 6. **Mixing up anode and cathode in the bias explanation:** "Forward biased" means **p-side positive, n-side negative**. Reversed terminology costs marks. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision — Memorize Before Exam** ### Core Concepts to Master - ✅ LED = forward-biased **direct bandgap** p-n junction - ✅ Operates on the principle of **electroluminescence** (radiative recombination) - ✅ The **hemispherical epoxy dome** reduces internal reflection and focuses the beam - ✅ The **p-layer is thin** to allow photon escape - ✅ LEDs are **highly sensitive to reverse bias** ($V_{BR} \approx 5$ V only) - ✅ **Series resistor is mandatory** in every LED circuit - ✅ Switching speed is very fast (1–100 ns) — enables optical communication - ✅ **Lifespan** of an LED (50,000+ hours) far exceeds incandescent (1,000 hours) ### Must-Memorize Formulas - ✅ $\lambda(\mu m) = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})}$ - ✅ $E_g(\text{eV}) = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda(\text{nm})}$ - ✅ $R_S = \dfrac{V_S - V_F}{I_F}$ - ✅ $P_{\text{elec}} = V_F \cdot I_F$ ### Color-Bandgap-Wavelength Mapping (Critical!) - ✅ **Infrared (IR)** → GaAs → 1.43 eV → 870 nm - ✅ **Red** → GaAsP → 1.9 eV → 635 nm - ✅ **Orange/Yellow** → GaP:N → 2.1 eV → 590 nm - ✅ **Green** → GaP:N → 2.25 eV → 555 nm - ✅ **Blue** → SiC/GaN → 2.8–3.0 eV → 470 nm - ✅ **White** → Blue LED + Yellow Phosphor (YAG:Ce) ### Direct vs. Indirect Bandgap (Conceptual Mastery) - ✅ **Direct bandgap:** CB minimum and VB maximum at same k → efficient photon emission - ✅ **Indirect bandgap (Si, Ge):** Different k values → phonon needed → non-radiative (heat) - ✅ **Consequence:** LEDs are made ONLY from III-V compounds, never from Si or Ge ### V-I Characteristic Key Points - ✅ Knee voltage $V_K$ is **color-dependent** (higher for blue, lower for red) - ✅ Forward current must be **externally limited** by a resistor - ✅ Reverse leakage is very small - ✅ Reverse breakdown occurs at a **low voltage** (~5 V) and is destructive ### Engineering & Application Facts - ✅ LED forward current: typically **20 mA** for standard indicators - ✅ LED forward voltage: **1.2 V (Red) to 3.5 V (Blue)** - ✅ Standard series resistor formula: $R = (V_S - V_F)/I_F$ - ✅ Power dissipated: $P = V_F \cdot I_F$ - ✅ External quantum efficiency: $\eta_{\text{ext}} = \text{photons out} / \text{electrons in}$ ### Common Exam Phrasings (Practice These!) - *"Why direct bandgap materials are used for LEDs"* → Radiative recombination efficiency - *"Explain the role of the epoxy dome"* → Refractive index matching, lens, mechanical protection - *"Derive $\lambda = 1.24/E_g$"* → Planck–Einstein relation with unit conversion - *"Why is a series resistor necessary?"* → Prevent thermal runaway and destruction > **End of Module 2 LED Notes — All the best for your KTU examinations!** ---

topics.title: Photodiode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES & CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Photodiode

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Photodiode** is a specialized semiconductor **p-n junction diode** that is engineered to operate in **reverse bias** and convert incident optical (light) energy into measurable electrical current through the **internal photoelectric effect**. When photons of energy greater than or equal to the semiconductor bandgap energy strike the depletion region, they generate **electron-hole pairs (EHPs)** that are swept apart by the built-in (or externally applied) electric field, producing a photocurrent proportional to the incident light intensity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCCIT302 - Module 2):**
> A photodiode is fundamentally a **reverse-biased p-n junction** used as a light-to-current transducer. The active parameter is the **photocurrent ($I_{ph}$)** flowing in the external circuit due to photon absorption.

### Structural Overview
- The device consists of a **p-n junction** with a **transparent window** at the top to allow incident photons to penetrate the semiconductor.
- The **p-region is kept thin** (typically on the order of the absorption length, ~1 µm) so that maximum light reaches the depletion region or the lightly doped side.
- An **anti-reflection coating** is deposited on the surface to minimize reflection losses at the air-semiconductor interface.
- Two ohmic contacts are provided: one on the p-side (anode) and one on the n-side (cathode).

### Standard Symbol of a Photodiode

> **Standard Notation used in KTU exam scripts:**
> - The cathode side has **two inward-pointing arrows** representing the incident light beam.
> - The anode is connected to the lower potential in **photoconductive mode** (reverse-biased configuration).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Analogy — The "Light Bucket" Model**
> Think of a photodiode as a **smart rain bucket** that counts how many raindrops fall into it:
> - **Raindrops → Photons** of light falling on the device.
> - **The bucket's narrow neck → The p-region** (only lets light in, doesn't let charges escape easily).
> - **The water flowing out the bottom → The photocurrent** in the external circuit.
> - **A heavier rainstorm → Brighter light**, which simply means *more drops per second*, and therefore *more current*.
> - **A reverse-bias battery (like a pump pushing water backwards)** → Creates an electric field that rapidly sweeps the generated electrons and holes out before they can recombine, ensuring a fast and faithful response.
>
> **Takeaway:** More light = More current. Darkness = Essentially no current (only a tiny dark current). The photodiode doesn't *amplify* the light signal — it faithfully *translates* it into an electrical signal.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are essential for KTU 2024 numerical problems on photodiodes:

- **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J·s}$
- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$
- **Electronic charge:** $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$
- **Boltzmann constant:** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$
- **Thermal voltage at 300 K:** $V_T = kT/q \approx 25.85\ \text{mV}$
- **Standard incident optical power reference:** $P_{in} = 1\ \text{mW}$ (when computing responsivity in A/W)
- **Typical silicon bandgap energy:** $E_g \approx 1.12\ \text{eV}$

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Exams:** 
> *"h and c travel together in light equations."* 
> Whenever you see $\lambda$ (wavelength) and $f$ (frequency), think **$c = f\lambda$** and **$E = hf = hc/\lambda$**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The operation of a photodiode can be understood as a chain of five well-defined physical events:

1. **Photon Incidence:** Light of suitable wavelength strikes the photodiode through the transparent window.
2. **Photon Absorption:** If the photon energy $E_{ph} = hf \geq E_g$ (bandgap), it is absorbed by a valence electron.
3. **Electron-Hole Pair (EHP) Generation:** The valence electron gains enough energy to jump into the conduction band, leaving behind a hole — an **EHP is created**.
4. **Charge Separation by Field:** The depletion-region electric field (either built-in or enhanced by reverse bias) sweeps the electron toward the **n-side** and the hole toward the **p-side**.
5. **External Photocurrent:** This directional movement of charge constitutes a **photocurrent $I_{ph}$** in the external circuit, which is the output signal.

### The Condition for Photon Absorption

The photon must have sufficient energy to overcome the bandgap. This gives the **cutoff wavelength**:

$$
\lambda_c = \frac{hc}{E_g}
$$

If $\lambda > \lambda_c$, the photon passes through unabsorbed. If $\lambda \leq \lambda_c$, the photon is absorbed and an EHP is generated.

---

## 2.2 Two Modes of Operation (HIGH-YIELD for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Distinction:** Photodiodes can be operated in two distinct electrical configurations. Examiners regularly ask you to compare them.

| Aspect | Photovoltaic Mode | Photoconductive Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Bias Condition | **Zero bias** ($V = 0$) | **Reverse bias** ($V < 0$) |
| Current Flow | Photocurrent flows in forward direction of diode | Photocurrent flows in reverse direction |
| Speed of Response | **Slow** (junction capacitance high) | **Fast** (depletion width larger, capacitance lower) |
| Noise Level | **Low** (no dark current from external bias) | **Higher** (shot noise + dark current) |
| Linearity | **Less linear** at low light | **Highly linear** over wide range |
| Sensitivity | Lower | Higher |
| Use Case | Solar cells, light meters | Optical communication, high-speed detectors |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** *"PV mode is slow, PC mode is fast."* — In **Photo-Conductive (PC)** mode, the reverse bias **increases the depletion width**, which **reduces junction capacitance** and **increases bandwidth**.

---

## 2.3 Current Equation of a Photodiode

The total current through a photodiode is the **algebraic sum of the dark (diode) current and the photocurrent**:

$$
I_{total} = I_{ph} - I_{dark}\left[\exp\left(\frac{qV}{kT}\right) - 1\right]
$$

Where:
- $I_{ph}$ = **Photocurrent** (proportional to incident optical power, flows in reverse direction)
- $I_{dark}$ = **Dark saturation current** (reverse saturation current of the diode)
- $V$ = **Applied forward bias voltage** across the diode terminals
- The term in brackets is the standard **Shockley diode equation factor**

> [!NOTE]
> In **photoconductive (reverse-biased)** mode, $V$ is negative and large in magnitude, so $\exp(qV/kT) \to 0$, and the equation simplifies to:
>
> $$I_{total} \approx I_{ph} + I_{dark}$$
>
> This shows that the photodiode acts as a **constant current source** (independent of bias) — a very useful property in linear detection circuits.

---

## 2.4 Key Performance Metrics (DEFINITION BOX)

- **Responsivity ($R_\lambda$):** The ratio of output photocurrent to incident optical power. **Unit: A/W**.
- **Quantum Efficiency ($\eta$):** The probability that an incident photon contributes a collected electron. **Unit: dimensionless (often expressed as %).**
- **Dark Current ($I_{dark}$):** The small reverse current that flows even with **no light** incident. **Unit: A or nA.**
- **Response Time / Cut-off Frequency:** The frequency at which the photodiode's responsivity drops to $1/\sqrt{2}$ of its DC value.
- **Noise Equivalent Power (NEP):** The minimum optical power detectable above the noise floor. **Unit: W/√Hz.**

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains EVERY formula that may be required for KTU 2024 ESE questions on Photodiodes.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Photon Energy | $E_{ph} = hf = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | eV or J | $h$ in J·s, $c$ in m/s, $\lambda$ in m |
| 2 | Cutoff Wavelength | $\lambda_c = \dfrac{hc}{E_g}$ | m or nm | Defines long-wavelength sensitivity limit |
| 3 | Total Photodiode Current | $I = I_{ph} - I_{dark}\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{kT}\right) - 1\right]$ | A | Universal equation |
| 4 | Reverse-Biased (PC) Mode Current | $I \approx I_{ph} + I_{dark}$ | A | $V$ large & negative, $V_T = kT/q$ |
| 5 | Photocurrent from Optical Power | $I_{ph} = R_\lambda \cdot P_{opt}$ | A | Direct proportionality |
| 6 | Responsivity | $R_\lambda = \dfrac{I_{ph}}{P_{opt}} = \dfrac{\eta q \lambda}{hc}$ | A/W | KTU favourite formula |
| 7 | Quantum Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{electrons collected/sec}}{\text{photons incident/sec}} = \dfrac{R_\lambda \cdot hc}{q\lambda}$ | — (or %) | Always $\leq 1$ for conventional photodiodes |
| 8 | Bandwidth-Time Constant | $f_{3dB} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{L} C_{j}}$ | Hz | $R_L$ = load resistance, $C_j$ = junction capacitance |
| 9 | Thermal Voltage (300 K) | $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx 25.85\ \text{mV}$ | V | Used in diode equation |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** In formula tables, never confuse $R_\lambda$ (responsivity in A/W) with $R_L$ (load resistance in ohms). The subscript $\lambda$ is reserved for optical/responsivity quantities.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Optical Fiber Communication:** PIN photodiodes and Avalanche Photodiodes (APDs) at the receiver end of long-distance telecom links.
- **Smoke Detectors & Security Systems:** Infrared detection circuits.
- **Barcode Scanners and Remote Controls:** Receivers for modulated IR signals.
- **Solar Cells (large-area version):** Photovoltaic energy conversion.
- **Medical Imaging (CT Scanners):** X-ray sensitive photodiodes.
- **Camera Sensors (CCD/CMOS):** Each pixel is essentially a tiny photodiode.
- **LIDAR and Range Finding:** Pulsed laser light detection for autonomous vehicles.
- **Optical Encoders & Position Sensors:** For robotics and CNC machines.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** Derivations of **Responsivity from Quantum Efficiency** and the **Total Current Equation** are high-yield topics in KTU 2024 ESE. Master the steps below thoroughly.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Relationship between Responsivity and Quantum Efficiency

### Statement to Prove:

$$
R_\lambda = \frac{\eta \cdot q \lambda}{hc}
$$

### Step 1: Define Quantum Efficiency

Quantum efficiency is the ratio of the number of charge carriers collected by the photodiode to the number of incident photons. Mathematically:

$$
\eta = \frac{\text{Number of electrons collected per second}}{\text{Number of incident photons per second}}
$$

### Step 2: Express the Number of Photons per Second

If $P_{opt}$ is the incident optical power (in Watts = J/s) and $E_{ph}$ is the energy of one photon, then:

$$
\text{Photons per second} = \frac{P_{opt}}{E_{ph}}
$$

### Step 3: Express the Number of Electrons per Second

The photocurrent $I_{ph}$ represents $I_{ph}/q$ electrons flowing per second (since each electron carries charge $q$):

$$
\text{Electrons collected per second} = \frac{I_{ph}}{q}
$$

### Step 4: Substitute into the Quantum Efficiency Definition

$$
\eta = \frac{I_{ph}/q}{P_{opt}/E_{ph}} = \frac{I_{ph} \cdot E_{ph}}{q \cdot P_{opt}}
$$

### Step 5: Use the Photon Energy Relation

We know that $E_{ph} = hc/\lambda$. Substituting:

$$
\eta = \frac{I_{ph}}{q \cdot P_{opt}} \cdot \frac{hc}{\lambda}
$$

### Step 6: Rearrange to Obtain Responsivity

By definition, responsivity is $R_\lambda = I_{ph}/P_{opt}$. Solving for $I_{ph}/P_{opt}$:

$$
\frac{I_{ph}}{P_{opt}} = \frac{\eta \cdot q \cdot \lambda}{hc}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{R_\lambda = \frac{\eta \cdot q \lambda}{hc}}
$$

### Step 7: Verification of Units

$$
\frac{[\eta] \cdot [C] \cdot [m]}{[J \cdot s] \cdot [m/s]} = \frac{1 \cdot C \cdot m}{J \cdot m} = \frac{C}{J} = \frac{C}{W \cdot s} = \frac{A}{W}
$$

Units check: ✓ **A/W**, which is correct for responsivity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cutoff Wavelength from Bandgap

### Statement to Prove:

$$
\lambda_c = \frac{hc}{E_g}
$$

### Step 1: Condition for EHP Generation

For a photon to generate an electron-hole pair, its energy must be at least equal to the bandgap energy $E_g$:

$$
E_{ph} \geq E_g
$$

### Step 2: Use Photon Energy Formula

$$
\frac{hc}{\lambda} \geq E_g
$$

### Step 3: Solve for $\lambda$

Rearranging the inequality:

$$
\lambda \leq \frac{hc}{E_g}
$$

The **maximum wavelength** at which absorption is still possible is the **cutoff wavelength**:

$$
\boxed{\lambda_c = \frac{hc}{E_g}}
$$

### Step 4: Numerical Verification (for Si)

For silicon, $E_g = 1.12\ \text{eV} = 1.12 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J} = 1.794 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{J}$.

$$
\lambda_c = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^{8})}{1.794 \times 10^{-19}}
$$

$$
\lambda_c = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{1.794 \times 10^{-19}} = 1.108 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m} \approx 1108\ \text{nm}
$$

This corresponds to the near-infrared region. Silicon photodiodes are therefore useful for visible light and near-IR detection, but insensitive to longer IR wavelengths.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Photodiode Total Current Equation

### Statement to Prove:

$$
I = I_{ph} - I_{dark}\left[\exp\left(\frac{qV}{kT}\right) - 1\right]
$$

### Step 1: Start with the Standard Diode Equation (No Light)

In the absence of any incident light, the photodiode behaves as an ordinary p-n junction diode:

$$
I_{diode} = I_{dark}\left[\exp\left(\frac{qV}{kT}\right) - 1\right]
$$

This current flows from the **p-side to the n-side** through the device when $V$ is forward-biased (positive).

### Step 2: Consider the Effect of Incident Light

When light is incident, the photogenerated current $I_{ph}$ flows in the **opposite direction** — from the **n-side to the p-side** internally, which corresponds to **reverse direction** in the standard diode convention.

### Step 3: Apply Superposition

Since the photodiode is a linear device (in first-order approximation) at moderate light levels, we can superpose the two current contributions. The current due to light is a **negative contribution** to the standard diode current:

$$
I = I_{diode} - I_{ph}
$$

### Step 4: Substitute the Diode Equation

$$
I = I_{dark}\left[\exp\left(\frac{qV}{kT}\right) - 1\right] - I_{ph}
$$

### Step 5: Rearrange to KTU Standard Form

$$
\boxed{I = I_{ph} - I_{dark}\left[\exp\left(\frac{qV}{kT}\right) - 1\right]}
$$

### Step 6: Special Case — Photoconductive Mode

In reverse bias, $V < 0$ and $\vert qV/kT \vert \gg 1$, so $\exp(qV/kT) \to 0$:

$$
I \approx I_{ph} - I_{dark}(-1) = I_{ph} + I_{dark}
$$

This shows that in photoconductive mode, the total reverse current is the **sum** of the photocurrent and the dark saturation current — a small, stable offset that can be calibrated out.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (Typical KTU Numerical)

> [!TIP]
> **Problem (KTU Pattern):**
> A silicon photodiode has a quantum efficiency of **65%** when illuminated by light of wavelength **900 nm**. Calculate:
> (a) The responsivity of the photodiode.
> (b) The photocurrent if the incident optical power is **50 µW**.

### Given Data

- $\eta = 0.65$
- $\lambda = 900\ \text{nm} = 900 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}$
- $P_{opt} = 50\ \mu\text{W} = 50 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{W}$
- $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J·s}$
- $c = 3 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$
- $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$

### Part (a): Calculate Responsivity

Using the derived formula:

$$
R_\lambda = \frac{\eta \cdot q \cdot \lambda}{h c}
$$

Step 1 — Compute the numerator:

$$
\eta \cdot q \cdot \lambda = 0.65 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 900 \times 10^{-9}
$$

$$
= 0.65 \times 1.602 \times 900 \times 10^{-28}
$$

$$
= 0.65 \times 1441.8 \times 10^{-28}
$$

$$
= 937.17 \times 10^{-28} = 9.3717 \times 10^{-26}\ \text{C·m}
$$

Step 2 — Compute the denominator:

$$
h c = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8} = 19.878 \times 10^{-26}\ \text{J·m}
$$

Step 3 — Divide:

$$
R_\lambda = \frac{9.3717 \times 10^{-26}}{19.878 \times 10^{-26}} = 0.4714\ \text{A/W}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_\lambda \approx 0.471\ \text{A/W}}
$$

### Part (b): Calculate the Photocurrent

$$
I_{ph} = R_\lambda \cdot P_{opt}
$$

$$
I_{ph} = 0.4714\ \text{A/W} \times 50 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{W}
$$

$$
I_{ph} = 23.57 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{A}
$$

$$
\boxed{I_{ph} \approx 23.57\ \mu\text{A}}
$$

### Verification via Direct Approach

Number of incident photons/sec:

$$
N_{ph} = \frac{P_{opt}}{hc/\lambda} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-6} \times 900 \times 10^{-9}}{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}
$$

$$
= \frac{45 \times 10^{-15}}{19.878 \times 10^{-26}} = 2.264 \times 10^{11}\ \text{photons/s}
$$

Electrons collected per second:

$$
N_e = \eta \cdot N_{ph} = 0.65 \times 2.264 \times 10^{11} = 1.472 \times 10^{11}\ \text{electrons/s}
$$

Photocurrent:

$$
I_{ph} = N_e \cdot q = 1.472 \times 10^{11} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} = 23.58\ \mu\text{A}
$$

This matches Part (b) — ✓ **Calculation verified.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Notice:** All diagrams below use strictly alphanumeric node IDs (e.g., `n1`, `p1`, `depl`) to avoid reserved-keyword conflicts. All node labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text (no bold, italics, or HTML).

---

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Photodiode

This diagram shows the complete signal flow from incident photons to the output electrical signal.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["INCIDENT PHOTON FLUX"] --> B1["ANTI REFLECTION COATING"]
    B1 --> C1["P REGION THIN LAYER"]
    C1 --> D1["DEPLETION REGION"]
    D1 --> E1["N REGION SUBSTRATE"]
    E1 --> F1["OHMIC CONTACT N SIDE"]
    C1 --> G1["OHMIC CONTACT P SIDE"]
    D1 --> H1["EHP GENERATION"]
    H1 --> I1["ELECTRIC FIELD SEPARATION"]
    I1 --> J1["ELECTRONS TO N SIDE"]
    I1 --> K1["HOLES TO P SIDE"]
    J1 --> L1["EXTERNAL CIRCUIT"]
    K1 --> L1
    L1 --> M1["PHOTOCURRENT OUTPUT IPH"]
    M1 --> N1["LOAD RESISTOR RL"]
    N1 --> O1["VOLTAGE OR CURRENT AMPLIFIER"]
    O1 --> P1["SIGNAL PROCESSING UNIT"]
```

---

## 4.2 Energy Band Diagram of a Photodiode Under Reverse Bias

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["P SIDE"] --> S2["CONDUCTION BAND EC"]
    S2 --> S3["DEPLETION REGION TILTED BANDS"]
    S3 --> S4["VALENCE BAND EV"]
    S4 --> S5["N SIDE"]
    S6["INCIDENT PHOTON HF EG"] --> S3
    S3 --> S7["ELECTRON EXCITED TO EC"]
    S3 --> S8["HOLE LEFT IN EV"]
    S7 --> S5
    S8 --> S1
    S9["REVERSE BIAS VR APPLIED"] -.-> S3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The reverse bias tilts the bands, creating a strong built-in + external electric field. Photogenerated electrons roll *down* the tilted conduction band to the n-side; photogenerated holes bubble *up* the tilted valence band to the p-side. This directional charge movement is the photocurrent.

---

## 4.3 Photodiode Operating Modes Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1["PHOTODIODE APPLICATION"] --> M2{"BIAS APPLIED?"}
    M2 -- "NO BIAS V 0" --> M3["PHOTOVOLTAIC MODE PV"]
    M2 -- "REVERSE BIAS V LESS THAN 0" --> M4["PHOTOCONDUCTIVE MODE PC"]
    M3 --> M5["SELF GENERATED EMF"]
    M3 --> M6["USED IN SOLAR CELLS"]
    M3 --> M7["ZERO DARK CURRENT"]
    M3 --> M8["SLOW RESPONSE"]
    M4 --> M9["WIDE DEPLETION WIDTH"]
    M4 --> M10["LOW JUNCTION CAPACITANCE"]
    M4 --> M11["FAST RESPONSE"]
    M4 --> M12["USED IN OPTICAL COMMUNICATION"]
```

---

## 4.4 I-V Characteristic Curve of a Photodiode (Schematic Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["FOURTH QUADRANT I V PLANE"] --> B["REVERSE BIAS REGION"]
    A --> C["FORWARD BIAS REGION"]
    B --> D["LINEAR CURVES FOR DIFFERENT LIGHT INTENSITIES"]
    D --> D1["CURVE P0 ZERO LIGHT DARK CURRENT ONLY"]
    D --> D2["CURVE P1 LOW LIGHT IPH1"]
    D --> D3["CURVE P2 MEDIUM LIGHT IPH2"]
    D --> D4["CURVE P3 HIGH LIGHT IPH3"]
    C --> E["STANDARD DIODE EXPONENTIAL CURVE"]
    D1 --> F["INTERCEPT ON VERTICAL AXIS IDARK NEGATIVE"]
    D2 --> F2["INTERCEPT IPH1 NEGATIVE"]
    D3 --> F3["INTERCEPT IPH2 NEGATIVE"]
    D4 --> F4["INTERCEPT IPH3 NEGATIVE"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read the I-V curves:** Each line is essentially a **vertical shift** of the dark current curve. The magnitude of the shift (intercept on the negative current axis) is exactly the **photocurrent $I_{ph}$** corresponding to that light intensity. Higher light → Larger downward shift → More negative reverse current.

---

## 4.5 Photodiode Equivalent Circuit (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1["INCIDENT LIGHT"] --> L2["CURRENT SOURCE IPH"]
    L2 -.-> L3["PARALLEL DIODE D"]
    L2 -.-> L4["PARALLEL JUNCTION CAPACITANCE CJ"]
    L2 --> L5["PARALLEL SHUNT RESISTANCE RSH"]
    L2 --> L6["SERIES RESISTANCE RS"]
    L6 --> L7["OUTPUT TERMINALS"]
    L3 --> L7
    L4 --> L7
    L5 --> L7
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The series resistance $R_s$ is ideally **zero** (good ohmic contacts), and the shunt resistance $R_{sh}$ is ideally **infinite** (no leakage). Deviation from these ideals degrades photodiode performance.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **All questions below are modeled strictly on KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Evaluation) patterns. Mark distribution: Part A (3 marks), Part B (14 marks, with internal choice between Q-A and Q-B).**

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]
**Define a photodiode. Mention any two advantages of operating a photodiode in photoconductive mode.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):
A photodiode is a **reverse-biased p-n junction diode** that converts incident light energy into electrical current using the **internal photoelectric effect**. When photons of energy $\geq E_g$ strike the depletion region, electron-hole pairs are generated and separated by the electric field, producing a photocurrent.

**Two advantages of photoconductive (reverse-biased) mode:** *(1 mark each)*
1. **Faster response time** — The reverse bias widens the depletion region, reducing junction capacitance and increasing the bandwidth.
2. **Better linearity** — The photocurrent becomes essentially independent of the bias voltage, giving a more linear relationship with incident optical power.

---

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]
**Define the term "Responsivity" of a photodiode. State its SI unit.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):
**Responsivity ($R_\lambda$)** of a photodiode is defined as the **ratio of the output photocurrent ($I_{ph}$) to the incident optical power ($P_{opt}$)** at a given wavelength.

$$
R_\lambda = \frac{I_{ph}}{P_{opt}}
$$

- **SI Unit:** Ampere per Watt (**A/W**).
- It is a measure of the **sensitivity** of the photodiode — higher responsivity means more current is produced for the same incident light power.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A (Choice 1): [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

#### Q-A(a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a p-n junction photodiode. Discuss the role of the depletion region in the photoelectric conversion process.**

#### Model Answer:

**Construction (3 marks):**
A photodiode consists of a **p-n junction** formed in a semiconductor (typically Si, Ge, or InGaAs). The **p-region is made very thin** (~1 µm) on top to allow maximum light penetration. A **transparent window** and an **anti-reflection coating** are provided on the top surface to minimize reflection losses. **Ohmic contacts** are made on both p-side (anode) and n-side (cathode). The device is encapsulated in a metal or plastic package with a glass lens for focused light collection.

**Working Principle (3 marks):**
1. Light of energy $hf \geq E_g$ enters through the transparent window and reaches the depletion region.
2. Each absorbed photon generates **one electron-hole pair (EHP)** in the depletion region.
3. The **built-in electric field** (enhanced by external reverse bias) sweeps the electron toward the n-side and the hole toward the p-side.
4. This directional flow of charge constitutes a **photocurrent $I_{ph}$** in the external circuit, which is proportional to the incident light intensity.

**Role of the Depletion Region (1 mark):**
The depletion region is the **active region** where photon absorption and charge separation occur simultaneously. Its width is critical:
- **Wider depletion region** → Higher probability of photon absorption (better quantum efficiency) and lower junction capacitance (faster response).
- Reverse biasing the photodiode **widens the depletion region**, making it more efficient.

**Diagram (to be drawn in exam):** Standard p-n junction with light arrows entering from the p-side, depletion region marked, and external circuit with reverse-bias battery.

---

#### Q-A(b) — 7 Marks
**Derive the relationship between responsivity and quantum efficiency of a photodiode. A silicon photodiode has a quantum efficiency of 70% at a wavelength of 800 nm. Calculate its responsivity.**

#### Model Answer:

**Derivation (5 marks):** *(See SECTION 3, Derivation 1 for the full step-by-step proof.)*

The final relationship is:

$$
R_\lambda = \frac{\eta \cdot q \cdot \lambda}{hc}
$$

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

**Given:** $\eta = 0.70$, $\lambda = 800\ \text{nm} = 800 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}$

$$
R_\lambda = \frac{0.70 \times (1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \times (800 \times 10^{-9})}{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}
$$

**Numerator:**

$$
0.70 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 800 \times 10^{-9} = 0.70 \times 1.2816 \times 10^{-25} = 8.971 \times 10^{-26}
$$

**Denominator:**

$$
6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8} = 19.878 \times 10^{-26}
$$

**Division:**

$$
R_\lambda = \frac{8.971 \times 10^{-26}}{19.878 \times 10^{-26}} \approx 0.4513\ \text{A/W}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_\lambda \approx 0.451\ \text{A/W}}
$$

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2): [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

#### Q-B(a) — 7 Marks
**Compare photovoltaic mode and photoconductive mode of photodiode operation. Which mode is preferred for high-speed applications and why?**

#### Model Answer:

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Parameter | Photovoltaic Mode | Photoconductive Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Bias | Zero bias (unbiased) | Reverse biased |
| Current flow | Forward direction of diode | Reverse direction |
| Response speed | Slow | **Fast** |
| Junction capacitance | Higher | Lower (wider depletion width) |
| Linearity | Poor at low light | **Excellent** over wide range |
| Noise | **Lowest** (no dark current contribution) | Higher (due to dark current) |
| Output | Voltage (open circuit) or current (short circuit) | Current |
| Typical use | Solar cells, light meters | **Optical communication, high-speed detectors** |

**Preferred Mode for High-Speed Applications (2 marks):**

The **photoconductive (reverse-biased) mode** is preferred for high-speed applications because:

1. **Reduced junction capacitance:** The reverse bias widens the depletion region, decreasing the junction capacitance. Since $f_{3dB} = 1/(2\pi R_L C_j)$, a smaller $C_j$ directly translates to a **higher bandwidth**.
2. **Faster carrier collection:** The strong reverse-bias electric field sweeps carriers out of the depletion region very rapidly, minimizing transit time delays.

This makes the photoconductive mode ideal for **optical fiber receivers, LIDAR, and high-frequency modulated light detection**.

---

#### Q-B(b) — 7 Marks
**The total current through a photodiode is given by the equation $I = I_{ph} - I_{dark}\left[\exp(qV/kT) - 1\right]$. Explain each term. If the photodiode is reverse-biased with $V = -5$ V, the dark current is $I_{dark} = 10$ nA, and the photocurrent is $I_{ph} = 5$ µA, calculate the total current through the device. Assume $kT/q = 25.85$ mV.**

#### Model Answer:

**Explanation of Terms (3 marks):**
- $I$ = Total current flowing through the photodiode (output current)
- $I_{ph}$ = **Photocurrent** generated due to incident light; flows in the **reverse direction**
- $I_{dark}$ = **Dark saturation current** — small reverse current that flows even with no light; arises from thermally generated EHPs
- $V$ = Voltage applied across the diode (forward bias positive, reverse bias negative)
- $kT/q$ = Thermal voltage at the operating temperature (~25.85 mV at 300 K)

**Numerical Calculation (4 marks):**

**Given:** $V = -5$ V, $I_{dark} = 10$ nA $= 10 \times 10^{-9}$ A, $I_{ph} = 5$ µA $= 5 \times 10^{-6}$ A, $V_T = 25.85$ mV $= 0.02585$ V

**Step 1: Compute the exponent**

$$
\frac{qV}{kT} = \frac{V}{V_T} = \frac{-5}{0.02585} = -193.42
$$

**Step 2: Evaluate the exponential**

$$
\exp(-193.42) \approx 0 \quad (\text{extremely small, far below } 10^{-80})
$$

**Step 3: Substitute into the current equation**

$$
I = I_{ph} - I_{dark}\left[\exp\left(\frac{qV}{kT}\right) - 1\right]
$$

$$
I = 5 \times 10^{-6} - (10 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot [0 - 1]
$$

$$
I = 5 \times 10^{-6} - (10 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot (-1)
$$

$$
I = 5 \times 10^{-6} + 10 \times 10^{-9}
$$

$$
I = 5000 \times 10^{-9} + 10 \times 10^{-9} = 5010 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{A}
$$

$$
\boxed{I \approx 5.01\ \mu\text{A}}
$$

**Conclusion:** The total current is essentially $I_{ph} + I_{dark}$, confirming that in strong reverse bias, the photodiode acts as a constant current source with the photocurrent as the dominant term and the dark current as a small offset.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Forgetting to convert nm to m in the responsivity formula. The value $hc = 19.878 \times 10^{-26}$ J·m is obtained **only** if $\lambda$ is in **meters**, not nanometers. Many students erroneously put $\lambda$ in nm and get a wildly incorrect answer.
>
> 2. **Confusing $I_{ph}$ and $I_{dark}$:** The photocurrent is generated by light; the dark current flows even without light. In reverse bias, both flow in the **same direction** (reverse). The total current is $I_{ph} + I_{dark}$, NOT $I_{ph} - I_{dark}$.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Constants of Planck ($h$) and Speed of Light ($c$):** Some students use only $h$ or only $c$ in photon energy calculations. Always remember: $E = hf = hc/\lambda$. The full product $hc = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8$ is required when $\lambda$ is given.
>
> 4. **Quantum Efficiency > 1 in Conventional Photodiodes is Impossible:** The answer for $R_\lambda$ must satisfy $R_\lambda \leq q\lambda/(hc)$ (i.e., $\eta \leq 1$). If a student gets a value higher than this, a sign or factor error has occurred.
>
> 5. **Wrong Current Direction in I-V Plot:** When drawing the I-V characteristic of a photodiode, photocurrent $I_{ph}$ appears as a **negative** intercept on the current axis (since reverse current is conventionally negative). Do not draw it on the positive side.
>
> 6. **Mixing up Photodiode and Phototransistor:** A phototransistor provides **current gain** ($\beta$ times base photocurrent), while a photodiode has **no internal gain**. This distinction is a frequent 1-mark trap in KTU MCQs.
>
> 7. **Diode Symbol Direction:** In the photodiode symbol, the two arrows **point INWARD** (toward the device), indicating incident light, NOT outward. Some students draw them outward, which represents an LED.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Photodiode (KTU Module 2)**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** A reverse-biased p-n junction that converts light into current via the **internal photoelectric effect**.
>
> ✅ **Core Mechanism:** Photon absorption → EHP generation in the depletion region → Electric field separates charges → Photocurrent $I_{ph}$ flows in reverse direction.
>
> ✅ **Universal Current Equation:** $I = I_{ph} - I_{dark}\left[\exp(qV/kT) - 1\right]$
>
> ✅ **Reverse-Bias Simplification:** $I \approx I_{ph} + I_{dark}$ (acts as a constant current source).
>
> ✅ **Cutoff Wavelength:** $\lambda_c = hc/E_g$ — beyond this, the photodiode is insensitive.
>
> ✅ **Responsivity Formula:** $R_\lambda = \eta q \lambda / (hc)$ — units A/W.
>
> ✅ **Quantum Efficiency:** $\eta \leq 1$; ratio of collected electrons to incident photons.
>
> ✅ **Two Operating Modes:**
>    - **Photovoltaic (PV):** No bias → Slow but low noise → Used in solar cells.
>    - **Photoconductive (PC):** Reverse bias → Fast, linear → Used in optical communication.
>
> ✅ **Bandwidth:** $f_{3dB} = 1/(2\pi R_L C_j)$ — lower $C_j$ (via wider depletion region) means higher speed.
>
> ✅ **Key Numerical Constants:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C, $V_T = 25.85$ mV at 300 K.
>
> ✅ **Symbol Rule:** Arrows point **INWARD** (incident light) — do not confuse with LED symbol.
>
> ✅ **Exam Mantra:** "Photodiode is the **translator**, not the amplifier." It faithfully converts light intensity into proportional current, but provides no internal gain (unlike a phototransistor).
>
> ✅ **Comparison Hook:** Photodiode vs. Phototransistor — Photodiode = fast, no gain, linear. Phototransistor = slower, high gain, non-linear.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phototransistors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES & CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Phototransistors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **phototransistor** is a three-terminal (or two-terminal) semiconductor photosensitive device in which the base current of a conventional bipolar junction transistor (BJT) is internally generated by incident optical (photon) radiation rather than by an externally applied electrical signal. The absorbed photons create electron–hole pairs in the reverse-biased base–collector depletion region; these photogenerated carriers act as the base current $I_B$, which is then amplified by the transistor's current gain $\beta$ (or $h_{FE}$), producing a collector current $I_C = (\beta + 1) I_{ph}$ where $I_{ph}$ is the primary photocurrent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Phototransistors are studied under *Special Semiconductor Devices* in Module 2, and questions typically appear in the form of *"Compare photodiode and phototransistor"*, *"Derive the responsivity expression"*, or *"Explain the working of a phototransistor with V-I characteristics."*

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Light-Triggered Amplifier"

Imagine a **microphone with a built-in amplifier**. A weak sound (your voice) is picked up by the microphone and converted to a tiny electrical signal. The amplifier then magnifies this weak signal into a strong, usable electrical output. 

In a phototransistor, the role of the **microphone is played by the base–collector photodiode junction** (sensitive to light, not sound), and the role of the **amplifier is played by the BJT structure itself**. The "weak sound" is the light photons, and the "loud amplified output" is the collector current. The genius of this design is that the phototransistor combines **light detection + current amplification** in a single device, eliminating the need for a separate external amplifier circuit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Architectural Difference from a Photodiode:**
> A photodiode produces a small photocurrent (microamperes), while a phototransistor produces a photocurrent that is internally amplified by a factor of $\beta$ (typically 100–1000), making it far more sensitive. However, this amplification comes at the cost of **reduced bandwidth** and **higher noise**.

### 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics (Industry Values)

The following empirical values are the standard benchmarks used by KTU examiners:

- **Typical DC Current Gain ($\beta$ or $h_{FE}$):** **100 to 1000**
- **Wavelength Sensitivity Range ($\lambda$):** **400 nm to 1100 nm** (visible to near-infrared for silicon)
- **Peak Sensitivity Wavelength ($\lambda_p$):** **≈ 800 nm** for silicon phototransistors
- **Responsivity ($R$):** **Typically 0.1 to 1 A/W** (much higher than photodiodes)
- **Response Time / Rise Time ($t_r$):** **2 µs to 50 µs** (slower than photodiodes)
- **Collector–Emitter Dark Current ($I_{CEO}$):** **nA to µA range** at room temperature
- **Operating Temperature Range:** **−40 °C to +85 °C** (commercial grade)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Construction of a Phototransistor

A phototransistor is essentially an **NPN (or PNP) bipolar junction transistor** where the **base region is exposed to incident light** through a transparent window. The construction consists of:

- **Emitter:** Heavily doped, narrow region (typically a small contact).
- **Base:** Thin, lightly doped region. Often **left electrically open** (floating) in two-terminal operation, or accessed for biasing in three-terminal operation.
- **Collector:** Moderately doped, large area to maximize light absorption.
- **Package Window:** A clear epoxy or glass lens that focuses light onto the base–collector junction.
- **Antireflection Coating:** Optional thin-film layer to minimize photon reflection at the surface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two-Terminal vs. Three-Terminal Operation:**
> - In **two-terminal mode** (most common), the base is left floating, and only the emitter and collector are connected. The device behaves as a light-controlled current source.
> - In **three-terminal mode**, an external base bias is applied. The base current from the external source sums with the photogenerated base current, allowing the user to *set the operating point* and modulate light sensitivity.

### 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Photon Incidence:** Light of energy $E_{ph} = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ strikes the depletion region of the reverse-biased base–collector junction.

2. **Bandgap Criterion:** Photons are absorbed only if $h\nu \geq E_g$ (the semiconductor bandgap). For silicon, $E_g \approx 1.12$ eV, giving a cutoff wavelength of:
$$\lambda_c = \frac{hc}{E_g} = \frac{1.24 \, \mu\text{m} \cdot \text{eV}}{E_g \, (\text{eV})}$$

3. **Photogeneration:** Each absorbed photon generates **one electron–hole pair** inside the depletion region. The strong electric field of the reverse-biased junction **sweeps electrons toward the collector** and **holes toward the base** (in an NPN device).

4. **Base Current Formation:** The accumulation of holes in the (floating) base region creates a **forward bias** on the base–emitter junction. This causes the BJT to turn on.

5. **Transistor Amplification:** A conventional BJT current flow begins. The collector current is:
$$I_C = \beta \cdot I_B$$

6. **Photocurrent Output:** The total collector current is the sum of the dark current and the amplified photocurrent:
$$I_C = I_{CEO} + (\beta + 1) \cdot I_{ph}$$

7. **External Circuit:** The collector current flows through an external load resistor $R_L$, producing an output voltage $V_{out} = V_{CC} - I_C R_L$.

### 2.3 Why Use a Phototransistor Instead of a Photodiode?

- **Built-in Amplification:** Sensitivity is enhanced by a factor of $\beta$, eliminating the need for external op-amp circuits.
- **Lower Noise Figure for the Same Sensitivity** when measured at low frequencies (< 10 kHz).
- **Simple Two-Terminal Interface:** Easy integration into digital logic circuits.
- **Trade-off:** The Miller capacitance at the collector–base junction limits high-frequency response, making it unsuitable for high-speed optical communication (where photodiodes or PIN diodes are preferred).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Remarks |
|---|---------------------|------------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Photon Energy | $E_{ph} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | eV or J | $h$ = Planck's constant, $c$ = speed of light |
| 2 | Cutoff Wavelength | $\lambda_c = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g} \, \mu\text{m}$ | µm | For Si: $\lambda_c \approx 1.11 \, \mu\text{m}$ |
| 3 | Quantum Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Electrons collected/sec}}{\text{Photons incident/sec}}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Typically 50%–90% |
| 4 | Responsivity | $R = \dfrac{I_{ph}}{P_{opt}} = \dfrac{\eta \lambda q}{hc}$ | A/W | $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C |
| 5 | Photogenerated Current | $I_{ph} = R \cdot P_{opt}$ | A | Directly proportional to optical power |
| 6 | Total Collector Current | $I_C = I_{CEO} + (\beta + 1) I_{ph}$ | A | $I_{CEO}$ is the dark current |
| 7 | Output Voltage | $V_{out} = V_{CC} - I_C R_L$ | V | Common-emitter configuration |
| 8 | Optical Power to Photocurrent | $P_{opt} = \dfrac{n_{ph} h c}{\lambda}$ where $n_{ph}$ = photons/sec | W | Useful for photon-flux problems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The relation $R = \dfrac{\eta \lambda q}{hc}$ is the **most-tested formula** in the ESE (End Semester Examination). Memorize it along with the value of $\dfrac{hc}{q} = 1.24 \, \mu\text{m} \cdot \text{V}$ for quick numerical substitutions.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Phototransistors are indispensable in:

- **Optical Encoders & Shaft Encoders** (precise angular/linear position sensing in CNC machines and robotics).
- **Object Detection & Proximity Sensors** in industrial automation.
- **Automatic Streetlight Control Systems** (LDRs are being replaced by phototransistors for faster, more linear response).
- **Bar-Code Scanners** at retail checkout counters.
- **Tape/Disk-Read Circuits** in legacy audio equipment.
- **Safety Systems:** Smoke detectors, intrusion alarms, beam-break intruder detection.
- **IR Remote-Control Receivers** in television sets and home appliances.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Responsivity from Quantum Efficiency

**Problem Statement:** A silicon phototransistor with a quantum efficiency of 70% is illuminated by light of wavelength 900 nm. Derive an expression for the responsivity and compute its numerical value.

**Step 1 — Define Quantum Efficiency.**
Quantum efficiency $\eta$ is defined as the ratio of the number of electrons collected at the collector per second to the number of photons incident on the active area per second:

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Electrons collected/sec}}{\text{Photons incident/sec}} = \frac{N_e}{N_{ph}}$$

**Step 2 — Express Photocurrent.**
The rate of electron collection gives the primary photocurrent (before transistor amplification):

$$I_{ph} = N_e \cdot q = \eta \cdot N_{ph} \cdot q$$

**Step 3 — Express Incident Optical Power.**
If the optical power incident on the device is $P_{opt}$, then the number of photons per second is:

$$N_{ph} = \frac{P_{opt}}{E_{ph}} = \frac{P_{opt} \lambda}{hc}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute to Find $I_{ph}$ in Terms of $P_{opt}$.**

$$I_{ph} = \eta \cdot \left(\frac{P_{opt} \lambda}{hc}\right) \cdot q = \frac{\eta \lambda q}{hc} \cdot P_{opt}$$

**Step 5 — Define Responsivity and Conclude.**

$$\boxed{R = \frac{I_{ph}}{P_{opt}} = \frac{\eta \lambda q}{hc}}$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Evaluation.**
Using $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C, $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s, $\eta = 0.70$, $\lambda = 900 \times 10^{-9}$ m:

$$R = \frac{(0.70)(900 \times 10^{-9})(1.6 \times 10^{-19})}{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^{8})}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the Denominator First.**

$$h c = (6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^{8}) = 1.9878 \times 10^{-25} \, \text{J·m}$$

**Step 8 — Compute the Numerator.**

$$\eta \lambda q = (0.70)(900 \times 10^{-9})(1.6 \times 10^{-19}) = 1.008 \times 10^{-25} \, \text{C·m}$$

**Step 9 — Divide.**

$$R = \frac{1.008 \times 10^{-25}}{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}} = 0.507 \, \text{A/W}$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 0.507 \, \text{A/W}}$$

**Step 10 — Sanity Check.**
The theoretical maximum responsivity at 900 nm (assuming $\eta = 1$) is:

$$R_{max} = \frac{\lambda q}{hc} = \frac{900 \times 10^{-9}}{1.24 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 0.726 \, \text{A/W}$$

Our computed value $0.507$ is 70% of this maximum, which matches $\eta = 0.70$. ✓

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Output Voltage Under Illumination

**Problem Statement:** A phototransistor with $\beta = 200$ is connected in a common-emitter configuration. It is illuminated such that the primary photocurrent is $I_{ph} = 1.5 \, \mu\text{A}$. The dark current is $I_{CEO} = 50$ nA. The supply is $V_{CC} = 12$ V and the load resistor is $R_L = 10$ k$\Omega$. Derive the output voltage.

**Step 1 — Identify the Governing Equation.**

$$I_C = I_{CEO} + (\beta + 1) I_{ph}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Values.**
The dark current term $I_{CEO} = 50$ nA is negligible compared to the amplified photocurrent term:

$$(\beta + 1) I_{ph} = (201)(1.5 \times 10^{-6}) = 301.5 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{A} = 301.5 \, \mu\text{A}$$

**Step 3 — Add the Dark Current.**

$$I_C = 50 \times 10^{-9} + 301.5 \times 10^{-6} \approx 301.55 \, \mu\text{A}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Voltage Drop Across $R_L$.**

$$V_{R_L} = I_C R_L = (301.55 \times 10^{-6})(10 \times 10^{3}) = 3.0155 \, \text{V}$$

**Step 5 — Apply KVL to Find $V_{out}$.**

$$V_{out} = V_{CC} - I_C R_L = 12 - 3.0155 = 8.98 \, \text{V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{out} \approx 8.98 \, \text{V}}$$

**Step 6 — Physical Interpretation.**
The output voltage is still "high" (≈ 9 V) because the phototransistor is in its *active region* (not saturated). For switching applications, the design must ensure that the device enters *saturation* — typically by increasing $R_L$ or increasing light intensity so that $I_C R_L > V_{CC} - V_{CE(sat)}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Physical Cross-Section (Block-Level Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Pkg["PHOTOTRANSISTOR PACKAGE"]
        L1["INCIDENT LIGHT PHOTONS"]
        L2["ANTI-REFLECTION COATING"]
        L3["EMITTER CONTACT E"]
        L4["P-TYPE BASE REGION"]
        L5["N-TYPE COLLECTOR REGION"]
        L6["TRANSPARENT EPOXY WINDOW"]
        L7["DEPLETION REGION AT B-C JUNCTION"]
        L8["SUBCOLLECTOR / BULK N+"]
    end

    L1 --> L6
    L6 --> L2
    L2 --> L7
    L7 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
    L5 --> L8
    L3 --> L4
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow (Light → Output)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["PHOTON INPUT lamda h nu"] --> A2["PHOTOGENERATION IN B-C JUNCTION"]
    A2 --> A3["ELECTRON HOLE PAIR SEPARATION"]
    A3 --> A4["HOLES INJECTED INTO BASE REGION"]
    A4 --> A5["FLOATING BASE FORWARD BIAS"]
    A5 --> A6["BJT TRANSISTOR ACTION TURNS ON"]
    A6 --> A7["COLLECTOR CURRENT IC equals beta times IB"]
    A7 --> A8["OUTPUT VOLTAGE Vout across RL"]
    A8 --> A9["AMPLIFIED ELECTRICAL SIGNAL"]
```

### 4.3 Common-Emitter Circuit Topology with Phototransistor

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CCT["COMMON EMITTER PHOTOTRANSISTOR CIRCUIT"]
        N1["VCC SUPPLY POSITIVE 12V"] --- N2["RESISTOR RL 10K"]
        N2 --- N3["COLLECTOR TERMINAL C"]
        N3 --- N4["PHOTOTRANSISTOR DEVICE"]
        N4 --- N5["EMITTER TERMINAL E"]
        N5 --- N6["GROUND GND 0V"]
        N7["OPTICAL INPUT LIGHT"] -.-> N4
        N8["OUTPUT Vout TAKEN AT COLLECTOR NODE"] --- N3
        N9["BASE TERMINAL LEFT FLOATING 2 WIRE MODE"] -.-> N4
    end
```

### 4.4 I-V Output Characteristics Family (Conceptual Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CHAR["I vs V CURVE FAMILIES FOR DIFFERENT LIGHT INTENSITIES"]
        D1["CURVE E0 DARKNESS VERY LOW IC"]
        D2["CURVE E1 LOW LIGHT"]
        D3["CURVE E2 MEDIUM LIGHT"]
        D4["CURVE E3 HIGH LIGHT SATURATION"]
        D5["X AXIS VCE COLLECTOR EMITTER VOLTAGE"]
        D6["Y AXIS IC COLLECTOR CURRENT"]
    end
    D5 --- D6
    D1 --> D2
    D2 --> D3
    D3 --> D4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Characteristics:** The collector current $I_C$ rises sharply with light intensity $E$. Above a certain $V_{CE}$ (the "knee voltage"), the curves become nearly horizontal, indicating the active region where the phototransistor behaves like a current source. Near $V_{CE} = 0$, the curves bend into the saturation region.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define a phototransistor. Why is the response time of a phototransistor slower than that of a photodiode?**

**Model Answer:**

A phototransistor is a light-sensitive bipolar junction transistor in which the base current is generated by incident photons striking the base–collector junction. The photocurrent is then amplified internally by the transistor's current gain $\beta$, producing a large collector current $I_C = (\beta + 1) I_{ph}$.

**Reason for slower response:**
The phototransistor has a large **base–collector junction capacitance (Miller capacitance)**, which must charge and discharge during switching. This capacitance, combined with the base–emitter junction capacitance, introduces a time constant $\tau = R C$, leading to rise times on the order of microseconds. A photodiode, having no transistor gain stage, has junction capacitances only in the picofarad range, giving nanosecond response. *(3 marks)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**List any three advantages and two disadvantages of phototransistors over photodiodes.**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages:**
1. Built-in amplification — output current is $\beta$ times larger, so no external amplifier is required. (1 mark)
2. Higher sensitivity to low light levels due to internal gain. (1 mark)
3. Simple two-terminal interfacing — works with digital logic directly. (1 mark)

**Disadvantages:**
1. Slower response time (µs range) due to junction capacitance. (½ mark)
2. Higher temperature sensitivity and higher noise at high frequencies. (½ mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam — Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**
**(a) With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of an NPN phototransistor. Discuss the role of the floating base in two-terminal operation.** (7 marks)
**(b) A silicon phototransistor has a quantum efficiency of 65% and is illuminated by light of wavelength 850 nm with an optical power of 2 µW. The current gain is $\beta = 250$. Calculate (i) the responsivity, (ii) the primary photocurrent, and (iii) the total collector current.** (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Construction:**
Refer to the block diagram in **Section 4.1**. The device is an NPN BJT with:
- **Emitter (N⁺):** Heavily doped, narrow region.
- **Base (P):** Thin, lightly doped, exposed to light through a transparent window in the package.
- **Collector (N):** Large area, moderately doped, bulk region.
- **Package:** Transparent epoxy or glass lens on top to focus incident photons onto the base region.

**Working Principle:**
1. Photons of energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ strike the base–collector depletion region. (1 mark)
2. Each photon generates an electron–hole pair. The reverse bias on the B–C junction sweeps electrons toward the collector and holes toward the base. (1 mark)
3. Holes accumulating in the floating base region forward-bias the B–E junction, turning on the transistor. (1 mark)
4. The transistor amplifies the small photogenerated base current $I_{ph}$ by a factor of $\beta$, producing a large collector current $I_C = \beta \cdot I_{ph}$. (1 mark)

**Role of Floating Base in Two-Terminal Operation:**
- In two-terminal mode, no external wire is connected to the base. The base is *floating* — i.e., its potential is determined entirely by the photogenerated hole current. (1 mark)
- This simplifies the external circuit to just two connections: collector and emitter, making the device behave purely as a light-controlled current source. (1 mark)
- The floating-base configuration provides maximum internal amplification because the entire base current originates from photon absorption, with no competing external current. (1 mark)

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $\eta = 0.65$, $\lambda = 850$ nm $= 850 \times 10^{-9}$ m, $P_{opt} = 2 \, \mu\text{W} = 2 \times 10^{-6}$ W, $\beta = 250$.

**(i) Responsivity:**

$$R = \frac{\eta \lambda q}{hc}$$

Using the shortcut $\dfrac{\lambda q}{hc} = \dfrac{\lambda (\mu\text{m})}{1.24 \, \mu\text{m} \cdot \text{V}}$:

$$R = 0.65 \times \frac{850 \times 10^{-9}}{1.24 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.65 \times 0.6855 = 0.4456 \, \text{A/W}$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 0.446 \, \text{A/W}} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**(ii) Primary Photocurrent:**

$$I_{ph} = R \cdot P_{opt} = (0.4456)(2 \times 10^{-6}) = 0.891 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{A}$$

$$\boxed{I_{ph} \approx 0.891 \, \mu\text{A}} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**(iii) Total Collector Current:**

$$I_C = (\beta + 1) I_{ph} = (251)(0.891 \times 10^{-6})$$

$$\boxed{I_C \approx 223.6 \, \mu\text{A}} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**Final summary line:** *(1 mark)*

The phototransistor amplifies a 0.89 µA photocurrent into a 224 µA collector current, confirming the internal gain of ≈ 250.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**
**(a) Derive the expression for the responsivity of a phototransistor in terms of quantum efficiency and wavelength. Briefly explain the V-I characteristics of a phototransistor with the help of a sketch.** (7 marks)
**(b) Compare phototransistors with photodiodes in terms of sensitivity, response time, bandwidth, output current, and application suitability. A photodiode has a responsivity of 0.4 A/W at 700 nm. Find its quantum efficiency.** (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Derivation of Responsivity** (4 marks):
Refer to **Derivation 1 in Section 3.1**. The key steps are:
- Define $\eta = N_e / N_{ph}$.
- Express $I_{ph} = \eta N_{ph} q$ and $N_{ph} = P_{opt} \lambda / hc$.
- Combine to obtain:

$$R = \frac{\eta \lambda q}{hc}$$

**V-I Characteristics Explanation** (3 marks):
The V-I characteristics of a phototransistor (Common-Emitter configuration) plot collector current $I_C$ versus collector–emitter voltage $V_{CE}$ for different illumination levels $E_1 < E_2 < E_3 < E_4$ (refer to the **Section 4.4** diagram).

- **Active Region:** For $V_{CE}$ above the knee voltage, $I_C$ is nearly constant and proportional to light intensity. The phototransistor behaves as a current source. (1 mark)
- **Saturation Region:** For low $V_{CE}$, the curves bend sharply downward. The device is fully on, and $I_C$ depends on both $V_{CE}$ and light. (1 mark)
- **Cutoff Region:** In total darkness, only the tiny dark current $I_{CEO}$ flows. (1 mark)

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Comparison Table** (3 marks):

| Parameter | Photodiode | Phototransistor |
|-----------|------------|-----------------|
| Sensitivity | Low (no internal gain) | High (gain of $\beta$) |
| Response Time | ns range | µs range |
| Bandwidth | GHz | kHz–MHz |
| Output Current | µA | mA (amplified) |
| Application | High-speed optical comms, fiber optics | Switching, encoders, low-frequency detection |

**Quantum Efficiency Calculation** (4 marks):

Given: $R = 0.4$ A/W, $\lambda = 700$ nm $= 700 \times 10^{-9}$ m.

Starting from:

$$R = \frac{\eta \lambda q}{hc} \implies \eta = \frac{R \cdot hc}{\lambda q}$$

Substituting:

$$\eta = \frac{(0.4)(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^{8})}{(700 \times 10^{-9})(1.6 \times 10^{-19})}$$

**Numerator:**

$$0.4 \times 1.9878 \times 10^{-25} = 7.951 \times 10^{-26}$$

**Denominator:**

$$700 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} = 1.12 \times 10^{-25}$$

**Divide:**

$$\eta = \frac{7.951 \times 10^{-26}}{1.12 \times 10^{-25}} = 0.710$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 71\%} \quad \text{(1 mark for final answer)}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to add 1 to $\beta$:** Many students write $I_C = \beta I_{ph}$ instead of $I_C = (\beta + 1) I_{ph}$. Since the emitter current $I_E = (\beta + 1) I_B$, the *collector* current formula is $I_C = \beta I_B$, but for total phototransistor output including base-recombination loss, the **$(\beta+1)$ form is used when the photocurrent is measured at the base terminal**. Read the question carefully! (−1 mark)
> 2. **Unit confusion in $R$:** Responsivity is in **A/W**, not W/A. (−1 mark)
> 3. **Wavelength in metres:** Always convert nm to m before substituting into the formula. (−0.5 mark)
> 4. **Forgetting quantum efficiency in the formula:** A common error is writing $R = \lambda q / hc$ without $\eta$, which gives the *maximum possible* responsivity only. (−1 mark)
> 5. **Skipping the sketch in V-I questions:** KTU explicitly awards 2 marks for a neat, labelled sketch. Always include one. (−2 marks)

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] A phototransistor is a **BJT whose base is driven by light**, not by an external wire.
- [x] The base–collector junction acts as a **photodiode**, and the entire BJT acts as a **current amplifier** of gain $\beta$.
- [x] In two-terminal mode, the base is **floating**; the device is a *light-controlled current source*.
- [x] The key current equation is **$I_C = I_{CEO} + (\beta + 1) I_{ph}$**, where $I_{CEO}$ is the dark current (typically negligible).
- [x] **Responsivity** $R = \dfrac{\eta \lambda q}{hc}$ — units A/W.
- [x] **Quantum efficiency** $\eta$ = electrons collected ÷ photons incident — always between 0 and 1.
- [x] **Cutoff wavelength** $\lambda_c = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g}$ µm — for Si, $E_g = 1.12$ eV, giving $\lambda_c \approx 1.11$ µm.
- [x] Phototransistor **advantages**: high sensitivity, simple interface, built-in amplification.
- [x] Phototransistor **disadvantages**: slow response (µs), narrow bandwidth, temperature-sensitive.
- [x] **Applications**: object detection, encoders, IR receivers, smoke detectors, automatic lighting.
- [x] **Common-emitter output**: $V_{out} = V_{CC} - I_C R_L$.
- [x] For high-speed applications (GHz), use **photodiodes or PIN diodes**, not phototransistors.
- [x] The shortcut $\dfrac{\lambda q}{hc} = \dfrac{\lambda(\mu\text{m})}{1.24 \, \mu\text{m} \cdot \text{V}}$ saves time in numerical problems.
- [x] For silicon devices, peak sensitivity is at $\lambda \approx 800$ nm, with usable range **400–1100 nm**.
- [x] Always include a **labelled V-I curve** in 7-mark descriptive questions for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Transistor Biasing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES & CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Transistor Biasing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transistor Biasing** is the process of applying external DC voltages and/or currents to a bipolar junction transistor (BJT) in order to establish a predefined **Quiescent Operating Point (Q-point)** — defined by the coordinates ($I_{CQ}$, $I_{BQ}$, $V_{CEQ}$) — such that the transistor operates reliably in the **Active Region** for all working conditions, regardless of variations in temperature ($\beta$, $I_{CO}$) or replacement of the device.

The **Q-point** is the intersection of the DC load line and the transistor's output characteristic curves. It represents the steady-state DC voltages and currents in the transistor when **no input AC signal** is applied to the base-emitter junction.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Plumbing Tap & Water Tank"

Imagine you want a faucet (tap) to deliver water at a perfectly controlled, steady rate — neither a trickle nor a flood — irrespective of whether the water tank's pressure fluctuates or the pipe wears out over time.

- The **transistor** is the *tap*.
- The **DC bias voltages** ($V_{BB}$, $V_{CC}$) are the *plumbing knobs* set by the engineer.
- The **Q-point** ($I_{CQ}$, $V_{CEQ}$) is the *steady flow rate* we choose before opening the modulating signal valve.
- The **AC input signal** ($v_i$) is the user turning the handle *slightly* around this preset to either increase or decrease the flow.
- **Bias stability** is the engineer's job to ensure that even when the ambient temperature rises (which would normally change the tap's mechanics), the steady flow remains unchanged.

Without preset biasing, the tap would either be fully closed (cutoff — no output) or fully open (saturation — distorted output). Biasing places the tap precisely in the **middle of its useful range**, so small AC wiggles on top of it can be cleanly amplified.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Biasing Mandatory for Amplification?**
> A BJT is a **non-linear device**. Its output current $I_C$ is an exponential function of $V_{BE}$. If we simply plug it in without setting a Q-point, any small AC signal applied will be severely distorted (only the positive half-cycles conduct). Biasing ensures the transistor operates in the **linear (active) region** where $I_C = \beta I_B$ holds true, enabling faithful, undistorted amplification.

## 1.3 Operating Regions of the BJT — Quick Reference

| Region | E-B Junction | C-B Junction | Use Case |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **Cutoff** | Reverse Biased | Reverse Biased | OFF Switch |
| **Active (Forward Active)** | Forward Biased | Reverse Biased | **Linear Amplifier** ✓ |
| **Saturation** | Forward Biased | Forward Biased | ON Switch |
| **Reverse Active** | Reverse Biased | Forward Biased | Rarely used (inverter) |

## 1.4 Standard Nomenclature for Key Quantities

- $V_{CC}$ → **DC supply voltage** at collector (typically **+12 V**, **+15 V**, or **+20 V**).
- $V_{BB}$ → **DC supply voltage** at base.
- $I_{CQ}$ → **Quiescent collector current** (DC value, with no AC signal).
- $V_{CEQ}$ → **Quiescent collector-emitter voltage**.
- $I_{BQ}$ → **Quiescent base current**.
- $I_{CBO}$ (or $I_{CO}$) → **Reverse saturation current** of the C-B junction with emitter open ($\approx$ **nA to µA**).
- $\beta_{DC}$ (or $h_{FE}$) → **DC current gain** (typically **50–300** for silicon BJTs).
- $\alpha_{DC}$ → **Common-base DC current gain** ($\alpha = \beta / (\beta + 1)$).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Biasing — Four Engineering Drivers

A transistor must be biased to simultaneously satisfy **four essential engineering goals**:

1. **Establishment of the Q-point:** Fixing $I_{CQ}$ and $V_{CEQ}$ at desired values (typically chosen such that $V_{CEQ} \approx V_{CC}/2$ for maximum symmetrical swing).
2. **Operation in the Active Region:** Ensures the BJT behaves as a **linear amplifier** rather than as a switch.
3. **Temperature / Parameter Independence:** Compensates against drifts in $I_{CBO}$, $V_{BE}$, and $\beta$, which are functions of temperature.
4. **Maximizing AC Output Swing:** The Q-point should lie at the **center of the DC load line** so the output AC voltage can swing equally in both positive and negative directions without clipping.

## 2.2 The DC Load Line — Visualizing the Q-Point

Applying KVL around the output loop ($V_{CC} \to R_C \to$ collector $\to$ emitter $\to$ ground):

$$V_{CC} = I_C R_C + V_{CE}$$

This is a **straight line** in the $I_C$–$V_{CE}$ plane. Its two endpoints are:

- **Saturation Point:** When $V_{CE} = 0$, $I_C = V_{CC} / R_C$ (called $I_{C(sat)}$).
- **Cutoff Point:** When $I_C = 0$, $V_{CE} = V_{CC}$.

The **Q-point** is the intersection of this load line with the $I_B = I_{BQ}$ curve on the output characteristics.

## 2.3 Bias Stability — The Central Problem

A transistor's collector current is a **strong function of three temperature-sensitive parameters**:

$$I_C = \beta I_B + (1+\beta) I_{CBO}$$

Any change in $\beta$, $I_{CBO}$, or $V_{BE}$ will shift the Q-point, possibly driving the transistor out of the active region. This phenomenon is termed **thermal runaway** if unchecked.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Runaway:** A positive-feedback loop where an increase in $I_C$ raises the junction temperature $T_J$, which increases $I_{CBO}$ (and decreases $V_{BE}$), which further increases $I_C$, eventually destroying the device. Bias circuits must **stabilize** $I_C$ against this loop.

## 2.4 Stability Factors — Quantifying Robustness

The **Stability Factor $S$** measures how much the Q-point ($I_C$) drifts for a unit change in a particular parameter. Lower $S$ = more stable design.

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Formula | Description | Typical Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $S(I_{CBO})$ | $\dfrac{\partial I_C}{\partial I_{CBO}}$ | Sensitivity to leakage current | Should be $\leq 10$ |
| $S(V_{BE})$ | $\dfrac{\partial I_C}{\partial V_{BE}}$ | Sensitivity to base-emitter voltage | Should be small |
| $S(\beta)$ | $\dfrac{\partial I_C}{\partial \beta}$ | Sensitivity to current gain | Should be small |
| $S_T$ (overall) | $S(I_{CBO}) \cdot S(V_{BE}) \cdot S(\beta)$ | Total stability factor | $\geq 1$ always |
| $I_C$ (active) | $\beta I_B + (1+\beta) I_{CBO}$ | Fundamental collector equation | — |
| $\alpha$ | $\dfrac{\beta}{\beta+1}$ | CB current gain relation | $\approx 1$ |
| $V_{CC}$ (load) | $I_C R_C + V_{CE}$ | DC load line equation | — |
| $I_{C(sat)}$ | $V_{CC} / R_C$ | Max collector current | — |
| $V_{CE(cutoff)}$ | $V_{CC}$ | Max collector-emitter voltage | — |
| $P_D$ | $V_{CE} \cdot I_C$ | Quiescent power dissipation | — |

## 2.5 Preview of Biasing Methods (Detailed in Subsequent Topics)

Although each method is covered exhaustively later in Module 3, an introductory overview establishes context:

| Biasing Method | $S(I_{CBO})$ | Complexity | Stability Rating |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **Fixed Bias (Base Bias)** | $\beta + 1$ | Very Low | **Poor** ✗ |
| **Collector-to-Base Bias** | $\dfrac{\beta+1}{1+\beta(R_C/R_B)}$ | Low | Moderate |
| **Self-Bias / Emitter Bias** | $\dfrac{\beta+1}{1+\beta}$ | Medium | Good |
| **Voltage-Divider Bias** | $\dfrac{(\beta+1)(1+R_E/R_B)}{(1+\beta) + R_E(1+\beta)/R_B}$ | Medium | **Excellent** ✓ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Voltage-divider bias** is by far the most widely used configuration in KTU-evaluated problems and real-world amplifier design because it provides the lowest $S(I_{CBO})$ and is largely independent of $\beta$.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio Amplifiers (Hi-Fi Systems):** Biased Class-A stages use a Q-point near mid-rail for linear reproduction of music.
- **RF Front-Ends (Mobile Phones):** Biasing sets the operating transconductance ($g_m$) of low-noise amplifiers.
- **Analog ICs (Op-Amps like µA741):** Internal bias networks establish reference currents that cascade throughout the chip.
- **Power Electronics:** Improper biasing leads to **secondary breakdown** and catastrophic device failure — a primary concern in motor drives and SMPS design.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Collector Current Equation ($I_C$ vs $I_B$, $\beta$, $I_{CBO}$)

### Step 1 — Start with the BJT current relationship (Kirchhoff's Current Law at the transistor)

The total emitter current $I_E$ splits between the collector and base terminals:

$$I_E = I_C + I_B$$

### Step 2 — Express $I_C$ in terms of $I_E$ and $\alpha$

By definition of the common-base current gain $\alpha$:

$$I_C = \alpha I_E + I_{CBO}$$

The term $I_{CBO}$ is the small reverse-leakage current that flows from collector to base when the emitter is open-circuited. It arises due to thermally generated minority carriers in the base region.
