\frac{1}{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{f_{L}}{f}\right)^{2} \right] \left[1 + \left(\dfrac{f}{f_{H}}\right)^{2} \right] } = \frac{1}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — For the upper cutoff region ($f \gg f_{L}$), the low-frequency term becomes negligible.**

The expression reduces to:

$$
1 + \left(\frac{f_{H(\text{overall})}}{f_{H1}}\right)^{2} = 2
$$

$$
\left(\frac{f_{H(\text{overall})}}{f_{H1}}\right)^{2} = 1
$$

For **n identical stages**, each contributing an identical 3-dB droop, the cascaded response at the overall cutoff is:

$$
\left[1 + \left(\frac{f_{H(\text{overall})}}{f_{H1}}\right)^{2}\right]^{n} = 2
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the overall upper cutoff.**

$$
1 + \left(\frac{f_{H(\text{overall})}}{f_{H1}}\right)^{2} = 2^{1/n}
$$

$$
\frac{f_{H(\text{overall})}}{f_{H1}} = \sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,f_{H(\text{overall})} = f_{H1} \cdot \sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}\,}
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the same procedure to the low-frequency pole.**

The low-frequency pole expression inverts, giving:

$$
\boxed{\,f_{L(\text{overall})} = \frac{f_{L1}}{\sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}}\,}
$$

**Step 7 — Overall Bandwidth.**

$$
BW_{\text{overall}} = f_{H(\text{overall})} - f_{L(\text{overall})}
$$

For audio amplifiers where $f_{H} \gg f_{L}$, this is approximated as:

$$
BW_{\text{overall}} \approx f_{H(\text{overall})}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Gain-Bandwidth Product Invariance

**Step 1 — Single-stage GBP.**

For a single stage with mid-band gain $A_{V1}$ and bandwidth $BW_1 = f_{H1} - f_{L1}$:

$$
GBP_{1} = \vert A_{V1} \vert \times BW_{1}
$$

**Step 2 — n-stage cascaded GBP.**

Using the cascade gain $A_{V(\text{total})} = A_{V1}^{n}$ and the new overall bandwidth:

$$
GBP_{n} = A_{V1}^{n} \times f_{H1} \cdot \sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}
$$

**Step 3 — For two identical CE stages (n = 2).**

Substituting $n = 2$:

$$
\sqrt{2^{1/2} - 1} = \sqrt{\sqrt{2} - 1} \approx 0.6436
$$

Therefore:

$$
f_{H(\text{overall})} \approx 0.6436 \cdot f_{H1}
$$

The bandwidth **shrinks by a factor of 0.6436** when two identical stages are cascaded, while the voltage gain **doubles logarithmically**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Numerical Behaviour vs. Number of Stages

Let a single stage have $A_{V1} = 50$ and $f_{H1} = 20\,\text{kHz}$.

| Number of Stages (n) | Total Gain (linear) | Total Gain (dB) | Overall $f_H$ (kHz) | Overall BW (kHz) | $GBP \times 10^{3}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50 | 33.98 | 20.00 | 20.00 | 1000 |
| 2 | 2500 | 67.96 | 12.87 | 12.87 | 32180 |
| 3 | 125000 | 101.94 | 10.86 | 10.86 | 1357500 |
| 4 | 6 250 000 | 135.92 | 9.71 | 9.71 | 6.07 \times 10^7 |

> **Observation:** As gain grows **exponentially** (linear scale), the bandwidth shrinks — but the **Gain-Bandwidth Product grows dramatically**, illustrating that *GBP is not constant across stages* when the per-stage gain is *assumed* fixed. The classical **GBP invariance** applies to a *single transistor's internal Miller-limited* response, not to arbitrary cascade gains. This is a frequent KTU conceptual trap.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — KTU Style

**Problem:** Two identical CE stages are cascaded. Each stage has $A_{V1} = 100$, $f_{L1} = 100\,\text{Hz}$, and $f_{H1} = 20\,\text{kHz}$. Calculate (a) overall voltage gain in dB, (b) overall bandwidth, (c) gain-bandwidth product.

**Solution:**

**(a) Overall Gain:**

$$
A_{V(\text{total})} = 100 \times 100 = 10{,}000
$$

$$
A_{V(\text{total})}\,[\text{dB}] = 20 \log_{10}(10{,}000) = 80\,\text{dB}
$$

**(b) Overall Bandwidth (using n = 2):**

$$
f_{H(\text{overall})} = 20\,\text{kHz} \times \sqrt{2^{1/2} - 1} = 20 \times 0.6436 \approx 12.87\,\text{kHz}
$$

$$
f_{L(\text{overall})} = \frac{100\,\text{Hz}}{0.6436} \approx 155.4\,\text{Hz}
$$

$$
BW_{\text{overall}} \approx 12{,}870 - 155.4 \approx 12.71\,\text{kHz}
$$

**(c) Gain-Bandwidth Product:**

$$
GBP = 10{,}000 \times 12.71\,\text{kHz} = 1.271 \times 10^{8}\,\text{Hz}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Multistage Amplifier Cascade

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the signal flow from source to load through n cascaded amplifier stages, including the coupling networks and the loading effect.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Source Signal v_in] --> cpl1[Coupling Network 1]
    cpl1 --> st1[Stage 1 Amplifier A_V1]
    st1 --> cpl2[Coupling Network 2]
    cpl2 --> st2[Stage 2 Amplifier A_V2]
    st2 --> cpl3[Coupling Network 3]
    cpl3 --> st3[Stage 3 Amplifier A_V3]
    st3 --> cplN[Coupling Network N]
    cplN --> stN[Stage N Amplifier A_VN]
    stN --> ld[Load R_L]
    st1 -.loads.-> st2
    st2 -.loads.-> st3
    st3 -.loads.-> stN
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Two-Stage CE-CE RC-Coupled Amplifier

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STAGE1[STAGE 1 - COMMON EMITTER]
        q1[Transistor Q1]
        rc1[RC Coupling Capacitor C1]
        bias1[Voltage Divider Bias R1 R2]
        re1[Emitter Resistor RE1 with Bypass CE1]
    end
    subgraph STAGE2[STAGE 2 - COMMON EMITTER]
        q2[Transistor Q2]
        rc2[RC Coupling Capacitor C2]
        bias2[Voltage Divider Bias R3 R4]
        re2[Emitter Resistor RE2 with Bypass CE2]
    end
    inp[Input v_in] --> C1in[C_in] --> STAGE1
    STAGE1 --> C1out[C1 coupling] --> STAGE2
    STAGE2 --> C2out[C_out] --> vout[v_out]
    STAGE1 -. R_i2 loads Stage 1 .- STAGE2
```

---

## 4.3 Frequency Response Curve — Cascaded vs. Single-Stage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    axisx[Frequency f in Hz] --> low[f_L overall HIGHER than f_L1]
    axisx --> mid[Mid-band: higher A_V in dB]
    axisx --> high[f_H overall LOWER than f_H1]
    note1[Gain rises from f_L] --> low
    note2[Gain flat in mid-band] --> mid
    note3[Gain falls after f_H] --> high
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Notice that in the cascaded response, the **lower cutoff shifts right** (bandwidth loss at low end) and the **upper cutoff shifts left** (bandwidth loss at high end), squeezing the overall passband compared to a single stage.

---

## 4.4 Gain-Bandwidth Trade-off Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    a[Single Stage] --> b{Aim: Higher Gain}
    b --> c[Cascade n Stages]
    c --> d{Aim: Wider Bandwidth}
    d --> e[Use Negative Feedback]
    e --> f[Use Cascode CE-CB]
    f --> g[Final: Optimized A_V and BW]
```

---

## 4.5 Signal Flow Summary Table

| Block | Function | Key Parameter |
|---|---|---|
| Source | Provides weak input signal | $v_{in}$, $R_{S}$ |
| Coupling Network | Blocks DC, passes AC | C, R values |
| Stage 1 (CE) | Voltage amplification | $A_{V1}$, $R_{in1}$ |
| Stage 2 (CE) | Further amplification | $A_{V2}$, $R_{in2}$ |
| Stage N (CC) | Impedance buffering (optional) | $A_{VN} \approx 1$, $R_{outN}$ small |
| Load | Receives amplified signal | $R_{L}$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Define a multistage amplifier. Why is cascading necessary in amplifier design?"**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A multistage amplifier is an amplifier formed by connecting two or more single-stage amplifier blocks in cascade, where the output of one stage feeds the input of the next. **(1 mark)**

Cascading is necessary because:
- A single stage provides limited gain; cascading multiplies the overall gain. **(1 mark)**
- It allows optimisation of input impedance, output impedance, and bandwidth through the choice of stage configuration (CE, CB, CC). **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"State and explain the Gain-Bandwidth Product of an amplifier."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP) is defined as the product of the mid-band voltage gain of an amplifier and its -3 dB bandwidth. **(1 mark)**

$$
GBP = \vert A_{V(mid)} \vert \times BW
$$

It is a **figure of merit** that remains approximately **constant** for a given transistor and bias point. **(1 mark)**
Thus, if gain is increased, bandwidth decreases proportionally, and vice versa. **(1 mark)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks with two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. The student answers EITHER Question A OR Question B in full.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the overall voltage gain of a two-stage cascaded RC-coupled amplifier, taking into account the loading effect of the second stage on the first. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A three-stage cascaded amplifier has identical CE stages, each with mid-band voltage gain $A_{V1} = 80$, lower cutoff $f_{L1} = 80\,\text{Hz}$, and upper cutoff $f_{H1} = 25\,\text{kHz}$. Calculate the overall voltage gain in dB, the overall bandwidth, and the rise time of the cascaded amplifier. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to A(a) — Step by Step

**Step 1:** Define output of Stage 1 with no load: $v_{o1} = A_{V1} \cdot v_{in}$ **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** Apply the loading voltage divider rule using $R_{o1}$ and $R_{i2}$:

$$
v_{in2} = v_{o1} \cdot \frac{R_{i2}}{R_{o1} + R_{i2}}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 3:** Effective gain of Stage 1 after loading:

$$
A'_{V1} = A_{V1} \cdot \frac{R_{i2}}{R_{o1} + R_{i2}}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 4:** Output of Stage 2:

$$
v_{o2} = A_{V2} \cdot A'_{V1} \cdot v_{in}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 5:** Total voltage gain:

$$
A_{V(\text{total})} = A'_{V1} \cdot A_{V2} = A_{V1} A_{V2} \cdot \frac{R_{i2}}{R_{o1} + R_{i2}}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 6:** Generalise to n stages:

$$
A_{V(\text{total})} = \prod_{k=1}^{n} A'_{Vk}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 7:** Comment on the importance of inter-stage impedance matching to minimise loading loss. **(1 mark)**

---

#### Solution to A(b) — Step by Step

**Step 1:** Overall voltage gain in linear scale:

$$
A_{V(\text{total})} = 80^{3} = 512{,}000
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** Convert to dB:

$$
A_{V(\text{total})}\,[\text{dB}] = 20 \log_{10}(512{,}000) = 114.18\,\text{dB}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 3:** Overall upper cutoff using n = 3:

$$
\sqrt{2^{1/3} - 1} = \sqrt{1.2599 - 1} = \sqrt{0.2599} \approx 0.5098
$$

**(1 mark)**

$$
f_{H(\text{overall})} = 25\,\text{kHz} \times 0.5098 \approx 12.74\,\text{kHz}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 4:** Overall lower cutoff:

$$
f_{L(\text{overall})} = \frac{80\,\text{Hz}}{0.5098} \approx 156.9\,\text{Hz}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 5:** Overall bandwidth:

$$
BW_{\text{overall}} = 12{,}740 - 156.9 \approx 12.58\,\text{kHz}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 6:** Rise time:

$$
t_{r} = \frac{0.35}{BW} = \frac{0.35}{12.58 \times 10^{3}} \approx 27.8\,\mu\text{s}
$$

**(1 mark)**

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the effect of cascading on the bandwidth of an amplifier. Derive the expression for the overall upper and lower cutoff frequencies for n identical cascaded stages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A two-stage amplifier has individual stage gains of $A_{V1} = 50$ and $A_{V2} = 80$, with stage bandwidths $BW_1 = 30\,\text{kHz}$ and $BW_2 = 40\,\text{kHz}$. Calculate the overall gain in dB, the overall bandwidth (assuming identical poles), and the gain-bandwidth product. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to B(a) — Step by Step

**Step 1:** State the effect qualitatively — cascading **reduces** the overall bandwidth because each stage contributes an additional 3-dB droop at its own cutoff. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** Write the single-stage transfer function magnitude:

$$
\vert A_{V}(f) \vert = \frac{A_{V(mid)}}{\sqrt{1 + (f_{L}/f)^{2}} \cdot \sqrt{1 + (f/f_{H})^{2}}}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 3:** For n identical cascaded stages, the squared response is:

$$
\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (f_{L}/f)^{2}} \cdot \sqrt{1 + (f/f_{H})^{2}}}\right]^{2n} = \frac{1}{2}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 4:** Upper cutoff region (neglect $f_L$ term):

$$
\left[1 + \left(\frac{f_{H(\text{overall})}}{f_{H1}}\right)^{2}\right]^{n} = 2
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 5:** Solve:

$$
f_{H(\text{overall})} = f_{H1} \cdot \sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 6:** Lower cutoff:

$$
f_{L(\text{overall})} = \frac{f_{L1}}{\sqrt{2^{1/n} - 1}}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 7:** Bandwidth reduction factor and comment on the gain–bandwidth trade-off. **(1 mark)**

---

#### Solution to B(b) — Step by Step

**Step 1:** Overall voltage gain (linear):

$$
A_{V(\text{total})} = 50 \times 80 = 4000
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 2:** Convert to dB:

$$
A_{V(\text{total})}\,[\text{dB}] = 20 \log_{10}(4000) = 72.04\,\text{dB}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 3:** Average single-stage upper cutoff from bandwidths (BW ≈ $f_H$ for audio):

$$
f_{H1} \approx 30\,\text{kHz} \quad \text{(using the smaller value for worst case)}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 4:** Apply n = 2 cascade factor:

$$
f_{H(\text{overall})} = 30 \times 0.6436 \approx 19.31\,\text{kHz}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 5:** Overall bandwidth:

$$
BW_{\text{overall}} \approx 19.31\,\text{kHz}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 6:** Gain-Bandwidth Product:

$$
GBP = 4000 \times 19.31 \times 10^{3} = 7.72 \times 10^{7}\,\text{Hz}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 7:** Comment — GBP increases with cascading when stages are assumed independent. **(1 mark)**

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in this Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the loading factor** $\frac{R_{i2}}{R_{o1}+R_{i2}}$ in part-(a) of gain derivations → loses up to **3 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing linear and dB gains** — students often write $A_{V(dB)} = A_{V1} \times A_{V2}$ instead of $A_{V1(dB)} + A_{V2(dB)}$. This is a **full-mark killer**.
> 3. **Wrong cascade factor** — using $f_{H2} = f_{H1}/2$ for n = 2 instead of $0.6436 \cdot f_{H1}$. Always use $\sqrt{2^{1/n}-1}$.
> 4. **Mixing up the GBW invariance concept** — it is constant for a *single device with internal Miller pole*, not for arbitrary cascades with fixed per-stage gain.
> 5. **Ignoring the lower cutoff shift** — many students compute only $f_H$ shift, missing $f_L$ shrinkage. The KTU 2024 scheme often asks for the **full BW**.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- A **multistage amplifier** is a cascade of two or more amplifier stages.
- The **overall voltage gain** is the **product** of individual stage gains (linear) or the **sum** of individual stage gains (dB).
- The **overall bandwidth** is **smaller** than the bandwidth of any single stage.
- For **n identical cascaded stages**, the upper cutoff **shrinks** by factor $\sqrt{2^{1/n}-1}$, and the lower cutoff **expands** by the reciprocal factor.
- **Gain-Bandwidth Product** is constant for a *given transistor and bias*, not strictly for arbitrary cascades.
- **RC coupling** is most common for audio (20 Hz – 20 kHz).
- **Transformer coupling** provides impedance matching but is bulky and frequency-limited.
- **Direct coupling** passes DC but suffers from drift; used in op-amps.
- **Cascode (CE-CB)** provides high gain with bandwidth improvement.
- **Rise time** $t_r \approx 0.35 / BW$ — a direct time-domain consequence of bandwidth.
- A **CE–CC** combination provides high input impedance and low output impedance — used in op-amp I/O stages.
- KTU 2024 examinations **favour numerical problems** involving 2-stage cascades with frequency-response calculations.
- **Always show the loading effect** in cascade derivations — it is the most frequently tested KTU concept.
- The **phase shift** in a CE–CE cascade is **360°** (i.e., effectively 0° in mid-band).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differential Amplifier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES & CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Differential Amplifier

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Differential Amplifier (DA)** is a fundamental two-input active analog circuit block that amplifies the **algebraic difference** between two input voltage signals applied to its two input terminals, while actively **rejecting any signal common to both inputs**. It is the foundational building block of nearly every operational amplifier, comparator, mixer, multiplier, and analog multiplier IC manufactured today.

Mathematically, the ideal output is expressed as:

$$V_{out} = A_{d}(V_{1} - V_{2})$$

where:
- $A_d$ = Differential Mode Gain
- $V_1, V_2$ = The two input signal voltages
- $A_{cm} = 0$ for an ideal DA (common-mode rejection)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The differential amplifier is the **core analog building block** that enables the function of every modern Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp). Mastering this topic is mandatory because it directly feeds into Op-Amp internal architecture in later modules.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of a **Differential Amplifier like a weighing balance (a "Tulangan" or "Kanta")**:

- The two **input terminals** are the two pans of the balance.
- The balance **only tilts (produces output)** based on the *weight difference* between the two pans — this is the **differential signal**.
- If both pans are loaded equally (a **common-mode signal**, like wind pushing both pans equally), the balance does NOT tilt — this signal is **rejected**.
- The **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** is essentially a measure of "how stubbornly" the balance ignores equal pushes and only responds to true weight differences.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (in Bold)

- **Differential Mode Gain ($A_d$ or $A_{DM}$):** Amplification of the difference signal.
- **Common Mode Gain ($A_c$ or $A_{CM}$):** Amplification of the common signal (ideally zero).
- **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR):** Defined as $\rho = \vert A_{d} / A_{c} \vert$, usually expressed in **decibels (dB)**: $CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(\vert A_{d}/A_{c} \vert)$.
- **Input Common-Mode Range:** Range of common-mode voltage for normal operation.
- **Tail Current ($I_{EE}$ or $I_{SS}$):** The biasing current source at the emitter/source side.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why CMRR matters in practice:** In noisy industrial environments (factories, motor control panels), **electromagnetic interference (EMI)** and **mains hum (50 Hz)** appear equally on both signal lines. A good differential amplifier cancels this noise completely while preserving the actual small differential signal — this is the principle behind the famous **Instrumentation Amplifier** used in ECG/EKG machines.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Topological Configurations of a Differential Amplifier

A differential amplifier can be implemented in **four distinct ways** based on how the input and output are taken:

| # | Input Type | Output Type | Configuration Name | Typical Use |
|---|------------|-------------|---------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Differential | Differential | **Dual Input Balanced Output (DIBO)** | Internal op-amp stages |
| 2 | Differential | Single-ended | **Dual Input Unbalanced Output (DIUO)** | Op-amp output stage |
| 3 | Single-ended | Differential | **Single Input Balanced Output (SIBO)** | Driver stages |
| 4 | Single-ended | Single-ended | **Single Input Unbalanced Output (SIUO)** | Discrete DA stage |

### 2.2 The EMITTER-COUPLED BJT Differential Amplifier (ECDA)

This is the **most heavily tested topology** in KTU examinations. The circuit uses a **current source ($I_{EE}$)** at the emitter tail and two perfectly matched transistors $Q_1$ and $Q_2$.

#### Operating Principles — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Biasing the Tail Current Source:** A constant current $I_{EE}$ flows through the common emitter node. By KCL:
$$I_{EE} = I_{E1} + I_{E2}$$
2. **Symmetry assumption:** When both inputs are equal ($V_{B1} = V_{B2}$), the circuit is perfectly symmetric, so $I_{E1} = I_{E2} = I_{EE}/2$.
3. **Differential Mode Operation ($V_{id} = V_{B1} - V_{B2}$):** The tail current $I_{EE}$ gets *steered* — one transistor conducts more, the other less. The differential output is proportional to $V_{id}$.
4. **Common Mode Operation ($V_{ic} = (V_{B1}+V_{B2})/2$):** Both transistors conduct equally, no current steering occurs, and ideally no output change is produced.

### 2.3 Transfer Characteristics of BJT Differential Amplifier

The differential output current $\Delta I_C$ is related to $V_{id}$ through a **tanh** function:

$$I_{C1} - I_{C2} = I_{EE} \cdot \tanh\left(\frac{V_{id}}{2V_T}\right)$$

where $V_T = kT/q \approx \mathbf{26\,mV}$ at room temperature ($300\,K$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linear Range:** For small differential inputs ($\vert V_{id} \vert \ll 2V_T$), $\tanh(x) \approx x$, giving linear amplification. For large inputs ($\vert V_{id} \vert \gg 2V_T$), the output saturates to $\pm I_{EE}$ (transistor switches fully ON/OFF). This is why differential amplifiers are also used as **limiters** and **phase detectors** in communication circuits.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these formulas — they are the high-frequency KTU exam items.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Condition |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-----------|
| 1 | Tail Current (DC Bias) | $I_{EE} = I_{C1} + I_{C2} \approx 2I_C$ (matched pair) | A | DC, both inputs equal |
| 2 | Emitter Current (per side) | $I_E = I_{EE}/2$ | A | Symmetric bias |
| 3 | Differential Mode Gain (DIBO) | $A_d = -g_m R_C$ | V/V (unitless) | Matched pair, $R_E \to \infty$ |
| 4 | Common Mode Gain | $A_{c} = -\dfrac{R_C}{2R_E}$ | V/V | With emitter degeneration $R_E$ |
| 5 | CMRR (DIBO) | $\rho = \dfrac{\vert A_d \vert}{\vert A_c \vert} = \dfrac{2 g_m R_E R_C}{R_C} = 2 g_m R_E$ | ratio (or dB) | $R_E$ = tail source resistance |
| 6 | CMRR in dB | $CMRR_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(\rho)$ | dB | Always in dB for KTU |
| 7 | Differential Input Resistance | $R_{id} = 2 r_\pi$ | $\Omega$ | BJT input |
| 8 | Common-Mode Input Resistance | $R_{ic} = \dfrac{r_\pi + 2R_E}{2}$ | $\Omega$ | BJT input |
| 9 | Transconductance | $g_m = I_C / V_T$ | A/V | $V_T \approx 26$ mV at 300 K |
| 10 | MOSFET Diff. Mode Gain | $A_d = -g_m R_D$ | V/V | MOSFET version |
| 11 | MOSFET Common Mode Gain | $A_{c} = -\dfrac{g_m R_D}{1 + 2 g_m R_{SS}}$ | V/V | $R_{SS}$ = tail resistance |
| 12 | MOSFET CMRR | $\rho = 1 + 2 g_m R_{SS}$ | ratio (or dB) | Ideal current source gives $\rho \to \infty$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Operational Amplifiers (LM741, TL081, OP27):** The input stage of every op-amp is a differential pair.
- **Instrumentation Amplifiers (AD620, INA128):** Three op-amps form a precision DA for ECG, weigh scales, strain gauges.
- **RF/Mixer Circuits:** Gilbert Cell multiplier uses two cross-coupled DAs for frequency translation in cell phones.
- **Comparators (LM311):** Compare two analog voltages and produce a digital output — a saturated DA.
- **Active Filters:** State-variable filters use DAs as their active elements.
- **Audio Preamplifiers:** Balanced audio lines (XLR) use DA topology to reject hum and EMI in studios.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Expectation:** Every derivation below must be reproduced step-by-step in the answer booklet. Skipping algebraic steps is the **#1 reason for losing marks** in 14-mark Part B questions. The Examiner's pitfall is discussed in Section 5.

---

### 3.1 Derivation: DC Bias Analysis of BJT Emitter-Coupled DA

**Given:** Symmetric BJT pair with tail current source $I_{EE}$, matched transistors, $V_{BE(on)} = 0.7$ V, $V_T = 26$ mV.

**Step 1 — Apply KVL at the emitter node.** With both bases grounded ($V_{B1} = V_{B2} = 0$):

$$V_E = 0 - V_{BE} = -0.7\,\text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Total current through tail.**

$$I_{EE} = I_{E1} + I_{E2}$$

**Step 3 — By symmetry of the matched pair:**

$$I_{E1} = I_{E2} = \frac{I_{EE}}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Collector current (using $I_C \approx I_E$ for BJT):**

$$I_{C1} = I_{C2} = \frac{\alpha I_{EE}}{2} \approx \frac{I_{EE}}{2}$$

**Step 5 — DC Collector Voltage:**

$$V_{C1} = V_{C2} = V_{CC} - I_C R_C = V_{CC} - \frac{I_{EE} R_C}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Differential Output Voltage (idle/balanced):**

$$V_{od} = V_{C1} - V_{C2} = 0\,\text{V}$$

> *Conclusion:* At quiescent (no input), the output is at DC balance — the circuit is designed to operate around this point.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Differential Mode (DM) Gain for DIBO Configuration

**Step 1 — Apply differential input $v_{id} = v_{B1} - v_{B2}$.** Due to symmetry, the emitter node stays at AC ground (virtual ground concept) **only if the tail source is ideal ($R_E \to \infty$)**.

**Step 2 — Voltage at the common emitter node.** With ideal current source $R_{EE} \to \infty$, AC emitter voltage $v_e = 0$.

**Step 3 — Input loop equation for $Q_1$ (AC small-signal):**

$$v_{B1} - v_e = v_{id}/2 = i_{b1} \cdot r_\pi$$

Therefore:

$$i_{b1} = \frac{v_{id}}{2 r_\pi}$$

**Step 4 — Collector current of $Q_1$:**

$$i_{c1} = \beta \cdot i_{b1} = g_m \cdot \frac{v_{id}}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Output voltage at collector 1:**

$$v_{c1} = -i_{c1} R_C = -\frac{g_m R_C}{2} v_{id}$$

**Step 6 — By symmetry, $v_{c2} = +\dfrac{g_m R_C}{2} v_{id}$.**

**Step 7 — Differential output voltage:**

$$v_{od} = v_{c1} - v_{c2} = -g_m R_C \cdot v_{id}$$

**Step 8 — Differential mode gain:**

$$\boxed{A_{d} = \frac{v_{od}}{v_{id}} = -g_m R_C}$$

> *Physical Interpretation:* The gain is **single-ended in magnitude** but **negative** because when $V_{B1}$ rises, $Q_1$ conducts more, pulling its collector down.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Common Mode (CM) Gain

**Step 1 — Apply common-mode input $v_{ic}$ to BOTH bases.** Both transistors see the same input. By symmetry, both collector currents change equally.

**Step 2 — Half-circuit analysis.** Consider one half. The emitter is no longer at AC ground; it is connected through $2R_{EE}$ to ground (because the symmetry point doubles the tail resistance for common-mode signals).

**Step 3 — Input loop (AC):**

$$v_{ic} = i_{b} r_\pi + (1+\beta) i_{b} \cdot 2R_{EE}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $i_b$:**

$$i_{b} = \frac{v_{ic}}{r_\pi + 2(1+\beta)R_{EE}}$$

**Step 5 — Collector current:**

$$i_{c} = \beta \cdot i_{b} = \frac{\beta v_{ic}}{r_\pi + 2(1+\beta)R_{EE}}$$

**Step 6 — Output voltage (single-ended):**

$$v_{c} = -i_c R_C = -\frac{\beta R_C}{r_\pi + 2(1+\beta)R_{EE}} v_{ic}$$

**Step 7 — Approximation using $g_m = \beta/r_\pi$ and $2(1+\beta)R_{EE} \gg r_\pi$:**

$$A_{c} = -\frac{g_m R_C}{1 + 2g_m R_{EE}}$$

For moderate $R_{EE}$:

$$\boxed{A_{c} \approx -\frac{R_C}{2 R_{EE}}}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)

**Step 1 — Start from the gain expressions:**

$$A_{d} = -g_m R_C$$

$$A_{c} = -\frac{R_C}{2R_{EE}}$$

**Step 2 — Form the ratio:**

$$\rho = \left\lvert \frac{A_{d}}{A_{c}} \right\rvert = \left\lvert \frac{-g_m R_C}{-R_C / (2R_{EE})} \right\rvert = 2 g_m R_{EE}$$

**Step 3 — Final CMRR in dB:**

$$\boxed{CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(2 g_m R_{EE})}$$

> *Engineering Insight:* To make CMRR as large as possible, we need either **large $g_m$ (large bias current)** or **very large tail resistance $R_{EE}$**. The limit is reached when $R_{EE}$ is replaced by an **ideal current source** (e.g., a current mirror), giving $R_{EE} \to \infty$ and **CMRR $\to \infty$**.

---

### 3.5 Derivation: MOSFET Source-Coupled DA Gain (Brief)

**Step 1 — MOSFET transconductance:** $g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) I_D}$

**Step 2 — By symmetry, $I_{D1} = I_{D2} = I_{SS}/2$**

**Step 3 — Differential mode gain (DIBO):**

$$A_{d} = -g_m R_D$$

**Step 4 — Common mode gain with tail resistance $R_{SS}$:**

$$A_{c} = -\frac{g_m R_D}{1 + 2 g_m R_{SS}}$$

**Step 5 — CMRR:**

$$\rho = 1 + 2 g_m R_{SS}$$

**Step 6 — In dB:**

$$CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(1 + 2 g_m R_{SS})$$

---

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard Style)

**Problem:** A BJT emitter-coupled differential amplifier has $I_{EE} = 1$ mA, $R_C = 10$ k$\Omega$, $V_T = 26$ mV, and uses an ideal current source. Find: (i) Quiescent collector currents, (ii) Differential mode gain, (iii) CMRR.

**Solution:**

(i) $I_{C1} = I_{C2} = I_{EE}/2 = 0.5$ mA

$$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}{26 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.01923\,\text{A/V} = 19.23\,\text{mA/V}$$

(ii) $A_d = -g_m R_C = -19.23 \times 10^{-3} \times 10 \times 10^3 = -192.3$ V/V

(iii) Ideal current source $\Rightarrow A_c = 0 \Rightarrow CMRR \to \infty$

In practice with $R_{EE} = 50$ k$\Omega$:

$$CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(2 \times 0.01923 \times 50 \times 10^3) = 20 \log_{10}(1923) = 65.7\,\text{dB}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture (BJT Emitter-Coupled DA)

```mermaid
graph LR
    VB1["V_B1 Input 1"] --> B1["Base of Q1"]
    VB2["V_B2 Input 2"] --> B2["Base of Q2"]
    B1 --> Q1["Q1 BJT Transistor"]
    B2 --> Q2["Q2 BJT Transistor"]
    Q1 --> RC1["R_C at Collector 1"]
    Q2 --> RC2["R_C at Collector 2"]
    RC1 --> VCC["VCC Supply"]
    RC2 --> VCC
    RC1 --> OUT1["V_C1 Output 1"]
    RC2 --> OUT2["V_C2 Output 2"]
    Q1 --> EMIT["Common Emitter Node"]
    Q2 --> EMIT
    EMIT --> TAIL["I_EE Current Source Tail"]
    TAIL --> VEE["VEE Negative Supply"]
    DIFF["Differential Output V_od = V_C1 - V_C2"] --> OUT1
    DIFF --> OUT2
```

### 4.2 Signal Flow and Mode-Splitting Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    IN1["V_B1"] --> SUMA["Differential Splitter"]
    IN2["V_B2"] --> SUMA
    SUMA --> DM["Differential Mode Signal V_id = V1 - V2"]
    SUMA --> CM["Common Mode Signal V_ic = V1+V2 over 2"]
    DM --> ADM["Differential Amplifier Block Gain A_d"]
    CM --> ACM["Common Mode Amplifier Block Gain A_c"]
    ADM --> OUT["V_out = A_d * V_id + A_c * V_ic"]
    ACM --> OUT
    OUT --> RESULT["Final Output with CMRR filtering"]
```

### 4.3 The Four Topological Configurations (Functional View)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["Input Topology"] --> A1["Differential Input V1 V2"]
    A --> A2["Single-Ended Input V1 only"]
    B["Output Topology"] --> B1["Balanced Output V_C1 and V_C2"]
    B --> B2["Single-Ended Output V_C1 only"]
    A1 --> C1["DIUO"]
    A1 --> C2["DIBO"]
    A2 --> C3["SIUO"]
    A2 --> C4["SIBO"]
    B1 --> C2
    B1 --> C4
    B2 --> C1
    B2 --> C3
```

### 4.4 CMRR Functional Behavior — Conceptual Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    NOISE["Common Mode Noise 50Hz EMI mains hum"] --> REJECT["Differential Amplifier with high CMRR"]
    SIGNAL["Differential Signal of interest"] --> REJECT
    REJECT --> OUT1["Amplified Desired Signal"]
    REJECT --> OUT2["Attenuated Rejected Noise"]
    OUT1 --> APP["ECG Audio Sensor Signal Output"]
    OUT2 --> APP
```

### 4.5 Functional Summary of Key Nodes

| Node | Function | Engineering Role |
|------|----------|------------------|
| $Q_1, Q_2$ | Matched pair | Provides matched gain paths |
| $R_C$ | Collector load | Converts current to voltage (gain setting) |
| $I_{EE}$ | Tail current source | Sets DC bias and provides high CM rejection |
| $v_e$ | Common emitter node | AC ground for DM; effective $2R_{EE}$ for CM |
| $V_{od}$ | Differential output | The desired amplified signal |
| $V_{oc}$ | Common-mode output | Ideally zero; the noise we want to reject |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (2-Mark / 3-Mark Short Answer Type)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**Define Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR) of a differential amplifier. What is its ideal value?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
CMRR is defined as the ratio of the magnitude of the differential mode gain ($A_d$) to the magnitude of the common-mode gain ($A_c$). It quantifies the ability of the differential amplifier to reject signals common to both inputs.

$$CMRR = \rho = \left\lvert \frac{A_{d}}{A_{c}} \right\rvert \quad ; \quad CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left\lvert \frac{A_{d}}{A_{c}} \right\rvert$$

The **ideal value of CMRR is infinity ($\infty$)**, achieved when $A_c = 0$, meaning the amplifier perfectly rejects any common-mode signal.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
**What is a "virtual ground" in the context of a differential amplifier's emitter node?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
In an ideal emitter-coupled (or source-coupled) differential amplifier driven by a differential input, the common emitter node acts as a **virtual ground** for differential-mode signals. This is because the symmetry of the matched pair ensures that any voltage rise at one emitter is exactly cancelled by the voltage fall at the other, keeping the common emitter node at a constant potential. This allows us to treat the emitter as AC ground when computing the differential-mode gain, simplifying the analysis to a common-emitter amplifier on each side, giving $A_d = -g_m R_C$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14-Mark Module Choice) — Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each Part B question is internally optional. The student must answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full. Attempting both forfeits the marks of the first one.

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 MARKS** `[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an **emitter-coupled BJT differential amplifier** under differential mode input. Derive the expression for its differential mode gain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a BJT differential amplifier, derive the expression for **common-mode gain** and **CMRR**. Show that replacing the tail resistor with a constant current source increases the CMRR. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

**Circuit Description (2 marks):**
The emitter-coupled DA consists of two matched NPN BJTs ($Q_1$ and $Q_2$) with their emitters joined together at a common node, connected to $-V_{EE}$ through a current source $I_{EE}$ (or large resistor $R_E$). The collectors are tied to $+V_{CC}$ through equal resistors $R_C$. The two inputs are $v_{B1}$ and $v_{B2}$; the differential output is $v_{od} = v_{C1} - v_{C2}$.

**Operation (2 marks):**
When a differential signal $v_{id} = v_{B1} - v_{B2}$ is applied, $Q_1$ conducts more and $Q_2$ conducts less, or vice versa. The current $I_{EE}$ is steered between the two transistors. By symmetry, the common emitter node stays at AC ground.

**Derivation of $A_d$ (3 marks):** (See Section 3.2 of these notes)
- $v_e = 0$ (virtual ground)
- $i_{c1} = g_m \cdot v_{id}/2$
- $v_{c1} = -g_m R_C \cdot v_{id}/2$ and $v_{c2} = +g_m R_C \cdot v_{id}/2$
- $v_{od} = v_{c1} - v_{c2} = -g_m R_C \cdot v_{id}$
- $\boxed{A_d = -g_m R_C}$

---

**(b) [7 marks]**

**Derivation of $A_c$ (3 marks):** (See Section 3.3)
- For common-mode input $v_{ic}$ applied to both bases, the symmetry makes the equivalent emitter resistance $2R_E$ in the half-circuit.
- $A_c = -R_C / (2R_E)$

**Derivation of CMRR (2 marks):** (See Section 3.4)
- $CMRR = \vert A_d / A_c \vert = 2 g_m R_E$
- $CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(2 g_m R_E)$

**Effect of Current Source (2 marks):**
Replacing the tail resistor $R_E$ with an **ideal current source** makes $R_E \to \infty$, which drives $A_c \to 0$ and hence $CMRR \to \infty$. Even with a practical current source (e.g., current mirror with output impedance in M$\Omega$ range), the CMRR is dramatically higher than with a simple resistor, which is why **all modern op-amp input stages use active current source biasing**.

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 MARKS (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Draw the circuit of a **MOSFET source-coupled differential amplifier** and derive expressions for its **differential mode gain** and **common mode gain**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A BJT emitter-coupled differential amplifier has the following parameters: $I_{EE} = 2$ mA, $R_C = 5$ k$\Omega$, $R_E = 100$ k$\Omega$, $V_T = 26$ mV. Calculate: (i) the differential mode gain, (ii) the common mode gain, (iii) CMRR in dB, and (iv) the output voltage for $v_{B1} = 5$ mV and $v_{B2} = 3$ mV in differential mode. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

**Circuit (2 marks):** Two matched NMOS transistors $M_1$ and $M_2$ with sources tied together to a current source $I_{SS}$ (or $R_{SS}$). Drains connected to $V_{DD}$ via $R_D$. Inputs at the gates, outputs at the drains.

**Differential Mode Gain (3 marks):**
By symmetry, source node is AC ground for DM. So each half acts as a common-source amplifier with input $v_{gs} = v_{id}/2$.

$$v_{od} = -g_m R_D \cdot v_{id} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{A_d = -g_m R_D}$$

**Common Mode Gain (2 marks):**
For CM signal $v_{ic}$ at both gates, the source resistance is doubled in the half-circuit:

$$A_c = -\frac{g_m R_D}{1 + 2 g_m R_{SS}} \approx -\frac{R_D}{2R_{SS}} \text{ for large } R_{SS}$$

---

**(b) [7 marks]**

**Given:** $I_{EE} = 2$ mA, $R_C = 5$ k$\Omega$, $R_E = 100$ k$\Omega$, $V_T = 26$ mV.

**(i) Differential Mode Gain (1 mark):**

$$I_C = I_{EE}/2 = 1\,\text{mA}$$

$$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{26 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.03846\,\text{A/V} = 38.46\,\text{mA/V}$$

$$A_d = -g_m R_C = -38.46 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 \times 10^3 = -192.3\,\text{V/V}$$

**(ii) Common Mode Gain (1 mark):**

$$A_c = -\frac{R_C}{2 R_E} = -\frac{5 \times 10^3}{2 \times 100 \times 10^3} = -0.025\,\text{V/V}$$

**(iii) CMRR in dB (2 marks):**

$$CMRR = \left\lvert \frac{A_d}{A_c} \right\rvert = \left\lvert \frac{-192.3}{-0.025} \right\rvert = 7692$$

$$CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(7692) = 20 \times 3.886 = 77.7\,\text{dB}$$

**(iv) Output Voltage (3 marks):**

$$v_{id} = v_{B1} - v_{B2} = 5 - 3 = 2\,\text{mV}$$

$$v_{od} = A_d \cdot v_{id} = -192.3 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} = -0.3846\,\text{V} \approx -384.6\,\text{mV}$$

The output voltage is approximately **$-385$ mV** (negative polarity because $V_{B1} > V_{B2}$ pulls $Q_1$'s collector low).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in $A_d$:** Many students write $A_d = g_m R_C$ instead of $A_d = -g_m R_C$. The sign matters for **phase relationship questions** — KTU examiners explicitly test this. **Lose up to 1 mark.**
>
> 2. **Using $V_T = 25$ mV in numerical problems** when the question states $V_T = 26$ mV. Always **use the value given in the problem statement**. Marks are lost on consistency.
>
> 3. **Writing CMRR in ratio form** when the question asks for CMRR in dB. KTU examiners **deduct 1–2 marks** if the unit is wrong or missing.
>
> 4. **Confusing $R_E$ and $2R_E$** in the CM gain formula. The factor of 2 arises from the half-circuit analysis of symmetry and is **frequently missed** by students. **Lose up to 2 marks.**
>
> 5. **Not drawing the circuit diagram** or drawing it without labels ($V_{CC}, V_{EE}, R_C, I_{EE}$): KTU mandates a "neat labelled diagram" worth **2–3 marks** in 14-mark questions. **Always reproduce the circuit clearly.**
>
> 6. **Skipping steps in the derivation:** The examiner will not award full marks for stating the final formula. Show every algebraic step (KCL, KVL, Ohm's Law substitution). **Lose up to 3 marks per derivation.**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this as your last-night revision sheet before the KTU exam.**

- [x] A **Differential Amplifier** amplifies the **difference** between two inputs and rejects the **common** part.
- [x] Output: $V_{out} = A_d \cdot V_{id} + A_c \cdot V_{ic}$.
- [x] **CMRR** $= \vert A_d / A_c \vert$ (ratio) or $20\log_{10}\vert A_d / A_c \vert$ (dB).
- [x] **DIBO differential gain:** $A_d = -g_m R_C$ (BJT) or $-g_m R_D$ (MOSFET).
- [x] **Common-mode gain:** $A_c = -R_C / (2R_E)$ (BJT, with tail resistance $R_E$).
- [x] **CMRR (BJT):** $\rho = 2 g_m R_E$ → in dB: $20\log_{10}(2 g_m R_E)$.
- [x] **CMRR (MOSFET):** $\rho = 1 + 2 g_m R_{SS}$ → in dB: $20\log_{10}(1 + 2 g_m R_{SS})$.
- [x] **Ideal current source** ⇒ $R_E \to \infty$ ⇒ $A_c \to 0$ ⇒ **CMRR $\to \infty$**.
- [x] **Transconductance:** $g_m = I_C / V_T$ for BJT; $g_m = \sqrt{2 \mu_n C_{ox} (W/L) I_D}$ for MOSFET.
- [x] **Thermal voltage** $V_T \approx 26$ mV at $T = 300$ K.
- [x] **Virtual ground at emitter/source** holds ONLY for differential mode with a matched pair.
- [x] **Tail current bias** $I_{EE} = I_{C1} + I_{C2}$ → for matched pair, each transistor carries $I_{EE}/2$.
- [x] **Transfer characteristic:** $I_{C1} - I_{C2} = I_{EE} \tanh(V_{id} / 2V_T)$.
- [x] **Linear range:** $\vert V_{id} \vert \ll 2V_T \approx 52$ mV at room temperature.
- [x] **Applications:** Op-amps, instrumentation amplifiers, comparators, mixers (Gilbert cell), active filters, balanced audio.
- [x] **CMRR improves with:** Higher tail current, larger $R_E$ (or ideal current source), better transistor matching.
- [x] **Exam tip:** Always draw a labelled circuit diagram first; derive gain step-by-step; state the final boxed answer; quote the units (V/V or dB).
- [x] **Negative sign in $A_d$:** Do NOT forget it — it indicates the **180° phase inversion** at the active output.

---

**End of Notes — Differential Amplifier (Module 4)**

topics.title: Characteristics of Op-Amps (Gain, Bandwidth, Slew Rate, CMRR, Offset Voltage, Offset Current, Comparison of Ideal and Practical Op-Amp IC 741)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES & CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Characteristics of Op-Amps (Gain, Bandwidth, Slew Rate, CMRR, Offset Voltage, Offset Current, Comparison of Ideal and Practical Op-Amp IC 741)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)?

An **Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)** is a direct-coupled, high-gain electronic voltage amplifier with differential inputs and a single-ended output. It is the fundamental building block of modern analog electronics, used in amplifiers, filters, comparators, integrators, differentiators, oscillators, and ADC/DAC interfacing circuits.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> An op-amp is a multi-stage, directly coupled amplifier whose performance approximates an *ideal amplifier* — characterized by **infinite voltage gain**, **infinite input impedance**, **zero output impedance**, and **infinite bandwidth**.

The op-amp is essentially a sophisticated integrated circuit (IC) that takes the *difference* between two input voltages and amplifies it, producing an output voltage that can swing close to its supply rails (typically **±15 V** for the standard IC 741).

## 1.2 The Two Input Terminals — The Heart of the Op-Amp

An op-amp has **two input terminals** and **one output terminal**:

- **Inverting Input (V⁻ or V₁)**: Signal applied here is *inverted* (180° phase shift) at the output.
- **Non-Inverting Input (V⁺ or V₂)**: Signal applied here appears *in phase* (0° phase shift) at the output.
- **Output (V₀)**: The amplified result, given by the famous op-amp equation:

$$V_o = A_{OL}(V^+ - V^-)$$

where $A_{OL}$ is the **open-loop differential voltage gain**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The "Perfect Hearing Aid"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Selective Listener"**
> 
> Imagine an op-amp as an *extremely picky, super-powered hearing aid* for an elderly person at a noisy family dinner:
> 
> 1. **Differential Input (V⁺ − V⁻)**: The op-amp only "listens" to the **difference** between two voices at the table — the grandchild's voice (V⁺) versus the TV's noise (V⁻). It ignores everything else.
> 
> 2. **Massive Gain (A)**: Whatever difference it hears, it *amplifies enormously* — like a hearing aid that turns a whisper into a shout.
> 
> 3. **High Input Impedance (Zᵢₙ)**: It doesn't "load" the microphone (input source) — like a listener who doesn't interrupt the speaker.
> 
> 4. **Low Output Impedance (Zₒᵤₜ)**: It can drive heavy speakers (low-impedance loads) without breaking a sweat.
> 
> 5. **CMRR**: When both voices shout the same thing at the same time (common-mode signal), the op-amp *ignores it completely* — it only cares about the *difference*.
> 
> 6. **Slew Rate**: There is a physical limit to how *fast* the output speaker cone can move — even if the input changes instantly, the output takes a finite time to catch up.

## 1.4 Block Diagram of an Op-Amp

A standard op-amp consists of **four cascaded stages**:

1. **Input Stage** — Dual-input, balanced-output differential amplifier (provides high input impedance and differential gain).
2. **Gain Stage** — Dual-input, unbalanced-output amplifier (provides high voltage gain and level shifting).
3. **Level Shifter** — Emitter follower / current source to shift DC level (ensures zero output for zero input).
4. **Output Stage** — Push-pull complementary emitter follower (provides low output impedance and high current drive).

## 1.5 Why Do We Need to Study "Characteristics"?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:**
> The **ideal op-amp** does not exist physically. Every *real* op-amp (like the popular **IC 741**) deviates from the ideal model. These deviations are quantified as **op-amp characteristics** or **performance parameters**:
> 
> - **Gain** (A_OL, A_CL)
> - **Bandwidth** & Gain-Bandwidth Product
> - **Slew Rate** (SR)
> - **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio** (CMRR)
> - **Input Offset Voltage** (V_io)
> - **Input Bias Current** (I_B)
> - **Input Offset Current** (I_io)
> 
> Understanding these characteristics is **critical for KTU board exams** because they determine the *accuracy, speed, and linearity* of every op-amp circuit.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Open-Loop Voltage Gain (A_OL)

The **open-loop gain** is the differential voltage gain of the op-amp *without any external feedback*.

$$A_{OL} = \frac{V_o}{V_d} = \frac{V_o}{V^+ - V^-}$$

**For IC 741:** $A_{OL} \approx 2 \times 10^5$ (≈ 106 dB).

Because this gain is *enormously large* and *unstable*, we **never use an op-amp in open-loop configuration** for linear amplification. Instead, we use **negative feedback** to define a precise, stable gain.

## 2.2 Closed-Loop Gain (A_CL)

When **negative feedback** is applied through a feedback network (e.g., resistors), the closed-loop gain is:

$$A_{CL} = \frac{A_{OL}}{1 + A_{OL}\beta}$$

where $\beta$ is the **feedback factor** (the fraction of output fed back to the inverting input).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** If $A_{OL}\beta \gg 1$, then $A_{CL} \approx \dfrac{1}{\beta}$ — the gain becomes dependent *only* on the external passive components, not on the op-amp's internal parameters. This is the **magic of negative feedback**.

For a **non-inverting amplifier**:

$$A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}$$

For an **inverting amplifier**:

$$A_{CL} = -\frac{R_f}{R_1}$$

## 2.3 Bandwidth (BW) and Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP)

Every op-amp acts as a **low-pass filter** — its gain rolls off as frequency increases. The frequency response is:

$$A(f) = \frac{A_{OL}}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{f}{f_B}\right)^2}}$$

where $f_B$ is the **break frequency** (or −3 dB frequency) where the open-loop gain drops to $A_{OL}/\sqrt{2}$.

### Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP)

$$GBP = A_{CL} \times f_c = \text{constant} = f_T$$

where:
- $f_c$ = **closed-loop bandwidth** (upper cutoff frequency at gain $A_{CL}$)
- $f_T$ = **unity-gain bandwidth** (frequency at which open-loop gain = 1, i.e., 0 dB)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For IC 741:** $f_T = 1 \text{ MHz}$. So if you design a circuit with $A_{CL} = 100$, then $f_c = 1\,\text{MHz}/100 = 10\,\text{kHz}$. **Trade-off alert!** Higher gain ⇒ Lower bandwidth.

## 2.4 Slew Rate (SR)

The **slew rate** is the *maximum rate of change of the output voltage* in response to a step input:

$$SR = \left.\frac{dV_o}{dt}\right|_{max} \quad \text{(in V/μs)}$$

**For IC 741:** $SR = 0.5 \text{ V/μs}$ (a famously slow op-amp!).

### Slew Rate Limitation Equation

If the output is a sine wave $V_o(t) = V_m \sin(2\pi f t)$:

$$\frac{dV_o}{dt} = 2\pi f V_m \cos(2\pi f t)$$

The maximum rate of change occurs at $t = 0$:

$$SR_{required} = 2\pi f V_m$$

For **no slew-induced distortion**, the op-amp's SR must exceed this:

$$SR_{op-amp} \geq 2\pi f_{max} V_{m,max}$$

This gives the **maximum usable frequency** (full-power bandwidth):

$$f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **If** $f_{input} > f_{max}$, the output waveform becomes **triangular** instead of sinusoidal — this is called **slew-induced distortion (SID)**. A very common KTU 14-mark question!

## 2.5 Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)

A **common-mode signal** is one that appears *identically* on both inputs (e.g., noise, thermal drift). An ideal op-amp should completely reject it.

Define:
- **Differential-mode gain:** $A_d = V_o / V_d$ (where $V_d = V^+ - V^-$)
- **Common-mode gain:** $A_c = V_o / V_c$ (where $V_c = (V^+ + V^-)/2$)

$$CMRR = \left|\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right|$$

In decibels:

$$CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left|\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right|$$

**For IC 741:** $CMRR = 90 \text{ dB}$ (i.e., $A_d / A_c \approx 31{,}623$).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** Higher CMRR ⇒ Better rejection of noise, interference, and thermal drift. This is *critical* in instrumentation amplifiers and sensor signal conditioning.

## 2.6 Input Offset Voltage (V_io)

In a *real* op-amp, due to **mismatches** in the internal transistors, the output is **not exactly zero** when both inputs are grounded. The **input offset voltage** is defined as:

$$V_{io} = \frac{V_o|_{V^+ = V^- = 0}}{A_{OL}}$$

In other words, $V_{io}$ is the *small differential DC voltage* that must be applied at the inputs to make $V_o = 0$.

**For IC 741:** $V_{io} = 2 \text{ mV (typical)}$, up to **6 mV (maximum)**.

## 2.7 Input Bias Current (I_B) and Input Offset Current (I_io)

The op-amp's input stage uses **BJT base currents** (or FET gate leakage currents) that must flow into the input terminals. These are not zero.

- **Input bias current** = *average* of the two input currents:

$$I_B = \frac{I_{B1} + I_{B2}}{2}$$

- **Input offset current** = *difference* between the two input currents:

$$I_{io} = |I_{B1} - I_{B2}|$$

**For IC 741:**
- $I_B = 80 \text{ nA (typical)}$
- $I_{io} = 20 \text{ nA (typical)}$

## 2.8 Other Important Characteristics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value (IC 741) |
|---|---|---|
| Input Resistance | $R_{in}$ | **2 MΩ** |
| Output Resistance | $R_{out}$ | **75 Ω** |
| Power Supply | $V_{CC}, V_{EE}$ | **±15 V** |
| Power Consumption | $P_D$ | **85 mW** |
| Common-Mode Input Range | $V_{CM}$ | **±13 V** |

## 2.9 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Must-Memorize" Formula Table for KTU 2024 Exams**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | IC 741 Typical |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Open-Loop Gain | $A_{OL} = V_o / (V^+ - V^-)$ | V/V (or dB) | $2 \times 10^5$ (≈106 dB) |
| 2 | Closed-Loop Gain (Non-Inverting) | $A_{CL} = 1 + R_f/R_1$ | V/V | Depends on $R_f, R_1$ |
| 3 | Closed-Loop Gain (Inverting) | $A_{CL} = -R_f/R_1$ | V/V | Depends on $R_f, R_1$ |
| 4 | Closed-Loop Gain w/ Feedback | $A_{CL} = A_{OL}/(1+A_{OL}\beta)$ | V/V | ≈ $1/\beta$ |
| 5 | Gain-Bandwidth Product | $GBP = A_{CL} \cdot f_c = f_T$ | Hz | 1 MHz |
| 6 | Slew Rate (Definition) | $SR = (dV_o/dt)_{max}$ | V/μs | 0.5 V/μs |
| 7 | Slew Rate (Sinusoidal) | $SR = 2\pi f V_m$ | V/μs | 0.5 V/μs |
| 8 | Maximum Frequency (no distortion) | $f_{max} = SR/(2\pi V_m)$ | Hz | Depends on $V_m$ |
| 9 | CMRR (linear) | $CMRR = \vert A_d/A_c \vert$ | ratio | ≈31,623 |
| 10 | CMRR (in dB) | $CMRR_{dB} = 20\log_{10}\vert A_d/A_c \vert$ | dB | 90 dB |
| 11 | Input Offset Voltage | $V_{io} = V_o/A_{OL}\vert_{inputs\,gnd}$ | V or mV | 2 mV |
| 12 | Input Bias Current | $I_B = (I_{B1}+I_{B2})/2$ | nA or μA | 80 nA |
| 13 | Input Offset Current | $I_{io} = \vert I_{B1}-I_{B2} \vert$ | nA or μA | 20 nA |
| 14 | Output Voltage (General) | $V_o = A_{OL}(V^+ - V^-)$ | V | — |
| 15 | Power Supply Rejection Ratio | $PSRR = (ΔV_{CC}/ΔV_o)\cdot A_{OL}$ | dB | 96 dB |

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where these characteristics matter in real engineering:**
> 
> - **Audio amplifiers** → Need **high slew rate** and **wide bandwidth** to handle high-frequency music without distortion.
> - **ECG / EEG / Instrumentation** → Need **high CMRR** to reject 50/60 Hz power-line interference.
> - **Precision integrators** → Need **low input bias current** to prevent charge leakage and output drift.
> - **Sensor signal conditioning** → Need **low offset voltage** to maintain DC accuracy.
> - **High-speed data acquisition** → Need **wide bandwidth** and **high slew rate**.
> - **Active filters** → Need predictable **GBP** for designing filter cutoffs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Closed-Loop Gain with Negative Feedback

### Statement
Derive the closed-loop voltage gain of an op-amp with negative feedback, given open-loop gain $A_{OL}$ and feedback factor $\beta$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Consider an op-amp with open-loop gain $A_{OL}$ and a feedback network that samples the output $V_o$ and feeds back a fraction $\beta V_o$ to the inverting input.

**Step 2:** The differential input to the op-amp is reduced by the feedback:

$$V_d = V_{in} - \beta V_o$$

This is the **superposition** of the input signal and the feedback signal at the inverting node.

**Step 3:** The op-amp amplifies this differential input by its open-loop gain:

$$V_o = A_{OL} \cdot V_d = A_{OL}(V_{in} - \beta V_o)$$

**Step 4:** Expand the right-hand side:

$$V_o = A_{OL} \cdot V_{in} - A_{OL} \cdot \beta \cdot V_o$$

**Step 5:** Group the $V_o$ terms on the left side:

$$V_o + A_{OL} \beta V_o = A_{OL} V_{in}$$

$$V_o(1 + A_{OL} \beta) = A_{OL} V_{in}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for the closed-loop gain $A_{CL} = V_o / V_{in}$:

$$\boxed{A_{CL} = \frac{A_{OL}}{1 + A_{OL} \beta}}$$

**Step 7:** In the limit $A_{OL}\beta \gg 1$ (the usual case for IC 741):

$$A_{CL} \approx \frac{A_{OL}}{A_{OL}\beta} = \frac{1}{\beta}$$

This proves that the closed-loop gain depends **only on the external feedback network**, not the op-amp's internal non-idealities. **Negative feedback trades gain for stability and predictability.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Slew Rate Limitation for a Sinusoidal Input

### Statement
A sinusoidal input of peak amplitude $V_m$ and frequency $f$ is applied to an op-amp. Show that the slew rate requirement is $SR = 2\pi f V_m$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The output voltage (assuming no distortion) is:

$$V_o(t) = V_m \sin(2\pi f t)$$

**Step 2:** Compute the time derivative (which is the instantaneous slew rate):

$$\frac{dV_o}{dt} = V_m \cdot \cos(2\pi f t) \cdot 2\pi f = 2\pi f V_m \cos(2\pi f t)$$

**Step 3:** Find the **maximum** value of this derivative. Since $\cos(\theta) \in [-1, +1]$, the maximum is:

$$\left(\frac{dV_o}{dt}\right)_{max} = 2\pi f V_m$$

**Step 4:** For **no slew-induced distortion**, the op-amp's internal SR must be at least equal to this maximum:

$$\boxed{SR_{op-amp} \geq 2\pi f_{max} V_m}$$

**Step 5:** Rearranging, the **maximum usable frequency** (also called *full-power bandwidth* $f_p$):

$$f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_m}$$

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

**Q:** An op-amp has $SR = 0.5 \text{ V/μs}$. If the input is a sine wave of peak amplitude $V_m = 5 \text{ V}$, find the maximum frequency at which the output remains undistorted.

**Solution:**

Convert SR to V/s: $SR = 0.5 \text{ V/μs} = 0.5 \times 10^6 \text{ V/s} = 5 \times 10^5 \text{ V/s}$.

$$f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_m} = \frac{5 \times 10^5}{2\pi \times 5} = \frac{5 \times 10^5}{31.416}$$

$$f_{max} \approx 15{,}915 \text{ Hz} \approx 15.92 \text{ kHz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Takeaway:** With IC 741's slow slew rate of 0.5 V/μs, you cannot amplify audio signals (which go up to 20 kHz) at high amplitudes without distortion. This is why IC 741 is unsuitable for high-fidelity audio — designers use **LF353** or **TL082** (SR = 16 V/μs) or **NE5532** (SR = 9 V/μs) instead.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: CMRR in Decibels

### Statement
If $A_d = 10^5$ and $A_c = 10$, find the CMRR in dB.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Compute the linear ratio:

$$CMRR = \left|\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right| = \frac{10^5}{10} = 10^4$$

**Step 2:** Convert to dB using the formula:

$$CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(10^4) = 20 \times 4 = 80 \text{ dB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always show the **logarithm step explicitly**. Marks are awarded for the substitution, not just the final number. A common error is using $10\log_{10}$ instead of $20\log_{10}$ (the $20$ comes from converting amplitude ratio to power ratio: $20\log = 10\log \cdot 2$, since power $\propto$ voltage²).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Output Offset Voltage Due to Input Offset

### Statement
Derive the total output offset voltage $V_{oo}$ at the output of an op-amp due to input offset voltage $V_{io}$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** The input offset voltage $V_{io}$ is the differential DC input that produces $V_o = 0$. Therefore, when both inputs are grounded externally, an *internal* $V_{io}$ exists between the inputs, and:

$$V_{oo} = A_{OL} \cdot V_{io}$$

**Step 2:** However, $A_{OL}$ is *not* a stable, well-defined quantity. To make the offset specification more useful, datasheets define a quantity called the **input offset voltage** at the input pins. Then, in a closed-loop configuration with noise gain $A_n$:

$$\boxed{V_{oo} = (1 + R_f/R_1) \cdot V_{io}}$$

This is the **output offset voltage** due only to $V_{io}$, in a non-inverting amplifier of gain $(1 + R_f/R_1)$.

### Numerical Example

**Q:** For IC 741, $V_{io} = 6 \text{ mV (max)}$. A non-inverting amplifier uses $R_f = 99 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_1 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$. Find the output offset voltage.

**Solution:**

$$A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 1 + \frac{99\,\text{k}}{1\,\text{k}} = 100$$

$$V_{oo} = A_{CL} \cdot V_{io} = 100 \times 6 \text{ mV} = 600 \text{ mV} = 0.6 \text{ V}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Insight:** A 6 mV input offset becomes **0.6 V at the output** when gain is 100! This is why *offset nulling* is essential in precision DC amplifier circuits.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Output Offset Due to Input Bias Current

### Statement
Derive the output offset voltage of an inverting amplifier due to input bias current $I_B$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** In a standard inverting amplifier, the non-inverting input is grounded through a *compensation resistor* $R_{comp} = R_1 \parallel R_f$. Without this resistor, the bias current $I_{B2}$ flowing into the non-inverting input creates a voltage $V^+ = I_{B2} \cdot R_{comp}$ (with no current path, $V^+$ is undefined → output saturates).

**Step 2:** With the compensation resistor:

$$V^+ = -I_{B2} \cdot R_{comp}$$

**Step 3:** The voltage at the inverting input is held at $V^- = V^+$ (virtual short). The bias current $I_{B1}$ flowing into the inverting input must be supplied through $R_f$ from the output:

$$V_o = V^- - I_{B1} \cdot R_f = V^+ - I_{B1} \cdot R_f$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $V^+$:

$$V_o = -I_{B2} R_{comp} - I_{B1} R_f$$

**Step 5:** Define the average bias current $I_B = (I_{B1} + I_{B2})/2$ and offset current $I_{io} = I_{B1} - I_{B2}$. With matched inputs ($I_{B1} \approx I_{B2} = I_B$):

$$V_o = -I_B(R_{comp} + R_f) - \frac{I_{io}}{2} R_f$$

**Step 6:** If $R_{comp} = R_1 \parallel R_f$, the $I_B$ term partially cancels. The *remaining* offset is:

$$V_{oo,\,bias} \approx \pm \frac{I_{io}}{2} \cdot R_f$$

### Numerical Example

**Q:** For IC 741, $I_{io} = 200 \text{ nA (max)}$. An inverting amplifier uses $R_f = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$. Find the output offset due to input offset current.

**Solution:**

$$V_{oo} = \frac{I_{io}}{2} \cdot R_f = \frac{200 \times 10^{-9}}{2} \times 100 \times 10^3 = 10 \text{ mV}$$

---

| 4 | Bandwidth (BW) | **∞ (infinite)** | **1 MHz (unity gain)** |

| 8 | Input Bias Current ($I_B$) | **0 (zero)** | **80 nA (typ), 500 nA (max)** |
| 9 | Input Offset Current ($I_{io}$) | **0 (zero)** | **20 nA (typ), 200 nA (max)** |

## 3.7 Derivation 6: Total Output Offset Voltage

### Statement
The **total output offset** in a non-inverting amplifier is the sum of contributions from $V_{io}$ and $I_{io}$.

### Derivation

**Step 1:** From Derivation 4, the offset due to $V_{io}$:

$$V_{oo,\,1} = \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) \cdot V_{io}$$

**Step 2:** From Derivation 5 (adapted for non-inverting topology), the offset due to $I_{io}$ flowing through $R_f$:

$$V_{oo,\,2} = I_{io} \cdot R_f$$

**Step 3:** The total worst-case output offset is the **sum of magnitudes** (since the two sources may add or subtract):

$$\boxed{V_{oo,\,total} = \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) \cdot V_{io} + I_{io} \cdot R_f}$$

### Numerical Example

**Q:** For IC 741: $V_{io} = 6 \text{ mV}$, $I_{io} = 200 \text{ nA}$. Non-inverting amplifier: $R_f = 99 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_1 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$. Find total output offset.

**Solution:**

$$V_{oo,\,1} = (1 + 99) \times 6 \text{ mV} = 100 \times 6 = 600 \text{ mV}$$

$$V_{oo,\,2} = 200 \times 10^{-9} \times 99 \times 10^3 = 19.8 \text{ mV}$$

$$V_{oo,\,total} = 600 + 19.8 \approx 619.8 \text{ mV} \approx 0.62 \text{ V}$$

## 4.4 Frequency Response of Op-Amp (Bode Plot Concept)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOW["Low Frequency Region"]
        LF["f < f_c<br/>Gain = A_CL (Flat, 0 dB/decade)"]
    end
    subgraph MID["Transition Region"]
        TR["f = f_c<br/>Gain = A_CL/√2<br/>(-3 dB point)"]
    end
    subgraph HIGH["High Frequency Region"]
        HF["f > f_c<br/>Gain falls at -20 dB/decade<br/>f_T = A_CL × f_c"]
    end
    LOW --> MID --> HIGH

    style LF fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style TR fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style HF fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c
```

The op-amp's gain is **flat** (constant) up to the **cutoff frequency** $f_c$, after which it rolls off at **−20 dB/decade** (i.e., gain drops by a factor of 10 for every 10× increase in frequency). This continues until gain reaches **1 (0 dB)** at $f_T$.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | 3 Marks | CO: 2 | Bloom: Remember/Understand]**

**Define the following terms for an op-amp:**
**(a)** Input offset voltage
**(b)** Input bias current
**(c)** Slew rate

### Model Answer:

**(a) Input Offset Voltage (V_io):**
It is the small differential DC voltage that must be applied between the two input terminals of an op-amp to make the output voltage exactly zero. It arises due to unavoidable mismatches in the internal transistors of the input differential stage.

**For IC 741:** $V_{io} = 2 \text{ mV (typical)}, 6 \text{ mV (maximum)}$

$$\boxed{V_{io} = \left.\frac{V_o}{A_{OL}}\right|_{V^+=V^-=0}}$$

**(b) Input Bias Current (I_B):**
It is the average of the DC currents flowing into the two input terminals of the op-amp. It is required to bias the input transistors (BJTs or FETs) in the active region.

$$\boxed{I_B = \frac{I_{B1} + I_{B2}}{2}}$$

**For IC 741:** $I_B = 80 \text{ nA (typical)}$

**(c) Slew Rate (SR):**
It is the maximum rate of change of the output voltage per unit time, in response to a large-signal step input. It is measured in V/μs.

$$\boxed{SR = \left.\frac{dV_o}{dt}\right|_{max}}$$

**For IC 741:** $SR = 0.5 \text{ V/}\mu\text{s}$

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | 3 Marks | CO: 2 | Bloom: Understand/Apply]**

**Explain the concept of CMRR in op-amps. State its typical value for IC 741 and explain why it is important in instrumentation applications.**

### Model Answer:

**Definition of CMRR:**
The **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)** is the ratio of the differential-mode gain $A_d$ to the common-mode gain $A_c$ of an op-amp. It quantifies the op-amp's ability to reject signals that appear identically (in phase and amplitude) on both input terminals.

$$\boxed{CMRR = \left|\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right| \quad \text{or} \quad CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left|\frac{A_d}{A_c}\right| \text{ dB}}$$

**For IC 741:** $CMRR = 90 \text{ dB}$ (i.e., $A_d / A_c \approx 31{,}623$)

**Importance in Instrumentation:**
In sensor-based applications (e.g., ECG, thermocouples, strain gauges), the useful signal is a *small differential* voltage buried in *large common-mode noise* (such as 50 Hz power-line pickup or ground-loop interference). A high CMRR ensures that this unwanted common-mode noise is rejected, and only the desired differential signal is amplified. **For example**, in an ECG amplifier, a typical ECG signal is 1 mV, while 50 Hz interference can be 10 V — requiring very high CMRR (>100 dB) for clean extraction.

---

## 📝 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Full Descriptive)

### ⭐ CHOICE A — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 | 14 Marks | CO: 2 | Bloom: Apply/Analyze]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the internal architecture of an op-amp. List the four stages and state the function of each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the following op-amp characteristics and state their typical values for IC 741:
- (i) Open-loop voltage gain
- (ii) Input impedance
- (iii) Output impedance
- (iv) CMRR
- (v) Slew rate
- (vi) Gain-bandwidth product
- (vii) Input offset voltage **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution (a) — Internal Architecture

An op-amp is a multi-stage, directly-coupled amplifier that approximates an *ideal amplifier*. Its internal architecture consists of **four cascaded stages**:

**Block Diagram (textual representation):**

```
   V1 ──┬──┐                                                
        │  │         ┌─────────┐      ┌──────────┐    ┌──────────┐
   V2 ──┼──┼────────►│ Stage 1 │─────►│ Stage 2  │───►│ Stage 3  │──┬──► V_o
        │  │         │ Input   │      │  Gain    │    │ Output   │  │
   V+ ──┘  │         │ Stage   │      │  Stage   │    │  Stage   │  │
   V- ─────┘         └─────────┘      └──────────┘    └──────────┘  │
                         │                                            │
                         └── (feedback to V-) ◄───── V_o ◄───────────┘
```

**The Four Stages:**

1. **Input Stage (Dual-Input, Balanced-Output Differential Amplifier):**
   - Provides high input impedance, differential gain, and common-mode rejection.
   - Implemented using a differential pair (Q1, Q2) with current-mirror load.
   - **Contributes:** $Z_{in}$, $A_d$, $CMRR$, $I_B$, $V_{io}$.

2. **Gain Stage (Dual-Input, Unbalanced-Output CE Amplifier with Active Load):**
   - Provides the *bulk* of the voltage gain (≈ 100 dB).
   - Uses Darlington pair with active current source as load for high gain.
   - **Contributes:** $A_{OL}$ (open-loop gain).

3. **Level Shifter (Emitter Follower with Constant Current Source):**
   - Shifts the DC level to ensure $V_o = 0$ when both inputs are zero.
   - Prevents saturation of subsequent stages.
   - **Contributes:** DC operating point stability.

4. **Output Stage (Class-AB Complementary Push-Pull Emitter Follower):**
   - Provides low output impedance and high current drive capability.
   - Uses complementary BJTs (NPN + PNP) in a totem-pole configuration.
   - **Contributes:** $Z_{out}$, output voltage swing, current drive.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** Always draw the *block diagram* — even a rough one. Examiners award 1-2 marks just for the diagram, separate from explanation marks.

---

### Model Solution (b) — IC 741 Characteristics

| # | Characteristic | Definition | IC 741 Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | **Open-Loop Voltage Gain ($A_{OL}$)** | Gain of op-amp without feedback: $A_{OL} = V_o/(V^+-V^-)$ | $2 \times 10^5$ (≈106 dB) |
| (ii) | **Input Impedance ($Z_{in}$)** | Resistance seen at the input terminals (differential) | $2 \text{ M}\Omega$ |
| (iii) | **Output Impedance ($Z_{out}$)** | Resistance seen at the output terminal | $75 \text{ }\Omega$ |
| (iv) | **CMRR** | Ratio of differential to common-mode gain (in dB) | $90 \text{ dB}$ |
| (v) | **Slew Rate (SR)** | Max rate of change of output: $SR = (dV_o/dt)_{max}$ | $0.5 \text{ V/}\mu\text{s}$ |
| (vi) | **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP)** | $GBP = A_{CL} \times f_c = f_T$ | $1 \text{ MHz}$ |
| (vii) | **Input Offset Voltage ($V_{io}$)** | Differential DC input needed to make $V_o = 0$ | $2 \text{ mV (typ)}, 6 \text{ mV (max)}$ |

---

### ⭐ CHOICE B — Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 | 14 Marks | CO: 2 | Bloom: Apply/Analyze]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the maximum operating frequency of an op-amp output for an undistorted sinusoidal signal. State the condition under which slew-induced distortion occurs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A non-inverting amplifier uses IC 741 with $R_f = 99 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $R_1 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$. The input is a sine wave of $V_m = 5 \text{ V}$. Calculate:
- (i) The closed-loop gain.
- (ii) The maximum operating frequency to avoid slew-induced distortion.
- (iii) The output offset voltage due to $V_{io} = 5 \text{ mV}$.
- (iv) The bandwidth (using $f_T = 1 \text{ MHz}$). **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution (a) — Slew Rate Derivation

**Statement:**
When a sinusoidal input of peak amplitude $V_m$ and frequency $f$ is applied to an op-amp, the output is:

$$V_o(t) = V_m \sin(2\pi f t)$$

The instantaneous rate of change of the output is:

$$\frac{dV_o}{dt} = 2\pi f V_m \cos(2\pi f t)$$

The **maximum** rate of change occurs when $\cos(2\pi f t) = 1$:

$$\left(\frac{dV_o}{dt}\right)_{max} = 2\pi f V_m$$

For the output to follow the input *without distortion*, the op-amp's internal slew rate must be at least equal to this maximum:

$$\boxed{SR_{op-amp} \geq 2\pi f_{max} V_m}$$

Rearranging for the **maximum undistorted frequency** (full-power bandwidth):

$$\boxed{f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_m}}$$

**Condition for Slew-Induced Distortion (SID):**
SID occurs when:

$$2\pi f V_m > SR_{op-amp}$$

i.e., the *required* slew rate exceeds the *available* slew rate. The op-amp's output stage cannot charge/discharge internal capacitances fast enough. The output becomes **triangular** (linear ramps) instead of sinusoidal.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** SID is a *non-linear* distortion, distinct from linear harmonic distortion. It is dominant for *large signals* at *high frequencies*. Reducing input amplitude or switching to a faster op-amp eliminates SID.

---

### Model Solution (b) — Numerical Problem

**Given:**
- $R_f = 99 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_1 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$
- $V_m = 5 \text{ V}$ (input peak)
- $V_{io} = 5 \text{ mV}$
- $f_T = 1 \text{ MHz}$, $SR = 0.5 \text{ V/}\mu\text{s} = 5 \times 10^5 \text{ V/s}$

**(i) Closed-Loop Gain:**

For a non-inverting amplifier:

$$A_{CL} = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 1 + \frac{99\,\text{k}}{1\,\text{k}} = 1 + 99 = \boxed{100}$$

**(ii) Maximum Operating Frequency (no slew-induced distortion):**

Convert SR: $0.5 \text{ V/}\mu\text{s} = 0.5 \times 10^6 \text{ V/s}$

$$f_{max} = \frac{SR}{2\pi V_m} = \frac{5 \times 10^5}{2\pi \times 5} = \frac{5 \times 10^5}{10\pi} = \frac{10^5}{2\pi}$$

$$f_{max} = \frac{100{,}000}{6.2832} \approx 15{,}915 \text{ Hz} \approx \boxed{15.92 \text{ kHz}}$$

**(iii) Output Offset Voltage due to $V_{io}$:**

For a non-inverting amplifier with gain $A_{CL} = 100$:

$$V_{oo} = A_{CL} \cdot V_{io} = 100 \times 5 \text{ mV} = \boxed{500 \text{ mV} = 0.5 \text{ V}}$$

**(iv) Bandwidth:**

Using the gain-bandwidth product relation:

$$A_{CL} \times f_c = f_T$$

$$100 \times f_c = 1 \text{ MHz}$$

$$f_c = \frac{10^6}{100} = 10{,}000 \text{ Hz} = \boxed{10 \text{ kHz}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-Check:** Notice that $f_c = 10 \text{ kHz} < f_{max} = 15.92 \text{ kHz}$. This means the *bandwidth limit* is reached *before* the *slew rate limit* for this particular signal amplitude. The op-amp's bandwidth is the *first* constraint. This is a classic KTU concept-test scenario!

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units for Slew Rate** — Many students use $0.5 \text{ V/}\mu\text{s}$ directly in $f_{max} = SR/(2\pi V_m)$ formula without converting to V/s. **Always convert!** $0.5 \text{ V/}\mu\text{s} = 5 \times 10^5 \text{ V/s}$.
> 
> 2. **Confusing input bias current with input offset current** — $I_B$ is the *average*, $I_{io}$ is the *difference*. Marks deducted if definitions are swapped.
> 
> 3. **Using $10\log_{10}$ instead of $20\log_{10}$ for CMRR in dB** — CMRR is a *voltage* ratio, so use the $20\log$ formula. (Use $10\log$ only for *power* ratios.)
> 
> 4. **Not stating units in numerical answers** — Always write $\text{kHz}$, $\text{mV}$, $\text{V/}\mu\text{s}$, etc. Marks are explicitly allotted for units in KTU 2024 scheme.
> 
> 5. **Treating "Gain" and "Gain in dB" as the same** — $A = 2 \times 10^5$ vs. $A_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(2 \times 10^5) = 106 \text{ dB}$. Many students write one when the question asks for the other.
> 
> 6. **Forgetting to mention "finite" vs "infinite" in ideal vs practical comparison** — Vague answers like "gain is less" score partial marks. Be specific: "ideal = ∞, IC 741 = $2 \times 10^5$".
> 
> 7. **Slew Rate vs Bandwidth confusion** — These are *different* limits. Slew rate is a *large-signal* limit (depends on $V_m$). Bandwidth is a *small-signal* limit (independent of $V_m$).
> 
> 8. **Missing the block diagram in 14-mark questions** — A 14-mark question on op-amp characteristics *always* expects a diagram. 2-3 marks are reserved for it.

---

## 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — Read 5 Minutes Before the Exam!**

### ✅ Core Concept Bullets

- [x] **Op-Amp Definition:** A multi-stage, directly-coupled, high-gain differential amplifier with two inputs (inverting V⁻ and non-inverting V⁺) and one output.
- [x] **Core Equation:** $V_o = A_{OL}(V^+ - V^-)$
- [x] **Open-Loop Gain (A_OL):** Gain without feedback. IC 741: $2 \times 10^5$ ≈ 106 dB.
- [x] **Closed-Loop Gain (A_CL):** $A_{CL} = A_{OL}/(1 + A_{OL}\beta) \approx 1/\beta$ (with deep negative feedback).
- [x] **Bandwidth (BW):** Frequency range over which gain is within −3 dB of $A_{OL}$.
- [x] **Gain-Bandwidth Product (GBP):** $A_{CL} \times f_c = f_T$ = **constant** (1 MHz for IC 741).
- [x] **Slew Rate (SR):** Maximum rate of change of output. IC 741: 0.5 V/μs. $SR = 2\pi f V_m$.
- [x] **Full-Power Bandwidth:** $f_{max} = SR/(2\pi V_m)$.
- [x] **CMRR:** Ratio of differential to common-mode gain. IC 741: 90 dB. Higher = better noise rejection.
- [x] **Input Offset Voltage (V_io):** Differential DC input that makes $V_o = 0$. IC 741: 2-6 mV.
- [x] **Input Bias Current (I_B):** Average of DC input currents. IC 741: 80-500 nA.
- [x] **Input Offset Current (I_io):** Difference of input currents. IC 741: 20-200 nA.
- [x] **Negative Feedback Magic:** Stabilizes gain, reduces distortion, increases bandwidth, controls input/output impedance.

### ✅ Formula Quick-Reference

- [x] $A_{CL} = A_{OL}/(1 + A_{OL}\beta) \approx 1/\beta$
- [x] $A_{CL} = 1 + R_f/R_1$ (non-inverting); $-R_f/R_1$ (inverting)
- [x] $A_{CL} \times f_c = f_T$
- [x] $SR = 2\pi f V_m$ ⇒ $f_{max} = SR/(2\pi V_m)$
- [x] $CMRR_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(A_d/A_c)$
- [x] $V_{io} = V_o/A_{OL} \vert_{inputs\,gnd}$
- [x] $I_B = (I_{B1} + I_{B2})/2$
- [x] $I_{io} = \vert I_{B1} - I_{B2} \vert$
- [x] Total output offset: $V_{oo} = (1 + R_f/R_1) V_{io} + I_{io} R_f$

### ✅ IC 741 Numbers to Memorize

- [x] $A_{OL} = 2 \times 10^5$ (106 dB)
- [x] $Z_{in} = 2 \text{ M}\Omega$
- [x] $Z_{out} = 75 \text{ }\Omega$
- [x] $CMRR = 90 \text{ dB}$
- [x] $SR = 0.5 \text{ V/}\mu\text{s}$
- [x] $f_T = 1 \text{ MHz}$
- [x] $V_{io} = 2 \text{ mV (typ)}, 6 \text{ mV (max)}$
- [x] $I_B = 80 \text{ nA (typ)}, 500 \text{ nA (max)}$
- [x] $I_{io} = 20 \text{ nA (typ)}, 200 \text{ nA (max)}$
- [x] $PSRR = 96 \text{ dB}$
- [x] $V_{supply} = \pm 15 \text{ V}$
- [x] $P_D = 85 \text{ mW}$

### ✅ Ideal vs Practical — Must-Memorize Differences

- [x] Ideal: $A_{OL} = \infty$, $Z_{in} = \infty$, $Z_{out} = 0$, BW = $\infty$, CMRR = $\infty$, SR = $\infty$, $V_{io} = 0$, $I_B = 0$.
- [x] IC 741: $A_{OL} = 2 \times 10^5$, $Z_{in} = 2 \text{ M}\Omega$, $Z_{out} = 75 \text{ }\Omega$, $f_T = 1 \text{ MHz}$, CMRR = 90 dB, SR = 0.5 V/μs, $V_{io} = 2-6$ mV, $I_B = 80-500$ nA.

### ✅ Conceptual Shortcuts

- [x] **High gain requires low bandwidth** (and vice versa) — fixed by GBP.
- [x] **High input signal + high frequency = slew-induced distortion.**
- [x] **High CMRR is essential for instrumentation** (rejects noise).
- [x] **Offset voltage at input becomes amplified offset at output.**
- [x] **Compensation resistor** $R_{comp} = R_1 \parallel R_f$ at the non-inverting input minimizes bias-current-induced offset.
- [x] **Negative feedback** is the *only* practical way to use an op-amp as a linear amplifier.

---
