topics.title: Applications of Op-Amps - Scale Changer, Sign Changer, Adder/Summing Amplifier, Subtractor, Integrator, Differentiator, Wien Bridge Oscillator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES & CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Applications of Op-Amps - Scale Changer, Sign Changer, Adder/Summing Amplifier, Subtractor, Integrator, Differentiator, Wien Bridge Oscillator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Operational Amplifier as a "Universal Building Block"

The **Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)** is a high-gain, DC-coupled electronic voltage amplifier with differential inputs and a single-ended output. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, beyond being a simple amplifier, the op-amp is treated as a *universal analogue signal-processing block* whose behaviour is entirely defined by its **external feedback network** rather than its internal transistor architecture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Focus:** The syllabus specifically concentrates on **linear applications** of an *ideal op-amp* (assumed to have **infinite open-loop gain $A = \infty$**, **infinite input impedance**, **zero output impedance**, and **zero output for zero differential input** — i.e., $V_d = 0$ between the two input terminals). This is the cornerstone assumption used in every derivation below.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Op-Amp as a "Smart Lever"

Imagine an op-amp as a *perfect mechanical balance scale*:

- The **non-inverting (+)** and **inverting (−)** terminals are the two pans.
- The op-amp constantly and instantly tilts its output to whatever voltage is necessary to **re-establish balance** ($V_+ = V_-$).
- The **feedback network** acts like ropes connecting the output back to one of the pans. Depending on how you tie these ropes, you can make the output:
  - **Multiply** the input by a constant (Scale Changer)
  - **Flip the sign** of the input (Sign Changer)
  - **Add** multiple inputs together (Adder)
  - **Subtract** one input from another (Subtractor)
  - **Integrate** the input over time (Integrator)
  - **Differentiate** the input with respect to time (Differentiator)
  - **Generate a continuous sine wave** without any external input (Wien Bridge Oscillator)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Exam Insight:** All KTU problems assume an **ideal op-amp**. Therefore, two golden rules apply to every circuit:
> 1. **Virtual Short Concept:** $V_+ = V_-$ (because $A \to \infty$ and $V_o$ is finite, so $V_d \to 0$).
> 2. **No Current into Input Terminals:** $I_+ = I_- = 0$ (because input impedance is infinite).

## 1.3 Block-Level Summary of the Seven Applications

| # | Application | Core Function | Output Equation (Ideal) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Scale Changer** | Multiplies input by a constant $-k$ | $V_o = -k V_i$ |
| 2 | **Sign Changer** | Inverts the polarity of input | $V_o = -V_i$ |
| 3 | **Inverting Adder** | Adds multiple inputs with scaling | $V_o = -\sum k_n V_n$ |
| 4 | **Non-Inverting Adder** | Adds multiple positive inputs | $V_o = \sum V_n$ (when balanced) |
| 5 | **Subtractor** | Difference between two inputs | $V_o = k(V_2 - V_1)$ |
| 6 | **Integrator** | Output is integral of input | $V_o = -k \int V_i \, dt$ |
| 7 | **Differentiator** | Output is derivative of input | $V_o = -k \frac{dV_i}{dt}$ |
| 8 | **Wien Bridge Oscillator** | Generates sustained sine wave | $f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ |

---

## 1.4 Why These Applications Matter in Real Engineering

- **Audio Mixing Consoles** use summing amplifiers to blend multiple microphone/line signals.
- **Analog Computers** (historical) used integrators and differentiators to solve differential equations.
- **Signal Conditioning** in instrumentation requires subtractors to eliminate common-mode noise (e.g., ECG machines).
- **Function Generators and Lab Instruments** use Wien Bridge oscillators to produce stable test tones at **1 kHz** and **10 kHz**.
- **Active Filters** are built from integrator and differentiator stages.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Golden Rules of Ideal Op-Amp Analysis

Every derivation in this module begins with these three assumptions (memorise verbatim):

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule 1 — Infinite Open-Loop Gain ($A = \infty$):** Forces the differential input voltage $V_d = V_+ - V_-$ to approach zero.
>
> **Rule 2 — Infinite Input Impedance ($R_{in} = \infty$):** No current flows into either input terminal, i.e., $I_+ = I_- = 0$.
>
> **Rule 3 — Zero Output Impedance ($R_{out} = 0$):** Output voltage is independent of load connected.

## 2.2 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown of Each Application

### 2.2.1 Scale Changer (Inverting Amplifier)

- **Topology:** Input $V_i$ is applied through resistor $R_1$ to the inverting (−) terminal; feedback resistor $R_f$ connects output back to the (−) terminal. The non-inverting (+) terminal is grounded.
- **Operation:** The closed-loop gain is determined entirely by the *ratio* of external resistors, not by the op-amp itself. By adjusting $R_f/R_1$, any scale factor $-k$ can be achieved.
- **Limit:** The output cannot exceed the op-amp's saturation voltage $\pm V_{sat}$ (typically $\pm 13\,\text{V}$ for $\pm 15\,\text{V}$ supplies).

### 2.2.2 Sign Changer

- A **special case** of the scale changer where the scale factor $k = 1$.
- **Realisation:** Set $R_f = R_1 = R$ in the inverting amplifier. The closed-loop gain becomes exactly $-1$, so $V_o = -V_i$.
- **Application:** Used in phase-inversion stages, polarity-reversal circuits, and as a building block for computing circuits.

### 2.2.3 Adder / Summing Amplifier

**Type A — Inverting Summing Amplifier:**
- Multiple input voltages $V_1, V_2, \ldots, V_n$ are applied through individual resistors $R_1, R_2, \ldots, R_n$ to the inverting node.
- The non-inverting terminal is grounded, forcing the inverting node to **virtual ground** ($V_- = 0$).
- Each input contributes an independent current; these currents sum at the inverting node and flow entirely through $R_f$ (KCL).
- **Advantage:** Inputs are completely isolated from each other — adjusting one input does not affect the others.

**Type B — Non-Inverting Summing Amplifier:**
- Inputs applied to the **non-inverting** terminal through resistors; feedback still applied to inverting terminal.
- Output is *positive* (non-inverted) and is **not isolated** between inputs.
- Rarely used in KTU exams; mostly covered for completeness.

### 2.2.4 Subtractor (Difference Amplifier)

- **Topology:** $V_1$ applied to inverting terminal via $R_1$, feedback $R_f$ across. $V_2$ applied to non-inverting terminal via $R_2$, with $R_3$ from (+) to ground.
- **Operation Principle:** Uses **Superposition Theorem** — analyse with $V_1 = 0$, then with $V_2 = 0$, and sum the two outputs.
- **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR):** When $R_1 = R_2$ and $R_f = R_3$ (matched), the circuit rejects any signal common to both inputs — the foundation of instrumentation amplifiers.

### 2.2.5 Integrator

- **Topology:** Replace $R_1$ of the inverting amplifier with a resistor, and $R_f$ with a **capacitor $C$**.
- **Operation:** The current through $R$ is $V_i/R$. Since this current cannot enter the op-amp, it charges the capacitor $C$, producing an output voltage that is the *negative integral* of the input.
- **Practical Problem:** At DC, the capacitor is an open circuit, so the feedback is *only capacitive*. DC input causes output to drift to saturation due to op-amp offsets.
- **Solution:** Add a large feedback resistor $R_f$ in parallel with $C$ — this is the **Practical Integrator**, which behaves as an integrator only up to a certain frequency $f_a = 1/(2\pi R_f C)$.

### 2.2.6 Differentiator

- **Topology:** Replace $R_1$ with a capacitor $C$ and $R_f$ with a resistor.
- **Operation:** The current through $C$ is $C \cdot dV_i/dt$. This current flows through $R_f$, producing an output proportional to the rate of change of input.
- **Practical Problem:** Acts as a *high-pass filter* with rising gain at high frequencies. Noise spikes (which have high $dV/dt$) get amplified enormously, causing instability.
- **Solution:** Add an input resistor $R_1$ in series with $C$ and a feedback capacitor $C_f$ in parallel with $R_f$ — the **Practical Differentiator** limits high-frequency gain.

### 2.2.7 Wien Bridge Oscillator

- **Classification:** RC sine-wave oscillator using a **non-inverting amplifier** with a *Wien Bridge* feedback network.
- **Network:** A series RC combination ($R, C$) and a parallel RC combination ($R, C$) form a frequency-selective *positive feedback* path.
- **Frequency of Oscillation:** Determined solely by the matched RC pairs.
- **Barkhausen Criterion:** Loop gain $|\beta A| = 1$ and total phase shift = $0^\circ$ (or $360^\circ$).
- **Gain Condition:** For sustained oscillation, the non-inverting amplifier must provide a gain of exactly $A = 3$, i.e., $R_f = 2 R_1$.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Memorise these formulas verbatim. In Part B (14-mark) questions, examiners expect the student to *state the formula first, then derive, then apply*. Use this table as your last-minute revision sheet.

| # | Application | Closed-Loop Gain / Output | Key Constraint / Frequency | Typical Component Choice |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Scale Changer** | $V_o = -\dfrac{R_f}{R_1} V_i$ | $k = R_f/R_1$ can be any positive real | $R_f, R_1$ from $1\,\text{k}\Omega$ to $1\,\text{M}\Omega$ |
| 2 | **Sign Changer** | $V_o = -V_i$ | $R_f = R_1 = R$ | Matched resistors (e.g., $10\,\text{k}\Omega$) |
| 3 | **Inverting Adder** | $V_o = -\left(\dfrac{R_f}{R_1} V_1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_2} V_2 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_3} V_3\right)$ | If $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_f$: $V_o = -(V_1+V_2+V_3)$ | Equal resistors for unity-weight summing |
| 4 | **Non-Inverting Adder** | $V_o = \left(1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_a}\right) \cdot \dfrac{V_1/R_1 + V_2/R_2 + V_3/R_3}{1/R_1 + 1/R_2 + 1/R_3}$ | Complex, depends on resistor matching | Used rarely |
| 5 | **Subtractor** | $V_o = \dfrac{R_f}{R_1}(V_2 - V_1)$ (when $R_1 = R_3$ and $R_f = R_2$) | Unity-gain subtractor if all four $R$ are equal | Matched resistors for CMRR |
| 6 | **Ideal Integrator** | $V_o(t) = -\dfrac{1}{RC} \displaystyle\int V_i(t)\,dt + V_o(0)$ | Lower frequency limit $f_b = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_f C}$ (practical) | $R \sim 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C \sim 0.01\,\mu\text{F}$ |
| 7 | **Practical Integrator** | Same as ideal above $f_b$, but gain rolls off at $f_a = 1/(2\pi R_f C)$ | Useful band: $f_b < f < f_a$ | $R_f \gg R$ (e.g., $R_f = 1\,\text{M}\Omega$) |
| 8 | **Ideal Differentiator** | $V_o(t) = -RC \dfrac{dV_i(t)}{dt}$ | Output saturation on sharp edges | $R \sim 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C \sim 0.01\,\mu\text{F}$ |
| 9 | **Practical Differentiator** | $V_o \approx -RC \dfrac{dV_i}{dt}$ for $f_a < f < f_b$ | $f_a = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_1 C}$, $f_b = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_f C_f}$ | $R_1 \ll R$, $C_f \ll C$ |
| 10 | **Wien Bridge Oscillator** | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Gain condition: $A \geq 3$ i.e. $R_f = 2R_1$ | $R = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C = 0.01\,\mu\text{F}$ gives $f_o \approx 1.59\,\text{kHz}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility Recap:**
> - Integrators and differentiators are the **building blocks of analog computers** (used to solve ODEs before digital computers).
> - Wien Bridge oscillators produce **low-distortion audio-frequency sine waves** for test equipment.
> - Subtractors with high CMRR form the **first stage of every ECG machine, strain gauge bridge, and thermocouple amplifier**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Convention Used Below:** Every node voltage and loop current is *labelled explicitly*. We use KCL (Kirchhoff's Current Law) at the inverting node and the virtual-short condition $V_- = V_+$ as the foundation. Each transition between equations is annotated with a **plain-English reason** in italics.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Inverting Amplifier (Scale Changer Foundation)

**Circuit Setup:**
- $V_i$ is connected through resistor $R_1$ to the inverting (−) input, node labelled $\mathbf{N}$.
- $R_f$ connects output $V_o$ back to node $\mathbf{N}$.
- Non-inverting (+) input is grounded, so $V_+ = 0$.

**Applying the Virtual Short Concept:**

Because $V_d = V_+ - V_- = 0$, and $V_+ = 0$, the inverting node is forced to the same potential — this is called **Virtual Ground**:

$$
V_- = V_+ = 0
$$

**Applying KCL at Node $\mathbf{N}$:**

Sum of currents entering node $\mathbf{N}$ = Sum of currents leaving node $\mathbf{N}$. Since no current enters the op-amp's input ($I_- = 0$), all current through $R_1$ must flow through $R_f$.

$$
I_{R_1} = I_{R_f}
$$

**Express Each Current Using Ohm's Law:**

Current through $R_1$ (from $V_i$ towards node $\mathbf{N}$):

$$
I_{R_1} = \frac{V_i - V_-}{R_1} = \frac{V_i - 0}{R_1} = \frac{V_i}{R_1}
$$

Current through $R_f$ (from node $\mathbf{N}$ towards $V_o$):

$$
I_{R_f} = \frac{V_- - V_o}{R_f} = \frac{0 - V_o}{R_f} = -\frac{V_o}{R_f}
$$

**Equating and Solving:**

$$
\frac{V_i}{R_1} = -\frac{V_o}{R_f}
$$

Multiply both sides by $-R_f$:

$$
V_o = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} V_i
$$

> **Conclusion:** The closed-loop voltage gain $A_{CL} = V_o/V_i = -R_f/R_1$. The negative sign indicates **$180^\circ$ phase inversion**, and the magnitude $R_f/R_1$ is the **scale factor** $k$. This is the foundational equation for the scale changer and sign changer.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Sign Changer

**Starting Point:** Use the inverting amplifier result:

$$
V_o = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} V_i
$$

**Apply the Sign Changer Constraint:** Set $R_f = R_1 = R$.

$$
V_o = -\frac{R}{R} V_i
$$

$$
V_o = -V_i
$$

> **Conclusion:** The output is an exact inverted replica of the input, with **unity magnitude**. The op-amp performs a pure sign reversal without any amplification or attenuation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Inverting Summing Amplifier (3-Input Adder)

**Circuit Setup:**
- Three input voltages $V_1, V_2, V_3$ are connected through resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3$ respectively to the inverting node $\mathbf{N}$.
- Feedback resistor $R_f$ connects output $V_o$ to node $\mathbf{N}$.
- Non-inverting terminal grounded.

**Virtual Short:** $V_- = V_+ = 0$ (node $\mathbf{N}$ is at virtual ground).

**KCL at Node $\mathbf{N}$:**

Sum of currents entering node = Current leaving through $R_f$ (since $I_- = 0$).

$$
\frac{V_1 - 0}{R_1} + \frac{V_2 - 0}{R_2} + \frac{V_3 - 0}{R_3} = \frac{0 - V_o}{R_f}
$$

**Simplifying:**

$$
\frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V_3}{R_3} = -\frac{V_o}{R_f}
$$

**Solving for $V_o$:**

Multiply both sides by $-R_f$:

$$
V_o = -R_f \left( \frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V_3}{R_3} \right)
$$

**Standard Form:**

$$
V_o = -\left( \frac{R_f}{R_1} V_1 + \frac{R_f}{R_2} V_2 + \frac{R_f}{R_3} V_3 \right)
$$

**Special Case (Unity-Weight Summer):** If $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_f = R$:

$$
V_o = -(V_1 + V_2 + V_3)
$$

> **Conclusion:** The output is the **negative sum** of all inputs, with each input independently scaled by the ratio $R_f/R_n$. This isolation is the key advantage over passive resistor summing.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Difference Amplifier (Subtractor)

**Circuit Setup:**
- $V_1$ applied via $R_1$ to inverting node $\mathbf{N}$.
- $R_f$ feedback between $V_o$ and node $\mathbf{N}$.
- $V_2$ applied via $R_2$ to non-inverting node $\mathbf{M}$.
- $R_3$ from node $\mathbf{M}$ to ground.

**Strategy:** Apply **Superposition Theorem**.

### Step (a): Set $V_1 = 0$ (Short $V_1$ source), Find $V_o'$ due to $V_2$ alone

With $V_1$ shorted, $R_1$ is grounded at its left end. The circuit is a **non-inverting amplifier** with $V_2$ feeding node $\mathbf{M}$ through $R_2$, and $R_3$ as the ground reference.

Voltage at non-inverting node (voltage divider, no current into op-amp):

$$
V_+ = V_2 \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}
$$

Since $V_- = V_+$ (virtual short):

$$
V_- = V_2 \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}
$$

Now apply KCL at node $\mathbf{N}$ (only $V_o'$ is active since $V_1 = 0$):

$$
\frac{0 - V_-}{R_1} = \frac{V_- - V_o'}{R_f}
$$

$$
-\frac{V_-}{R_1} = \frac{V_- - V_o'}{R_f}
$$

Multiply by $R_f$:

$$
-\frac{R_f}{R_1} V_- = V_- - V_o'
$$

Rearrange for $V_o'$:

$$
V_o' = V_- + \frac{R_f}{R_1} V_- = V_- \left( 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} \right)
$$

Substitute $V_-$:

$$
V_o' = V_2 \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} \right)
$$

### Step (b): Set $V_2 = 0$ (Short $V_2$ source), Find $V_o''$ due to $V_1$ alone

With $V_2 = 0$, both $R_2$ and $R_3$ are grounded at node $\mathbf{M}$, so $V_+ = 0$. By virtual short, $V_- = 0$ (virtual ground).

KCL at node $\mathbf{N}$:

$$
\frac{V_1 - 0}{R_1} + \frac{0 - V_o''}{R_f} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{V_1}{R_1} = \frac{V_o''}{R_f}
$$

$$
V_o'' = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} V_1
$$

### Step (c): Apply Superposition

$$
V_o = V_o' + V_o'' = V_2 \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} \right) - \frac{R_f}{R_1} V_1
$$

**Matched Resistor Condition:** Set $R_1 = R_3$ and $R_f = R_2$.

The factor $\dfrac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}$ becomes $\dfrac{R_1}{R_f + R_1}$, and $1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} = \dfrac{R_1 + R_f}{R_1}$.

So their product simplifies:

$$
\frac{R_1}{R_f + R_1} \cdot \frac{R_1 + R_f}{R_1} = 1
$$

Hence:

$$
V_o = V_2 - \frac{R_f}{R_1} V_1 = \frac{R_f}{R_1}(V_2 - V_1)
$$

If additionally $R_f = R_1$ (all four resistors equal):

$$
V_o = V_2 - V_1
$$

> **Conclusion:** With matched resistors, the output is precisely the **difference** $V_2 - V_1$, scaled by unity gain. This is the basis of every instrumentation amplifier front-end.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Ideal Integrator

**Circuit Setup:**
- Input $V_i$ applied through resistor $R$ to inverting node $\mathbf{N}$.
- Feedback element is a **capacitor $C$** connecting $V_o$ to node $\mathbf{N}$.
- Non-inverting input grounded.

**Virtual Short:** $V_- = V_+ = 0$, so node $\mathbf{N}$ is at virtual ground.

**Current Through Resistor $R$:**

$$
i_R = \frac{V_i - 0}{R} = \frac{V_i}{R}
$$

**Current Into Capacitor $C$:**

The capacitor voltage (with the convention that the current flows from node $\mathbf{N}$ towards $V_o$) is $V_C = V_- - V_o = -V_o$.

The current through a capacitor is:

$$
i_C = C \frac{dV_C}{dt} = C \frac{d(-V_o)}{dt} = -C \frac{dV_o}{dt}
$$

**Applying KCL at Node $\mathbf{N}$:**

Since $I_- = 0$, all current through $R$ flows through $C$:

$$
i_R = i_C
$$

$$
\frac{V_i}{R} = -C \frac{dV_o}{dt}
$$

Rearrange:

$$
\frac{dV_o}{dt} = -\frac{V_i}{RC}
$$

**Integrate Both Sides with Respect to Time:**

$$
\int \frac{dV_o}{dt} \, dt = -\frac{1}{RC} \int V_i \, dt
$$

$$
V_o(t) = -\frac{1}{RC} \int_{0}^{t} V_i(\tau) \, d\tau + V_o(0)
$$

Where $V_o(0)$ is the initial voltage on the capacitor at $t = 0$.

> **Conclusion:** The output voltage is proportional to the **negative time integral** of the input, scaled by $-1/RC$. If $V_i$ is a constant DC voltage $V$, then $V_o(t) = -\dfrac{V}{RC} \cdot t$, producing a linearly decreasing ramp.

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6: Practical Integrator (with Feedback Resistor)

**Modification:** Add a large resistor $R_f$ in parallel with $C$ across the feedback path.

**Total Feedback Impedance:**

$$
Z_f = R_f \parallel \frac{1}{j\omega C} = \frac{R_f}{1 + j\omega R_f C}
$$

**Closed-Loop Gain (Frequency Domain):**

$$
A(j\omega) = -\frac{Z_f}{R} = -\frac{R_f}{R(1 + j\omega R_f C)}
$$

**Magnitude Response:**

$$
\vert A(j\omega) \vert = \frac{R_f/R}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega R_f C)^2}}
$$

**Behaviour:**
- For $\omega \ll 1/(R_f C)$: gain $\approx R_f/R$ (constant, like an inverting amplifier).
- For $\omega \gg 1/(R_f C)$: gain $\approx \dfrac{1}{\omega R C}$ ($-20\,\text{dB/decade}$ integrator roll-off).

The corner frequency is:

$$
f_a = \frac{1}{2\pi R_f C}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The practical integrator behaves as an ideal integrator for frequencies **above $f_a$**, but its DC gain is **finite** ($= R_f/R$), preventing saturation from input offset voltages.

---

## 3.7 Derivation 7: Ideal Differentiator

**Circuit Setup:**
- Input $V_i$ applied through **capacitor $C$** to inverting node $\mathbf{N}$.
- Feedback element is resistor $R$.
- Non-inverting input grounded.

**Virtual Short:** $V_- = V_+ = 0$.

**Current Through Capacitor $C$:**

The voltage across $C$ is $V_i - V_- = V_i$. So:

$$
i_C = C \frac{dV_i}{dt}
$$

**Current Through Resistor $R$ (from node $\mathbf{N}$ to output):**

$$
i_R = \frac{V_- - V_o}{R} = -\frac{V_o}{R}
$$

**Applying KCL at Node $\mathbf{N}$:**

$$
i_C = i_R \quad \Rightarrow \quad C \frac{dV_i}{dt} = -\frac{V_o}{R}
$$

**Solving for $V_o$:**

$$
V_o(t) = -RC \frac{dV_i(t)}{dt}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The output is proportional to the **negative time derivative** of the input, scaled by $RC$. If $V_i$ is a ramp $V_i(t) = kt$, then $V_o = -RCk$, a constant negative voltage. If $V_i$ is a sine wave $V_i = V_m \sin(\omega t)$, then $V_o = -RC\omega V_m \cos(\omega t)$, shifted in phase by $-90^\circ$.

---

## 3.8 Derivation 8: Practical Differentiator

**Modification:** Add input resistor $R_1$ in series with $C$, and feedback capacitor $C_f$ in parallel with $R_f$.

**Transfer Function:**

$$
A(j\omega) = -\frac{R_f \parallel \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_f}}{R_1 + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C}}
$$

After simplification:

$$
A(j\omega) = -\frac{j\omega R_f C}{(1 + j\omega R_1 C)(1 + j\omega R_f C_f)}
$$

**Corner Frequencies:**

$$
f_a = \frac{1}{2\pi R_1 C}, \qquad f_b = \frac{1}{2\pi R_f C_f}
$$

**Behaviour:**
- For $f_a < f < f_b$: gain is proportional to $f$ (true differentiator region, slope $+20\,\text{dB/decade}$).
- For $f < f_a$: gain is constant (low-frequency noise is rejected).
- For $f > f_b$: gain rolls off (high-frequency noise is suppressed).

> **Conclusion:** The practical differentiator limits both low- and high-frequency noise while preserving differentiation in the usable band.

---

## 3.9 Derivation 9: Wien Bridge Oscillator — Frequency of Oscillation

**Circuit Topology:**
- **Non-inverting amplifier** with gain $A = 1 + R_f/R_1$ provides amplification.
- **Wien Bridge network** between output and non-inverting input provides frequency-selective positive feedback.

The Wien network consists of:
- **Series arm:** $R$ in series with $C$.
- **Parallel arm:** $R$ in parallel with $C$.

**Feedback Factor of the Wien Network:**

The voltage divider ratio (output to input of the network):

$$
\beta = \frac{V_f}{V_o} = \frac{Z_p}{Z_s + Z_p}
$$

where $Z_s = R + 1/(j\omega C)$ and $Z_p = R \parallel 1/(j\omega C) = \dfrac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}$.

**Compute $Z_s + Z_p$:**

$$
Z_s + Z_p = R + \frac{1}{j\omega C} + \frac{R}{1 + j\omega RC}
$$

Common denominator approach — after algebraic simplification:

$$
\beta = \frac{1}{3 + j\left(\omega RC - \frac{1}{\omega RC}\right)}
$$

**Barkhausen Phase Condition:** The total phase shift around the loop must be zero (or $360^\circ$). The non-inverting amplifier contributes $0^\circ$, so the Wien network must also contribute $0^\circ$. The imaginary part of the denominator must vanish:

$$
\omega RC - \frac{1}{\omega RC} = 0
$$

$$
\omega RC = \frac{1}{\omega RC}
$$

$$
\omega^2 R^2 C^2 = 1
$$

$$
\omega_o = \frac{1}{RC}
$$

**Frequency of Oscillation:**

$$
f_o = \frac{\omega_o}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}
$$

**Barkhausen Magnitude Condition:** $|\beta A| = 1$ at $\omega_o$. At resonance, $\beta = 1/3$, so:

$$
A \cdot \frac{1}{3} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad A = 3
$$

For the non-inverting amplifier: $A = 1 + \dfrac{R_f}{R_1} = 3$, which gives:

$$
\frac{R_f}{R_1} = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_f = 2R_1
$$

> **Conclusion:** A Wien Bridge oscillator with $R = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.01\,\mu\text{F}$ generates a sine wave at:
> $$
> f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^4 \times 10^{-8}} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1591.5\,\text{Hz}
> $$
> approximately **1.59 kHz**.

---

## 3.10 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Integrator | Differentiator | Wien Oscillator |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Domain** | Time | Time | Frequency |
| **Output Type** | Ramp / Slow sinusoid | Spike / Quick sinusoid | Pure sine wave (steady state) |
| **Stability Issue** | DC saturation | High-frequency noise | Amplitude limiting needed |
| **Practical Fix** | Add $R_f \parallel C$ | Add $R_1$ in series, $C_f \parallel R_f$ | Use nonlinear gain control (e.g., diodes, lamp) |
| **Barkhausen** | Not applicable | Not applicable | $A\beta = 1$, $\phi = 0$ |
| **Phase Shift** | $-90^\circ$ | $+90^\circ$ lead | $0^\circ$ at $f_o$ |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Note:** All schematics below use **Mermaid flowchart syntax** for circuit topology. For op-amp circuits, the op-amp is represented as a triangular block with `+` (non-inverting) and `-` (inverting) inputs and a single output. Resistors and capacitors are shown as labelled boxes with connection lines indicating the closed-loop topology.

---

## 4.1 Scale Changer / Inverting Amplifier

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeVi["Vi (Input Voltage)"] -- R1 Resistor --> nodeN["Node N (Inverting Input)"]
    nodeN -- Op-Amp Block --> nodeVo["Vo (Output)"]
    nodeVo -- Rf Feedback Resistor --> nodeN
    nodeGND["Ground (0V)"] -- Direct Connection --> nodeP["Node P (Non-Inverting Input)"]
    nodeN -- Inverting --> opamp["Op-Amp -"]
    nodeP -- Non-Inverting --> opamp2["Op-Amp +"]
```

**Topology Description:**
- $V_i \to R_1 \to$ inverting input (node $\mathbf{N}$).
- $R_f$ bridges from $V_o$ back to node $\mathbf{N}$ (negative feedback).
- Non-inverting input tied directly to ground.

---

## 4.2 Sign Changer

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeVi["Vi Input"] -- Resistor R --> nodeN["Inverting Node N"]
    nodeN --> opampMinus["Op-Amp -"]
    nodeGND["Ground"] --> opampPlus["Op-Amp +"]
    opampMinus --> opampBody["Op-Amp Body"]
    opampPlus --> opampBody
    opampBody --> nodeVo["Vo Output"]
    nodeVo -- Resistor R --> nodeN
```

**Topology Description:** Identical to scale changer, but with the constraint $R_f = R_1 = R$. Output equals $-V_i$.

---

## 4.3 Inverting Summing Amplifier (3-Input Adder)

```mermaid
graph LR
    v1["V1"] -- R1 --> nodeN["Summing Node N (Virtual Ground)"]
    v2["V2"] -- R2 --> nodeN
    v3["V3"] -- R3 --> nodeN
    nodeN --> opMinus["Op-Amp -"]
    gnd["Ground"] --> opPlus["Op-Amp +"]
    opMinus --> opAmp["Op-Amp"]
    opPlus --> opAmp
    opAmp --> vo["Vo Output"]
    vo -- Rf --> nodeN
```

**Topology Description:** Three parallel input branches meet at node $\mathbf{N}$; their currents sum and flow through $R_f$ to produce a weighted negative sum.

---

## 4.4 Subtractor / Difference Amplifier

```mermaid
graph LR
    v1["V1 Input"] -- R1 --> nodeN["Inverting Node N"]
    nodeN --> opMinus["Op-Amp -"]
    nodeN -- Rf Feedback --> vo["Vo Output"]
    vo --> opAmp["Op-Amp"]
    v2["V2 Input"] -- R2 --> nodeM["Non-Inverting Node M"]
    nodeM --> opPlus["Op-Amp +"]
    nodeM -- R3 --> gnd["Ground"]
    opPlus --> opAmp
```

**Topology Description:** $V_1$ enters inverting input through $R_1$; $V_2$ enters non-inverting input through $R_2$ with $R_3$ as ground reference. Output is proportional to $(V_2 - V_1)$ when resistors are matched.

---

## 4.5 Ideal Integrator

```mermaid
graph LR
    vi["Vi Input"] -- R Resistor --> nodeN["Inverting Node N"]
    nodeN --> opMinus["Op-Amp -"]
    gnd["Ground"] --> opPlus["Op-Amp +"]
    opMinus --> opAmp["Op-Amp"]
    opPlus --> opAmp
    opAmp --> vo["Vo Output"]
    vo -- C Capacitor --> nodeN
```

**Topology Description:** Feedback element is a capacitor instead of a resistor. Output is the negative integral of input scaled by $1/RC$.

---

## 4.6 Practical Integrator

```mermaid
graph LR
    vi["Vi Input"] -- R --> nodeN["Inverting Node N"]
    nodeN --> opMinus["Op-Amp -"]
    gnd["Ground"] --> opPlus["Op-Amp +"]
    opMinus --> opAmp["Op-Amp"]
    opPlus --> opAmp
    opAmp --> vo["Vo Output"]
    vo -- C Capacitor --> nodeN
    vo -- Rf Resistor --> nodeN
```

**Topology Description:** $R_f$ in parallel with $C$ provides DC feedback path, preventing drift. Integrator behaviour above $f_a = 1/(2\pi R_f C)$.

---

## 4.7 Ideal Differentiator

```mermaid
graph LR
    vi["Vi Input"] -- C Capacitor --> nodeN["Inverting Node N"]
    nodeN --> opMinus["Op-Amp -"]
    gnd["Ground"] --> opPlus["Op-Amp +"]
    opMinus --> opAmp["Op-Amp"]
    opPlus --> opAmp
    opAmp --> vo["Vo Output"]
    vo -- R Resistor --> nodeN
```

**Topology Description:** Input element is a capacitor; output is the negative derivative of input scaled by $RC$.

---

## 4.8 Wien Bridge Oscillator — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    vo["Vo Sine Output"] -- Wien Network --> vp["Positive Feedback to Non-Inverting Input"]
    vo -- Gain Stage --> vn["Negative Feedback to Inverting Input"]
    vn --> opAmp["Non-Inverting Amplifier A=3"]
    opAmp --> vo
    vp -- RC Series and RC Parallel --> vo
```

**Block-Level Functional Architecture:**

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph WienNetwork["Wien Bridge Feedback Network"]
        zs["Zs = R + 1/jωC (Series Arm)"]
        zp["Zp = R || 1/jωC (Parallel Arm)"]
        zs --> zp
    end
    subgraph AmplifierStage["Non-Inverting Amplifier"]
        amp["A = 1 + Rf/R1 = 3"]
        r1["R1 Resistor"]
        rf["Rf = 2R1 Resistor"]
        r1 --> amp
        rf --> amp
    end
    outputNode["Sine Wave Output Vo"] --> zs
    zp --> inputNonInv["Non-Inverting Input +"]
    outputNode --> inputInv["Inverting Input -"]
    inputNonInv --> amp
    inputInv --> amp
    amp --> outputNode
```

**Topology Description:**
- **Wien Network** (series and parallel RC) feeds positive feedback to the (+) input — provides frequency selectivity.
- **Resistor Divider** ($R_1$ and $R_f$) feeds negative feedback to the (−) input — sets gain to exactly 3.
- When $\omega_o = 1/RC$, the loop phase is zero and $|\beta A| = 1$, producing sustained oscillation.

---
