# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Pattern Reference (KTU 2024 ESE / Internal):**
> - **Part A:** Short-answer questions, **2–3 marks each**, direct definitions or one-line derivations.
> - **Part B:** Long-answer questions, **14 marks each**, with internal choice. Typically split as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.
> - **Module Weightage:** Module 4 (Applications of Op-Amps) carries approximately **15–20%** of the total syllabus marks in PCCIT302.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Define a scale changer using an op-amp. Derive the expression for its output voltage.**

**Model Answer:**

A scale changer is an op-amp circuit that produces an output voltage proportional to the input voltage by a constant scale factor $k$.

**Circuit:** Inverting amplifier configuration — input $V_i$ through $R_1$ to inverting input, $R_f$ as feedback, non-inverting input grounded.

**Derivation:**
Applying virtual ground concept, $V_- = 0$, and KCL at inverting node:

$$
\frac{V_i}{R_1} = -\frac{V_o}{R_f}
$$

Therefore:

$$
V_o = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} V_i = -k V_i
$$

where the scale factor $k = R_f/R_1$. By choosing appropriate resistor ratios, any magnitude of $k$ can be achieved. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Distinguish between an ideal integrator and a practical integrator. Why is the practical integrator preferred?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Ideal Integrator | Practical Integrator |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback element | Pure capacitor $C$ | Capacitor $C$ with resistor $R_f$ in parallel |
| DC gain | Infinite (causes saturation) | Finite, equal to $R_f/R$ |
| Initial condition | $V_o(0)$ can drift indefinitely | Output stabilises due to $R_f$ DC path |
| Low-frequency behaviour | Pure integration down to DC | Integrator behaviour only above $f_a = 1/(2\pi R_f C)$ |
| Practical use | Theoretical only | Preferred for real circuits |

The **practical integrator** is preferred because the feedback resistor $R_f$ provides a path for the op-amp's bias currents and offset voltages, preventing the output from saturating at $\pm V_{sat}$ due to DC drift. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Inverting Summing Amplifier**

**With the help of a neat circuit diagram, derive the expression for the output voltage of an inverting summing amplifier using an op-amp with three inputs. Mention any two applications.**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Description:** (Refer Section 4.3)
- Three input voltages $V_1, V_2, V_3$ applied via resistors $R_1, R_2, R_3$ to inverting node $\mathbf{N}$.
- Feedback resistor $R_f$ between $V_o$ and node $\mathbf{N}$.
- Non-inverting input grounded.

**Derivation:**

By virtual short, $V_- = V_+ = 0$. Applying KCL at node $\mathbf{N}$:

$$
\frac{V_1 - 0}{R_1} + \frac{V_2 - 0}{R_2} + \frac{V_3 - 0}{R_3} + \frac{V_o - 0}{R_f} = 0
$$

(The current through $R_f$ flows *from* node $\mathbf{N}$ *to* $V_o$, hence its sign is positive when written as leaving the node.)

Rearranging:

$$
\frac{V_o}{R_f} = -\left(\frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V_3}{R_3}\right)
$$

$$
V_o = -R_f\left(\frac{V_1}{R_1} + \frac{V_2}{R_2} + \frac{V_3}{R_3}\right)
$$

If $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = R_f$, then:

$$
V_o = -(V_1 + V_2 + V_3)
$$

**Applications:**
1. **Audio mixing consoles** — to combine signals from multiple microphones or instruments.
2. **Digital-to-Analog Converters (DAC)** — using R-2R ladder networks based on summing amplifier topology. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Difference Amplifier**

**Derive the expression for the output of a difference amplifier. What is the condition for common-mode rejection?**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Description:** (Refer Section 4.4)
- $V_1$ via $R_1$ to inverting node, $R_f$ feedback.
- $V_2$ via $R_2$ to non-inverting node, $R_3$ to ground.

**Using Superposition Theorem:**

**Case 1:** $V_1 = 0$, $V_2$ acting alone.

Voltage at non-inverting node:

$$
V_+ = V_2 \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3}
$$

This $V_+$ acts as input to a non-inverting amplifier:

$$
V_{o}' = V_+ \cdot \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) = V_2 \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right)
$$

**Case 2:** $V_2 = 0$, $V_1$ acting alone.

Now $V_+ = 0$, and the circuit is a standard inverting amplifier:

$$
V_{o}'' = -\frac{R_f}{R_1} V_1
$$

**Total Output:**

$$
V_o = V_{o}' + V_{o}'' = V_2 \cdot \frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) - \frac{R_f}{R_1} V_1
$$

**Condition for matched (unity-gain) difference amplifier:**

Set $R_1 = R_3$ and $R_f = R_2$. Then:

$$
\frac{R_3}{R_2 + R_3} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1}\right) = \frac{R_1}{R_f + R_1} \cdot \frac{R_1 + R_f}{R_1} = 1
$$

So:

$$
\boxed{V_o = \frac{R_f}{R_1}(V_2 - V_1)}
$$

**Common-Mode Rejection Condition:**

When $V_1 = V_2 = V_{cm}$ (common-mode signal), the ideal output should be zero. This requires perfect matching of resistor ratios: $\dfrac{R_f}{R_1} = \dfrac{R_2}{R_3}$. Any mismatch causes a non-zero output proportional to $V_{cm}$, reducing the **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: Integrator**

**Derive the expression for the output of an ideal integrator using an op-amp. Explain how the practical integrator overcomes the limitations of the ideal one.**

**Model Solution:**

**Ideal Integrator Derivation:** (Refer Section 3.5)

**Circuit:** Input $V_i$ via resistor $R$ to inverting node; feedback capacitor $C$ from output to inverting node.

**Virtual short:** $V_- = 0$.

Current through $R$:

$$
i_R = \frac{V_i}{R}
$$

This same current charges the capacitor. Voltage across capacitor is $V_C = -V_o$ (from inverting node to output). Current through capacitor:

$$
i_C = C \frac{dV_C}{dt} = -C \frac{dV_o}{dt}
$$

KCL at inverting node ($i_R = i_C$):

$$
\frac{V_i}{R} = -C \frac{dV_o}{dt}
$$

Integrating:

$$
V_o(t) = -\frac{1}{RC} \int_{0}^{t} V_i(\tau) \, d\tau + V_o(0)
$$

**Limitations of Ideal Integrator:**
1. **DC saturation:** At $\omega = 0$, capacitor is open circuit, so DC gain is infinite. Any small DC offset at input drives output to saturation.
2. **Bias current drift:** Input bias currents integrate indefinitely, causing output drift.
3. **Initial condition errors:** Output depends heavily on the initial capacitor charge, which is hard to control.

**Practical Integrator:** (Refer Section 3.6)

Add a large feedback resistor $R_f$ in parallel with $C$.

**Transfer Function:**

$$
A(j\omega) = -\frac{R_f}{R(1 + j\omega R_f C)}
$$

**Behaviour:**
- For $\omega \gg 1/(R_f C)$: behaves as ideal integrator, $|A| \approx 1/(\omega R C)$.
- For $\omega \ll 1/(R_f C)$: gain is finite and constant at $R_f/R$, preventing DC saturation.

**Corner Frequency:**

$$
f_a = \frac{1}{2\pi R_f C}
$$

**Typical Values:** $R = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C = 0.01\,\mu\text{F}$, $R_f = 1\,\text{M}\Omega$ — gives $f_a \approx 15.9\,\text{Hz}$, so signals above ~16 Hz are integrated while DC is rejected. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: Wien Bridge Oscillator**

**With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Wien Bridge Oscillator using an op-amp. Derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation and the condition for sustained oscillations.**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:** (Refer Section 4.8)

The Wien Bridge Oscillator consists of two parts:
1. **Non-inverting amplifier** with gain $A = 1 + R_f/R_1$.
2. **Wien Bridge network** — a series RC arm and a parallel RC arm providing positive feedback.

**Wien Network Analysis:** (Refer Section 3.9)

The feedback factor is:

$$
\beta = \frac{1}{3 + j\left(\omega RC - \dfrac{1}{\omega RC}\right)}
$$

**Barkhausen Phase Condition:** For oscillation, total loop phase = 0. The non-inverting amplifier contributes $0^\circ$, so the Wien network's imaginary part must vanish:

$$
\omega RC - \frac{1}{\omega RC} = 0
$$

Solving:

$$
\omega_o RC = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_o = \frac{1}{RC}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}}
$$

**Barkhausen Magnitude Condition:** $|\beta A| = 1$.

At $\omega_o$, $\beta = 1/3$, so we need $A = 3$:

$$
1 + \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{R_f}{R_1} = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_f = 2R_1
$$

**Practical Amplitude Stabilisation:**
- For $|A| > 3$: oscillations grow until saturation → distorted output.
- For $|A| < 3$: oscillations die out.
- For $|A| = 3$: stable sine wave of constant amplitude.

In practice, $R_f$ is implemented as a **nonlinear element** (e.g., a tungsten lamp, thermistor, or back-to-back Zener diodes with a resistor) to provide automatic gain control — gain is slightly greater than 3 at startup, and reduces to exactly 3 once amplitude stabilises.

**Numerical Example:** $R = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C = 0.01\,\mu\text{F}$:

$$
f_o = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 10^4 \times 10^{-8}} \approx 1591.5\,\text{Hz}
$$

**Applications:**
- Audio signal generators.
- Function generators for laboratory testing.
- Reference sine wave sources in measurement instruments. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks — DO NOT make these mistakes:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign in the inverting amplifier output.** The output is $-R_f V_i / R_1$, **not** $+R_f V_i / R_1$. Marks are deducted for missing the inversion.
>
> 2. **Using $V_- = V_o$ instead of $V_- = 0$ in virtual ground.** The virtual ground concept only applies at the **inverting input node**, not at the output. Many students confuse these and write incorrect KCL equations.
>
> 3. **Not stating the ideal op-amp assumptions explicitly** before starting a derivation. Examiners in KTU 2024 Scheme expect the first line of every solution to begin with "Assuming an ideal op-amp with $A = \infty$, $R_{in} = \infty$, $R_{out} = 0$...". Skipping this loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 4. **Confusing ideal and practical integrator/differentiator.** Practical versions have additional components; their frequency response is different. Examiners test this distinction explicitly.
>
> 5. **In Wien Bridge problems, students often write $f_o = 1/RC$ instead of $f_o = 1/(2\pi RC)$.** Remember the conversion from angular frequency $\omega$ to frequency $f$.
>
> 6. **Subtractor problems: not applying Superposition correctly.** Always split into two cases (each input acting alone) and sum the outputs.
>
> 7. **Not drawing circuit diagrams.** Even in purely derivation questions, a neat labelled diagram is worth **at least 1–2 marks** in KTU valuation.
>
> 8. **Using KCL incorrectly with sign conventions.** Adopt a consistent convention (e.g., "currents entering the node are positive") and stick to it throughout the derivation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Section Before Exam**

### Core Concepts to Memorise
- ✅ All seven applications derive from **two golden rules**: virtual short ($V_+ = V_-$) and zero input current ($I_+ = I_- = 0$).
- ✅ The **inverting amplifier** is the foundation: $V_o = -(R_f/R_1) V_i$.
- ✅ **Sign changer** is a special case where $R_f = R_1 = R$.
- ✅ **Scale changer** generalises the inverting amplifier for any gain magnitude.

### Formulae to Memorise Verbatim
- ✅ Inverting amplifier: $V_o = -(R_f/R_1) V_i$
- ✅ Inverting summer: $V_o = -R_f \sum (V_n/R_n)$
- ✅ Difference amplifier (matched): $V_o = (R_f/R_1)(V_2 - V_1)$
- ✅ Ideal integrator: $V_o(t) = -(1/RC) \int V_i \, dt$
- ✅ Ideal differentiator: $V_o(t) = -RC \, dV_i/dt$
- ✅ Wien oscillator frequency: $f_o = 1/(2\pi RC)$
- ✅ Wien oscillator gain condition: $A = 3$, i.e., $R_f = 2 R_1$

### Practical vs Ideal — Key Differences
- ✅ **Practical integrator** has $R_f \parallel C$ — finite DC gain prevents saturation.
- ✅ **Practical differentiator** has $R_1$ in series with $C$, and $C_f \parallel R_f$ — limits noise.
- ✅ **Wien oscillator in practice** uses nonlinear elements (lamps, diodes) for amplitude stabilisation.

### Numerical Values to Remember
- ✅ $f_o = 1\,\text{kHz}$ for Wien oscillator → typical $R = 15.9\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C = 0.01\,\mu\text{F}$ (or use $R = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$, $C = 0.016\,\mu\text{F}$).
- ✅ Practical integrator corner frequency: $f_a = 1/(2\pi R_f C)$ — choose $R_f \gg R$ (typically $100\times$).

### Common Pitfalls Avoided
- ✅ Always state ideal op-amp assumptions at the start.
- ✅ Draw circuit diagrams with labelled nodes, even in derivation problems.
- ✅ Use Superposition for difference amplifier.
- ✅ Include $2\pi$ in Wien frequency, not just $\omega = 1/RC$.
- ✅ In integrators/differentiators, note the sign inversion explicitly.

### One-Line Definitions for Quick Recall
- **Scale Changer:** Op-amp circuit that multiplies input by a constant $-R_f/R_1$.
- **Sign Changer:** Special scale changer with $R_f = R_1$, giving $V_o = -V_i$.
- **Adder:** Op-amp circuit that produces $V_o = -\sum k_n V_n$ (inverting summer).
- **Subtractor:** Op-amp circuit with $V_o = k(V_2 - V_1)$ when resistors are matched.
- **Integrator:** Op-amp with capacitor in feedback, $V_o = -(1/RC) \int V_i \, dt$.
- **Differentiator:** Op-amp with capacitor at input, $V_o = -RC \, dV_i/dt$.
- **Wien Bridge Oscillator:** RC oscillator using non-inverting amplifier with Wien network, $f_o = 1/(2\pi RC)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Integrated Circuits: Low Pass and High Pass Active Filters Using Op-Amp
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES & CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Integrated Circuits: Low Pass and High Pass Active Filters Using Op-Amp

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is an Active Filter?

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCCIT302 – Electronic Devices & Circuits)**, an **Active Filter** is an analog frequency-selective electronic network that uses an active component — typically an **Operational Amplifier (Op-Amp)** — combined with passive elements (Resistors and Capacitors) to achieve a desired magnitude and phase response over a specific band of frequencies. Unlike passive filters (built only with R, L, C), active filters provide **gain, buffering, and eliminate the need for bulky inductors**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The focus is on **first-order Low Pass Filter (LPF)** and **first-order High Pass Filter (HPF)** realized using an Op-Amp in inverting configuration. Higher-order filters (Butterworth, Chebyshev) are typically covered in advanced courses.

### 1.2 The Two Fundamental Filter Types Covered

| Filter Type | Function | Signal Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Low Pass Filter (LPF)** | Passes low frequencies, attenuates high frequencies | Allows $0$ to $f_c$ Hz through |
| **High Pass Filter (HPF)** | Passes high frequencies, attenuates low frequencies | Allows frequencies above $f_c$ Hz through |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition – Cutoff Frequency ($f_c$):** The frequency at which the magnitude of the transfer function drops to **$\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$** (i.e., $-3$ dB point) of its maximum passband value. It defines the boundary between the passband and the stopband.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **water tap with a sieve**:
- A **Low Pass Filter (LPF)** is like a sieve that lets **fine sand (low frequencies, slow signals)** pass through, but blocks **pebbles (high frequencies, noise, fast fluctuations)**. Real-life example: the **bass-isolator circuit in subwoofers**.
- A **High Pass Filter (HPF)** is the opposite — like a sieve that blocks sand but lets pebbles fall through. Real-life example: the **DC blocking capacitor at the input of every audio amplifier** that removes the unwanted DC offset.

In the electronic domain, the **Op-Amp acts as the active muscle** of the filter, providing **gain and isolation**, while the **RC network acts as the sieve**.

### 1.4 Why Use an Op-Amp? – The Three Key Advantages

1. **Gain (\textbf{Gain} $\neq 1$):** Op-amp provides signal amplification within the passband, which passive filters cannot.
2. **High Input Impedance (\textbf{Z}$_{in} \to \infty$):** The filter does not load the source.
3. **Low Output Impedance (\textbf{Z}$_{out} \to 0$):** The filter can drive low-impedance loads without loss.
4. **No Inductors Required:** IC-friendly, as fabricating inductors on silicon is impractical.

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Metric for KTU:** For all first-order active filters, the **roll-off rate** after cutoff is **$-20$ dB/decade** (or equivalently **$-6$ dB/octave**). Memorize this — it is a frequently asked **3-mark question**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 First-Order Active Low Pass Filter (LPF) – Operational Analysis

**Circuit Topology:** A non-inverting input of the Op-Amp is grounded. The feedback network consists of $R_F$ in parallel with $C_F$ between the output and the inverting input. The input is applied through $R_1$ to the inverting input.

**Step-by-Step Working Logic:**
- At **low frequencies ($f \to 0$)**: Capacitor $C_F$ acts as an **open circuit** $\Rightarrow$ feedback impedance is purely $R_F$ $\Rightarrow$ circuit behaves as an **inverting amplifier** with gain $-R_F/R_1$. Signal is passed.
- At **very high frequencies ($f \to \infty$)**: Capacitor $C_F$ acts as a **short circuit** $\Rightarrow$ feedback impedance becomes zero $\Rightarrow$ output is virtually **shorted to the inverting input** $\Rightarrow$ output tends to **zero**. Signal is blocked.
- At **cutoff frequency ($f = f_c$)**: The magnitude is $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ of the passband gain.

**Why it works:** The Op-Amp's virtual ground concept forces the inverting node to be at **0 V**, and the negative feedback ensures that the output is exactly the negative of the input scaled by the feedback-to-input impedance ratio.

### 2.2 First-Order Active High Pass Filter (HPF) – Operational Analysis

**Circuit Topology:** Identical to LPF, but the **positions of R and C are swapped** in the input branch. Input is applied through a **series capacitor $C_1$** and resistor $R_1$ to the inverting input, while feedback is through $R_F$ only.

**Step-by-Step Working Logic:**
- At **low frequencies ($f \to 0$)**: Capacitor $C_1$ acts as an **open circuit** $\Rightarrow$ input signal cannot reach the Op-Amp $\Rightarrow$ output is **zero**. Signal is blocked.
- At **very high frequencies ($f \to \infty$)**: Capacitor $C_1$ acts as a **short circuit** $\Rightarrow$ full input signal reaches the Op-Amp $\Rightarrow$ circuit behaves as an inverting amplifier with gain $-R_F/R_1$. Signal is passed.
- At **cutoff frequency ($f = f_c$)**: Again, magnitude is $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ of passband gain.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are HIGH-YIELD for KTU University Exam. Memorize the table verbatim.**

| S.No. | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes / KTU Tip |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Cutoff Frequency (LPF & HPF) | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R_1 C_F}$ (LPF) / $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R_1 C_1}$ (HPF) | Hz | Frequency at $-3$ dB point |
| 2 | Passband Gain (LPF) | $A_{LPF} = -\dfrac{R_F}{R_1}$ | Unitless | Negative sign indicates $180^\circ$ phase inversion |
| 3 | Passband Gain (HPF, at $f \gg f_c$) | $A_{HPF} = -\dfrac{R_F}{R_1}$ | Unitless | Gain approaches this value asymptotically |
| 4 | Transfer Function (LPF) | $H(s) = -\dfrac{R_F / R_1}{1 + s R_F C_F}$ | — | Replace $s = j\omega$ for frequency response |
| 5 | Transfer Function (HPF) | $H(s) = -\dfrac{s R_F C_1}{1 + s R_F C_1}$ | — | Standard form of first-order HPF |
| 6 | Roll-off Rate | $-20$ dB/decade | dB | Above $f_c$ for LPF; Below $f_c$ for HPF |
| 7 | Stopband Attenuation Slope | $-6$ dB/octave | dB | Equivalent to $-20$ dB/decade |
| 8 | Attenuation at $f = 2 f_c$ (LPF) | $-7$ dB | dB | $20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1+4}}\right) \approx -6.99$ dB |
| 9 | Attenuation at $f = 10 f_c$ (LPF) | $-20$ dB | dB | One full decade beyond cutoff |
| 10 | Phase Shift (LPF at $f_c$) | $+45^\circ$ (between input and output) | Degrees | Plus the $180^\circ$ from inverting config $\Rightarrow +45^\circ$ total wrt input |
| 11 | Phase Shift (HPF at $f_c$) | $-45^\circ$ | Degrees | Plus the $180^\circ$ from inverting config $\Rightarrow -45^\circ$ total |

> [!NOTE]
> **Absolute Value Pitfall:** When computing magnitudes, **never** use the vertical pipe symbol `|H(jω)|` inside markdown tables. Use **$\vert H(j\omega) \vert$** to avoid table-breaking.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **LPF:** Anti-aliasing filter before an ADC, noise suppression in audio systems, demodulator in communication receivers, EMI suppression.
- **HPF:** AC coupling in oscilloscopes, DC offset removal in instrumentation amplifiers, audio crossover networks (tweeter circuit), noise filtering in biomedical ECG/EEG signals.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: First-Order Active Low Pass Filter (LPF)

**Given:** Op-Amp in inverting configuration. $R_1$ is the input resistor. The feedback impedance $Z_F$ consists of $R_F$ in **parallel** with $C_F$.

**Step 1: Express the feedback impedance $Z_F$**

The feedback branch has $R_F \parallel C_F$. Using the parallel combination formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_F &= R_F \parallel \dfrac{1}{s C_F} \\[6pt]
Z_F &= \dfrac{R_F \cdot \dfrac{1}{s C_F}}{R_F + \dfrac{1}{s C_F}} \\[6pt]
Z_F &= \dfrac{R_F}{1 + s R_F C_F}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the ideal inverting amplifier gain equation**

For an ideal Op-Amp, the closed-loop gain in the inverting configuration is:

$$
A_{CL} = -\dfrac{Z_F}{Z_{in}}
$$

Here, $Z_{in} = R_1$ (purely resistive input branch). Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(s) = \dfrac{V_o(s)}{V_i(s)} &= -\dfrac{Z_F}{R_1} \\[6pt]
H(s) &= -\dfrac{R_F / R_1}{1 + s R_F C_F}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Convert to frequency response by substituting $s = j\omega$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(j\omega) &= -\dfrac{R_F / R_1}{1 + j\omega R_F C_F}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Find the magnitude response**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert H(j\omega) \vert &= \dfrac{R_F / R_1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega R_F C_F)^2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Find the cutoff frequency $f_c$**

Cutoff is defined as the frequency where $\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{A_{passband}}{\sqrt{2}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{R_F / R_1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega_c R_F C_F)^2}} &= \dfrac{R_F / R_1}{\sqrt{2}} \\[6pt]
\sqrt{1 + (\omega_c R_F C_F)^2} &= \sqrt{2} \\[6pt]
(\omega_c R_F C_F)^2 &= 1 \\[6pt]
\omega_c &= \dfrac{1}{R_F C_F} \\[6pt]
f_c &= \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R_F C_F}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on the KTU convention:** In many KTU textbooks, the input resistor is also denoted $R_1$ and feedback components are $R_F$, $C_F$. The cutoff frequency expression $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R_F C_F}$ is the **KTU-board standard** for the inverting first-order LPF.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: First-Order Active High Pass Filter (HPF)

**Given:** Op-Amp in inverting configuration. The input branch has a **series capacitor $C_1$ and resistor $R_1$** (i.e., $R_1$ and $C_1$ are in series, not parallel). Feedback is through $R_F$ only.

**Step 1: Express the input impedance $Z_{in}$**

The input branch has $R_1$ in series with $C_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Z_{in} &= R_1 + \dfrac{1}{s C_1} \\[6pt]
Z_{in} &= \dfrac{1 + s R_1 C_1}{s C_1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the inverting amplifier gain equation**

Feedback is purely resistive: $Z_F = R_F$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(s) = \dfrac{V_o(s)}{V_i(s)} &= -\dfrac{Z_F}{Z_{in}} = -\dfrac{R_F}{\dfrac{1 + s R_1 C_1}{s C_1}} \\[6pt]
H(s) &= -\dfrac{s R_F C_1}{1 + s R_1 C_1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Convert to frequency response**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(j\omega) &= -\dfrac{j\omega R_F C_1}{1 + j\omega R_1 C_1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Find the magnitude response**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert H(j\omega) \vert &= \dfrac{\omega R_F C_1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega R_1 C_1)^2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Behavior at frequency extremes**

- As $\omega \to 0$: numerator $\to 0$, so $\vert H \vert \to 0$ (low frequencies blocked).
- As $\omega \to \infty$: $\vert H \vert \to R_F / R_1$ (passband gain).

**Step 6: Find the cutoff frequency $f_c$**

At cutoff, $\vert H(j\omega_c) \vert = \dfrac{R_F / R_1}{\sqrt{2}}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{\omega_c R_F C_1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega_c R_1 C_1)^2}} &= \dfrac{R_F / R_1}{\sqrt{2}} \\[6pt]
\dfrac{\omega_c R_F C_1 \sqrt{2}}{R_F / R_1} &= \sqrt{1 + (\omega_c R_1 C_1)^2} \\[6pt]
\text{After algebraic simplification:} \quad \omega_c R_1 C_1 &= 1 \\[6pt]
f_c &= \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R_1 C_1}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: LPF vs HPF Comparison

| Property | Low Pass Filter (LPF) | High Pass Filter (HPF) |
|:---|:---:|:---:|
| Passband Region | $0 \le f \le f_c$ | $f \ge f_c$ |
| Stopband Region | $f \ge f_c$ | $0 \le f \le f_c$ |
| Input Impedance | $R_1$ (resistive) | $R_1 + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_1}$ (complex) |
| Feedback Impedance | $R_F \parallel C_F$ (complex) | $R_F$ (resistive) |
| Roll-off Direction | Above $f_c$ (high $f$) | Below $f_c$ (low $f$) |
| Slope | $-20$ dB/decade | $-20$ dB/decade |
| At $f = 0$ | Full passband gain $R_F/R_1$ | Zero (blocked) |
| At $f = \infty$ | Zero (blocked) | Full passband gain $R_F/R_1$ |
| Phase at $f_c$ (relative to inverting node) | $+45^\circ$ | $-45^\circ$ |
| DC Blocking? | No | Yes (couples AC only) |
| Use Case | Smoothing, anti-aliasing | DC removal, AC coupling |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: First-Order Active LPF Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["INPUT Vi sine wave"] --> B["INPUT BRANCH Resistor R1"]
    B --> C["SUMMING NODE Virtual Ground V- approx 0"]
    D["FEEDBACK BRANCH RF parallel to CF"] --> C
    C --> E["OPAMP Ideal Vout"]
    E --> F["OUTPUT Vo filtered low frequency"]
    E --> D
    G["DC SUPPLY plus or minus 15V"] --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The signal flows from $V_i$ through $R_1$ to the inverting node. The parallel $R_F C_F$ network provides frequency-dependent feedback. The non-inverting input (not shown explicitly) is grounded. The dashed arrow from Output back to Feedback Branch indicates the negative feedback loop that defines the filter's response.

### 4.2 Block Diagram: First-Order Active HPF Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A["INPUT Vi with possible DC offset"] --> B["INPUT BRANCH Series C1 and R1"]
    B --> C["SUMMING NODE Virtual Ground V- approx 0"]
    D["FEEDBACK BRANCH Resistor RF only"] --> C
    C --> E["OPAMP Ideal Vout"]
    E --> F["OUTPUT Vo AC only DC blocked"]
    E --> D
    G["DC SUPPLY plus or minus 15V"] --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The series $C_1$-$R_1$ branch blocks DC and low frequencies. Only AC signals above $f_c$ pass through to the Op-Amp. The feedback is purely resistive ($R_F$), giving a constant passband gain at high frequencies.

### 4.3 Frequency Response Comparison (Magnitude Plot)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph MAG["MAGNITUDE RESPONSE PLOT H of j omega in dB"]
        A1["At f = 0: LPF gain max equals RF by R1"] --> A2["At f = fc: LPF drops by 3 dB"]
        A2 --> A3["Above fc: LPF rolls off at minus 20 dB per decade"]
    end
    subgraph MAG2["HIGH PASS FILTER"]
        B1["At f = 0: HPF gain equals minus infinity dB"] --> B2["At f = fc: HPF rises to minus 3 dB below max"]
        B2 --> B3["Above fc: HPF flat at RF by R1 passband"]
    end
```

### 4.4 Pin-Level Schematic Mapping (Conceptual Block)

| Functional Block | Internal Components | KTU Function |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Input Stage | $R_1$ (LPF) or $C_1 + R_1$ (HPF) | Frequency-selective impedance |
| Active Element | Op-Amp (e.g., IC 741) | Provides gain, isolation |
| Feedback Network | $R_F \parallel C_F$ (LPF) or $R_F$ (HPF) | Sets gain and frequency response |
| Power Supply | $\pm 15$ V dual supply | Biases the Op-Amp |
| Output Stage | Direct coupled to load | Delivers amplified, filtered signal |

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU Board Exams:** When asked to "draw the circuit diagram," you must show: (1) Op-Amp triangle with inverting (-), non-inverting (+), and output terminals, (2) all R and C values labeled, (3) DC supply rails $\pm V_{CC}$ clearly marked, (4) input $V_i$ and output $V_o$ nodes labeled, (5) virtual ground indicated at the inverting node. Drawing 5 extra elements correctly can earn full marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A: 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

> **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]`
> **Define cutoff frequency. What is its significance in active filters?**

**Model Answer:**
The **cutoff frequency ($f_c$)** is the frequency at which the magnitude of the transfer function drops to $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ (i.e., $-3$ dB) of its maximum passband value. It is the boundary frequency that separates the **passband** (signals passed with minimum attenuation) from the **stopband** (signals attenuated). In a first-order LPF, frequencies below $f_c$ are passed; in a first-order HPF, frequencies above $f_c$ are passed. The expression is:
$$
f_c = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R C}
$$
where $R$ and $C$ are the relevant resistor and capacitor determining the response. **(3 marks)**

---

> **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`
> **Why are active filters preferred over passive filters in integrated circuit applications? List any three reasons.**

**Model Answer:**
Active filters (using Op-Amps with RC networks) are preferred over passive filters in IC applications for the following reasons:
1. **Gain:** Active filters provide signal gain greater than unity; passive filters have a maximum gain of 1 (i.e., 0 dB).
2. **No Inductors:** IC fabrication cannot practically realize inductors. Active filters use only R, C, and Op-Amps, which are IC-compatible.
3. **Isolation/Impedance:** Op-Amps provide high input impedance and low output impedance, so the filter does not load the source or get loaded by the next stage (cascading is easy).
**(3 marks)**

---

### 📕 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Full Descriptive – Internal Choice)

---

> **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **(a)** Design a first-order active **low pass filter** using an Op-Amp with a cutoff frequency of **$10$ kHz** and a passband gain of **$-4$**. Assume a suitable capacitor value of $C_F = 10$ nF. Draw the circuit diagram and verify the gain.
> **(b)** Derive the transfer function of a first-order active **high pass filter** using an Op-Amp. From the transfer function, show that the gain rolls off at $-20$ dB/decade below the cutoff frequency.

#### 🔹 Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Identify the cutoff frequency formula**

$$
f_c = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R_F C_F}
$$

**Step 2: Solve for $R_F$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_F &= \dfrac{1}{2 \pi f_c C_F} \\[6pt]
R_F &= \dfrac{1}{2 \pi \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}} \\[6pt]
R_F &= \dfrac{1}{2 \pi \times 10^{-4}} \\[6pt]
R_F &= \dfrac{10^4}{2 \pi} \approx 1591.55 \ \Omega \approx 1.59 \ k\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Solve for $R_1$ from passband gain condition**

Passband gain of inverting LPF: $A_v = -\dfrac{R_F}{R_1}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
-4 &= -\dfrac{R_F}{R_1} \\[6pt]
R_1 &= \dfrac{R_F}{4} = \dfrac{1591.55}{4} \approx 397.89 \ \Omega \approx 400 \ \Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Verify passband gain**

$$
A_v = -\dfrac{1591.55}{397.89} = -4.00 \ \checkmark
$$

**Step 5: Circuit Diagram (textual representation)**

```
   Vi o----[ R1 = 400 Ω ]----o---------o
                              |         |
                              |   ___   |
                              +--| - \  |
              o-------------------|+ >--+----o Vo
              | (GND)            |___/  |
              |                       |
              o-------------||--------o
                          Cf = 10 nF
              (R_F in parallel with C_F from Vo to inverting input)
```

**Design Summary:**

| Component | Value |
|:---:|:---:|
| $R_F$ | $1.59$ k$\Omega$ |
| $C_F$ | $10$ nF |
| $R_1$ | $400$ $\Omega$ |
| $f_c$ | $10$ kHz |
| $A_v$ | $-4$ |

**(7 marks — full marks awarded for derivation + diagram + verification)**

---

#### 🔹 Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Recall the transfer function of first-order HPF**

From the earlier derivation (Section 3.2):

$$
H(j\omega) = -\dfrac{j\omega R_F C_1}{1 + j\omega R_1 C_1}
$$

**Step 2: Express the magnitude in dB**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert H(j\omega) \vert_{dB} &= 20 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{\omega R_F C_1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega R_1 C_1)^2}} \right) \\[6pt]
&= 20 \log_{10}(\omega R_F C_1) - 10 \log_{10}\left(1 + (\omega R_1 C_1)^2\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Analyze behavior when $\omega \ll \omega_c$ (i.e., $\omega R_1 C_1 \ll 1$)**

The denominator $\sqrt{1 + (\omega R_1 C_1)^2} \approx 1$, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert H(j\omega) \vert &\approx \omega R_F C_1 \\[6pt]
\vert H(j\omega) \vert_{dB} &\approx 20 \log_{10}(\omega R_F C_1) = 20 \log_{10}(\omega) + 20 \log_{10}(R_F C_1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Show the $-20$ dB/decade slope**

As $\omega$ increases by a factor of $10$ (one decade), the magnitude in dB increases by $20 \log_{10}(10) = 20$ dB. Conversely, as $\omega$ decreases by a decade, the magnitude in dB **decreases by 20 dB**.

**Conclusion:** In the stopband (below $f_c$), the gain **rolls off at $-20$ dB per decade**. **(7 marks)**

---

> **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **first-order active high pass filter** using an Op-Amp. Derive its transfer function and cutoff frequency.
> **(b)** For a first-order active LPF with $R_1 = 10$ k$\Omega$, $R_F = 50$ k$\Omega$, and $C_F = 1$ nF, calculate the **(i)** passband gain, **(ii)** cutoff frequency, and **(iii)** output voltage magnitude for an input of $V_i = 100$ mV at $f = 2 f_c$.

#### 🔹 Part (a) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Circuit Operation:** Refer to the HPF block diagram in Section 4.2. Input $V_i$ is applied through a series combination of $C_1$ and $R_1$ to the inverting input. Feedback resistor $R_F$ connects the output to the inverting input. The non-inverting input is grounded.

**At low $f$:** $C_1$ is open $\Rightarrow$ no signal reaches Op-Amp $\Rightarrow V_o = 0$.
**At high $f$:** $C_1$ is short $\Rightarrow$ full signal reaches Op-Amp $\Rightarrow V_o = -(R_F/R_1) V_i$.

**Derivation (recap from Section 3.2):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(s) &= -\dfrac{s R_F C_1}{1 + s R_1 C_1} \\[6pt]
H(j\omega) &= -\dfrac{j\omega R_F C_1}{1 + j\omega R_1 C_1} \\[6pt]
f_c &= \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R_1 C_1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(7 marks — full marks for diagram + qualitative operation + derivation)**

---

#### 🔹 Part (b) — Model Solution (7 Marks)

**Given:** $R_1 = 10$ k$\Omega$, $R_F = 50$ k$\Omega$, $C_F = 1$ nF, $V_i = 100$ mV.

**(i) Passband Gain:**

$$
A_v = -\dfrac{R_F}{R_1} = -\dfrac{50 \ k\Omega}{10 \ k\Omega} = -5
$$

**(ii) Cutoff Frequency:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_c &= \dfrac{1}{2 \pi R_F C_F} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi \times 50 \times 10^3 \times 1 \times 10^{-9}} \\[6pt]
f_c &= \dfrac{1}{2 \pi \times 50 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{10^6}{100 \pi} \approx 3183.1 \ \text{Hz} \approx 3.18 \ \text{kHz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Output at $f = 2 f_c$:**

The magnitude of LPF at frequency $f$ is:

$$
\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \dfrac{\vert A_v \vert}{\sqrt{1 + (f/f_c)^2}}
$$

At $f = 2 f_c$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vert H(j 2\omega_c) \vert &= \dfrac{5}{\sqrt{1 + 4}} = \dfrac{5}{\sqrt{5}} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, output voltage magnitude:

$$
\vert V_o \vert = \vert H \vert \times \vert V_i \vert = 2.236 \times 100 \ \text{mV} \approx 223.6 \ \text{mV}
$$

**Verification in dB:** Attenuation at $2 f_c$ is $-6.99$ dB. The passband gain is $20 \log_{10}(5) = 13.98$ dB. Total = $13.98 - 6.99 = 6.99$ dB $\Rightarrow$ $V_o = 100 \ \text{mV} \times 10^{6.99/20} = 223.6$ mV. ✓

**(7 marks — full marks for each sub-calculation)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls
>
> 1. **Sign of the gain:** KTU strictly evaluates the **negative sign** in $-R_F/R_1$. Students who write $R_F/R_1$ lose 1 mark in Part A and up to 2 marks in Part B.
> 2. **Units in cutoff frequency:** Writing $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ without converting $R$ to ohms and $C$ to farads leads to **unit error** and **zero credit** in numerical problems.
> 3. **Confusing LPF and HPF cutoff formula:** In LPF, $f_c$ uses $R_F$ and $C_F$ (feedback components). In HPF, $f_c$ uses $R_1$ and $C_1$ (input components). Swapping these is a **fatal error** — full marks deduction.
> 4. **Forgetting the DC supply rails:** When asked to draw the circuit, omitting $\pm V_{CC}$ connections to the Op-Amp costs **1 mark** in KTU valuation.
> 5. **Inverting vs Non-inverting confusion:** Active filters in KTU 2024 Module 4 are predominantly **inverting**. Writing the gain as $+R_F/R_1$ (non-inverting) is incorrect.
> 6. **Phase at cutoff:** LPF has $+45^\circ$ phase shift (input to output) at $f_c$, HPF has $-45^\circ$. This is a **favourite 2-mark sub-question**.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **LPF passes low $f$, blocks high $f$**; **HPF passes high $f$, blocks low $f$** (and DC).
- ✅ **Cutoff frequency formula** is $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ — components depend on filter topology.
- ✅ **First-order filters** have a roll-off of **$-20$ dB/decade** (or $-6$ dB/octave).
- ✅ **Passband gain** of inverting active filter is $A_v = -\dfrac{R_F}{R_1}$ (note the negative sign).
- ✅ **LPF topology:** $R_1$ in input branch, $R_F \parallel C_F$ in feedback.
- ✅ **HPF topology:** $R_1$ in series with $C_1$ in input branch, $R_F$ alone in feedback.
- ✅ **At $f = f_c$:** Magnitude is $-3$ dB below passband; phase shift is $\pm 45^\circ$ (LPF $= +45^\circ$, HPF $= -45^\circ$).
- ✅ **DC blocking:** LPF does NOT block DC; HPF DOES block DC.
- ✅ **IC 741** is the typical Op-Amp used; supply $\pm 15$ V; saturation $\pm 13$ V approx.
- ✅ **First-order LPF/HPF** is the only filter order in Module 4; higher orders (Butterworth, Chebyshev) are out of scope.
- ✅ **KTU standard frequency response plots:** Always label $-3$ dB point, passband gain, cutoff frequency, and asymptotic roll-off line.
- ✅ **Virtual ground** at the inverting input is the key conceptual tool to derive all transfer functions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Circuit Diagram and Working of Timer IC 555
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES & CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Circuit Diagram and Working of Timer IC 555

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Timer IC 555** (also known as the *555 Timer* or *NE555 / SE555 / LM555*) is a highly stable, monolithic integrated circuit used for generating accurate time delays or oscillations. Introduced by **Signetics Corporation in 1972** (designed by Hans Camenzind), it is one of the most popular and widely manufactured analog ICs in history, with billions of units produced annually.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCCIT302 Module 4):**
> *The 555 Timer is an 8-pin mixed-signal IC containing two precision comparators, a bistable SR flip-flop, a discharge transistor, and a resistive voltage divider network formed by three equal **5 kΩ** resistors (hence the name "**555**"). It operates from a single supply of **+5 V to +15 V** and can source/sink up to **200 mA** of output current.*

The IC is highly versatile and can be configured in three primary operating modes:
1. **Monostable Multivibrator** (One-Shot) — generates a single fixed-width pulse.
2. **Astable Multivibrator** (Free-Running Oscillator) — generates a continuous square wave.
3. **Bistable Multivibrator** (Flip-Flop) — acts as a basic SR latch.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy 🪜

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of the 555 Timer as a "Smart Water Bucket with Two Sensors":**

Imagine a water bucket connected to a tap. The bucket has **two water-level sensors**:
- A **LOW sensor** (at 1/3 height) — when the water drops below this, it *triggers* the tap to OPEN.
- A **HIGH sensor** (at 2/3 height) — when the water rises above this, it *triggers* the tap to CLOSE.

A **valve at the bottom** can drain the bucket instantly on command.

| Mode | Real-World Analogy | What Happens |
|------|-------------------|--------------|
| **Monostable** | You press a button (trigger) → tap opens for a fixed time → closes automatically | One pulse per trigger |
| **Astable** | The tap and valve take turns — fills up to 2/3, drains to 1/3, fills again... forever | Continuous oscillation |
| **Bistable** | Two buttons — "SET" (fill) and "RESET" (drain) — state stays until the other button is pressed | Latch / memory element |

This analogy directly maps to:
- **Capacitor (C)** → Water bucket
- **Resistor (R)** → Tap controlling fill rate
- **Discharge transistor** → Drain valve
- **2 Comparators** → Two water-level sensors
- **SR Flip-flop** → Memory that remembers whether tap is ON or OFF

## 1.3 Pin Configuration (8-Pin DIP Package)

| Pin No. | Pin Name | Function | KTU Key Point |
|---------|----------|----------|---------------|
| **1** | **GND** | Ground reference (0 V) | Common reference for all signals |
| **2** | **TRIG** | Trigger input (active LOW) | Detects voltage < **VCC/3** |
| **3** | **OUT** | Output | Sources/Sinks **200 mA** |
| **4** | **RESET** | Master Reset (active LOW) | Forces output LOW |
| **5** | **CONT** | Control Voltage | External modulation input |
| **6** | **THRES** | Threshold input | Detects voltage > **2VCC/3** |
| **7** | **DISCH** | Discharge (open-collector) | Discharges timing capacitor |
| **8** | **VCC** | Supply voltage | **+5 V to +15 V** (typical **+9 V** or **+12 V**) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 555 derives its name from the **three 5 kΩ resistors** inside the voltage divider network that produce the reference voltages **VCC/3** and **2VCC/3**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Architecture — Block-by-Block Analysis

The 555 IC contains **six functional blocks**. Understanding each is critical for KTU derivations.

### Block 1: Resistive Voltage Divider (Three 5 kΩ Resistors)
- Forms a potential divider between **VCC** and **GND**.
- Two reference voltages are generated:
  - **Lower reference** at **Pin 5 (CONT)** = **2VCC/3** (at the top of the lower 5 kΩ).
  - **Upper comparator reference** = **VCC/3** (at the junction of lower two 5 kΩ).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 5 kΩ? Why not 10 kΩ or 1 kΩ?**
> The 5 kΩ value provides low enough impedance to override any stray leakage while keeping current consumption low. The internal divider draws only **3VCC / 15 kΩ = 0.2 mA** at 10 V — negligible power loss.

### Block 2: Upper Comparator (Threshold Comparator — COMP1)
- **Non-inverting input (+):** Pin 6 (THRES)
- **Inverting input (−):** Internal reference **2VCC/3**
- **Behavior:** Output is HIGH ⇔ V(Pin 6) > **2VCC/3**
- This output drives the **RESET** input of the internal SR flip-flop.

### Block 3: Lower Comparator (Trigger Comparator — COMP2)
- **Inverting input (−):** Pin 2 (TRIG)
- **Non-inverting input (+):** Internal reference **VCC/3**
- **Behavior:** Output is HIGH ⇔ V(Pin 2) < **VCC/3**
- This output drives the **SET** input of the internal SR flip-flop.

### Block 4: SR Flip-Flop (Bistable Latch)
- Stores the state of the timer.
- **SET (S):** From lower comparator → forces Q = HIGH, $\overline{Q}$ = LOW.
- **RESET (R):** From upper comparator → forces Q = LOW, $\overline{Q}$ = HIGH.
- Dominant **MASTER RESET** (Pin 4) overrides both — forces Q = LOW directly.

### Block 5: Discharge Transistor (Q1 — NPN Open-Collector)
- Base is driven by $\overline{Q}$.
- When $\overline{Q}$ = HIGH → transistor **ON** → Pin 7 is shorted to GND → capacitor **discharges**.
- When $\overline{Q}$ = LOW → transistor **OFF** → Pin 7 is **open-circuit** → capacitor **charges** through external R.

### Block 6: Output Stage (Totem-Pole Driver)
- A push-pull complementary output stage.
- Can **source or sink** up to **200 mA**, making it capable of directly driving relays, LEDs, speakers, and small motors.
- Inverts the Q output: Output = $\overline{Q}$ in some datasheets (KTU convention: Output follows Q for active output).

## 2.2 Functional Truth Table (Master Table for All Modes)

| Pin 4 (RESET) | Pin 2 (TRIG) | Pin 6 (THRES) | Pin 3 (OUT) | Pin 7 (DISCH) |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| LOW | × | × | LOW | ON (LOW) |
| HIGH | < VCC/3 | < 2VCC/3 | HIGH | OFF (open) |
| HIGH | > VCC/3 | > 2VCC/3 | LOW | ON (LOW) |
| HIGH | > VCC/3 | < 2VCC/3 | **Retains previous state** | **Retains previous state** |
| HIGH | < VCC/3 | > 2VCC/3 | ❌ Not allowed (ambiguous) | — |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table — these are the 6 most-tested formulas in KTU 2024 exams on this topic.**

| # | Configuration | Parameter | Formula | Units | Typical Range |
|:-:|:-|:--|:--|:-:|:-:|
| 1 | **Monostable** | Pulse Width | $T = RC \ln(3) \approx 1.1\,RC$ | seconds | $10\,\mu s$ to several minutes |
| 2 | **Astable** | HIGH time | $t_H = (R_1 + R_2)C \ln(2) \approx 0.693(R_1 + R_2)C$ | seconds | — |
| 3 | **Astable** | LOW time | $t_L = R_2 C \ln(2) \approx 0.693\,R_2 C$ | seconds | — |
| 4 | **Astable** | Time Period | $T = 0.693(R_1 + 2R_2)C$ | seconds | — |
| 5 | **Astable** | Frequency | $f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2)C}$ | Hz | < 500 kHz |
| 6 | **Astable** | Duty Cycle | $D = \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2R_2} \times 100\%$ | % | Always > 50% |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Duty cycle of standard 555 astable is **always > 50%**. To get a 50% (square wave) duty cycle, you need a diode bypass across R₂ (modified circuit, not in basic syllabus).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications 🏭

- **Automotive electronics:** Turn-signal flashers, windshield wiper delay circuits.
- **Consumer electronics:** Toy sound generators, kitchen timer buzzers, alarm circuits.
- **Industrial control:** Missing-pulse detectors, sequential timing chains, PWM motor control.
- **Instrumentation:** Function generators, precision time-delay circuits.
- **Embedded systems:** Clock generation for microcontrollers, debounce circuits for switches.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Pulse Width of Monostable Multivibrator ⏱️

**Setup:** A 555 in monostable mode has a resistor R connected between VCC and Pin 7, and a capacitor C from Pin 7 to GND. Pin 2 is the trigger input.

**Step 1: Identify the stable state (before trigger).**
- Initially, Pin 2 is held HIGH (V > VCC/3) by a pull-up resistor.
- The internal flip-flop is in RESET state → Q = LOW, $\overline{Q}$ = HIGH.
- Discharge transistor Q1 is **ON** → Pin 7 is grounded → capacitor C is **fully discharged** (V_C = 0).
- Output (Pin 3) = LOW (stable state).

**Step 2: Apply a negative trigger pulse.**
- Trigger pulse makes V(Pin 2) drop below **VCC/3** momentarily.
- Lower comparator output goes HIGH → SET input of flip-flop is activated.
- Q = HIGH, $\overline{Q}$ = LOW.
- Discharge transistor turns **OFF** → Pin 7 becomes open circuit.
- Output (Pin 3) goes **HIGH** (entering quasi-stable state).

**Step 3: Capacitor charges through R toward VCC.**
- With Q1 OFF, the capacitor charges from V_C(0) = 0 toward VCC through resistor R.
- The standard RC charging equation:

$$v_C(t) = V_{CC}\left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)$$

**Step 4: Determine when the quasi-stable state ends.**
- The capacitor voltage is fed to Pin 6 (THRES).
- When V_C reaches **2VCC/3**, the upper comparator trips → RESET the flip-flop.
- Quasi-stable state ends. So, substitute V_C(T) = **2VCC/3** and t = T:

$$V_{CC}\left(1 - e^{-T/RC}\right) = \frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$$

**Step 5: Solve the equation step by step.**

$$1 - e^{-T/RC} = \frac{2}{3}$$

$$e^{-T/RC} = 1 - \frac{2}{3} = \frac{1}{3}$$

Taking natural logarithm on both sides:

$$-\frac{T}{RC} = \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) = -\ln(3)$$

**Step 6: Final expression for pulse width.**

$$\boxed{T = RC \ln(3) \approx 1.0986\,RC \approx 1.1\,RC}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The natural logarithm of 3 is approximately **1.0986**. KTU questions frequently substitute this as **1.1 RC**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Frequency of Astable Multivibrator 🔁

**Setup:** Resistor R₁ is between VCC and Pin 7. Resistor R₂ is between Pin 7 and Pin 6 (and Pin 2 shorted to Pin 6). Capacitor C is from Pin 6 to GND.

### Phase 1: Charging Cycle (Output HIGH)

**Step 1:** At the start of charging, the capacitor voltage is **VCC/3** (just reached the lower threshold).
- Discharge transistor Q1 is **OFF**.
- Capacitor charges through **(R₁ + R₂)** in series toward VCC.
- Charging equation from initial voltage V_i = VCC/3:

$$v_C(t) = V_{CC} - \left(V_{CC} - \frac{V_{CC}}{3}\right)e^{-t/(R_1+R_2)C}$$

$$v_C(t) = V_{CC} - \frac{2V_{CC}}{3} \cdot e^{-t/(R_1+R_2)C}$$

**Step 2:** Charging ends when V_C reaches **2VCC/3** (this is the upper threshold).

$$\frac{2V_{CC}}{3} = V_{CC} - \frac{2V_{CC}}{3} \cdot e^{-t_H/(R_1+R_2)C}$$

$$\frac{2V_{CC}}{3} \cdot e^{-t_H/(R_1+R_2)C} = V_{CC} - \frac{2V_{CC}}{3} = \frac{V_{CC}}{3}$$

$$e^{-t_H/(R_1+R_2)C} = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Solving for t_H (HIGH time):

$$-\frac{t_H}{(R_1+R_2)C} = \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -\ln(2)$$

$$\boxed{t_H = (R_1 + R_2)\,C\,\ln(2) \approx 0.693\,(R_1 + R_2)\,C}$$

### Phase 2: Discharging Cycle (Output LOW)

**Step 4:** Once V_C crosses 2VCC/3, the upper comparator resets the flip-flop. $\overline{Q}$ goes HIGH → Q1 turns ON.
- Capacitor now **discharges** through R₂ only (R₁ is blocked by Q1) into Pin 7.
- Discharge path: C → R₂ → Pin 7 → GND.
- Discharging equation from V_i = 2VCC/3:

$$v_C(t) = \frac{2V_{CC}}{3} \cdot e^{-t/R_2 C}$$

**Step 5:** Discharging ends when V_C reaches **VCC/3**.

$$\frac{V_{CC}}{3} = \frac{2V_{CC}}{3} \cdot e^{-t_L/R_2 C}$$

$$e^{-t_L/R_2 C} = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 6:** Solving for t_L (LOW time):

$$\boxed{t_L = R_2\,C\,\ln(2) \approx 0.693\,R_2 C}$$

### Phase 3: Total Time Period and Frequency

**Step 7:** Total time period:

$$T = t_H + t_L = 0.693(R_1 + R_2)C + 0.693\,R_2 C = 0.693(R_1 + 2R_2)C$$

**Step 8:** Frequency of oscillation:

$$\boxed{f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{0.693(R_1 + 2R_2)C} = \frac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2)C}}$$

**Step 9:** Duty Cycle (percentage of time output is HIGH):

$$\boxed{D = \frac{t_H}{T} \times 100\% = \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2R_2} \times 100\%}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mode Selection Guide

| Property | Monostable | Astable | Bistable |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| **Number of stable states** | 1 (stable) + 1 (quasi-stable) | 0 — always oscillating | 2 stable states |
| **External components** | 1 Resistor, 1 Capacitor | 2 Resistors, 1 Capacitor | Only 2 pull-up resistors |
| **Trigger requirement** | External negative pulse | Self-triggered (no trigger) | External SET/RESET pulses |
| **Output frequency** | Single pulse per trigger | f = 1.44/[(R₁+2R₂)C] | DC level (no oscillation) |
| **Common use** | Timer, missing-pulse detector | Clock generator, LED flasher | Latch, switch debouncer |
| **Pin connections** | Pin 6 & 2 separate | Pin 2 & 6 shorted together | Pin 6 & 2 used as separate inputs |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🔷 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Internal Block Diagram of IC 555

```mermaid
graph TD
    VCCNODE[VCC Pin 8] --> R5KA[5K Resistor Top]
    R5KA --> R5KB[5K Resistor Middle]
    R5KB --> R5KC[5K Resistor Bottom]
    R5KC --> GNDNODE1[GND Pin 1]
    
    R5KB --> REFHI[2VCC by 3 Reference]
    R5KC --> REFLO[VCC by 3 Reference]
    
    PIN6[Pin 6 THRESHOLD] --> UCOMP{Upper Comparator}
    REFHI --> UCOMP
    UCOMP -->|Pin 6 more than 2VCC by 3| RINPUT[R Input of SR FF]
    
    PIN2[Pin 2 TRIGGER] --> LCOMP{Lower Comparator}
    REFLO --> LCOMP
    LCOMP -->|Pin 2 less than VCC by 3| SINPUT[S Input of SR FF]
    
    PIN4[Pin 4 RESET] --> MRINPUT[Master Reset]
    
    RINPUT --> SRFF[SR Flip Flop]
    SINPUT --> SRFF
    MRINPUT --> SRFF
    
    SRFF --> QOUT[Q Output]
    SRFF --> QBAR[Q Bar Output]
    
    QBAR --> DISCTRANS[Discharge Transistor Q1]
    PIN7[Pin 7 DISCHARGE] --> DISCTRANS
    
    QOUT --> OUTBUF[Output Buffer Driver]
    OUTBUF --> PIN3[Pin 3 OUTPUT]
    
    PIN5[Pin 5 CONTROL VOLTAGE] -.Modulates.-> REFHI
```

## 4.2 Pin Configuration Diagram (8-Pin DIP — Top View)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph DIP8[IC 555 Top View]
        P1((1 GND)) --- P2((2 TRIG))
        P2 --- P3((3 OUT))
        P3 --- P4((4 RESET))
        P4 --- P5((5 CTRL))
        P5 --- P6((6 THRES))
        P6 --- P7((7 DISCH))
        P7 --- P8((8 VCC))
    end
    
    P1 --> P8
```

## 4.3 Monostable Multivibrator Circuit (One-Shot)

```mermaid
graph LR
    VCC[VCC] --> R1[Resistor R]
    R1 --> PIN7[Pin 7 DISCH]
    PIN7 --> CAP[Capacitor C]
    CAP --> GND[GND]
    
    VCC --> PULLUP[Pull up 10K]
    PULLUP --> PIN2[Pin 2 TRIG]
    
    TRIGSRC[External Trigger Pulse] -.Negative Edge.-> PIN2
    
    PIN7 -.-> PIN6[Pin 6 THRES]
    PIN6 -.-> PIN2
    
    PIN3[Pin 3 OUTPUT] --> LOAD[Load LED or Relay]
    LOAD --> GND
    
    PIN4[Pin 4 RESET] --> VCC
    PIN5[Pin 5 CTRL] --> CDEC[0.01 uF Decoupling Cap]
    CDEC --> GND
```

**Working Summary:**
- **Stable state:** Pin 2 HIGH → Capacitor fully discharged (Pin 7 ON) → Output LOW.
- **Trigger event:** Negative pulse on Pin 2 (< VCC/3) → flip-flop SETs → Output goes HIGH, transistor OFF.
- **Timing phase:** Capacitor charges from 0 V toward VCC through R.
- **End of pulse:** When V_C = 2VCC/3, upper comparator RESETS flip-flop → Output LOW, transistor ON, C discharges rapidly.

## 4.4 Astable Multivibrator Circuit (Free-Running Oscillator)

```mermaid
graph LR
    VCCNODE[VCC] --> RA[Resistor R1]
    RA --> NODE7[Node 7]
    NODE7 --> RB[Resistor R2]
    RB --> NODEC[Node C]
    NODEC --> CAP[Capacitor C]
    CAP --> GNDNODE[GND]
    
    NODE7 -.-> PIN7[Pin 7 DISCH]
    NODEC -.-> PIN6[Pin 6 THRES]
    NODEC -.-> PIN2[Pin 2 TRIG]
    
    PIN4[Pin 4 RESET] --> VCCNODE
    PIN5[Pin 5 CTRL] --> CDEC2[0.01 uF Bypass Cap]
    CDEC2 --> GNDNODE
    
    PIN3[Pin 3 OUTPUT] --> OUTSIG[Square Wave Output]
```

**Working Summary:**
- No external trigger needed — circuit is self-oscillating.
- **Charging path:** VCC → R₁ → R₂ → C → GND (transistor OFF).
- **Discharging path:** C → R₂ → Pin 7 → GND (transistor ON).
- Capacitor voltage swings between **VCC/3** and **2VCC/3** continuously.

## 4.5 Bistable Multivibrator Circuit (Flip-Flop Mode)

```mermaid
graph LR
    VCCNODE[VCC] --> PUS1[Pull up R1]
    PUS1 --> PIN2[Pin 2 TRIG]
    
    VCCNODE --> PUS2[Pull up R2]
    PUS2 --> PIN6[Pin 6 THRES]
    
    SETBTN[Set Push Button] -.Active LOW.-> PIN2
    RESETBTN[Reset Push Button] -.Active LOW.-> PIN6
    
    PIN7[Pin 7 DISCH] -.Unused.-> OPEN1[Left Open]
    PIN5[Pin 5 CTRL] --> CDEC3[0.01 uF]
    CDEC3 --> GNDNODE
    
    PIN3[Pin 3 OUTPUT] --> OUTSIG2[Stable DC Output]
```

**Working Summary:**
- No capacitor — no timing.
- Pressing **SET button** pulls Pin 2 below VCC/3 → Output latches HIGH.
- Pressing **RESET button** pulls Pin 6 above 2VCC/3 → Output latches LOW.
- State is **retained** indefinitely until the opposite button is pressed.

## 4.6 Timing Waveforms Comparison (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph MONO[Monostable Waveforms]
        TRIGM[Trigger Pin 2] --> TRIGM_WF[Short negative pulse]
        CAPM[Capacitor Voltage] --> CAPM_WF[Charges from 0 to 2VCC by 3]
        OUTM[Output Pin 3] --> OUTM_WF[Single HIGH pulse of width T = 1.1 RC]
    end
    
    subgraph AST[Astable Waveforms]
        CAPA[Capacitor Voltage] --> CAPA_WF[Sawtooth between VCC by 3 and 2VCC by 3]
        OUTA[Output Pin 3] --> OUTA_WF[Continuous square wave]
    end
```

---

# 🔷 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 📝 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List the basic building blocks of IC 555 timer. Draw its pin diagram and explain the function of each pin.**

**Model Answer:**

The 555 timer IC consists of the following internal building blocks:
1. A **voltage divider** with three equal 5 kΩ resistors producing references of **VCC/3** and **2VCC/3**.
2. **Two comparators** — the upper (threshold) comparator and the lower (trigger) comparator.
3. A **bistable SR flip-flop** to store the state.
4. A **discharge transistor** (NPN, open-collector).
5. A **totem-pole output stage** capable of sourcing/sinking 200 mA.

**Pin Functions (8-pin DIP):**
| Pin | Name | Function |
|:--|:--|:--|
| 1 | GND | Ground reference (0 V) |
| 2 | TRIG | Trigger input; output goes HIGH when V < VCC/3 |
| 3 | OUT | Output terminal (200 mA drive) |
| 4 | RESET | Master reset (active LOW); output forced LOW |
| 5 | CONT | Control voltage; modulates 2VCC/3 threshold |
| 6 | THRES | Threshold input; output goes LOW when V > 2VCC/3 |
| 7 | DISCH | Open-collector discharge pin for timing capacitor |
| 8 | VCC | Positive supply (+5 V to +15 V) |

*(3 marks — Marks distribution: 1 mark block diagram, 1 mark pin diagram, 1 mark function description)*

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the function of the control voltage terminal (Pin 5) of IC 555.**

**Model Answer:**

Pin 5 (Control Voltage) is the **non-inverting input of the upper comparator** and provides external access to the **2VCC/3 reference voltage** node of the internal divider.

**Functions:**
1. **Frequency modulation:** Applying an external voltage to Pin 5 directly changes the comparator threshold, which alters the charging/discharging reference levels. This **frequency modulates** an astable 555.
2. **Pulse-width modulation:** In monostable mode, varying Pin 5 changes the upper threshold (2VCC/3) and thus modulates the output pulse width.
3. **Bypassing for noise immunity:** In most applications, Pin 5 is decoupled to ground via a **0.01 µF capacitor** to filter out supply noise and prevent false triggering.

> [!NOTE]
> By default (Pin 5 open or bypassed), the upper comparator reference is fixed at **2VCC/3**.

*(3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 📝 PART B: Long Answer Questions — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of **IC 555 in monostable mode**. Derive the expression for the pulse width. (7 marks)

**(b)** Design a monostable multivibrator using IC 555 to produce an output pulse of **50 ms** duration. (7 marks)

---

#### ✅ SOLUTION (a): Monostable Operation & Pulse Width Derivation

**Circuit Description:**
- Resistor R is connected between VCC (Pin 8) and Pin 7 (Discharge).
- Capacitor C is connected between Pin 7 and GND.
- Pin 6 (Threshold) is externally shorted to Pin 7, so it monitors the capacitor voltage.
- Pin 2 (Trigger) is the input, normally held HIGH via a pull-up resistor.
- Pin 4 (Reset) is tied to VCC; Pin 5 is bypassed to GND via 0.01 µF.

**Operation:**

**Step 1 (Stable state):** Pin 2 is HIGH (> VCC/3). The lower comparator output is LOW. The flip-flop remains in RESET state (Q = LOW, $\overline{Q}$ = HIGH). Discharge transistor Q1 is **ON** → Pin 7 is grounded → V_C = 0 V. Output (Pin 3) = **LOW**.

**Step 2 (Trigger):** A negative pulse on Pin 2 takes V(Pin 2) < VCC/3 momentarily. The lower comparator SETs the flip-flop → Q = HIGH, $\overline{Q}$ = LOW. Output (Pin 3) goes **HIGH**. Transistor Q1 turns **OFF** (Pin 7 becomes open).

**Step 3 (Quasi-stable / Timing phase):** Capacitor C charges from 0 V toward VCC through R:

$$v_C(t) = V_{CC}\left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)$$

**Step 4 (End of pulse):** When v_C reaches **2VCC/3**, the upper comparator RESETS the flip-flop. Output goes LOW. Q1 turns ON, rapidly discharging C back to 0 V. The circuit returns to the stable state.

**Derivation of pulse width T:**

$$V_{CC}\left(1 - e^{-T/RC}\right) = \frac{2V_{CC}}{3}$$

$$1 - e^{-T/RC} = \frac{2}{3} \quad\Rightarrow\quad e^{-T/RC} = \frac{1}{3}$$

$$T = RC \ln(3) \approx 1.1\,RC$$

*(7 marks — 2 marks circuit, 3 marks operation, 2 marks derivation)*

---

#### ✅ SOLUTION (b): Design for T = 50 ms

**Given:** T = 50 ms = 50 × 10⁻³ s
**Formula:** T = 1.1 RC → RC = T / 1.1 = 50 / 1.1 = **45.45 ms**

**Step 1: Choose a convenient capacitor value.**
Select C = **10 µF** (standard value, non-polarized preferred).

**Step 2: Calculate R.**

$$R = \frac{T}{1.1 \cdot C} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.1 \times 10 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{11 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$R = \frac{50}{11} \times 10^{3} = 4.545\ \text{k}\Omega \approx 4.7\ \text{k}\Omega \text{ (standard value)}$$

**Step 3: Verify the design.**
- C = 10 µF, R = 4.7 kΩ
- T = 1.1 × 4.7 × 10³ × 10 × 10⁻⁶ = 1.1 × 47 × 10⁻³ = **51.7 ms** ≈ 50 ms ✓

**Design Summary:**
- **R = 4.7 kΩ** (connected between VCC and Pin 7)
- **C = 10 µF** (connected between Pin 7 and GND)
- **Pull-up resistor at Pin 2 = 10 kΩ**
- **Supply VCC = +5 V or +12 V**
- **Decoupling capacitor at Pin 5 = 0.01 µF**

*(7 marks — 2 marks formula application, 3 marks design steps, 2 marks component selection)*

---

### **❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Draw the circuit diagram of **IC 555 connected as an astable multivibrator**. Explain its working with relevant waveforms. Derive the expression for the frequency of oscillation. (7 marks)

**(b)** For an astable multivibrator using IC 555, R₁ = **4.7 kΩ**, R₂ = **1 kΩ**, and C = **0.1 µF**. Calculate the **(i)** charging time, **(ii)** discharging time, **(iii)** frequency, and **(iv)** duty cycle. (7 marks)

---

#### ✅ SOLUTION (a): Astable Circuit, Working & Frequency Derivation

**Circuit:** (R₁ between VCC and Pin 7, R₂ between Pin 7 and the C node, C from that node to GND, Pin 2 and Pin 6 tied together, Pin 4 to VCC, Pin 5 bypassed.)

**Working (Two phases):**

**Charging Phase (Output HIGH):**
- Capacitor charges through R₁ and R₂ in series.
- V_C rises exponentially from VCC/3 toward VCC.
- Output stays HIGH until V_C = 2VCC/3.
- Duration: $t_H = 0.693(R_1 + R_2)C$

**Discharging Phase (Output LOW):**
- Upper comparator resets the flip-flop → transistor Q1 turns ON.
- Capacitor discharges through R₂ (R₁ is bypassed via Pin 7 to GND).
- V_C falls from 2VCC/3 toward 0.
- Discharging stops at V_C = VCC/3.
- Duration: $t_L = 0.693\,R_2 C$

**Waveforms:** Square wave at Pin 3; sawtooth (RC charge/discharge) at V_C.

**Frequency Derivation:**

$$T = t_H + t_L = 0.693(R_1 + 2R_2)C$$

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2)C}$$

*(7 marks — 2 marks circuit, 2 marks working, 3 marks derivation)*

---

#### ✅ SOLUTION (b): Numerical Problem

**Given:** R₁ = 4.7 kΩ, R₂ = 1 kΩ, C = 0.1 µF

**(i) Charging time (t_H):**

$$t_H = 0.693(R_1 + R_2)C = 0.693 \times (4700 + 1000) \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$t_H = 0.693 \times 5700 \times 10^{-7} = 3.95 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{s} = 395\,\mu s$$

**(ii) Discharging time (t_L):**

$$t_L = 0.693 \times R_2 \times C = 0.693 \times 1000 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$t_L = 0.693 \times 10^{-7} = 6.93 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{s} = 69.3\,\mu s$$

**(iii) Frequency:**

$$f = \frac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2)C} = \frac{1.44}{(4700 + 2000) \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$f = \frac{1.44}{6700 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{1.44}{6.7 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 2149.25\,\text{Hz} \approx 2.15\,\text{kHz}$$

**(iv) Duty Cycle:**

$$D = \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2R_2} \times 100\% = \frac{4700 + 1000}{4700 + 2000} \times 100\%$$

$$D = \frac{5700}{6700} \times 100\% \approx 85.07\%$$

*(7 marks — 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 split; full marks require correct units and unit conversions)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Writing "T = 0.693 × 5700 × 0.1" without converting 0.1 µF to 10⁻⁷ F → lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing R₁ and R₂ in duty cycle:** Many students use R₂ only — the standard duty cycle is **(R₁+R₂)/(R₁+2R₂)**, not R₂/(R₁+R₂).
> 3. **Forgetting to convert µ to exponent:** 0.1 µF = 0.1 × 10⁻⁶ F, NOT 0.1 F. KTU explicitly checks this.
> 4. **Writing "1.1 RC" without stating RC in seconds** → 0.5 mark deduction in numerical design.
> 5. **Not mentioning Pin 4 tied to VCC and Pin 5 bypassed in circuit diagrams** → lose 0.5–1 mark for incomplete circuit.

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 4 — IC 555 Timer**

### ⚡ Architecture Essentials
- ✅ The name "**555**" comes from the **three 5 kΩ resistors** in the internal voltage divider.
- ✅ Two comparator references: **VCC/3** (lower) and **2VCC/3** (upper).
- ✅ Internal blocks: **Voltage divider + 2 comparators + SR flip-flop + Discharge transistor + Output buffer**.
- ✅ Supply range: **+5 V to +15 V**; Output current: **±200 mA**.

### ⚡ Pin Function Mastery
- ✅ **Pin 2 (TRIG):** Active LOW — fires when V < VCC/3 → SETs flip-flop.
- ✅ **Pin 6 (THRES):** Active HIGH — fires when V > 2VCC/3 → RESETs flip-flop.
- ✅ **Pin 4 (RESET):** Master reset, active LOW — overrides everything.
- ✅ **Pin 5 (CONT):** Direct access to 2VCC/3 reference for modulation.
- ✅ **Pin 7 (DISCH):** Open-collector — sinks current to GND when ON.
- ✅ **Pin 3 (OUT):** Totem-pole output.

### ⚡ Formula Arsenal (Memorize All 6)
- ✅ **Monostable pulse width:** $T = 1.1 RC$
- ✅ **Astable HIGH time:** $t_H = 0.693(R_1 + R_2)C$
- ✅ **Astable LOW time:** $t_L = 0.693 R_2 C$
- ✅ **Astable period:** $T = 0.693(R_1 + 2R_2)C$
- ✅ **Astable frequency:** $f = \dfrac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2)C}$
- ✅ **Astable duty cycle:** $D = \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2R_2} \times 100\%$

### ⚡ Mode Comparison Tricks
- ✅ **Monostable** = 1 stable + 1 quasi-stable → one pulse per trigger.
- ✅ **Astable** = 0 stable states → continuous oscillation, NO trigger needed.
- ✅ **Bistable** = 2 stable states → no capacitor, no timing, behaves like SR latch.
- ✅ Duty cycle of basic astable is **always > 50%** — examiner's favorite trick question.

### ⚡ Numerical Problem Strategy
- ✅ Always **convert µF, nF, kΩ, ms** to standard SI units (F, Ω, s) **before** substituting.
- ✅ Show the formula first, then plug in values, then state the final answer with **units**.
- ✅ For design problems, **assume a standard C** (0.01, 0.1, 1, 10 µF) and solve for R.
- ✅ Use **1.1 RC** for monostable and **1.44 / [(R₁+2R₂)C]** for astable frequency — these are KTU's gold standard constants.

### ⚡ Common KTU Examiner Pet Topics
- ✅ Difference between astable, monostable, and bistable — **asked in almost every exam**.
- ✅ Pin diagram with **labelled functions** — high-weightage 3-mark question.
- ✅ Derivation of pulse width or frequency — **guaranteed 7-mark derivation** in Part B.
- ✅ Numerical design — given T or f, find R/C values.
- ✅ Function of Pin 5 (Control Voltage) — frequently asked as 3-marker.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Astable and Monostable Multivibrators Using 555
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCCIT302 - ELECTRONIC DEVICES & CIRCUITS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Astable and Monostable Multivibrators Using 555

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The 555 Timer — Foundation Block

The **IC 555 Timer** is a highly stable, monolithic integrated circuit used for generating accurate time delays or oscillations. Designed by **Signetics Corporation in 1971**, it contains two comparators, an SR flip-flop, a discharge transistor, and a resistive voltage divider network. It can operate in three modes: **Astable**, **Monostable**, and **Bistable**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** Students are expected to derive the timing equations, explain the charging/discharging mechanism, draw the circuit and waveforms, and solve numerical problems on frequency and pulse width for both Astable and Monostable configurations.

### 1.2 Astable Multivibrator using 555

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** An *Astable Multivibrator* (also called a *Free-Running Multivibrator*) has **no stable state**. It continuously switches between two quasi-stable states, producing a **rectangular/square wave output** without any external triggering pulse.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Automatic Swing:** Imagine a child on a swing that you push once gently — the swing goes back and forth (quasi-stable extreme points), but the child's motion continuously switches direction without any external input. Similarly, the 555 astable circuit "swings" between HIGH and LOW automatically, generating a continuous train of pulses.

**Key Characteristics:**
- No external trigger required (self-triggered)
- **Two quasi-stable states** (no permanent state)
- Output is a **square wave / rectangular wave**
- Frequency determined by external R and C
- Threshold levels: **1/3 Vcc** and **2/3 Vcc**

### 1.3 Monostable Multivibrator using 555

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** A *Monostable Multivibrator* (also called a *One-Shot*) has **ONE stable state and ONE quasi-stable state**. It remains in the stable state until an external trigger pulse is applied, after which it switches to the quasi-stable state for a predetermined duration and then automatically returns to the stable state.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Doorbell:** A doorbell is normally silent (stable state). When you press the button (trigger), it rings for a fixed duration (quasi-stable state) and then automatically stops, returning to silence. You must press again for another ring. The 555 monostable behaves identically.

**Key Characteristics:**
- Requires an **external negative trigger pulse** at Pin 2 (< 1/3 Vcc)
- **One stable state** (output LOW in standard configuration)
- **One quasi-stable state** (output HIGH for a fixed duration T = **1.1 RC**)
- Generates a **single pulse of fixed width**

### 1.4 Pin Configuration of IC 555

| Pin No. | Name | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | GND | Ground reference (**0 V**) |
| 2 | TRIGGER | Negative edge trigger input (comparator 1, threshold = 1/3 Vcc) |
| 3 | OUTPUT | Output waveform (HIGH or LOW) |
| 4 | RESET | Active-low reset (connects to Vcc to disable) |
| 5 | CONTROL | External voltage to modulate thresholds (bypassed by 0.01 µF) |
| 6 | THRESHOLD | Upper threshold input (comparator 2, threshold = 2/3 Vcc) |
| 7 | DISCHARGE | Open-collector discharge transistor |
| 8 | Vcc | Supply voltage (**+5 V to +18 V**) |

> [!NOTE]
> The internal voltage divider consists of **three equal 5 kΩ resistors**, which is why the chip is called the "555" timer. The two threshold voltages are **Vcc/3** and **2Vcc/3**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Internal Block Diagram of 555

The IC 555 consists of:
- A **Resistive Voltage Divider** (three 5 kΩ resistors) producing Vcc/3 and 2Vcc/3 reference voltages
- **Two Comparators** (C1 and C2): 
  - Comparator 1 (Trigger Comparator): compares Pin 2 with Vcc/3
  - Comparator 2 (Threshold Comparator): compares Pin 6 with 2Vcc/3
- An **SR Flip-Flop** (latch) with active-HIGH reset
- A **Discharge Transistor** (Q1, open-collector NPN) at Pin 7
- An **Output Buffer/Driver** (totem-pole) at Pin 3

### 2.2 Astable Multivibrator — Circuit & Operation

**Circuit Configuration:**
- Pin 1 → GND
- Pin 8 → Vcc
- Pin 4 → Vcc (no reset)
- Pin 5 → 0.01 µF to GND (decoupling)
- **R1** connects from Vcc to Pin 7
- **R2** connects from Pin 7 to Pin 6 and Pin 2 (junction)
- **Capacitor C** connects from Pin 6/Pin 2 to GND
- Pin 3 → Output

**Charging Phase (Output HIGH):**
1. Initially, discharge transistor Q1 is OFF.
2. Capacitor C charges through **(R1 + R2)** towards Vcc.
3. Voltage across C rises exponentially from **Vcc/3** to **2Vcc/3**.
4. When Vc reaches **2Vcc/3**, Comparator 2 (Threshold) triggers, RESETs the flip-flop.
5. Output goes LOW; Q1 turns ON.

**Discharging Phase (Output LOW):**
1. With Q1 ON, Pin 7 is effectively shorted to GND.
2. Capacitor discharges through **R2** (and Q1) from **2Vcc/3** to **Vcc/3**.
3. When Vc falls to **Vcc/3**, Comparator 1 (Trigger) triggers, SETs the flip-flop.
4. Output goes HIGH; Q1 turns OFF — cycle repeats.

### 2.3 Monostable Multivibrator — Circuit & Operation

**Circuit Configuration:**
- Pin 1 → GND
- Pin 8 → Vcc
- Pin 4 → Vcc
- Pin 5 → 0.01 µF to GND
- **Resistor R** connects from Vcc to Pin 7 and Pin 6
- **Capacitor C** connects from Pin 6 to GND
- Pin 2 receives the **negative trigger pulse**
- Pin 3 → Output

**Stable State (Output LOW):**
1. Q1 is ON, capacitor is shorted to GND, Vc = 0.
2. Comparator 1 sees Vc (at Pin 6) = 0 < 2Vcc/3 → no threshold trigger.
3. Output is LOW.

**Triggering:**
1. External negative pulse at Pin 2 drives it below Vcc/3.
2. Comparator 1 SETs the flip-flop → Output goes HIGH, Q1 turns OFF.

**Quasi-Stable State (Output HIGH):**
1. Capacitor charges through R from 0 to 2Vcc/3.
2. When Vc = 2Vcc/3, Comparator 2 triggers RESET → Output LOW, Q1 ON.
3. C rapidly discharges → returns to stable state.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Mode | Parameter | Formula | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Astable** | Charging Time $T_H$ | $T_H = 0.693 \times (R_1 + R_2) \times C$ | seconds |
| **Astable** | Discharging Time $T_L$ | $T_L = 0.693 \times R_2 \times C$ | seconds |
| **Astable** | Total Period $T$ | $T = T_H + T_L = 0.693 \times (R_1 + 2R_2) \times C$ | seconds |
| **Astable** | Frequency $f$ | $f = \dfrac{1.45}{(R_1 + 2R_2) \times C}$ | Hz |
| **Astable** | Duty Cycle $D$ | $D = \dfrac{T_H}{T} \times 100\% = \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2R_2} \times 100\%$ | % |
| **Monostable** | Pulse Width $T$ | $T = 1.1 \times R \times C$ | seconds |
| **Monostable** | Trigger Condition | $V_{trigger} \leq \dfrac{V_{cc}}{3}$ | volts |
| **Both** | Threshold Voltages | $V_{TH} = \dfrac{2V_{cc}}{3}$, $V_{TL} = \dfrac{V_{cc}}{3}$ | volts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Astable**: Used in LED flashers, PWM generators, tone generators, clock pulse generators for digital circuits, and missing-pulse detectors.
> - **Monostable**: Used in debouncing switches, frequency dividers, missing-pulse detectors, and one-shot timing applications in control systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Astable Timing Equations

#### Step 1: Charging Equation

During charging, the capacitor charges from **Vc(0) = Vcc/3** towards **Vcc** through the series combination of **R1 and R2**.

The general first-order RC charging equation is:

$$V_c(t) = V_{\text{final}} - (V_{\text{final}} - V_c(0)) \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

Here, $V_{\text{final}} = V_{cc}$ and $\tau = (R_1 + R_2)C$.

Substituting the values:

$$V_c(t) = V_{cc} - \left(V_{cc} - \frac{V_{cc}}{3}\right) e^{-t/((R_1+R_2)C)}$$

$$V_c(t) = V_{cc} - \frac{2V_{cc}}{3} \cdot e^{-t/((R_1+R_2)C)}$$

#### Step 2: Apply the Upper Threshold Condition

Charging stops when the capacitor voltage reaches the **upper threshold $V_{TH} = 2V_{cc}/3$** at time $t = T_H$.

Substituting:

$$\frac{2V_{cc}}{3} = V_{cc} - \frac{2V_{cc}}{3} \cdot e^{-T_H/((R_1+R_2)C)}$$

Dividing both sides by $V_{cc}$:

$$\frac{2}{3} = 1 - \frac{2}{3} \cdot e^{-T_H/((R_1+R_2)C)}$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{2}{3} \cdot e^{-T_H/((R_1+R_2)C)} = 1 - \frac{2}{3} = \frac{1}{3}$$

$$e^{-T_H/((R_1+R_2)C)} = \frac{1/3}{2/3} = \frac{1}{2}$$

Taking natural logarithm on both sides:

$$-\frac{T_H}{(R_1+R_2)C} = \ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -0.693$$

$$\boxed{T_H = 0.693 \times (R_1 + R_2) \times C}$$

#### Step 3: Discharging Equation

During discharge, the capacitor discharges from **Vc(0) = 2Vcc/3** through **R2** (and the discharge transistor, which is saturated) towards **0 V**.

The general first-order RC discharge equation is:

$$V_c(t) = V_c(0) \cdot e^{-t/\tau}$$

Here, $\tau = R_2 C$.

$$V_c(t) = \frac{2V_{cc}}{3} \cdot e^{-t/(R_2 C)}$$

#### Step 4: Apply the Lower Threshold Condition

Discharging stops when the capacitor reaches the **lower threshold $V_{TL} = V_{cc}/3$** at time $t = T_L$.

$$\frac{V_{cc}}{3} = \frac{2V_{cc}}{3} \cdot e^{-T_L/(R_2 C)}$$

Dividing both sides by $V_{cc}/3$:

$$1 = 2 \cdot e^{-T_L/(R_2 C)}$$

$$e^{-T_L/(R_2 C)} = \frac{1}{2}$$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$-\frac{T_L}{R_2 C} = \ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -0.693$$

$$\boxed{T_L = 0.693 \times R_2 \times C}$$

#### Step 5: Total Period and Frequency

$$\begin{aligned}
T &= T_H + T_L \\
T &= 0.693(R_1 + R_2)C + 0.693 R_2 C \\
T &= 0.693(R_1 + 2R_2)C
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore, the frequency of oscillation:

$$\boxed{f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1.45}{(R_1 + 2R_2)C}}$$

#### Step 6: Duty Cycle

$$\begin{aligned}
D &= \frac{T_H}{T} \times 100\% \\
D &= \frac{0.693(R_1 + R_2)C}{0.693(R_1 + 2R_2)C} \times 100\% \\
D &= \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2R_2} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> To achieve a **Duty Cycle of 50%**, a diode is connected in parallel with R2. In this case, charging occurs only through R1, and discharging occurs only through R2, giving a symmetric square wave.

### 3.2 Derivation of Monostable Pulse Width

#### Step 1: Charging Equation

When triggered, the discharge transistor turns OFF. The capacitor charges from **Vc(0) = 0 V** towards **Vcc** through resistor **R**.

$$V_c(t) = V_{cc}\left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)$$

#### Step 2: Apply the Threshold Condition

The quasi-stable state ends when **Vc = 2Vcc/3** at time $t = T$.

$$\frac{2V_{cc}}{3} = V_{cc}\left(1 - e^{-T/RC}\right)$$

Dividing both sides by $V_{cc}$:

$$\frac{2}{3} = 1 - e^{-T/RC}$$

$$e^{-T/RC} = 1 - \frac{2}{3} = \frac{1}{3}$$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$-\frac{T}{RC} = \ln\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) = -1.0986$$

$$\boxed{T = 1.0986 \times RC \approx 1.1 \times RC}$$

### 3.3 Solved Numerical Examples (KTU Pattern)

**Example 1 (Astable):** For an astable multivibrator using 555, $R_1 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 2 \text{ k}\Omega$, and $C = 0.01 \text{ μF}$. Calculate the frequency and duty cycle.

**Solution:**

$$T_H = 0.693 \times (1\text{k} + 2\text{k}) \times 0.01\mu = 0.693 \times 3 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-8} = 20.79 \text{ μs}$$

$$T_L = 0.693 \times 2\text{k} \times 0.01\mu = 0.693 \times 2 \times 10^3 \times 10^{-8} = 13.86 \text{ μs}$$

$$T = 0.693 \times (1\text{k} + 2 \times 2\text{k}) \times 0.01\mu = 34.65 \text{ μs}$$

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{34.65 \times 10^{-6}} = 28.86 \text{ kHz}$$

$$D = \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2R_2} \times 100 = \frac{3}{5} \times 100 = 60\%$$

**Example 2 (Monostable):** Design a monostable circuit using 555 to generate a pulse width of 100 ms. Take $C = 1 \text{ μF}$.

**Solution:**

$$T = 1.1 RC \implies R = \frac{T}{1.1 \times C} = \frac{100 \times 10^{-3}}{1.1 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}} = 90.9 \text{ k}\Omega$$

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of 555 Internal Architecture

flowchart LR
    A[Vcc at Pin 8] --> B[Resistive Divider]
