    B --> D[Reference 2Vcc by 3]
    C --> E[Comparator 1 Trigger]
    D --> F[Comparator 2 Threshold]
    G[Pin 2 Trigger Input] --> E
    H[Pin 6 Threshold Input] --> F
    E --> I[SR Flip Flop Latch]
    F --> I
    J[Pin 4 Reset] --> I
    I --> K[Output Buffer Pin 3]
    I --> L[Discharge Transistor Pin 7]
    M[GND Pin 1] --> N[Common Ground]
```

flowchart LR
    A[Vcc Supply] --> R1A[Resistor R1]

    R2A[Resistor R2] --> nodeJunction
    nodeJunction --> pin7[Pin 7 Discharge]
    nodeJunction --> pin6[Pin 6 Threshold]
    nodeJunction --> pin2[Pin 2 Trigger]
    CapA[Capacitor C] --> nodeJunction
    CapA --> GNDA[GND]
    pin2 --> Comp1A[Comparator 1]
    pin6 --> Comp2A[Comparator 2]
    Comp1A --> FFA[Flip Flop]
    Comp2A --> FFA
    FFA --> OutA[Pin 3 Output]
    FFA --> TransA[Discharge Transistor]
    TransA --> pin7
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vcc Supply] --> ResM[Resistor R]

    nodeJunctionM --> pin6M[Pin 6 Threshold]
    nodeJunctionM --> pin7M[Pin 7 Discharge]
    CapM[Capacitor C] --> nodeJunctionM
    CapM --> GNDM[GND]

```

### 4.4 Astable Output Waveform — State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StateCharging
    StateCharging: STATE A: Output HIGH Charging through R1 plus R2
    StateCharging --> StateThresholdHigh: Vc reaches 2Vcc by 3
    StateThresholdHigh: Threshold Comparator trips Flip Flop Resets
    StateThresholdHigh --> StateDischarging: Output goes LOW Transistor ON
    StateDischarging: STATE B: Output LOW Discharging through R2 only
    StateDischarging --> StateThresholdLow: Vc reaches Vcc by 3
    StateThresholdLow: Trigger Comparator trips Flip Flop Sets
    StateThresholdLow --> StateCharging: Output goes HIGH Transistor OFF
    StateThresholdLow --> [*]: Continuous oscillation
```

### 4.5 Monostable Output Waveform — State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StateStable
    StateStable: STATE A: Stable Output LOW Capacitor shorted Vc equals 0
    StateStable --> StateTrigger: External trigger at Pin 2 less than Vcc by 3
    StateTrigger: Trigger Comparator SETs Flip Flop Transistor OFF
    StateTrigger --> StateQuasi: Output goes HIGH
    StateQuasi: STATE B: Quasi Stable Output HIGH Capacitor charging through R
    StateQuasi --> StateThreshold: Vc reaches 2Vcc by 3
    StateThreshold: Threshold Comparator RESETs Flip Flop Transistor ON
    StateThreshold --> StateStable: Output returns LOW Auto recovery
    StateThreshold --> [*]: Pulse width equals 1.1 RC
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define an astable multivibrator. List any two applications of a 555 timer in astable mode.

**Model Answer:**
An astable multivibrator is a circuit that has **no stable state** and continuously switches between two quasi-stable states, producing a rectangular output waveform without any external trigger. The output is a **free-running square wave** whose frequency is set by external R and C.

**Applications:**
1. Square wave / clock pulse generator for digital systems.
2. Flasher circuits (LED flashers) and PWM signal generation.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** What is the function of the **discharge transistor (Pin 7)** in a 555 timer? Why is Pin 4 (Reset) connected to Vcc in normal operation?

**Model Answer:**
The **discharge transistor (Pin 7)** is an open-collector NPN transistor used to rapidly discharge the external timing capacitor. During the discharge phase of an astable (or stable state of a monostable), this transistor is turned ON, providing a low-resistance path (~10 Ω) to ground for the capacitor to discharge quickly.

**Pin 4 (Reset)** is an **active-low input**. When held LOW, it forces the flip-flop to reset (output LOW). For normal operation, it must be tied to Vcc to **disable the reset function** and allow the timer to operate normally.

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an **astable multivibrator using IC 555**. Derive the expressions for the **time period** and **frequency of oscillation**. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Description:** The astable multivibrator uses **R1, R2, and C** as external timing components. Pin 2 (Trigger) and Pin 6 (Threshold) are shorted together and connected to the junction of R2 and C. Pin 7 (Discharge) is connected between R1 and R2. Pins 4 and 8 are connected to Vcc. Pin 1 to GND. Pin 5 is decoupled to GND via 0.01 µF.

**Operation:**

**Phase 1 — Charging (Output HIGH):** Initially, let the capacitor be at Vc = Vcc/3. The internal flip-flop is SET, output = HIGH, and the discharge transistor Q1 is OFF. The capacitor charges through the series combination (R1 + R2) from Vcc/3 toward Vcc.

**Phase 2 — Threshold Trigger:** When Vc reaches the upper threshold of 2Vcc/3, the **Threshold Comparator (Comparator 2)** activates, RESETting the flip-flop. The output goes LOW, and the discharge transistor Q1 turns ON.

**Phase 3 — Discharging (Output LOW):** With Q1 ON, Pin 7 is pulled to ground. The capacitor now discharges through R2 (and Q1) from 2Vcc/3 towards 0 V.

**Phase 4 — Trigger Activation:** When Vc falls to the lower threshold of Vcc/3, the **Trigger Comparator (Comparator 1)** activates, SETting the flip-flop. The output returns HIGH, and Q1 turns OFF. The cycle repeats indefinitely.

**Derivation:**

Using the standard RC charging equation:

$$V_c(t) = V_{cc} - \frac{2V_{cc}}{3} e^{-t/((R_1+R_2)C)}$$

Setting $V_c(T_H) = 2V_{cc}/3$:

$$T_H = 0.693(R_1 + R_2)C$$

Using the standard RC discharge equation:

$$V_c(t) = \frac{2V_{cc}}{3} e^{-t/(R_2 C)}$$

Setting $V_c(T_L) = V_{cc}/3$:

$$T_L = 0.693 R_2 C$$

Total period and frequency:

$$T = T_H + T_L = 0.693(R_1 + 2R_2)C \implies f = \frac{1.45}{(R_1 + 2R_2)C}$$

**(b)** In a 555 astable circuit, $R_1 = 4.7 \text{ k}\Omega$, $R_2 = 1 \text{ k}\Omega$, and $C = 0.1 \text{ μF}$. Calculate the **(i) charging time, (ii) discharging time, (iii) frequency of oscillation, and (iv) duty cycle.** (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Charging Time:**

$$T_H = 0.693 \times (R_1 + R_2) \times C = 0.693 \times (4700 + 1000) \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$T_H = 0.693 \times 5700 \times 10^{-7} = 3.95 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 395 \text{ μs}$$

**(ii) Discharging Time:**

$$T_L = 0.693 \times R_2 \times C = 0.693 \times 1000 \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6} = 6.93 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 69.3 \text{ μs}$$

**(iii) Frequency:**

$$f = \frac{1.45}{(R_1 + 2R_2)C} = \frac{1.45}{(4700 + 2000) \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.45}{6.7 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$f = 2164.18 \text{ Hz} \approx 2.16 \text{ kHz}$$

**(iv) Duty Cycle:**

$$D = \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2R_2} \times 100\% = \frac{5700}{6700} \times 100\% = 85.07\%$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the working of a **monostable multivibrator using IC 555** with a neat circuit diagram and waveforms. Derive the expression for the **pulse width**. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit Description:** A resistor R is connected between Vcc and Pin 7 (Discharge). The junction of R and C is connected to Pin 6 (Threshold). The other terminal of C is connected to GND. The external trigger pulse is applied to Pin 2. Pins 4 and 8 are connected to Vcc.

**Stable State (Output LOW):** Initially, the discharge transistor Q1 is ON, holding the capacitor voltage Vc = 0. The Threshold Comparator sees Vc < 2Vcc/3, so it does not trigger. The output remains LOW.

**Triggering:** When a **negative trigger pulse** (< Vcc/3) is applied to Pin 2, the Trigger Comparator SETs the flip-flop. The output goes HIGH, and the discharge transistor Q1 turns OFF.

**Quasi-Stable State (Output HIGH):** With Q1 OFF, the capacitor charges from 0 V toward Vcc through resistor R. The output remains HIGH during this period.

**Return to Stable State:** When Vc reaches the upper threshold 2Vcc/3, the Threshold Comparator RESETs the flip-flop. The output returns LOW, and Q1 turns ON, rapidly discharging the capacitor back to 0 V. The circuit awaits the next trigger.

**Derivation of Pulse Width:**

The capacitor charges from 0 V to 2Vcc/3 according to:

$$V_c(t) = V_{cc}(1 - e^{-t/RC})$$

Setting $V_c(T) = 2V_{cc}/3$:

$$\frac{2}{3} = 1 - e^{-T/RC}$$

$$e^{-T/RC} = \frac{1}{3}$$

$$T = RC \cdot \ln(3) = 1.0986 \cdot RC \approx 1.1 RC$$

**(b)** A 555 monostable circuit uses $R = 100 \text{ k}\Omega$ and $C = 0.47 \text{ μF}$. Calculate the **pulse width**. If a **trigger pulse of 1 µs width** is applied, what is the minimum time before the **next trigger** can be applied? (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Pulse Width:**

$$T = 1.1 \times R \times C = 1.1 \times 100 \times 10^3 \times 0.47 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$T = 1.1 \times 4.7 \times 10^{-2} = 5.17 \times 10^{-2} \text{ s} = 51.7 \text{ ms}$$

**Recovery Time:** After the output pulse ends, the capacitor must discharge from 2Vcc/3 back to 0 V through the discharge transistor (saturation resistance ~10 Ω). The discharge time constant is very small.

For a monostable to be retriggered, the capacitor must fully discharge to **0 V (Vcc/3 or less)** so that the next trigger can produce a full pulse width. The minimum retrigger time is approximately equal to the pulse width itself, since:

$$T_{recovery} \approx 3 \times \tau_{discharge} \approx 3 \times R_{sat} \times C$$

This is negligible compared to 51.7 ms, so the **next trigger can be applied almost immediately** after the pulse ends (~a few microseconds).

**Total minimum cycle time:**

$$T_{cycle,min} = T_{pulse} + T_{recovery} \approx 51.7 \text{ ms} + \text{a few μs} \approx 51.7 \text{ ms}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting Pin 4 connection:** Students often forget to mention that Pin 4 (Reset) must be tied to Vcc. If left floating, the IC may randomly reset, causing erratic operation. (**−2 marks**)
> 2. **Confusing threshold levels:** Writing Vcc/2 instead of 2Vcc/3 for the upper threshold. The 555 has **two thresholds: Vcc/3 and 2Vcc/3** — remember both. (**−2 marks**)
> 3. **Using 0.7 instead of 0.693:** Always use **ln(2) = 0.693** for time period derivations, not 0.7. (**−1 mark**)
> 4. **Duty cycle > 50% confusion:** In astable mode without a diode, the duty cycle is **always greater than 50%** because both R1 and R2 are in the charging path. (**−2 marks**)
> 5. **Monostable trigger polarity:** The trigger at Pin 2 must be **active-LOW** (less than Vcc/3). Students frequently write "positive trigger" — this is wrong. (**−3 marks**)

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 4 — 555 Timer):**

- ⭐ **IC 555 Pinout:** Pin 1=GND, Pin 2=Trigger, Pin 3=Output, Pin 4=Reset, Pin 5=Control, Pin 6=Threshold, Pin 7=Discharge, Pin 8=Vcc.
- ⭐ **Internal threshold levels:** $V_{TL} = V_{cc}/3$ (lower), $V_{TH} = 2V_{cc}/3$ (upper).
- ⭐ **Astable:** No stable state, free-running. Charging through **(R1+R2)**, discharging through **R2 only**.
- ⭐ **Astable frequency formula:** $f = \dfrac{1.45}{(R_1 + 2R_2)C}$ — **MUST MEMORIZE.**
- ⭐ **Astable duty cycle:** $D = \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2R_2} \times 100\%$ — always > 50% (without diode).
- ⭐ **Monostable:** One stable state (LOW) + one quasi-stable state (HIGH of duration 1.1 RC).
- ⭐ **Monostable pulse width:** $T = 1.1 RC$ — derived from $\ln(3) = 1.0986$.
- ⭐ **Trigger requirement:** Pin 2 must be driven **below $V_{cc}/3$** (active-LOW, negative edge).
- ⭐ **Reset (Pin 4):** Active-LOW; must be tied to $V_{cc}$ in normal operation.
- ⭐ **Control voltage (Pin 5):** Decouple to GND via **0.01 µF** to prevent noise from modulating thresholds.
- ⭐ **Discharge transistor (Pin 7):** ON during LOW output (discharges C); OFF during HIGH output.
- ⭐ **KTU Numerical Tip:** Use $R$ in $\Omega$ and $C$ in Farads to get $T$ directly in seconds. Convert using $1 \text{ μF} = 10^{-6} \text{ F}$, $1 \text{ k}\Omega = 10^3 \text{ }\Omega$.
- ⭐ **50% duty cycle trick:** Add a diode in parallel with R2 (cathode toward Pin 7) to bypass R2 during charging.
- ⭐ **Frequency Range:** Practical astable operation: ~0.1 Hz to 500 kHz.
- ⭐ **Pulse width range (monostable):** ~10 µs to several seconds.
- ⭐ **Differentiator at trigger input:** For monostable, the trigger pulse width should be **much less than** the desired output pulse width.
- ⭐ **Compare with Bistable:** Bistable has TWO stable states, no RC timing — used as a flip-flop (not in KTU Module 4 syllabus, but useful for context).
